Training loss plateaus while validation loss keeps dropping
Training loss keeps decreasing while validation loss stops improving and begins to rise
quiz.explanation: Overfitting is the train/val *gap* opening up: the model keeps fitting the training set while validation stops benefiting and turns upward. Both falling together is healthy training; both rising signals a broken setup (e.g. too-high learning rate), not overfitting.

quiz.q: Weight decay turns the SGD step into $w \leftarrow (1-\eta\lambda)\,w - \eta\,\nabla L$, where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $\lambda$ the decay strength. What does the extra $(1-\eta\lambda)$ factor do each step?
options: It increases the weights proportionally to push them away from zero
It multiplies every weight by a number slightly less than 1, shrinking it toward zero before the gradient step is applied
It rescales the gradient so the effective learning rate grows over time
It injects random noise into each weight
quiz.explanation: For typical small $\eta\lambda$, the factor $(1-\eta\lambda)$ is just under 1, so each weight is nudged toward the origin every step -- literal 'decay'. It does not push weights outward (that would be the opposite sign) and it is not a gradient rescaling or noise term.

quiz.q: In plain SGD, adding an L2 penalty and applying weight decay produce the same update, but in Adam they do not. Why, and what is the standard fix?
options: They differ because Adam uses a larger learning rate; simply lower the learning rate
They differ because Adam lacks momentum; add momentum to restore equivalence
Adam's adaptive per-parameter scaling rescales the L2 gradient unevenly, breaking the equivalence; AdamW decouples weight decay from the adaptive step to fix it
They are actually identical in Adam too, and AdamW is merely a renaming
quiz.explanation: Adam divides each parameter's update by a running estimate of its gradient magnitude. When L2 is implemented by adding $\lambda w$ to the gradient, that decay term passes through the same uneven division, so weights are decayed inconsistently. AdamW applies the $(1-\eta\lambda)$ shrink separately from the adaptive step. Lowering the learning rate or adding momentum does not address the coupling.

quiz.q: You trained with dropout $p=0.5$ and deploy the model, but you forget to call `model.eval()`. What happens at inference?
options: Dropout stays active, so neurons keep being randomly zeroed and the same input gives different, noisy predictions
Nothing changes, because dropout automatically turns itself off after training
The model crashes because the dropout mask is undefined at test time
Predictions are correct but exactly twice as large as they should be
quiz.explanation: Dropout only switches off when the module is in eval mode. Left in train mode it keeps sampling masks, so outputs become stochastic and non-reproducible. It does not auto-disable, does not crash, and (because inverted dropout already scaled during training) the issue is *noise*, not a fixed 2x magnitude error.

quiz.q: With inverted dropout and keep-probability $1-p$, surviving neurons are multiplied by $\frac{1}{1-p}$ during training. The purpose of this scaling is to:
options: Make dropped neurons recover faster in later epochs
Keep the expected output magnitude of each layer the same as with no dropout, so inference needs no rescaling
Increase the regularization strength as $p$ grows
Compensate for a learning rate that is too small
quiz.explanation: Dropping a fraction $p$ of neurons reduces the summed output; multiplying survivors by $\frac{1}{1-p}$ restores the expected value (with $p=0.5$, multiply by 2). Because the correction happens in training, test time uses all neurons with no scaling. It is not about recovery speed, regularization strength, or the learning rate.

quiz.q: Dropout is said to prevent 'co-adaptation.' What does that mean?
options: It stops two layers from having the same number of neurons
It prevents the weights of different layers from ever becoming correlated
It synchronizes all neuron activations across the mini-batch
It prevents neurons from relying on specific other neurons being present, forcing each feature to be independently useful
quiz.explanation: Because any neuron may be dropped on any pass, no neuron can depend on a particular partner always being there, so features must each carry useful signal on their own. It has nothing to do with matching layer sizes, synchronizing activations, or forbidding correlations.

quiz.q: Why is training with dropout described as training an ensemble? For a layer of $n$ neurons with $p=0.5$:
options: Each forward pass samples a different sub-network (a different mask); there are up to $2^n$ possible sub-networks, and full-network inference approximates averaging them
It literally stores $2^n$ separate copies of the network in memory and trains each one
It trains $n$ independent networks, one per neuron
Ensembling only happens at test time when masks are freshly re-randomized
quiz.explanation: Each mask selects a subset of active neurons -- a sub-network -- and with $n$ independently kept/dropped neurons there are up to $2^n$ of them, all sharing weights. Using all neurons at test time approximates averaging that ensemble. No separate copies are stored, it is not one network per neuron, and inference *removes* randomness rather than adding it.

quiz.q: Early stopping is called 'implicit regularization' with an effect similar to L2. The best explanation is:
options: It explicitly adds $\lambda\sum w_i^2$ to the loss function
It gradually shrinks the learning rate as training proceeds
Weights start small and grow during training, so halting early limits how far they travel in parameter space, keeping them small -- much like L2's pull toward small weights
It permanently removes neurons from the network like dropout
quiz.explanation: Early stopping never modifies the loss, yet by stopping before weights grow large it produces an L2-like shrinkage -- 'less distance travelled' from the small initialization means less capacity used. Adding $\lambda\sum w_i^2$ is *explicit* L2; shrinking the learning rate is a schedule; removing neurons is dropout.

quiz.q: Which data augmentation would VIOLATE the label-preserving constraint?
options: Horizontally flipping a cat photo in a cat-vs-dog classifier
Vertically flipping the digit '6' in a handwritten-digit classifier, where it now looks like a '9'
Randomly cropping a dog photo for a dog detector
Applying mild color jitter to a natural image
quiz.explanation: An augmentation must never change the correct label. Vertically flipping a '6' produces a '9', so the image and label disagree. A flipped cat is still a cat, a cropped dog is still a dog, and color jitter does not change the class -- all label-preserving.

quiz.q: BatchNorm mainly stabilizes training, but it also regularizes. Where does the regularization come from?
options: It permanently zeros a fraction of activations, just like dropout
It adds a $\lambda\sum w_i^2$ penalty to the loss

The batch mean $\mu_B$ and variance $\sigma_B^2$ are noisy estimates from a random mini-batch, so each sample's normalization jitters with its batch-mates; smaller batches give noisier stats and thus stronger regularization
It steadily shrinks the learning rate during training
quiz.explanation: Because the per-batch statistics are estimated from a small random sample, each example is normalized slightly differently depending on its batch neighbors -- noise that acts as a mild regularizer, stronger for smaller batches. At test time fixed running averages remove this noise. BatchNorm does not zero activations, add an L2 term, or schedule the learning rate.

quiz.q: Your image classifier is overfitting. Following the course's decision framework, which should you generally try FIRST, before reaching for dropout?
options: Immediately add dropout $=0.7$ to every layer
Remove early stopping so the model can train longer
Increase the model size so it fits the data better
Data augmentation (flips, crops, rotation, color jitter), which adds free, label-preserving diversity
quiz.explanation: The framework front-loads cheap, side-effect-light fixes: data augmentation first, then weight decay and early stopping, and only then dropout. A huge dropout rate can flip overfitting into underfitting, growing the model worsens overfitting, and removing early stopping lets it overfit more.

descriptive.q: Starting from the L2-penalized loss, show how the SGD update acquires its 'weight decay' factor and explain geometrically what that factor does. Then explain why L2 and weight decay are NOT equivalent in Adam, and what AdamW changes.
descriptive.answer:
**1. From penalty to update.** The L2-penalized loss is
$$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{original}} + \lambda \sum_i w_i^2$$
where $L_{\text{original}}$ is the data-fitting loss, $w_i$ is each weight, and $\lambda$ sets the penalty strength. Differentiate with respect to a single weight $w$. The penalty $\lambda w^2$ has derivative $2\lambda w$ (the constant 2 is usually absorbed into $\lambda$, so we write $\lambda w$):
$$\nabla L_{\text{total}} = \nabla L_{\text{original}} + \lambda w$$
Substitute into the plain SGD step $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\nabla L_{\text{total}}$ (with learning rate $\eta$):
$$w \leftarrow w - \eta\big(\nabla L_{\text{original}} + \lambda w\big) = (1 - \eta\lambda)\,w - \eta\,\nabla L_{\text{original}}$$

**2. Geometric meaning.** The factor $(1-\eta\lambda)$ is a number just under 1. *Before* the usual gradient step, it multiplies the weight vector down, pulling it **toward the origin** (the zero-weight point) in parameter space. Repeat every step and weights continuously shrink unless the data-gradient actively pushes them back out -- hence *weight decay*. Equivalently, L2 keeps the solution inside a small 'budget' ball around the origin.

**3. Why it breaks in Adam.** Adam does not use one learning rate; it divides each parameter's update by a running estimate of that parameter's own gradient magnitude (an adaptive per-parameter scale). If you implement L2 by adding $\lambda w$ to the gradient, that decay term gets pushed through the **same adaptive division**, so parameters with large historical gradients are decayed *less* and those with small gradients are decayed *more*. The clean 'multiply every weight by $(1-\eta\lambda)$' behavior is lost -- decay becomes uneven and entangled with the optimizer's scaling.

**4. AdamW's fix.** AdamW **decouples** weight decay: it performs the Adam update using only the data gradient, then **separately** multiplies the weights by $(1-\eta\lambda)$. That restores the uniform shrink-every-weight-equally behavior.

> [!KEY] In SGD, L2 penalty and weight decay are identical. In adaptive optimizers like Adam they are not -- always use **AdamW** for proper, decoupled weight decay.

descriptive.q: Walk through inverted dropout from training to inference. Why do we scale surviving neurons by $\frac{1}{1-p}$ during training, what does the network do at test time, and what concretely goes wrong if you forget `model.eval()`? Use $p=0.5$ in a worked example.
descriptive.answer:
**Training.** On every forward pass, each neuron's output is set to zero with probability $p$ (the drop probability), and the survivors are multiplied by $\frac{1}{1-p}$, where $1-p$ is the keep-probability. A *fresh* random mask is drawn each pass.

**Why the $\frac{1}{1-p}$ scaling?** Dropping neurons reduces the total signal leaving a layer. We want the layer's **expected output magnitude** to stay constant so the next layer sees a consistent scale whether or not dropout is active.

> [!EXAMPLE] Take $p=0.5$. On average half the neurons are zeroed, so a layer's summed output is roughly **halved**. The keep-probability is $1-p=0.5$, so multiply each survivor by $\frac{1}{0.5}=2$. Drop half, double the rest, and the expected output returns to its original size.

Formally, if a neuron's clean output is $a$, its expected contribution after dropout is
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{output}] = (1-p)\cdot \frac{a}{1-p} + p\cdot 0 = a,$$
i.e. unchanged -- the first term is 'kept (prob. $1-p$) and rescaled by $\frac{1}{1-p}$' and the second is 'dropped (prob. $p$), contributing zero.'

**Inference.** Because the scale was *already* corrected during training, at test time we simply **use all neurons, with no dropping and no scaling**. The output is deterministic: the same input always yields the same prediction. That is the whole point of *inverted* dropout -- push the correction into training so inference stays clean.

**Forgetting `model.eval()`.** In PyTorch, dropout only deactivates when the module is in eval mode. If you forget, dropout stays active at test time: neurons keep getting randomly zeroed, so repeated forward passes of the *same* input produce **different, noisy** outputs, and your evaluation metrics become unreliable. (The same `eval()` call also switches BatchNorm to running averages, so forgetting it corrupts those statistics too.)

> [!KEY] Inverted dropout corrects the scale *during training* (survivors $\times \frac{1}{1-p}$) so inference needs no changes -- but you must still call `model.eval()`.

descriptive.q: Compare dropout, L2/weight decay, and early stopping as regularizers. For each, explain the mechanism by which it constrains the model, and show how all three connect to the common theme of 'keep the model simple.'
descriptive.answer:
All three fight the same enemy -- a high-capacity network that would rather **memorize** than generalize -- but they pull different levers.

| Technique | Mechanism | What it constrains | Cost / hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L2 / weight decay** | add $\lambda\sum w_i^2$ to the loss (equivalently multiply weights by $(1-\eta\lambda)$ each step) | the **magnitude** of weights, pulling them toward zero | one knob $\lambda$ |
| **Dropout** | randomly zero neurons (prob. $p$) each training pass | **co-adaptation** -- forces redundant, distributed features; acts as an ensemble | one knob $p$; adds train-time noise |
| **Early stopping** | halt when val loss stops improving, restore best checkpoint | how **far weights travel** from their small initialization | almost none (just `patience`) |

**The common thread -- 'keep it simple.'**
- **L2** makes simplicity explicit: small weights give smoother, less wiggly decision boundaries, i.e. a simpler function.
- **Early stopping** reaches the *same place by a different road*: weights start small and grow during training, so stopping early keeps them small -- an **implicit** L2-like shrinkage. Limiting distance travelled in parameter space limits capacity used.
- **Dropout** enforces simplicity structurally: by killing co-adaptation it stops the network from building fragile, over-specific feature combinations, and its ensemble-averaging effect smooths predictions (lower variance).

> [!INTUITION] L2 says "do not let any weight get big," early stopping says "do not train long enough for weights to get big," and dropout says "do not let any neuron become indispensable." Three different sentences, one message: **use less of your capacity so you learn the pattern, not the noise.**

In practice they **stack** -- L2 + early stopping + dropout together is common and complementary.

descriptive.q: Why is dropout described as an ensemble method, and how does that reduce overfitting? Connect it to Random Forests, quantify the number of sub-networks, and explain what happens if the drop probability $p$ is set too high.
descriptive.answer:
**The ensemble view.** Each training forward pass draws a different random dropout mask, so a different **subset of neurons is active** -- effectively a different **sub-network**. Crucially, the weights are *shared* across all these sub-networks, so training with dropout simultaneously nudges an enormous family of related sub-networks toward good predictions.

**How many sub-networks?** For a layer of $n$ neurons, each independently kept or dropped, there are $2^n$ possible on/off patterns -- so up to $2^n$ distinct sub-networks (an exponential count). You train that entire ensemble for the price of one network.

**Inference = averaging.** At test time you turn dropout off and use **all** neurons (with the inverted-dropout scaling already baked in). Using the full network approximates **averaging the predictions** of those sub-networks, and averaging many models is exactly what reduces **variance**, hence overfitting.

> [!ANALOGY] This is the **Random Forest** idea from classical ML: train many weak, decorrelated trees and average them into one strong, low-variance predictor. Dropout delivers the same variance reduction, except the 'trees' are sub-networks living inside a single shared network, generated for free.

**Why dropout also kills co-adaptation.** Because any neuron may vanish on any pass, no neuron can rely on a specific partner always being present. Features must be **independently useful**, producing redundant, distributed representations that do not shatter when one unit goes missing.

**If $p$ is too high.** Higher $p$ means stronger regularization, but past a point you zero so much of the network on each pass that too little capacity remains to learn the signal -- the model **underfits** (high bias; both train and validation accuracy drop). The drop rate is a dial: too low does nothing, too high cripples learning. Common values are $p=0.2$ at the input and $p=0.5$ for hidden fully-connected layers.

descriptive.q: Explain why BatchNorm has a regularizing side-effect even though its main job is stabilizing training. Address the noise mechanism, the effect of batch size, why test-time behavior is deterministic, and why BatchNorm and dropout are usually not combined.
descriptive.answer:
**Main job vs. side-effect.** BatchNorm's primary purpose is to **stabilize training** and allow higher learning rates by normalizing each layer's activations. The regularization is a **byproduct** of *how* it computes that normalization.

**The noise mechanism.** For each mini-batch, BatchNorm normalizes using the batch's own statistics:
$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2 + \epsilon}}, \qquad y_i = \gamma\hat{x}_i + \beta$$
where $\mu_B$ and $\sigma_B^2$ are the **mean and variance of the current mini-batch**, $\epsilon$ prevents division by zero, and $\gamma,\beta$ are a learned scale and shift. The catch: $\mu_B$ and $\sigma_B^2$ come from a *random handful* of samples (e.g. 32), so they are **noisy estimates** of the true statistics. Each example therefore gets normalized slightly differently depending on **which other examples share its batch** -- randomness that behaves like injected noise, conceptually similar to dropout's random masking.

**Batch-size effect.** Smaller batches mean fewer samples per mean/variance estimate, hence **noisier statistics** and **stronger** regularization. Very large batches give nearly exact statistics, so the regularizing noise (and effect) shrinks.

**Why test time is deterministic.** During evaluation BatchNorm stops using per-batch statistics and switches to **fixed running averages** accumulated during training. With fixed statistics there is no batch-dependent jitter, so the same input always yields the same output -- exactly analogous to dropout switching off at test time. (This is also why you must call `model.eval()`.)

**Why not combine BN + dropout.** Both inject training-time noise and both go quiet at test time, so stacking them is partly redundant. Worse, they can interact badly: dropout changes the variance of the activations that BatchNorm is trying to estimate, creating a *train/test mismatch* in the running statistics. In practice BatchNorm already provides enough regularization that modern nets (e.g. ResNets) use little or no dropout, and the two are usually **not** placed together.

> [!KEY] BatchNorm regularizes because mini-batch statistics are noisy; smaller batch = more noise = more regularization; test time uses deterministic running averages. That overlap with dropout is why they are rarely combined.

descriptive.q: You train an image classifier and observe 99% training accuracy but 72% validation accuracy. Diagnose what is happening, propose an ordered remediation plan following the course's decision framework, and for each step say what it does to the bias-variance tradeoff.
descriptive.answer:
**Diagnosis.** A 27-point gap between training (99%) and validation (72%) accuracy is the textbook signature of **overfitting**, i.e. **high variance**: the model fits the training set almost perfectly but fails to generalize. It is *not* underfitting (training accuracy is high), so the cure is to **reduce variance**, accepting a small increase in bias.

[[fig:bias-variance]]

**Ordered remediation plan (course decision framework):**

1. **Data augmentation first** -- random flips, crops, rotation, color jitter. The model effectively sees more, more varied data. *Bias-variance:* lowers variance (varied data is harder to memorize) at negligible bias cost; the cheapest, highest-value first move.
2. **Weight decay (AdamW, $\lambda \approx 10^{-4}$)** -- penalize large weights for smoother boundaries. *Bias-variance:* trades a little bias for lower variance; too-large $\lambda$ over-shifts toward bias and underfits.
3. **Early stopping (patience 10 to 30)** -- halt when validation accuracy stops improving and restore the best checkpoint. *Bias-variance:* caps how far weights grow, so lower variance and slightly higher bias; nearly free.
4. **Dropout ($p=0.3$ to $0.5$ on FC layers)** -- if still overfitting. Forces distributed features plus ensemble averaging. *Bias-variance:* lowers variance; too-high $p$ raises bias and underfits.
5. **More data or a smaller model** -- collecting real data is the ultimate variance reducer; shrinking the model lowers capacity (raises bias, lowers variance).

> [!WARNING] Do not reach for an aggressive `dropout=0.7` everywhere as your *first* move. The framework deliberately front-loads augmentation, weight decay, and early stopping because they are cheap and side-effect-light; dropout adds training complexity and, if overdone, flips you from overfitting straight into underfitting.

> [!KEY] High train / low val = high variance = overfitting. Fix it by **reducing variance** in cheap-to-expensive order: augmentation, then weight decay, then early stopping, then dropout, then more data / smaller model.

topics.title: Initialization & Normalization
topics.summary: Why deep networks live or die at the very first step: weight initialization. We see how gradients vanish or explode through repeated multiplication, fix it with Xavier and He initialization, stabilize activations with BatchNorm and LayerNorm, and learn to debug broken training with gradient checking and a systematic checklist.

notes.heading: The Core Problem: Gradients Are Products
notes.body:
Before we talk about clever fixes, let's understand the disease. When a network learns, it has to send an error signal from the very end (the loss) all the way back to the very first layer's weights. The catch: that signal isn't *added* layer to layer, it is **multiplied** layer to layer.

> [!ANALOGY] It's the telephone game. A message is whispered down a line of 50 people. If every person makes it a little quieter, by the end you hear nothing (vanishing). If every person shouts it a little louder, by the end it's deafening noise (exploding). The signal degrades with every relay.

When backpropagation computes how the loss $L$ depends on an early weight $w_1$, the chain rule strings together one factor per layer:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a_n}\cdot\frac{\partial a_n}{\partial a_{n-1}}\cdot\frac{\partial a_{n-1}}{\partial a_{n-2}}\cdots\frac{\partial a_2}{\partial w_1}$$

> [!MATH] In words: "how much the loss changes when I nudge the first weight" equals a long **product** of "how much each layer's output changes when the layer before it changes." Here $L$ is the loss (how wrong we are), $a_n$ is the activation (output) of layer $n$, and each fraction $\frac{\partial a_k}{\partial a_{k-1}}$ is one link in the chain — how strongly layer $k$ reacts to layer $k-1$.

The danger is purely about the **size** of those factors. Multiply many numbers together and the result runs away from you fast:

| Factor per layer | After 10 layers | After 50 layers |
|---|---|---|
| $0.5$ | $0.001$ | $\approx 10^{-15}$ (vanished) |
| $0.9$ | $0.35$ | $0.005$ |
| $1.0$ | $1.0$ | $1.0$ (perfect) |
| $1.1$ | $2.6$ | $117$ |
| $1.5$ | $57.7$ | $\approx 637{,}621$ (exploded) |

[[fig:vanishing-exploding]]

> [!KEY] Even *slightly* away from $1.0$, repeated multiplication causes **exponential** growth or decay. This single fact is why networks in the 1990s couldn't reliably go beyond 3–5 layers.

The two failure modes feel very different in practice:

| | Vanishing gradients | Exploding gradients |
|---|---|---|
| Symptom | Early layers barely update; loss **plateaus** | Loss jumps to **NaN** |
| Speed | Slow, quiet | Fast, dramatic (first few epochs) |
| Detectability | Sneaky — looks like "just not learning" | Obvious — it crashes |

> [!WARNING] Vanishing is arguably the **worse** of the two, precisely because it doesn't crash. At least a NaN screams "something is broken." A vanishing gradient just silently wastes your time while the early layers learn nothing.

notes.heading: Why Random Init Fails, and the One Goal That Fixes It
notes.body:
So the factors in that product need to stay near $1$. Where do those factors come from? Largely from the **weights** and how they interact with the activations. If we pick weights badly, the *outputs* of each layer either shrink toward zero or blow up as the signal moves forward — and the gradients follow the same fate backward.

> [!INTUITION] Picture the "spread" (variance) of a layer's outputs as the volume knob on the signal. We don't want each layer to crank the volume up or down. We want every layer to pass the signal along at **roughly the same volume** it received. Keep the spread constant, and neither vanishing nor exploding can build up.

[[fig:weight-init|Three init strategies pushed through a deep stack: too-small weights collapse the spread toward zero (vanish), too-large weights blow it up (explode), and a well-chosen variance keeps it flat across layers.]]

That gives us a precise design goal:

$$\mathrm{Var}(\text{activation at layer } l) \approx \mathrm{Var}(\text{activation at layer } l-1)$$

> [!MATH] "$\mathrm{Var}$" means variance — a measure of how spread out the numbers are. This says: the spread of one layer's outputs should match the spread of the previous layer's outputs. No shrinking, no growing.

Now we can actually *solve* for the right weights. Take a plain linear layer (no activation yet), $y = Wx$, where $W$ holds the weights and $x$ is the incoming vector. A standard variance calculation gives:

$$\mathrm{Var}(y) = n_{in}\cdot \mathrm{Var}(w)\cdot \mathrm{Var}(x)$$

> [!MATH] Here $n_{in}$ is the **fan-in** — the number of inputs feeding into each neuron. $\mathrm{Var}(w)$ is how spread out we make the weights, and $\mathrm{Var}(x)$ is the spread of the incoming signal. The output spread is the input spread, scaled by the fan-in times the weight spread.

To force $\mathrm{Var}(y) = \mathrm{Var}(x)$ (keep the volume constant), the $n_{in}\cdot\mathrm{Var}(w)$ part must equal $1$:

$$\mathrm{Var}(w) = \frac{1}{n_{in}}$$

> [!KEY] The whole field of initialization is basically this one idea: **set the weight variance so the per-layer scaling factor is 1.** More inputs ($n_{in}$ large) means each weight must be smaller, so the sum doesn't blow up. Everything below (Xavier, He) is a refinement of $\mathrm{Var}(w) = \frac{1}{n_{in}}$.

notes.heading: Xavier (Glorot) Initialization — for tanh and sigmoid
notes.body:
The simple rule $\mathrm{Var}(w) = \frac{1}{n_{in}}$ only worried about the **forward** pass (keeping activations stable). But gradients flow **backward** too, and backward the relevant count is $n_{out}$ — the number of outputs a neuron feeds into. Xavier initialization splits the difference so that *both* directions stay stable.

> [!INTUITION] Think of a neuron as a doorway. The forward pass cares how many people walk *in* ($n_{in}$); the backward pass cares how many rooms they walk *out* to ($n_{out}$). Xavier sizes the doorway using both counts so traffic flows smoothly in both directions.

$$\mathrm{Var}(w) = \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}$$

> [!MATH] $n_{in}$ is the number of inputs into the layer; $n_{out}$ is the number of outputs. We use their **average** (that's what $\frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$ encodes) so neither the forward signal nor the backward gradient gets squashed.

In practice you draw weights either from a normal or a uniform distribution with that variance:

$$w \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\ \tfrac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}\right) \qquad\text{or}\qquad w \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left(-\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{n_{in}+n_{out}}},\ \sqrt{\tfrac{6}{n_{in}+n_{out}}}\right)$$

> [!MATH] $\mathcal{N}(0, v)$ means "random numbers centered at $0$ with variance $v$" (a bell curve). $\mathcal{U}(-a, a)$ means "random numbers spread evenly between $-a$ and $a$." Both are tuned to give the same target variance.

> [!KEY] Xavier is designed for **symmetric** activations like $\tanh$ and sigmoid — functions whose derivative is roughly $1$ near zero, so they don't systematically shrink the signal. This was the 2010 breakthrough (Glorot and Bengio) that first made 5+ layer networks train reliably.

> [!WARNING] Xavier assumes the activation doesn't throw away half the signal. ReLU does exactly that — which is why Xavier + ReLU still vanishes, and why we need He init next.

notes.heading: He (Kaiming) Initialization — the modern default for ReLU
notes.body:
ReLU is the workhorse activation of modern networks, but it has a quirk that breaks Xavier's careful math: ReLU sets every negative input to exactly zero.

> [!INTUITION] ReLU is a one-way valve. Roughly half the activations are negative, and ReLU clamps all of them to $0$. So after a ReLU, only about **half** the signal's "energy" survives — the spread (variance) is cut in half at every layer. Stack 20 ReLU layers tuned with Xavier and the signal quietly halves, halves, halves... right back into the vanishing regime.

He et al. (2015) fixed this with a one-word change: **double** the variance to pre-compensate for the halving.

$$\mathrm{Var}(w) = \frac{2}{n_{in}}$$

> [!MATH] Same $n_{in}$ (fan-in, the number of inputs) as the basic rule, but the numerator is $2$ instead of $1$. That extra factor of $2$ exactly cancels ReLU's habit of killing half the activations, so the spread stays constant layer after layer.

> [!EXAMPLE] A layer with $n_{in}=256$ inputs. The plain rule gives $\mathrm{Var}(w)=\tfrac{1}{256}\approx 0.0039$. He gives $\mathrm{Var}(w)=\tfrac{2}{256}\approx 0.0078$ — twice as large, so that after ReLU eats half of it, the surviving spread lands right back at the target. Note that $0.01$ (a popular "small random" choice) is in the same ballpark but is *not* tied to $n_{in}$, so it fails as the network gets deep or wide.

The two rules side by side:

| Activation | Use | Weight variance |
|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid / Tanh (symmetric) | Xavier (Glorot) | $\mathrm{Var}(w) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$ |
| ReLU / LeakyReLU / ELU | He (Kaiming) | $\mathrm{Var}(w) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}$ |

> [!KEY] Since ~99% of modern networks use a ReLU-family activation, **He initialization is the default you reach for.** Good news: PyTorch already initializes `nn.Linear` and `nn.Conv2d` with Kaiming (He) uniform, so you rarely change it.

> [!EXAM] A favorite exam framing: "Good init doesn't *guarantee* convergence, but **bad init guarantees failure**." Initialization gets you to a sane starting point on the loss surface; the optimizer still has to do the rest. To override defaults in PyTorch you'd write `nn.init.kaiming_normal_(layer.weight, mode='fan_in')` or `nn.init.xavier_normal_(layer.weight)`.

notes.heading: Batch Normalization: Standardize, Then Let the Network Decide
notes.body:
Good initialization sets the volume correctly at the *start*. But as training proceeds and early layers change their weights, the later layers suddenly see inputs with a different mean and spread than before — a moving target the slides call **internal covariate shift**. Batch Normalization (BN) re-stabilizes the signal *during* training, not just at init.

> [!ANALOGY] You're a chef and your supplier's ingredients vary a little every day — slightly saltier, slightly riper. BN is the prep step that **standardizes every ingredient to a known baseline** before you cook, so your recipe behaves the same no matter what came in today.

For each mini-batch, BN does two things — first standardize, then re-scale with *learned* knobs:

$$\mathrm{BN}(x) = \gamma\cdot\frac{x - \mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$$

> [!MATH] $\mu_B$ is the **mean** of this feature over the current batch, $\sigma_B^2$ is its **variance** over the batch, and $\epsilon$ is a tiny constant (like $10^{-5}$) added so we never divide by zero. The fraction turns the values into a standardized "z-score" (mean $0$, spread $1$). Then $\gamma$ (gamma, a learned scale) and $\beta$ (beta, a learned shift) let the network rescale and re-center to whatever actually helps.

[[fig:batchnorm]]

> [!EXAMPLE] One feature, batch of 4 values $[2, 4, 6, 8]$.
> 1. Mean: $\mu_B = \frac{2+4+6+8}{4} = 5$.
> 2. Variance: $\sigma_B^2 = \frac{(2-5)^2+(4-5)^2+(6-5)^2+(8-5)^2}{4} = \frac{9+1+1+9}{4}=5$.
> 3. Standardize: $\hat{x} = \frac{x-5}{\sqrt{5}} = [-1.34, -0.45, 0.45, 1.34]$.
> 4. Scale and shift with $\gamma=2,\ \beta=1$: $y = 2\hat{x}+1 = [-1.68, 0.10, 1.90, 3.68]$.
> This happens **independently per feature** — column by column across the batch.

> [!KEY] Why bother with $\gamma$ and $\beta$? Forcing every activation to be exactly mean-$0$, spread-$1$ might throw away useful information. So we let the network **learn** the best scale ($\gamma$) and shift ($\beta$). If it sets $\gamma=\sigma_B$ and $\beta=\mu_B$, BN becomes a **no-op** — it undoes itself. That's why "BN can never hurt: at worst it learns to do nothing."

**Where to place it:** the standard pattern is `Linear → BatchNorm → ReLU` — normalize *before* the activation so ReLU receives well-distributed inputs. And drop the bias in that Linear layer (`bias=False`), because BN's learned $\beta$ already plays the role of a bias; keeping both is redundant.

> [!WARNING] **Train vs. eval is the #1 BN bug.** During training BN uses the current batch's $\mu_B, \sigma_B^2$ (noisy, different every batch) and quietly accumulates *running averages*. At inference you must call `model.eval()` so BN switches to those running averages. Forget it, and your predictions depend on whichever other examples happen to share the test batch — non-deterministic and unreliable.

notes.heading: Layer Normalization, and BN vs. LN
notes.body:
Batch Normalization has a structural weakness: it needs a **batch**. Its statistics are computed *across the samples* in the mini-batch, so every sample's output secretly depends on its batch-mates.

| BN limitation | Why it bites |
|---|---|
| Batch size $=1$ | Can't compute a meaningful mean/variance from one sample |
| Small batches | Noisy statistics, unstable training |
| Variable-length sequences | Each timestep would need its own stats |
| Couples samples | Output of sample A depends on its random neighbors in the batch |

Layer Normalization (LN) sidesteps all of this by changing **which direction** we average over. Instead of normalizing one feature across the batch, LN normalizes one sample across all of *its own* features.

[[fig:norm-types]]

> [!INTUITION] Imagine a spreadsheet where each **row** is one sample and each **column** is one feature. BN goes **down the columns** (one feature, all samples). LN goes **across the rows** (one sample, all its features). LN never looks at other samples, so it doesn't care how big the batch is.

$$\mathrm{LN}(x) = \gamma\cdot\frac{x - \mu_{\text{features}}}{\sqrt{\sigma_{\text{features}}^2 + \epsilon}} + \beta$$

> [!MATH] Same shape as BN, but now $\mu_{\text{features}}$ and $\sigma_{\text{features}}^2$ are the mean and variance computed **across that single sample's features** — not across the batch. $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are still learned scale and shift, $\epsilon$ still guards the division.

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Normalizes over | Each feature, across the batch | Each sample, across its features |
| Needs multiple samples? | Yes | No (works at batch size $1$) |
| Train vs. eval differ? | Yes (running averages at test) | No — identical behavior |
| Couples samples? | Yes | No (self-contained) |
| Home turf | CNNs / vision with big batches | Transformers, RNNs, variable-length sequences |

> [!KEY] Because Transformers process **variable-length sequences** and often run at **batch size 1** during inference, BN's batch dependence would break them. LN has *no* batch dependence and behaves the same at train and test time — which is why virtually every Transformer (BERT, GPT, ...) uses LayerNorm.

notes.heading: Gradient Checking: Catch a Broken Backward Pass
notes.body:
Suppose you wrote a custom layer and its `backward` (the analytical gradient). How do you know the math is right and not silently off by a sign or a factor? **Gradient checking** verifies it against a brute-force numerical estimate.

> [!INTUITION] The gradient is just "how much does the output change if I nudge this input a tiny bit?" So nudge the input up by a hair, nudge it down by a hair, see how much the output moved, and divide. If your hand-derived formula disagrees with that measurement, your formula is wrong.

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \approx \frac{f(x+\epsilon) - f(x-\epsilon)}{2\epsilon}\qquad(\epsilon \approx 10^{-5})$$

> [!MATH] This is the **central difference**. $f(x+\epsilon)$ is the output after nudging the input **up** by a tiny $\epsilon$; $f(x-\epsilon)$ is the output after nudging it **down**. The difference divided by the total nudge $2\epsilon$ is the slope. Using both sides (not just $x+\epsilon$) makes the estimate far more accurate.

Then compare your analytical gradient to this numerical one with a **relative** error (so the scale of the numbers doesn't matter):

$$\text{relative error} = \frac{|g_{\text{analytical}} - g_{\text{numerical}}|}{\max(|g_{\text{analytical}}|,\ |g_{\text{numerical}}|)}$$

> [!MATH] $g_{\text{analytical}}$ is the gradient your code computed; $g_{\text{numerical}}$ is the brute-force estimate. The top is how far apart they are; dividing by the larger magnitude turns it into a fraction, so we judge *proportional* agreement, not raw distance.

| Relative error | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $< 10^{-7}$ | Excellent |
| $< 10^{-5}$ | Acceptable |
| $> 10^{-3}$ | Something is wrong |

> [!WARNING] Gradient checking costs **two forward passes per parameter** — far too slow to run during training. It is a **debugging tool only**: turn it on to validate a custom backward, confirm it passes, then turn it off. PyTorch ships `torch.autograd.gradcheck` for exactly this.

notes.heading: Debugging Training Systematically
notes.body:
When a network won't train, the instinct is to randomly tweak the learning rate and pray. Don't. There is a **checklist**, and you follow it in order.

A first, almost-free sanity check: **what should the loss be before any training happens?** For a $k$-class classifier with random weights, the model should output roughly uniform probabilities ($\tfrac{1}{k}$ each), so the cross-entropy loss should be about $\ln(k)$.

> [!MATH] $\ln(k)$ is the natural logarithm of the number of classes $k$. For $10$ classes, $\ln(10)\approx 2.3$. If your starting loss is wildly higher than this, something is broken **before training even begins** — likely a bad init, a wrong loss function, mislabeled data, or applying softmax twice.

> [!EXAMPLE] You train a 10-class classifier and the initial loss prints as $4.5$ instead of $\approx 2.3$. That's a red flag: the most common culprits are a doubled softmax (softmax inside the model *and* inside the loss), the wrong loss function, or scrambled labels — not a learning-rate problem.

Common failure modes map cleanly to causes and fixes:

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Loss stuck near $\ln(k)$ | Not learning at all | Check data pipeline, loss fn, learning rate |
| Loss $\to$ NaN | Exploding gradients | Lower LR, gradient clipping, check init |
| Loss plateaus early | Vanishing gradients | Check activations, He init, skip connections |
| Train $\downarrow$, val $\uparrow$ | Overfitting | Regularize (dropout, weight decay, early stopping) |

The full checklist — **follow in order, never skip ahead:**

1. **Overfit ONE batch.** Take ~8 samples and train to *zero* loss. If the model can't even memorize 8 examples, stop — something is fundamentally broken. This is always the first move when a model isn't learning.
2. **Check tensor shapes.** Print shapes at every layer; silent broadcasting mismatches cause baffling bugs.
3. **Verify loss at init** $\approx \ln(k)$. A wrong value means bad init or a wrong loss.
4. **Gradient check.** Compare numerical vs. analytical gradients to catch custom-backward bugs.
5. **Train on a small subset** (~100 samples). It should overfit quickly, confirming the pipeline works end to end.
6. **Scale up.** Only now add full data, regularization, and hyperparameter tuning.

> [!KEY] The three pillars of this whole session: **(1) Initialize right** — Xavier for tanh, He for ReLU. **(2) Normalize** — BN for vision, LN for sequences. **(3) Debug systematically** — run the checklist, and never skip step 1 (overfit one batch).

> [!EXAM] Classic quick-fire questions: "30-layer ReLU net with dead gradients in early layers — fix?" → **He initialization.** "`model.eval()` drops accuracy by 10% — why?" → **BN running statistics weren't properly accumulated during training.** "Model not learning at all — first step?" → **Overfit one batch.**

quiz.q: Every weight in a 50-layer network is set to a small constant like 0.01. During backprop, what happens to the gradients reaching the earliest layers?
options: They vanish toward zero, because each layer multiplies the signal by a factor well below 1, and that product shrinks exponentially with depth
They explode toward infinity, because tiny weights make the loss surface extremely steep
They stay roughly constant, because a fixed weight produces a fixed, stable gradient at every layer
They oscillate between large and small values from layer to layer
quiz.explanation: Gradients are a product of one factor per layer (chain rule). Weights of 0.01 make each factor far below 1, so $0.01 \times 0.01 \times \cdots$ collapses toward 0 exponentially — vanishing gradients, so early layers barely learn. The 'explode' option reverses the logic: tiny factors shrink the product, they don't enlarge it. A constant weight does NOT give a constant gradient, because the gradient is the *product* across all layers, which compounds with depth.

quiz.q: Why does Xavier (Glorot) initialization fail to keep activations stable when used with ReLU activations?
options: Xavier sets the weight variance to exactly zero for ReLU layers, killing all signal immediately
ReLU's derivative is greater than 1, so Xavier's weights make the activations explode
ReLU zeroes out all negative inputs, which halves the variance at each layer, so under Xavier's variance budget the signal still shrinks layer by layer
Xavier only works for convolutional layers, and ReLU is typically used after linear layers
quiz.explanation: Xavier was derived assuming a symmetric activation (like tanh) whose derivative is about 1 near zero, so it preserves variance. ReLU instead clamps every negative value to 0, discarding roughly half the signal and halving the variance each layer — so activations still vanish. He init fixes this by doubling the variance to $2/n_{in}$. The 'derivative greater than 1' option is wrong: ReLU's slope is 0 or 1, never above 1, so the problem is shrinking, not exploding.

quiz.q: He initialization uses Var(w) = 2/n_in while the basic variance-preserving rule is Var(w) = 1/n_in. What is the extra factor of 2 compensating for?
options: It accounts for both the forward and backward passes, like Xavier's averaging of n_in and n_out
ReLU discards about half the activations (the negatives), so doubling the weight variance restores the spread that ReLU removes
It doubles the learning rate effect, letting the network train twice as fast
It compensates for using a uniform distribution instead of a normal distribution
quiz.explanation: ReLU sets negatives to zero, cutting the variance roughly in half at each layer. Doubling the target variance ($2/n_{in}$ instead of $1/n_{in}$) pre-compensates so the post-ReLU spread lands back on target. The forward/backward-averaging description belongs to Xavier ($2/(n_{in}+n_{out})$), not to He's factor of 2. The factor has nothing to do with learning rate or the choice between uniform and normal sampling.

quiz.q: For a plain linear layer y = Wx, the output variance is Var(y) = n_in * Var(w) * Var(x). If you want each layer to preserve the spread of the signal, what must Var(w) be?
options: n_in * n_out, the total number of connections in the layer
n_in, so that wider layers get larger weights
1 / Var(x), so the weight variance cancels the input variance
1 / n_in, so that n_in * Var(w) equals 1 and Var(y) matches Var(x)
quiz.explanation: To keep $\mathrm{Var}(y)=\mathrm{Var}(x)$, the multiplier $n_{in}\cdot\mathrm{Var}(w)$ must equal 1, giving $\mathrm{Var}(w)=1/n_{in}$. More inputs means each weight must be *smaller*, not larger — so 'n_in' has it backwards. Choosing $1/\mathrm{Var}(x)$ confuses the weight spread with the input spread; the rule must not depend on the data's variance, only on the fan-in.

quiz.q: A teammate argues exploding gradients are the more dangerous failure mode because they produce NaN. Why might vanishing gradients actually be considered worse?
options: Vanishing gradients only happen with batch sizes above 1, so they are harder to reproduce
Vanishing gradients always corrupt the weights into NaN, just more slowly than exploding ones
Vanishing gradients make the learning rate increase uncontrollably
Vanishing gradients give no crash or obvious signal — the loss just plateaus and early layers silently fail to learn, so the problem is easy to miss
quiz.explanation: Exploding gradients announce themselves with a NaN, which at least tells you something is wrong. Vanishing gradients are sneaky: no crash, the loss merely plateaus and the early layers don't update — you can waste a lot of time before noticing. Vanishing gradients do NOT produce NaN (that's the exploding case), don't touch the learning rate, and are unrelated to batch size.

quiz.q: At inference time, where does a BatchNorm layer get the mean and variance it uses to normalize?
options: From the gamma and beta parameters learned during training
From the current test batch's statistics, recomputed fresh for every batch
From running averages of the mean and variance accumulated during training, not from the current test batch
From the global mean and variance of the entire test set computed in one pass
quiz.explanation: During training BN tracks running averages of $\mu_B$ and $\sigma_B^2$; at inference (after `model.eval()`) it uses those fixed averages, making predictions deterministic. Using the *current test batch* is exactly the bug you get if you forget `model.eval()` — predictions then depend on batch-mates. $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are the learned scale/shift applied *after* normalization; they are not the normalization statistics.

quiz.q: You deploy a model with BatchNorm but forget to call model.eval() before inference. What is the most likely consequence?
options: Predictions become batch-dependent and noisy, because BN normalizes using each test batch's own statistics instead of the running averages
The model raises an error and refuses to run inference at all
Gradients explode and the loss becomes NaN during inference
Inference becomes deterministic but uniformly less accurate by a fixed offset
quiz.explanation: Without `model.eval()`, BN stays in training mode and normalizes each test batch using *that batch's* mean and variance. So the same example gets different outputs depending on which other samples share its batch — non-deterministic, noisy predictions. It won't raise an error (it runs fine, just wrongly), and inference has no backprop so gradients/NaN aren't the issue. The error is variable, not a clean fixed offset — re-running on a different batch changes the result.

quiz.q: Why does BatchNorm include the learnable parameters gamma (scale) and beta (shift) after standardizing activations?
options: They are needed to compute the batch mean and variance during the forward pass
Gamma and beta replace the running averages used at inference time
Forcing every activation to mean 0 and variance 1 can discard useful information, so gamma and beta let the network learn the best scale and shift — even learning to undo the normalization entirely
They guarantee the activations always have exactly mean 0 and variance 1 regardless of the data
quiz.explanation: Rigidly fixing activations at mean 0, variance 1 may be the wrong distribution for the task, so $\gamma$ and $\beta$ give the network freedom to rescale and recenter — if it sets $\gamma=\sigma_B,\ \beta=\mu_B$ it recovers the original signal (BN becomes a no-op, 'can never hurt'). They are applied *after* standardization, so they don't compute the statistics, aren't the running averages, and the last option is backwards: $\gamma,\beta$ let BN *depart* from mean 0/variance 1, not enforce it.

quiz.q: A Transformer processes variable-length sequences and often runs at batch size 1 during inference. Why is LayerNorm preferred over BatchNorm here?
options: LayerNorm normalizes each feature across the batch, giving more stable statistics for long sequences
LayerNorm normalizes each sample across its own features, so it has no batch dependence and works fine even with a single sample
LayerNorm is faster because it skips the scale and shift parameters that BatchNorm uses
LayerNorm only works during training, so it avoids the eval-mode bug entirely
quiz.explanation: LN computes its mean and variance *across one sample's features*, so it never needs other samples — it works at batch size 1, with variable-length sequences, and behaves identically at train and test time. The second option describes BatchNorm's axis (across the batch), which is exactly what fails at batch size 1. LN still uses learnable $\gamma,\beta$, and it runs at both train and test time, so the last two options are wrong.

quiz.q: The standard pattern is Linear -> BatchNorm -> ReLU, and the tip is to set bias=False in that Linear layer. Why drop the bias?
options: The bias would double the number of running averages BatchNorm has to store
A bias term would cause BatchNorm to divide by zero during normalization
Biases must always be disabled before any ReLU activation to avoid dead neurons
BatchNorm's learned shift parameter beta already adds a per-feature offset, so a separate bias in the Linear layer is redundant
quiz.explanation: BN subtracts the batch mean and then adds its own learnable shift $\beta$, which serves the exact role of a bias. Any bias added by the preceding Linear layer is removed by the mean-subtraction and then re-added by $\beta$ anyway, so it's redundant — drop it. It doesn't cause division by zero (that's what $\epsilon$ guards), isn't a universal rule about ReLU, and doesn't affect how many running averages BN stores.

quiz.q: When gradient checking, the numerical gradient uses the central difference (f(x+eps) - f(x-eps)) / (2*eps) and is compared to the analytical gradient via relative error. Which statement is correct?
options: It is a slow debugging-only check (two forward passes per parameter); a relative error below about 1e-7 is excellent, while above 1e-3 signals a bug
It should be run every training step to keep the analytical gradients accurate
A relative error above 1e-3 is excellent agreement between the two gradients
The one-sided difference (f(x+eps) - f(x)) / eps is preferred because it needs fewer evaluations
quiz.explanation: Gradient checking compares your analytical gradient against a numerical estimate; it costs two forward passes per parameter, so it's far too slow for training and is used only to validate a custom backward. Thresholds: $<10^{-7}$ excellent, $<10^{-5}$ acceptable, $>10^{-3}$ means a bug — so calling $>10^{-3}$ 'excellent' is exactly backwards. The central (two-sided) difference is more accurate than the one-sided version, which is why it's the one used despite needing both evaluations.

quiz.q: You train a 10-class classifier and the very first loss value (before any updates) is 4.5, but you expected about 2.3. What does this most strongly suggest?
options: The learning rate is too high and should be lowered
Something is broken before training even starts — e.g. a doubled softmax, the wrong loss function, or mislabeled data — because random init on 10 classes should give loss about ln(10) = 2.3
The model is overfitting and needs more regularization
Training is proceeding normally and the loss will settle to 2.3 within a few epochs
quiz.explanation: With random weights a $k$-class model should output near-uniform probabilities, giving cross-entropy $\approx \ln(k)$; for $k=10$ that's $\ln(10)\approx 2.3$. A starting loss of 4.5 means the very setup is wrong — applying softmax twice, a mismatched loss function, or scrambled labels — not a training-dynamics issue. Learning rate and overfitting affect how loss *evolves*, not its value at step 0, and overfitting shows up as a train/val gap later, not a high initial loss.

descriptive.q: Explain why Xavier initialization breaks down when paired with ReLU, and justify the factor of 2 in He initialization's Var(w) = 2/n_in. Reference the role of variance preservation.
descriptive.answer:
**The shared goal.** Both Xavier and He exist to satisfy one constraint: keep the spread (variance) of activations roughly constant from layer to layer, so the signal neither vanishes nor explodes as it passes through a deep stack.

**Why Xavier assumes too much.** Xavier was derived assuming a **symmetric activation** like $\tanh$ or sigmoid, whose derivative is about $1$ near zero. Such functions pass the signal through without systematically shrinking its variance, so Xavier's budget — $\mathrm{Var}(w)=\frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$, here $n_{in}$ is the number of inputs and $n_{out}$ the number of outputs — keeps activations stable.

**What ReLU does differently.** ReLU clamps every negative input to exactly $0$:

> [!MATH] $\mathrm{ReLU}(z)=\max(0,z)$ — keep $z$ if positive, otherwise output $0$. Since roughly half of a zero-centered signal is negative, ReLU discards about half the activations, cutting the variance to **about half** at each layer.

Under Xavier's budget, that halving means the spread shrinks geometrically: $\tfrac12, \tfrac14, \tfrac18, \dots$ — so activations still vanish, just one ReLU at a time. Xavier was tuned for a function that *doesn't* throw away half the signal, so it under-provisions the variance for ReLU.

**Justifying the factor of 2.** He et al. fix this by *doubling* the target variance:

$$\mathrm{Var}(w)=\frac{2}{n_{in}}$$

The intuition: if ReLU is going to remove half the variance downstream, supply twice as much upstream so the post-ReLU spread lands back on target. Concretely, the pre-activation variance is $n_{in}\cdot\mathrm{Var}(w)\cdot\mathrm{Var}(x)$; ReLU then multiplies the surviving variance by about $\tfrac12$. Setting $\mathrm{Var}(w)=\frac{2}{n_{in}}$ makes $n_{in}\cdot\frac{2}{n_{in}}\cdot\frac12 = 1$, exactly preserving the spread.

> [!KEY] Xavier preserves variance for activations that don't discard signal; He adds a factor of 2 to undo ReLU's habit of zeroing half the values. Since nearly all modern nets use ReLU-family activations, He is the default — and PyTorch already uses Kaiming/He for `nn.Linear` and `nn.Conv2d`.

descriptive.q: Derive the variance-preserving condition Var(w) = 1/n_in for a linear layer, and explain how Xavier extends it to account for the backward pass.
descriptive.answer:
**Setup.** Consider a single linear layer $y = Wx$ with no activation, where $W$ holds the weights and $x$ is the incoming vector with $n_{in}$ components (the fan-in). Assume the weights are zero-mean and independent of the inputs, and the inputs are zero-mean.

**The variance of one output.** Each output is a sum of $n_{in}$ independent products $w_i x_i$. For independent zero-mean terms, variances add, and the variance of a product of independent zero-mean variables is the product of their variances. So:

$$\mathrm{Var}(y) = n_{in}\cdot \mathrm{Var}(w)\cdot \mathrm{Var}(x)$$

> [!MATH] In words: each of the $n_{in}$ inputs contributes $\mathrm{Var}(w)\cdot\mathrm{Var}(x)$ of spread, and because the contributions are independent we simply add them up — hence the factor $n_{in}$.

**Imposing variance preservation.** We want the output spread to equal the input spread, $\mathrm{Var}(y)=\mathrm{Var}(x)$. Cancel $\mathrm{Var}(x)$ from both sides:

$$n_{in}\cdot\mathrm{Var}(w) = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathrm{Var}(w) = \frac{1}{n_{in}}$$

So a layer with more inputs needs *smaller* weights — each weight counts for less so the sum doesn't blow up.

**The backward pass.** That derivation only stabilized the **forward** signal. During backprop the gradient flows the other way, and the analogous calculation involves $n_{out}$ — the number of outputs each neuron feeds into. Preserving gradient variance backward gives $\mathrm{Var}(w)=\frac{1}{n_{out}}$.

**Xavier reconciles both.** You generally can't satisfy $\frac{1}{n_{in}}$ and $\frac{1}{n_{out}}$ at once (unless $n_{in}=n_{out}$). Xavier compromises by using the **harmonic-style average**, taking the variance as $\frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$:

$$\mathrm{Var}(w)=\frac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$$

> [!INTUITION] One rule keeps the forward signal steady, the other keeps the backward gradient steady; Xavier splits the difference so **both** the activations and the gradients stay near constant variance across layers. He init drops back to a fan-in-only rule ($2/n_{in}$) because, for ReLU, compensating the activation halving in the forward direction is the dominant concern.

descriptive.q: Compare BatchNorm and LayerNorm: what does each normalize over, how do they behave at train vs. eval time, and why do Transformers prefer LayerNorm?
descriptive.answer:
**The shared formula, different axis.** Both compute a z-score and then apply a learned scale $\gamma$ and shift $\beta$:

$$\text{Norm}(x)=\gamma\cdot\frac{x-\mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2+\epsilon}}+\beta$$

> [!MATH] $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are a mean and variance, $\epsilon$ is a tiny constant preventing division by zero, and $\gamma,\beta$ are learned. The *only* difference between BN and LN is **which numbers $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are computed over.**

**The axis difference.** Picture a table where each row is a sample and each column is a feature.

[[fig:norm-types]]

- **BatchNorm** goes *down each column*: for one feature, it averages across all samples in the batch. It needs many samples to get a meaningful statistic, and it **couples** samples — sample A's output depends on its batch-mates.
- **LayerNorm** goes *across each row*: for one sample, it averages across all of that sample's features. It never looks at other samples.

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Statistics over | Each feature, across the batch | Each sample, across its features |
| Works at batch size 1? | No | Yes |
| Train vs. eval differ? | Yes | No |
| Couples samples? | Yes | No |

**Train vs. eval.** This is where BN is subtle. During **training** BN uses the current mini-batch's $\mu_B,\sigma_B^2$ (noisy, different each batch) and accumulates **running averages**. During **inference** it must switch to those running averages — which is why you must call `model.eval()`. LayerNorm has **no train/eval distinction**: it computes the same per-sample statistics either way.

**Why Transformers prefer LN.** Transformers process **variable-length sequences** and frequently run at **batch size 1** during inference. BN's statistics would be meaningless or unstable in those regimes, and its sample-coupling plus train/eval mismatch add fragility. LayerNorm is self-contained, batch-independent, and identical at train and test time — so BERT, GPT, and essentially every Transformer use it.

> [!KEY] BN normalizes a feature across the batch (great for CNNs with big batches); LN normalizes a sample across its features (essential for sequences). Choose by whether your statistics can rely on a batch.

descriptive.q: Walk through a BatchNorm forward pass on the batch [2, 4, 6, 8] with gamma=2 and beta=1. Then explain the purpose of gamma and beta and the claim that 'BatchNorm can never hurt.'
descriptive.answer:
**The computation, step by step** (one feature, batch of 4):

1. **Batch mean:** $\mu_B=\dfrac{2+4+6+8}{4}=5$.
2. **Batch variance:** $\sigma_B^2=\dfrac{(2-5)^2+(4-5)^2+(6-5)^2+(8-5)^2}{4}=\dfrac{9+1+1+9}{4}=5$.
3. **Standardize** (the z-score): $\hat{x}=\dfrac{x-\mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2+\epsilon}}\approx\dfrac{x-5}{\sqrt{5}}=[-1.34,\,-0.45,\,0.45,\,1.34]$.
4. **Scale and shift** with $\gamma=2,\ \beta=1$: $y=\gamma\hat{x}+\beta=2\hat{x}+1=[-1.68,\,0.10,\,1.90,\,3.68]$.

> [!MATH] $\hat{x}$ now has mean $0$ and variance $1$. The final $y$ has mean $\beta=1$ and standard deviation $\gamma=2$ — exactly the scale and center the parameters dictate.

**Why $\gamma$ and $\beta$ exist.** Forcing every activation to mean $0$, variance $1$ might be the *wrong* distribution for what the next layer needs — it can strip away useful structure (for instance, pushing a sigmoid's inputs into its near-linear region). So instead of hard-coding mean $0$/variance $1$, BN lets the network **learn** the ideal scale ($\gamma$, an effective standard deviation) and shift ($\beta$, an effective mean) for each feature.

**Why 'BN can never hurt.'** Because $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are learnable, the network retains the option to **undo** the normalization. If it learns $\gamma=\sigma_B$ and $\beta=\mu_B$, then:

$$\gamma\cdot\frac{x-\mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2+\epsilon}}+\beta \approx \sigma_B\cdot\frac{x-\mu_B}{\sigma_B}+\mu_B = x$$

i.e. BN reduces to the **identity** — a no-op. So in the worst case BN can learn to do nothing, and otherwise it helps; hence it 'can never hurt.'

> [!WARNING] The $\epsilon$ inside the square root (here dropped because $\sigma_B^2=5$ is comfortably nonzero) is essential in real code: it prevents division by zero when a feature happens to be constant across the batch ($\sigma_B^2=0$).

descriptive.q: You forget to call model.eval() before inference on a network with BatchNorm. As a stress test you run the same batch through the model 20 times. Explain what goes wrong and why the predictions vary.
descriptive.answer:
**What forgetting model.eval() does.** It leaves BatchNorm in **training mode**. In training mode BN normalizes using the **current batch's** mean $\mu_B$ and variance $\sigma_B^2$ rather than the **running averages** accumulated during training. So each example is normalized relative to whatever other examples happen to be in its batch at inference time.

**Why predictions become batch-dependent.** Because a sample's normalized value $\frac{x-\mu_B}{\sqrt{\sigma_B^2+\epsilon}}$ depends on $\mu_B,\sigma_B^2$ — which are statistics of the *whole batch* — the same input produces different activations depending on its batch-mates. Predictions are no longer a property of the input alone.

**Why repeated runs on the 'same batch' still vary.** Here's the subtle part of the stress test. If you literally feed the identical, fixed batch 20 times with no other randomness, BN's batch statistics are the same each time, so the *deterministic* part is unchanged. Variance appears because:

- BN keeps **updating its running averages** while in training mode, but those running stats aren't even used for the output in training mode — so that alone wouldn't change outputs. The real culprits are any **remaining stochastic layers left in training mode**, most notably **Dropout**, which randomly zeroes different neurons on every forward pass. `model.eval()` would have disabled Dropout too.
- If the batch composition changes at all between runs (different sampling, shuffling, or batch size), the BN statistics $\mu_B,\sigma_B^2$ shift, directly changing every prediction.

> [!KEY] `model.eval()` flips BN to use fixed running averages **and** turns Dropout off, making inference deterministic. Skipping it means BN couples each prediction to its batch and Dropout injects per-run noise — so the same input yields different, unreliable outputs.

**The fix and the lesson.** Always call `model.eval()` (and `torch.no_grad()`) before inference; re-enable training mode with `model.train()` afterward. A 10% accuracy drop that *appears* when you finally add `model.eval()` is a different but related red flag: it usually means BN's **running statistics were never properly accumulated** during training (e.g., too few or too small batches), so the fixed stats it now uses are bad.

descriptive.q: Describe gradient checking (the central-difference formula and the relative-error verdicts) and the 6-step debugging checklist. Why should the initial loss of a k-class classifier be about ln(k)?
descriptive.answer:
**Gradient checking.** When you hand-write a layer's backward pass, gradient checking confirms the analytical gradient by comparing it to a brute-force numerical estimate using the **central difference**:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\approx\frac{f(x+\epsilon)-f(x-\epsilon)}{2\epsilon},\qquad \epsilon\approx 10^{-5}$$

> [!MATH] Nudge the input **up** by a tiny $\epsilon$ to get $f(x+\epsilon)$, **down** to get $f(x-\epsilon)$, and divide the change by the total nudge $2\epsilon$. Using both sides cancels first-order error, so it's far more accurate than a one-sided difference.

Then compare to your analytical gradient with a scale-free **relative error**:

$$\text{relative error}=\frac{|g_{\text{analytical}}-g_{\text{numerical}}|}{\max(|g_{\text{analytical}}|,|g_{\text{numerical}}|)}$$

| Relative error | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $<10^{-7}$ | Excellent |
| $<10^{-5}$ | Acceptable |
| $>10^{-3}$ | Something is wrong |

> [!WARNING] It costs two forward passes per parameter, so it's a **debugging-only** tool — never run it inside the training loop. PyTorch provides `torch.autograd.gradcheck`.

**The 6-step checklist (follow in order, never skip ahead):**

1. **Overfit ONE batch** (~8 samples) to zero loss. If it can't memorize 8 examples, stop — something is fundamentally broken.
2. **Check tensor shapes** at every layer; silent broadcasting mismatches cause baffling bugs.
3. **Verify loss at init** $\approx\ln(k)$.
4. **Gradient check** to catch custom-backward bugs.
5. **Train on a small subset** (~100 samples); it should overfit quickly, confirming the pipeline.
6. **Scale up** — full data, regularization, tuning — only after steps 1–5 pass.

**Why initial loss should be $\ln(k)$.** With random weights the model has learned nothing, so its softmax outputs are roughly **uniform** — about $\frac{1}{k}$ probability on each of the $k$ classes. Cross-entropy for the true class is $-\ln(p_{\text{true}})$, and with $p_{\text{true}}\approx\frac{1}{k}$:

$$L_0 \approx -\ln\!\left(\tfrac{1}{k}\right) = \ln(k)$$

> [!EXAMPLE] For 10 classes, $\ln(10)\approx 2.3$. If your first loss is much higher (say 4.5), training hasn't gone wrong — the *setup* has: a doubled softmax, the wrong loss function, or mislabeled data. Checking this one number before training catches a whole class of bugs for free.

topics.title: Convolutions
topics.summary: Convolutions replace fully-connected layers for images by wiring each neuron to a small local patch and sliding a shared filter across the input. This session covers local connectivity, filters and feature maps, padding and stride (with the output-size formula), parameter sharing, receptive fields, and pooling — the building blocks of a CNN.

notes.heading: Why Fully-Connected Layers Choke on Images
notes.body:
Picture handing a network an ordinary photo. A modest colour image of size $224\times224\times3$ already contains $224\times224\times3 = 150{,}528$ numbers — here the first two numbers are the **height** and **width** in pixels and the $3$ is the three colour channels (red, green, blue). That is the input to **one** layer.

Now connect those $150{,}528$ inputs to a fully-connected (FC) layer of just $1000$ neurons. Every neuron carries one weight per input, so the layer holds

$$150{,}528 \times 1000 = 150{,}528{,}000 \approx 150.5\text{ million weights}.$$

Here each **weight** is one tunable number saying how much a particular input pixel matters to a particular neuron. And that is **one** layer; a real network stacks dozens.

> [!KEY] Two things are wrong with FC layers on images: they need a colossal number of parameters (slow, memory-hungry, overfits instantly), and they treat every pixel as equally related to every other pixel — they completely **ignore spatial structure**.

Why is ignoring spatial structure so bad? In a real image, **nearby pixels are correlated** (a patch of sky is blue together, not one blue pixel surrounded by noise) while **distant pixels are usually unrelated** — a pixel in the top-left corner tells you almost nothing about the bottom-right. An FC layer throws this away by wiring everything to everything.

> [!ANALOGY] Finding Waldo: you don't compare every pixel to every other pixel at once. Your eye **scans small local patches** looking for the red-and-white stripes, and once you know the pattern you can spot it anywhere. Convolutions do exactly this.

notes.heading: Local Connectivity and the Filter
notes.body:
The fix is simple to state: **connect each neuron to only a small local patch of the input**, not the whole image. If a neuron only looks at, say, a $3\times3$ window, it has $9$ weights instead of $150{,}528$.

The device that does this is a **filter** (also called a **kernel**): a small grid of **learnable weights**, e.g. $3\times3$. You slide it across the image; at each position you line it up with the pixels underneath, **multiply each weight by the pixel it covers, and add everything up** into a single output number. Slide one step, repeat.

> [!ANALOGY] A filter is like a magnifying glass scanning a document — the **same lens** (same weights) moved over **different locations**. It is hunting for one specific pattern wherever it appears.

> [!KEY] A filter is a small, reusable pattern-detector. It outputs a **large** value where the pattern it is tuned for appears, and roughly **zero** on flat, uninteresting regions.

One filter produces one **feature map** — a new grid showing where that one pattern was found. Use many filters and you get many feature maps, each highlighting something different (edges, corners, textures...). Typical layers use $32$, $64$, $128$ or $256$ filters, so $N$ filters give $N$ feature maps.

notes.heading: The Convolution Operation: Slide, Multiply, Sum
notes.body:
Let's do the arithmetic once, slowly. Place the filter over the top-left corner of the image. **Element-wise multiply** (each weight times the pixel under it), then **sum** all those products — that single number is the output for this position. Then slide the filter one step right and do it again, marching across and down.

[[fig:convolution-op]]

> [!EXAMPLE] Take a checkerboard input and a **vertical-edge filter**
> $$\begin{bmatrix}1&0&-1\\1&0&-1\\1&0&-1\end{bmatrix}.$$
> The top-left $3\times3$ patch of the checkerboard is
> $$\begin{bmatrix}1&0&1\\0&1&0\\1&0&1\end{bmatrix}.$$
> Multiply matching cells and add:
> $$(1\cdot1)+(0\cdot0)+(1\cdot{-1})+(0\cdot1)+(1\cdot0)+(0\cdot{-1})+(1\cdot1)+(0\cdot0)+(1\cdot{-1}) = 0.$$
> The output is $0$ — this patch has no vertical edge, so the edge-detector stays quiet.

Why does that filter detect vertical edges? Its left column is $+1$ and its right column is $-1$. If the left side of the patch is bright and the right side dark (a vertical edge), the sum is large and positive. If left and right match (a flat region), the pluses and minuses cancel to $\approx 0$.

> [!MATH] "Convolution" here means: for every window, take the **dot product** of the filter weights with the pixels under them. A dot product is just "multiply pairs, then add them all" — one number measuring how well the window matches the filter.

> [!WARNING] Strictly, what deep-learning libraries call "convolution" is really **cross-correlation** (the filter is not flipped). The course — and PyTorch — use this sliding dot-product version; don't be thrown if a maths textbook flips the kernel first.

notes.heading: Feature Maps and the Channel Dimension
notes.body:
A real image has **depth**: a colour image is $H\times W\times C$, where $H$ is height, $W$ is width, and $C$ is the number of **channels** ($3$ for RGB). So a filter cannot be a flat $3\times3$ grid — it must also have depth $C$ to cover all channels at once. A single filter is therefore $F\times F\times C$, where $F$ is the filter's height and width (e.g. $3$) and $C$ matches the input channels.

> [!KEY] **Filter depth always equals the input's channel count.** One filter spans all input channels yet still produces a single 2-D feature map.

Stack $N$ filters and the layer outputs $N$ feature maps — an output of shape $H'\times W'\times N$. The output's **depth becomes the number of filters**, no matter how many channels went in.

| Component | Dimensions |
|---|---|
| Input | $H\times W\times C$ (e.g. $224\times224\times3$) |
| One filter | $F\times F\times C$ (spans all input channels) |
| $N$ filters | output $H'\times W'\times N$ (e.g. $224\times224\times64$) |

> [!EXAMPLE] Input $32\times32\times3$, a layer of $16$ filters each $3\times3$. One filter has $3\times3\times3 = 27$ weights; add $1$ bias $\Rightarrow 28$. The whole layer has $16\times28 = 448$ parameters — and that count does **not** depend on the image being $32\times32$ or $1000\times1000$.

notes.heading: Padding and Stride: Controlling Output Size
notes.body:
**The shrinking problem.** Every time a $3\times3$ filter slides over an image with no border, the output comes out a little smaller than the input (a $5\times5$ becomes $3\times3$). Stack several layers and the feature map shrinks toward nothing — after enough layers it would vanish.

[[fig:padding-stride]]

**Padding** fixes this by wrapping the input in a border of zeros before convolving. With the right border width, the output comes out the **same size** as the input.

- **Valid padding** = no zeros added; output shrinks.
- **Same padding** = a ring of zeros so output size $=$ input size. Most common in practice.

**Stride** is how far the filter **jumps** between positions. Stride $1$ visits every position (the default). Stride $2$ skips every other one, roughly **halving** the output — a deliberate way to **downsample**, and a modern alternative to pooling.

> [!MATH] Output size along one dimension:
> $$\text{Output} = \frac{W - F + 2P}{S} + 1.$$
> In words: take the input length $W$, subtract the filter size $F$ (the window can't hang off the edge), add $2P$ because padding $P$ adds $P$ zeros on **each** side, divide by the stride $S$ (bigger jumps $\Rightarrow$ fewer positions), then add $1$ for the starting position.

| $W$ | $F$ | $P$ | $S$ | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | 3 | 0 | 1 | $(7-3+0)/1+1 = 5$ |
| 7 | 3 | 1 | 1 | $(7-3+2)/1+1 = 7$ (same!) |
| 7 | 3 | 1 | 2 | $(7-3+2)/2+1 = 4$ |
| 32 | 5 | 2 | 1 | $(32-5+4)/1+1 = 32$ (same!) |

> [!KEY] For "same" output at stride $1$, set $P = \lfloor F/2 \rfloor$ — so $P=1$ for a $3\times3$ filter, $P=2$ for $5\times5$. The $\lfloor\,\rfloor$ means round down.

> [!WARNING] "Same padding" only preserves size when **stride is $1$**. Row 3 above uses same padding ($P=1$) but stride $2$, and the $7\times7$ still drops to $4\times4$. Stride downsamples regardless of padding.

notes.heading: Parameter Sharing: The Key Efficiency Trick
notes.body:
Here is the move that makes convolutions so cheap. The **same filter weights are used at every spatial location**. You do not learn a separate detector for the top-left and another for the bottom-right — you learn one filter and slide it everywhere.

Why is that allowed? Because a vertical edge looks the same whether it appears top-left or bottom-right. A pattern's identity doesn't depend on where it sits, so one shared detector suffices.

> [!EXAMPLE] One $3\times3$ filter $= 9$ weights. Reuse it across all $224\times224 = 50{,}176$ positions of an image and it is **still just $9$ weights**. The fully-connected equivalent wiring would need on the order of $150$ million parameters.

> [!KEY] **Parameter sharing** decouples the number of parameters from the image size. A conv layer's cost depends on filter size, channels, and filter count — never on how big the picture is.

> [!WARNING] A conv layer does **not** use different weights at each position (a favourite exam trap). Different *positions* see different *pixels*, but they all apply the *same* shared weights.

notes.heading: Receptive Fields: What a Neuron Sees
notes.body:
A neuron deep in the network produces one number — but how much of the **original image** did that number depend on? That region is the neuron's **receptive field (RF)**.

[[fig:receptive-field]]

A single $3\times3$ conv gives each output a $3\times3$ receptive field. Stack a second $3\times3$ conv and each new neuron sees a $3\times3$ patch of the previous layer — but each of those already saw $3\times3$ of the input, so the second-layer neuron effectively sees $5\times5$ of the original. A third layer reaches $7\times7$.

| Layers (each $3\times3$, stride 1) | Receptive field on input |
|---|---|
| 1 | $3\times3$ |
| 2 | $5\times5$ |
| 3 | $7\times7$ |

> [!MATH] For $L$ stacked conv layers of filter size $F$ (stride 1):
> $$\text{RF} = 1 + L\,(F-1).$$
> In words: start from a single pixel ($1$), and every layer adds $F-1$ pixels of reach. For $F=3$ each layer adds $2$, so three layers give $1 + 3\times2 = 7$.

> [!KEY] This is **why we stack conv layers**: early layers (small RF) catch edges and colours; middle layers (medium RF) catch textures and parts; deep layers (large RF) see whole objects and scenes. Depth builds a hierarchy of features.

> [!INTUITION] A neuron can only learn about a "face" if its receptive field is big enough to actually contain a face — which only happens after several layers (or after pooling, which enlarges the RF quickly).

notes.heading: Pooling: Downsample and Summarize
notes.body:
**Pooling** shrinks a feature map by summarizing small windows. The classic is **max pooling** with a $2\times2$ window and stride $2$: slide a non-overlapping $2\times2$ box over the feature map and keep only the **largest** value in each box. A $4\times4$ map becomes $2\times2$.

[[fig:pooling]]

> [!EXAMPLE] Max-pool this $4\times4$ with a $2\times2$ window, stride $2$:
> $$\begin{bmatrix}1&3&2&4\\5&6&1&2\\7&8&3&0\\2&4&9&1\end{bmatrix} \rightarrow \begin{bmatrix}6&4\\8&9\end{bmatrix}$$
> Top-left box $\{1,3,5,6\}\to6$, top-right $\{2,4,1,2\}\to4$, bottom-left $\{7,8,2,4\}\to8$, bottom-right $\{3,0,9,1\}\to9$.

Why pool?
