1. **Less computation downstream** — $224\to112\to56$ shrinks the work for later layers.
2. **Translation invariance** — a small shift in the input often leaves the pooled output unchanged (the max in a region usually stays the max). The network cares **that** a feature is present, not exactly **where**.
3. **Bigger receptive field** — halving the map means each later neuron covers more of the input.

> [!WARNING] The tradeoff: pooling **discards exact spatial location**. You learn a feature exists in a region but lose precisely where. That hurts pixel-precise tasks like **segmentation** or **super-resolution**.

Variants: **max pooling** (classic), **average pooling** (mean of the window), and **global average pooling** (collapse each whole feature map to a single number, common at the very end of a network).

> [!EXAMPLE] The standard block is **Conv $\to$ ReLU $\to$ Pool**, repeated. Shape trace on MNIST ($28\times28\times1$):
>
> | After | Shape |
> |---|---|
> | Input | $28\times28\times1$ |
> | Conv1 (16 filters, $3\times3$, pad 1) | $28\times28\times16$ |
> | MaxPool $2\times2$ | $14\times14\times16$ |
> | Conv2 (32 filters, $3\times3$, pad 1) | $14\times14\times32$ |
> | MaxPool $2\times2$ | $7\times7\times32$ |
> | Flatten | $1568$ |
> | FC $\to$ 10 classes | $10$ |
>
> Total $\approx 20$K parameters vs $\approx 100$K for an FC-only network — fewer parameters **and** better accuracy.

> [!EXAM] Be ready to (1) compute output sizes with the formula, (2) count conv parameters as $N\times(F\times F\times C + 1)$, (3) give the receptive field of stacked $3\times3$ convs, and (4) state the pooling tradeoff.

quiz.q: An input of size $13\times13$ is convolved with a $5\times5$ filter using padding $P=2$ and stride $S=2$. What is the output's spatial size?
options: $5\times5$
$6\times6$
$7\times7$
$13\times13$
quiz.explanation: Apply $\frac{W-F+2P}{S}+1 = \frac{13-5+4}{2}+1 = \frac{12}{2}+1 = 7$, so $7\times7$. The tempting $13\times13$ assumes padding always preserves size, but that only holds at stride $1$; here stride $2$ downsamples.

quiz.q: A $64\times64$ grayscale image is fully connected to a layer of $100$ neurons. How many weights does this single layer have?
quiz.explanation: Each neuron has one weight per input pixel: $64\times64 = 4096$ inputs times $100$ neurons $= 409{,}600$. The value $6{,}400$ forgets to multiply by the $100$ neurons. This explosion for a single tiny layer is exactly why FC layers are wasteful on images.

quiz.q: Three $3\times3$ convolution layers (stride 1, no pooling) are stacked. What region of the original input does one neuron in the third layer depend on?
options: $3\times3$
$5\times5$
$9\times9$
$7\times7$
quiz.explanation: Receptive field $= 1 + L(F-1) = 1 + 3(3-1) = 7$, so $7\times7$. The answer $9\times9$ wrongly multiplies $3\times3$; each layer adds only $F-1=2$ pixels of reach, not a full $3$.

quiz.q: You apply $3\times3$ filters to an input with $3$ channels (RGB). How many weights does a single filter contain, ignoring bias?
quiz.explanation: A filter must span all input channels, so its true shape is $F\times F\times C = 3\times3\times3 = 27$. Answering $9$ forgets the depth $C$; a filter is never flat when the input has multiple channels.

quiz.q: An input of shape $32\times32\times3$ passes through a conv layer of $16$ filters, each $3\times3$, each with its own bias. How many learnable parameters does the layer have?
quiz.explanation: Per filter: $3\times3\times3 = 27$ weights $+1$ bias $= 28$. Times $16$ filters $= 448$. The value $432$ omits the biases ($16\times27$). Note the count is independent of the $32\times32$ image size, thanks to parameter sharing.

quiz.q: Using a $5\times5$ filter with stride $1$, what padding $P$ gives an output the same spatial size as the input?
quiz.explanation: Same padding needs $P=\lfloor F/2\rfloor = \lfloor 5/2\rfloor = 2$. Check: $(W-5+2\cdot2)/1+1 = W$. Choosing $1$ would preserve size only for a $3\times3$ filter, not a $5\times5$ one.

quiz.q: Which statement correctly describes parameter sharing in a convolutional layer?
options: Each output pixel gets its own independent bias term.
Each spatial position learns its own separate set of filter weights.
Weights are shared across channels but differ at every position.
The same filter weights are applied at every spatial position.
quiz.explanation: Parameter sharing means one filter's weights are reused at all positions — that is what makes a $3\times3$ filter cost only $9$ weights regardless of image size. The 'own weights per position' option describes a fully-connected layer, the very thing convolutions avoid.

quiz.q: Why does max pooling give a network a degree of translation invariance?
options: Pooling adds learnable weights that memorize shifted versions of features.
A small shift of the input usually leaves the maximum within each pooling window unchanged.
Pooling increases the number of channels so that shifts get averaged out.
Pooling sharpens exact pixel locations so shifts are detected and corrected.
quiz.explanation: If a feature moves a little, the largest value in a $2\times2$ region is typically still the largest, so the pooled output barely changes — the network registers that the feature is present without pinning down where. Pooling has no learnable weights, which rules out the 'memorize' option.

quiz.q: A filter has $+1$ in its left column, $0$ in the middle, and $-1$ in its right column. On a large region of uniform brightness, what does it output, and what does it detect?
options: Near $0$ on uniform regions; it detects horizontal edges.
A large positive value everywhere; it detects overall brightness.
Near $0$ on uniform regions; it detects vertical edges.
A large negative value on uniform regions; it detects corners.
quiz.explanation: On a flat patch the $+1$ and $-1$ contributions cancel, giving $\approx 0$; it fires only where the left side is bright and the right dark — a vertical edge. A horizontal-edge filter would instead use $+1$/$-1$ rows, not columns.

quiz.q: A conv layer applies $64$ filters to an input of shape $56\times56\times128$ with same padding and stride $1$. What is the depth (number of channels) of the output?
quiz.explanation: Output depth equals the number of filters, $64$ — each filter produces one feature map. The input's $128$ channels are spanned inside each filter (filter depth $128$); they do not pass through as output channels.

quiz.q: An input of $7\times7$ is convolved with a $3\times3$ filter using padding $P=1$ (the 'same' value for a $3\times3$) but stride $S=2$. What is the output size?
options: $4\times4$
$7\times7$
$3\times3$
$2\times2$
quiz.explanation: $(7-3+2)/2+1 = 6/2+1 = 4$, so $4\times4$. The $7\times7$ answer assumes 'same' padding always preserves size, but that guarantee holds only at stride $1$; stride $2$ still halves the map.

quiz.q: In modern architectures, what is the main purpose of using a convolution with stride greater than $1$?
options: To reduce the number of channels in the output.
To increase the spatial resolution of the feature map.
To guarantee the output is the same size as the input.
To downsample the feature map, as an alternative to pooling.
quiz.explanation: A stride of $2$ skips positions and roughly halves height and width — a deliberate downsampling that some networks use instead of pooling. It cannot increase resolution, and it shrinks rather than preserves the spatial size; channel count is set by the number of filters, not the stride.

descriptive.q: Explain why fully-connected layers are a poor fit for images, and how local connectivity and parameter sharing fix the problem. Support your answer with concrete parameter counts.
descriptive.answer:
**The problem.** A fully-connected (FC) layer wires every input to every neuron. For a $224\times224\times3$ image that is $150{,}528$ inputs; a single FC layer of $1000$ neurons therefore needs $150{,}528\times1000 \approx 150.5$ million weights — for **one** layer. This is slow, memory-hungry, and overfits instantly. Worse, it **ignores spatial structure**: it treats a top-left pixel and a bottom-right pixel as equally related to every neuron, even though in images nearby pixels are correlated and distant ones are not.

**Fix 1 — Local connectivity.** Connect each neuron to only a small patch (e.g. $3\times3$). Now a neuron has $9$ weights instead of $150{,}528$, and it focuses on the local region where correlations actually live.

**Fix 2 — Parameter sharing.** Use the *same* $3\times3$ filter at every position. A vertical edge looks the same anywhere, so one detector suffices everywhere. The whole layer's spatial weight cost is just $9$ (per filter, per input channel), regardless of image size.

| | FC layer | Conv layer |
|---|---|---|
| Connectivity | every pixel to every neuron | small local patch |
| Weights | $\sim150$M (one layer) | $9$ per filter (shared) |
| Spatial structure | ignored | exploited |

> [!KEY] Local connectivity cuts the *fan-in* per neuron; parameter sharing then makes the cost independent of image size. Together they turn $\sim150$M parameters into a handful, while actually generalizing better.

descriptive.q: State the convolution output-size formula and explain the role of each term in plain language. Then work two examples, including one with stride greater than 1, and explain what happens when the division is not exact.
descriptive.answer:
The output size of a conv layer along one dimension is
$$\text{Output} = \frac{W - F + 2P}{S} + 1,$$
where $W$ is the input length, $F$ the filter size, $P$ the padding added on **each** side, and $S$ the stride.

**Reading each term:**
- $W - F$: a filter of width $F$ can start at the first column and must stop before it hangs off the edge, so the last valid start is $W-F$ positions in.
- $+2P$: padding adds $P$ zeros on **each** side, lengthening the usable input by $2P$.
- $\div\,S$: with stride $S$ you keep only every $S$-th start position, so the count of positions divides by $S$.
- $+1$: you must also count the very first position.

> [!EXAMPLE] $W=13, F=5, P=2, S=2$: $(13-5+4)/2+1 = 12/2+1 = 7 \Rightarrow 7\times7$.
> $W=32, F=5, P=2, S=1$: $(32-5+4)/1+1 = 32 \Rightarrow$ same size (this is why $P=\lfloor F/2\rfloor$ gives 'same' padding at stride 1).

> [!WARNING] When the division is not exact, libraries take the **floor** (round down): the filter simply cannot take a partial step off the edge. E.g. $W=8, F=3, P=0, S=2$ gives $(8-3)/2+1 = 2.5+1 \to \lfloor 3.5\rfloor = 3$.

> [!KEY] Padding raises the output; filter size and stride lower it. 'Same' padding ($P=\lfloor F/2\rfloor$) cancels the shrink **only at stride 1**.

descriptive.q: What is a receptive field? Derive the receptive-field size for L stacked F x F convolutions at stride 1, and explain why growing receptive fields are the reason we stack convolutional layers.
descriptive.answer:
The **receptive field (RF)** of a neuron is the region of the **original input** that influences its value.

**Building it up.** One $3\times3$ conv: each output sees $3\times3$ of the input. Add a second $3\times3$ conv: each new neuron sees $3\times3$ of layer-1, but those neurons each already saw $3\times3$ of the input, so the reach grows to $5\times5$ (the windows overlap, adding $F-1=2$ in total). A third layer reaches $7\times7$.

> [!MATH] For $L$ stacked layers of filter size $F$ at stride 1:
> $$\text{RF} = 1 + L\,(F-1).$$
> Start from $1$ pixel; each layer adds $F-1$. For $F=3$: $L=1\to3$, $L=2\to5$, $L=3\to7$.

**Why it matters.** A neuron can only learn a concept that fits inside its RF. Early layers (small RF) see edges and colours; middle layers (medium RF) see textures and object parts; deep layers (large RF) see whole objects and scenes. This is precisely **why we stack conv layers** — each one adds context, and the network builds a hierarchy: simple patterns $\to$ complex structures $\to$ full objects.

> [!INTUITION] Pooling and stride enlarge the RF much faster than plain stacking, because they shrink the map so each later filter step covers more original pixels.

descriptive.q: Compare valid and same padding, explain the shrinking problem they address, and discuss stride as a downsampling tool. Be explicit about when 'same' padding does NOT preserve the spatial size.
descriptive.answer:
**The shrinking problem.** A $3\times3$ filter with no border turns $5\times5$ into $3\times3$, then $1\times1$, then nothing. Each layer eats the border, so deep stacks would run out of feature map.

**Valid vs same padding:**

| | Valid padding | Same padding |
|---|---|---|
| Zeros added | none | ring of $P=\lfloor F/2\rfloor$ zeros |
| Output (stride 1) | shrinks ($5\to3$ for $3\times3$) | equals input ($5\to5$) |
| Use | when shrinkage is acceptable | most common default |

**Stride as downsampling.** Stride is how far the filter jumps. Stride $1$ visits every position; stride $2$ skips every other, roughly **halving** height and width. Modern networks sometimes use stride-2 convolutions **instead of pooling** to downsample (Springenberg et al., 2015, argue pooling can be replaced entirely this way).

> [!WARNING] 'Same padding' only preserves size at **stride 1**. With $P=1$ on a $3\times3$ filter but stride $2$, a $7\times7$ input still drops to $(7-3+2)/2+1 = 4\times4$. Padding controls the border; stride independently controls downsampling.

descriptive.q: Explain what max pooling does and list its benefits. What is its main tradeoff, and in what kind of task would aggressive pooling hurt performance? Mention common pooling variants.
descriptive.answer:
**What pooling does.** It shrinks a feature map by summarizing small windows into one value. **Max pooling** ($2\times2$, stride 2) keeps the largest value in each non-overlapping $2\times2$ box, turning $4\times4$ into $2\times2$.

**Why pool:**
1. **Less downstream computation** — $224\to112\to56$ cuts the work of later layers.
2. **Translation invariance** — a small shift usually leaves the per-window max unchanged, so the output is stable. The message is 'a strong feature exists somewhere in this region', not its exact pixel.
3. **Larger receptive field** — a smaller map means each later neuron covers more of the input.

**Types:** max pooling (classic), average pooling (mean of the window), global average pooling (collapse each feature map to a single number, often at the network's end).

> [!WARNING] **The tradeoff:** pooling **discards exact spatial location**. You know a feature is present in a region but lose precisely where. This hurts tasks needing pixel-level precision — **semantic segmentation**, **super-resolution**, keypoint localization — where 'where exactly' is the whole point. In those settings aggressive pooling can degrade performance.

> [!EXAMPLE] $\begin{bmatrix}1&3&2&4\\5&6&1&2\\7&8&3&0\\2&4&9&1\end{bmatrix}\to\begin{bmatrix}6&4\\8&9\end{bmatrix}$ — each output is the max of its $2\times2$ corner.

descriptive.q: Why does parameter sharing make sense for image data but NOT for a fully-connected layer processing tabular features? Connect your answer to the idea of translation symmetry and to how filters span channels.
descriptive.answer:
**Parameter sharing** reuses the *same* filter weights at every spatial position. Whether this is sensible depends on whether 'the same pattern can appear in different places'.

**Images: yes.** A vertical edge, a corner, a patch of fur looks the same in the top-left as in the bottom-right. A single shared detector slid everywhere is exactly right — and it makes the parameter count independent of image size (a $3\times3$ filter is $9$ weights whether the image is $32\times32$ or $4000\times4000$). The spatial axes (height, width) are **homogeneous and translation-equivariant**: position $(i,j)$ has no fixed meaning beyond 'a location'.

**Tabular data: no.** In a spreadsheet, column 1 might be *age* and column 5 *income*. These are **not interchangeable** — different units, different meanings — and 'sliding' a detector from the age column to the income column is nonsensical. There is no translation symmetry to exploit, so a fully-connected layer (its own weight for each feature) is appropriate.

| | Image pixels | Tabular features |
|---|---|---|
| Do the axes have fixed meaning? | no — just locations | yes — age, income, ... |
| Same pattern reappears elsewhere? | yes | no |
| Share weights? | yes (convolution) | no (fully-connected) |

> [!KEY] Weight sharing is justified by **translation symmetry**. Use it when shifting the input shouldn't change what a feature *means* (images, audio, time) — not when each input slot is a distinct, named quantity.

> [!INTUITION] Relatedly, each conv filter spans **all input channels** ($F\times F\times C$) but is shared across **spatial** positions — sharing happens over space, where the symmetry holds, not over channels, which carry distinct information.

topics.title: CNN Architectures
topics.summary: A guided tour of the landmark CNN architectures from 1998 to 2015 — LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, Inception/GoogLeNet, and ResNet — showing how each solved a specific limitation of its predecessor and distilling the five enduring design principles: small stacked filters, 1x1 channel bottlenecks, halve-spatial-double-channels, global average pooling, and skip connections.

notes.heading: The 3-Year Revolution: How Vision Got Solved
notes.body:
Imagine a competition where, for years, the best computer-vision systems made roughly 1 mistake in every 4 images. Then in just three years (2012 to 2015) the error rate fell below what a careful human achieves. That competition was **ImageNet**, and the story of this session is the chain of architectures that drove that drop.

The big idea to hold onto: **nobody invented one magic trick.** Each new network fixed a concrete problem that the previous one ran into. LeNet was too small for real photos; AlexNet scaled it up using GPUs; VGG made it deeper and cleaner; Inception made it efficient; ResNet made it *very* deep without breaking.

[[fig:cnn-timeline]]

> [!KEY]
> Read these architectures as a conversation, not a list. Each one is an *answer* to a *limitation* of the one before it. If you can state the limitation each network solved, you understand the session.

| Architecture | Year | Depth | Params | Top-5 Error | What it added |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LeNet-5 | 1998 | 5 | 60K | — | The Conv -> Pool -> FC pattern |
| AlexNet | 2012 | 8 | 60M | 16.4% | ReLU + Dropout + GPU + augmentation |
| VGG-16 | 2014 | 16 | 138M | 7.3% | Uniform 3x3 filters, depth |
| GoogLeNet | 2014 | 22 | 5M | 6.7% | Inception module + 1x1 bottlenecks |
| ResNet-152 | 2015 | 152 | 60M | 3.6% | Skip connections |

> [!INTUITION]
> Notice GoogLeNet has the *fewest* parameters (5M) yet beats the 138M-parameter VGG. "Smarter beats bigger" is one of the loudest lessons of this whole story.

notes.heading: LeNet-5 (1998): The Funnel Pattern
notes.body:
**LeNet-5**, built by Yann LeCun in 1998, was designed to read handwritten digits (think: zip codes on envelopes). It had only about **60,000 parameters** — tiny — and used **sigmoid/tanh** activations. But it established the template every later CNN follows.

That template is the **funnel**. As the image flows through the network, the spatial size (height x width) **shrinks** while the number of channels (feature maps) **grows**:

- Spatial: $32 \to 28 \to 14 \to 10 \to 5$ — here each number is the width/height of the feature map as it gets pooled down.
- Channels: $1 \to 6 \to 16$ — here each number is how many different feature detectors are active at that stage.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Think of squeezing a wide, shallow river into a narrow, deep canyon. You lose *where exactly* something is (spatial resolution) but gain *what* it is — more and richer feature types (channels). The network trades location for meaning as you go deeper.

LeNet also pinned down three ideas that all CNNs reuse:

- **Local connectivity** — each neuron only looks at a small patch of the input, not the whole image.
- **Weight sharing** — the same filter slides across the whole image, so a feature detected in one corner is detected everywhere.
- **Subsampling (pooling)** — progressively shrink the spatial size to build bigger-picture features.

> [!WARNING]
> LeNet's limitation is the launch pad for everything else: it was simply **too small** for natural images (a real photo is $224\times224$, not $32\times32$), and its sigmoid/tanh activations cause **vanishing gradients** when you try to stack many layers. Both problems get fixed by AlexNet.

> [!EXAM]
> A favorite question: *"Why couldn't LeNet-5 be applied directly to ImageNet?"* The answer is capacity, not just size — 60K parameters cannot model 1000 classes on $224\times224$ images.

notes.heading: AlexNet (2012): Same Recipe, 1000x Bigger Kitchen
notes.body:
**AlexNet** is the "Big Bang" of modern deep learning. In 2012 it won ImageNet with a **16.4% top-5 error**, crushing the best non-deep-learning runner-up at 26.2%. That single result convinced the field that neural networks actually work at scale.

Architecturally it is *LeNet, scaled up*: **5 convolutional layers + 3 fully-connected layers**, about **60 million parameters**, trained across **2 GPUs** (a necessity at the time that turned into an innovation).

But the architecture alone is not why it won. AlexNet bundled together several practical tricks:

1. **ReLU activation** — about **6x faster training** than tanh. ReLU is just $f(x)=\max(0,x)$ — output the input if positive, otherwise output zero. Because it does not saturate (flatten out) for large positive values, the gradient stays healthy and learning is fast.
2. **Dropout (0.5)** in the FC layers — randomly switch off half the neurons during training to prevent over-reliance on any one, a regularizer that needs no extra data.
3. **Data augmentation** — random crops, horizontal flips, color jitter — turning one labelled image into many.
4. **GPU training** — made 60M parameters actually feasible to train.
5. **Local Response Normalization** — a normalization scheme now considered **obsolete**, replaced by BatchNorm.

> [!KEY]
> The lesson of AlexNet is *combination*. It was not one trick but **ReLU + Dropout + Augmentation + GPU together** that unlocked scale. If an exam asks for the *single biggest factor for training speed*, the answer is **ReLU** (no saturation, fast gradients).

| | LeNet-5 (1998) | AlexNet (2012) |
|---|---|---|
| Input size | 32 x 32 | 224 x 224 |
| Parameters | 60K | 60M |
| Depth | 5 layers | 8 layers |
| Activation | Sigmoid/Tanh | ReLU |
| Training | CPU, minutes | GPU, days |
| Task | 10 digits | 1000 classes |

notes.heading: VGGNet (2014): The 3x3 Philosophy
notes.body:
The Oxford Visual Geometry Group asked a clean question: *what if we only ever use $3\times3$ convolutions and just go deeper?* The result, **VGG**, came in two sizes — **VGG-16** (13 conv + 3 FC) and **VGG-19** (16 conv + 3 FC) — and hit **7.3% top-5 error**.

The heart of VGG is **receptive-field equivalence**. The *receptive field* is how much of the original image a single neuron ultimately "sees." The surprising fact:

- Two stacked $3\times3$ convs see the **same** area as one $5\times5$ conv.
- Three stacked $3\times3$ convs see the **same** area as one $7\times7$ conv.

So why stack small ones instead of using one big one? Two reasons — fewer parameters **and** more non-linearity.

> [!MATH]
> Compare parameters for the same $C$ input and $C$ output channels.
> One $7\times7$ filter costs $7 \times 7 \times C^2 = 49C^2$ weights — here $C^2$ comes from $C$ input channels times $C$ output channels, and $49$ is the $7\times7$ window.
> Three $3\times3$ filters cost $3 \times (3 \times 3 \times C^2) = 27C^2$ weights — three layers, each a $3\times3$ window over $C$ channels into $C$ channels.
> Since $27C^2$ is about **45% fewer** than $49C^2$, the deep-small-filter stack is cheaper.

> [!INTUITION]
> The second win is *non-linearity*. The single $7\times7$ applies **one** ReLU; the three $3\times3$ convs apply **three** ReLUs. More non-linear steps means the network can carve more complex decision boundaries from the same receptive field. Cheaper *and* more expressive — that is why this principle underpins essentially all modern CNNs.

VGG also popularized a simple, repeatable design rule:

> [!KEY]
> **Halve the spatial size, double the channels** at each stage: spatial $224 \to 112 \to 56 \to 28 \to 14 \to 7$ while channels $64 \to 128 \to 256 \to 512 \to 512$. This keeps the computation per layer roughly constant and makes the network easy to scale.

> [!WARNING]
> VGG's flaw: it is **wasteful**. Of its **138M parameters**, about **124M live in the fully-connected layers alone**. That single observation motivates GoogLeNet's next move — getting rid of big FC layers.

notes.heading: Inception / GoogLeNet (2014): Use Every Filter Size
notes.body:
VGG forces you to *choose* a filter size for each layer. The Inception team flipped that: **don't choose — use them all and let the network decide.** An **Inception module** runs the same input through several filter sizes ($1\times1$, $3\times3$, $5\times5$) plus a pooling path **in parallel**, then concatenates the results.

[[fig:inception-module]]

There is an obvious danger here: a **channel explosion**.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Take an input of $28\times28\times256$. If you naively apply 256 filters of each size in parallel, you get three stacks of $28\times28\times256$ that concatenate into $28\times28\times768$ — and it keeps growing at every module. Computation blows up.

The fix is the session's single most reusable trick: the **$1\times1$ convolution**.

A $1\times1$ conv looks at exactly **one pixel at a time** and blends together its channels. It does **not** touch the spatial dimensions (height/width are unchanged); it acts like a tiny fully-connected layer applied independently at every pixel. Crucially, it can **cheaply reduce the number of channels** — say $256 \to 64$.

> [!MATH]
> The bottleneck recipe is: **squeeze, then process.** First a $1\times1$ conv reduces $256$ channels to $64$ (cheap, since a $1\times1$ filter has only $1\times1\times256$ weights per output channel). *Then* run the expensive $3\times3$ or $5\times5$ conv on just $64$ channels instead of $256$. Because conv cost scales with the input-channel count, cutting channels by 4x before the expensive op is a massive saving.

This efficiency let GoogLeNet be **22 layers deep with only 5M parameters** — about **28x fewer than VGG-16** — while getting *better* accuracy (**6.7% top-5 error**, won ImageNet 2014).

> [!KEY]
> The other half of GoogLeNet's efficiency is **Global Average Pooling (GAP)**. Instead of flattening the final feature maps into a giant FC layer, GAP averages each channel's entire spatial map down to a single number. This **eliminates the millions of FC parameters** that bloated VGG.

> [!EXAM]
> Two classic prompts: *"What do 1x1 convs do in Inception?"* -> reduce channel dimensionality before expensive convolutions (the bottleneck). *"Why is GoogLeNet so parameter-efficient vs VGG?"* -> 1x1 bottlenecks **and** global average pooling instead of large FC layers.

notes.heading: The Degradation Problem (2015): Deeper Got Worse
notes.body:
By 2015 the recipe seemed clear: go deeper. But researchers found something disturbing.

> [!INTUITION]
> A **56-layer plain network had *higher training error* than a 20-layer network.** Not higher *test* error — higher **training** error. The deeper network was worse at the thing it was directly optimizing.

[[fig:plain-vs-resnet]]

This matters because it rules out the obvious explanation.

> [!WARNING]
> This is **NOT overfitting.** Overfitting means *low* training error but *high* test error — the model memorizes and fails to generalize. Here the **training error itself is worse**, so the model cannot even fit the data it sees. This is an **optimization (degradation) problem**, not a generalization problem.

Why should adding layers hurt? In principle, the extra 36 layers could just learn to "do nothing" — pass their input straight through (the **identity mapping**) — and the 56-layer net would match the 20-layer net exactly. The unsettling discovery: a plain stack of conv layers **cannot easily learn even the identity function**. Forcing a pile of weight-and-ReLU layers to reproduce their input unchanged turns out to be a hard optimization target.

> [!KEY]
> State the problem precisely: deeper plain networks degrade because they **struggle to optimize**, in particular they cannot easily represent the identity mapping in the extra layers. The cure is to *change the architecture so identity becomes the default*, which is exactly what ResNet does next.

notes.heading: ResNet (2015): Learning the Residual
notes.body:
ResNet's fix is almost embarrassingly simple. Instead of asking a block of layers to learn the full desired mapping, ask it to learn only the **change** (the *residual*) and **add the input back** at the end.

[[fig:residual-block]]

> [!MATH]
> Let $H(x)$ be the function we ultimately want a block to compute, where $x$ is the input. A plain block tries to learn $H(x)$ directly. A **residual block** instead learns $F(x) = H(x) - x$ — the *residual*, the part that needs to change — and produces the output $F(x) + x$. The $+x$ is a **skip (shortcut) connection** that copies the input straight to the output.

Why is this such a big deal? It makes the troublesome identity mapping **the easy default**.

> [!INTUITION]
> If the best thing a block can do is "leave the input alone" ($H(x)=x$), the residual block just needs $F(x)=0$ — push the weights to zero, which is trivial (weight decay even pushes them there for free). A plain block, by contrast, would have to learn a precise non-trivial weight pattern to reproduce its input. **Zero is easy; identity-from-scratch is hard.**

Skip connections help in three reinforcing ways:

1. **Gradient highway.** During backprop, the gradient flows through the $+x$ shortcut *without* being multiplied by a chain of weight matrices. With output $y = F(x) + x$, the gradient is $\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1$ — that **$+1$** means the signal can never fully vanish, no matter how deep the network.
2. **Easy identity baseline.** Worst case, a layer learns $F(x)=0$ and does no harm.
3. **Ensemble effect.** A ResNet behaves like an ensemble of many shorter paths through the network.

> [!ANALOGY]
> A highway with exit ramps. Traffic (the gradient) can flow straight down the main road even if some exits (layers) are blocked.

The payoff was historic: **ResNet-152 reached 3.57% top-5 error — superhuman** — and won ImageNet, COCO detection, and COCO segmentation in the same year.

| Variant | Layers | Params | Top-5 Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| ResNet-18 | 18 | 11M | 10.9% |
| ResNet-34 | 34 | 21M | 7.8% |
| ResNet-50 | 50 | 25M | 6.7% |
| ResNet-101 | 101 | 44M | 6.0% |
| ResNet-152 | 152 | 60M | 3.6% |

> [!KEY]
> Deeper finally **is** better — but only **once you add skip connections.** The deeper variants in the table really do score lower error, reversing the degradation problem.

notes.heading: Design Principles: The Five Rules
notes.body:
Strip away the names and dates and the whole session compresses into five reusable rules. These are what you actually take into building your own architecture.

1. **Use small filters ($3\times3$) stacked deep** — same receptive field as big filters, but fewer parameters and more non-linearity (the VGG lesson).
2. **Halve spatial, double channels at each stage** — keeps computation balanced across the network (VGG/LeNet funnel).
3. **Use $1\times1$ convs for channel reduction (bottleneck)** — squeeze channels before expensive ops to control cost (Inception, and reused in ResNet's bottleneck block).
4. **Add skip connections when going beyond ~20 layers** — without them, deep plain nets degrade (ResNet).
5. **Use Global Average Pooling instead of large FC layers** at the end — kills the parameter bloat (GoogLeNet).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Rule 3 even shows up *inside* ResNet. The deep variants (ResNet-50/101/152) use a **bottleneck block**: $1\times1$ conv (reduce channels) -> $3\times3$ conv -> $1\times1$ conv (expand channels back), with the skip connection wrapped around all three. That is literally the Inception $1\times1$ idea borrowed to keep very deep networks affordable. The shallower ResNet-18/34 use a simpler **basic block** of two $3\times3$ convs.

> [!KEY]
> If you remember one sentence from this session: **small stacked filters, $1\times1$ bottlenecks, balanced funnel, global average pooling, and skip connections** are the toolkit that turned a 25%-error problem into a superhuman one in three years.

| Architecture | Year | Depth | Params | Top-5 Err |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LeNet-5 | 1998 | 5 | 60K | — |
| AlexNet | 2012 | 8 | 60M | 16.4% |
| VGG-16 | 2014 | 16 | 138M | 7.3% |
| GoogLeNet | 2014 | 22 | 5M | 6.7% |
| ResNet-152 | 2015 | 152 | 60M | 3.6% |

quiz.q: Three stacked 3x3 convolution layers and a single 7x7 convolution layer operate on the same C input channels and produce C output channels. Which statement is correct?
options: The three 3x3 layers see a smaller region of the input than the single 7x7
The single 7x7 layer uses fewer parameters because it is only one layer
Both use exactly the same number of parameters because they share the same receptive field
The three 3x3 layers use about 45% fewer parameters (27C^2 vs 49C^2) and apply more non-linearities
quiz.explanation: Three 3x3 convs cost 3 x (3x3xC^2) = 27C^2 weights; one 7x7 costs 7x7xC^2 = 49C^2, so the stack is about 45% cheaper. They also see the *same* receptive field (three 3x3 = one 7x7), and the stack applies three ReLUs instead of one, so it is more expressive. The tempting wrong answer counts 'one layer' as fewer parameters, but it ignores that 49 > 27.

quiz.q: A 56-layer plain CNN shows higher TRAINING error than a 20-layer plain CNN. What does this demonstrate?
options: An optimization/degradation problem — the deeper net cannot even fit the training data well
Overfitting — the deeper net memorized the training set
A vanishing-gradient bug that only ever affects test-time accuracy
Insufficient data augmentation in the deeper network
quiz.explanation: Overfitting means LOW training error with HIGH test error. Here training error itself is worse, so the network cannot fit the data — this is the degradation (optimization) problem; the extra layers cannot even learn the identity mapping. The overfitting distractor is the classic trap because 'deeper = overfit' is a common reflex, but it contradicts the observed *training* error.

quiz.q: In an Inception module, what is the primary purpose of the 1x1 convolutions placed before the 3x3 and 5x5 convolutions?
options: Apply a non-linear activation that 3x3 and 5x5 filters cannot provide
Increase the spatial resolution of the feature maps before convolving
Reduce the channel count cheaply so the following expensive convolutions operate on fewer channels
Detect edges at a finer scale than larger filters can
quiz.explanation: A 1x1 conv mixes channels at each pixel without changing spatial size; placed first, it squeezes (e.g. 256->64) channels so the costly 3x3/5x5 convs run on far fewer channels — the bottleneck trick that prevents the channel explosion. It does NOT change spatial resolution (the option claiming it raises resolution is false; 1x1 leaves H and W untouched).

quiz.q: GoogLeNet (22 layers, 5M params) beats VGG-16 (16 layers, 138M params) in both accuracy and efficiency. Which pair of design choices best explains GoogLeNet's tiny parameter count?
options: Skip connections plus batch normalization
1x1 bottleneck convolutions plus global average pooling instead of large FC layers
Larger 7x7 filters plus sigmoid activations
Dropout in every layer plus Local Response Normalization
quiz.explanation: VGG wastes ~124M of its 138M parameters in fully-connected layers. GoogLeNet removes large FC layers using global average pooling, and uses 1x1 convs to bottleneck channels before expensive ops. Skip connections (the distractor) are a ResNet feature, not what makes GoogLeNet small; GoogLeNet predates ResNet.

quiz.q: A residual block computes the output y = F(x) + x. If the ideal mapping for this block is the identity (H(x) = x), why is the residual formulation easier to optimize than a plain block?
options: The skip connection adds extra trainable parameters that memorize the identity
The block only needs to drive F(x) to 0, which is trivial, instead of learning weights that reproduce x exactly
Adding x removes the need for any activation functions in the block
The residual block has a larger receptive field, so identity is automatic
quiz.explanation: With y = F(x) + x, achieving identity means F(x) = 0 — just push weights toward zero, which weight decay does almost for free. A plain block would have to learn a precise non-trivial weight pattern to output its own input. The skip connection is a parameter-free addition (option about 'extra trainable parameters' is wrong — the shortcut adds none).

quiz.q: How do skip connections specifically prevent vanishing gradients in very deep networks?
options: They normalize the gradients to unit variance at every layer
They replace ReLU with a non-saturating activation that keeps gradients large
The shortcut gives the gradient a +1 path so the backward signal is not forced through a long chain of weight multiplications
They reduce the number of layers the gradient must traverse to one
quiz.explanation: For y = F(x) + x, the derivative dy/dx = dF/dx + 1. That +1 term means the gradient can flow straight through the shortcut without being repeatedly multiplied by small weight Jacobians, so it cannot fully vanish — the 'gradient highway.' Skip connections do not change the activation function, and they do not reduce the actual depth (the gradient still has the long path available too).

quiz.q: Which single innovation is credited with the biggest impact on AlexNet's TRAINING SPEED specifically?
options: Local Response Normalization
Dropout at 0.5 in the fully-connected layers
Random-crop and flip data augmentation
ReLU activation, which does not saturate and gives fast gradients (~6x faster than tanh)
quiz.explanation: ReLU = max(0,x) does not flatten out for large positive inputs, so gradients stay strong and training runs ~6x faster than with tanh. Dropout and augmentation improve *generalization*, not raw training speed, and LRN is now obsolete. The trap is confusing 'most important overall trick' with 'biggest impact on speed' — for speed it is ReLU.

quiz.q: VGG-16 has 138M parameters. Where do the vast majority of those parameters live?
options: In the fully-connected layers (about 124M of the 138M)
Evenly spread across all 13 convolutional layers
In the 3x3 convolution filters of the deepest stage
In the global average pooling layer
quiz.explanation: About 124M of VGG-16's 138M parameters sit in the three FC layers — that is the architecture's central inefficiency and exactly what GoogLeNet removes with global average pooling. VGG has no global average pooling layer at all (that distractor describes GoogLeNet's fix, not VGG).

quiz.q: Following the standard CNN funnel rule, as data flows deeper through a network the spatial dimensions and channel dimensions change how?
options: Both spatial size and channel count grow together
Spatial size shrinks while the number of channels grows
Spatial size grows while channels shrink
Both stay constant; only the values change
quiz.explanation: The funnel (seen since LeNet, formalized by VGG as 'halve spatial, double channels') shrinks height/width via pooling/stride while increasing channel count — trading spatial location for richer feature meaning, and keeping per-layer computation roughly balanced. Growing both at once would explode computation; the network deliberately does the opposite on the spatial axis.

quiz.q: ResNet-50/101/152 use a 'bottleneck block' built as 1x1 conv -> 3x3 conv -> 1x1 conv with a skip connection. What is the role of the two 1x1 convolutions?
options: Downsample the spatial resolution by a factor of two each
Add two extra non-linearities that the skip connection would otherwise remove
Increase the receptive field beyond what the 3x3 conv provides
Reduce channels before the 3x3 conv and expand them back after, keeping computation affordable in very deep nets
quiz.explanation: The first 1x1 squeezes channels (reduce), the 3x3 does the spatial work cheaply, and the second 1x1 restores channel count (expand) — the same Inception bottleneck idea reused so 50-152 layer nets stay computationally manageable. 1x1 convs do not change spatial resolution, so the 'downsample by two' option is wrong.

quiz.q: Why could LeNet-5 NOT simply be applied directly to the ImageNet (224x224, 1000-class) task?
options: It had too many parameters to fit on 2012 GPUs
It used ReLU, which is incompatible with color images
With only ~60K parameters it lacks the capacity to model 1000 classes on large natural images
Its skip connections caused gradient explosion on large inputs
quiz.explanation: LeNet's ~60K parameters and 32x32 input were built for 10-class digit recognition; that capacity cannot represent 1000 classes over 224x224 photos. It used sigmoid/tanh (not ReLU), had far too FEW parameters (not too many), and had no skip connections at all — so every other option is factually wrong.

quiz.q: Across the ResNet family, ResNet-18 scores 10.9% top-5 error while ResNet-152 scores 3.6%. What general principle does this trend establish?
options: Deeper genuinely is better — once skip connections remove the degradation problem
Accuracy always improves with depth regardless of architecture
Parameter count is the only thing that determines accuracy
Beyond 50 layers accuracy plateaus and cannot improve further
quiz.explanation: The monotone drop in error with depth shows depth helps — but the crucial qualifier is 'with skip connections,' since plain nets degraded with depth. 'Depth always helps regardless of architecture' is exactly the false belief the degradation problem disproved. Note ResNet-152 (60M) ties AlexNet's param count yet is far more accurate, so params alone do not explain it.

descriptive.q: Derive why three stacked 3x3 convolutions are preferred over a single 7x7 convolution. Address both parameter count and expressiveness, and state the receptive-field relationship that makes the comparison fair.
descriptive.answer:
The comparison is only fair because the two options **see the same area of the input** — this is *receptive-field equivalence*. Stacking convolutions enlarges the receptive field: two 3x3 convs see the same region as one 5x5, and three 3x3 convs see the same region as one 7x7. So both candidates have an effective 7x7 view; the question is which builds it more efficiently.

**Parameter count.** Assume $C$ input channels and $C$ output channels throughout.

> [!MATH]
> One 7x7 filter: $7 \times 7 \times C^2 = 49C^2$ weights — the $7\times7$ window over $C$ input channels, repeated for $C$ output channels.
> Three 3x3 filters: $3 \times (3 \times 3 \times C^2) = 27C^2$ weights — three layers, each a $3\times3$ window with $C^2$ channel pairings.

Since $27C^2$ is about **45% fewer** than $49C^2$, the small-filter stack is cheaper for the same receptive field.

**Expressiveness.** A single 7x7 conv applies **one** ReLU non-linearity; three stacked 3x3 convs apply **three** ReLUs. More non-linear steps let the network represent more complex functions over the same receptive field.

> [!KEY]
> Three 3x3 convs win on both axes simultaneously: **fewer parameters (27C^2 vs 49C^2) AND more non-linearity (3 ReLUs vs 1).** This is the VGG principle that underpins essentially all modern CNN design.

descriptive.q: Explain the degradation problem in deep plain networks. Why is it definitely NOT overfitting, and how does residual learning (y = F(x) + x) solve it?
descriptive.answer:
**The observation.** A 56-layer *plain* CNN has **higher training error** than a 20-layer plain CNN. Naively, adding layers should never hurt: the deeper net could set its extra layers to the identity ("do nothing") and exactly match the shallower net. Instead it does worse.

**Why it is not overfitting.**

> [!WARNING]
> Overfitting is defined as **low training error, high test error** — the model fits the training data *too* well and fails to generalize. In the degradation problem the **training error itself is higher**. The model cannot even fit the data it is shown, so generalization is not the issue at all. It is an **optimization problem**: the deep plain stack is hard to train, and in particular it cannot easily learn the identity mapping in the extra layers.

**How residual learning solves it.** Instead of asking a block to learn the full target $H(x)$ directly, ResNet asks it to learn the **residual** $F(x) = H(x) - x$ and adds the input back via a skip connection: output $= F(x) + x$.

> [!INTUITION]
> Now the identity mapping is the *easy default*. If the best thing the block can do is leave the input alone ($H(x) = x$), it just needs $F(x) = 0$ — push the weights toward zero, which weight decay encourages for free. A plain block, by contrast, would have to learn a precise non-trivial weight pattern to reproduce its own input.

The skip path also creates a **gradient highway**: with $y = F(x) + x$, the backward gradient is $\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial x} + 1$ — here the $+1$ lets the gradient flow straight through without being multiplied by a long chain of weight matrices, so it does not vanish even at 152 layers. The result: deeper finally becomes better, with ResNet-152 reaching 3.57% top-5 error.

descriptive.q: What exactly is a 1x1 convolution, and why is it the key enabler of both the Inception module and ResNet bottleneck blocks? Include a concrete cost argument.
descriptive.answer:
**What it is.** A 1x1 convolution looks at exactly **one pixel at a time** and combines that pixel's values **across channels**. It behaves like a small fully-connected layer applied independently at every spatial location. Critically, it **does not change the spatial dimensions** (height and width are untouched); it only transforms the channel dimension — which means it can **cheaply increase or decrease the channel count** (e.g. $256 \to 64$).

**Why Inception needs it.** An Inception module runs $1\times1$, $3\times3$, $5\times5$, and pooling paths in parallel and concatenates them.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Input $28\times28\times256$. Naively applying 256 filters of each size gives a concatenated output of $28\times28\times768$ — and the channel count keeps growing every module, an unsustainable explosion. By first using a $1\times1$ conv to reduce $256 \to 64$ channels, then running the expensive $3\times3$/$5\times5$ on only 64 channels, the cost collapses while the parallel multi-scale idea survives.

**The cost argument.** Convolution cost scales with the *input channel count*. A $3\times3$ conv on $256$ input channels costs roughly $4\times$ as much per output as the same conv on $64$ channels. Inserting a cheap $1\times1$ squeeze first (its own cost is small because the window is just $1\times1$) cuts the dominant expense dramatically — the slides note savings up to ~90%.

**Why ResNet reuses it.** The deep ResNets (50/101/152) wrap their skip connection around a **bottleneck block**: $1\times1$ (reduce channels) $\to 3\times3$ (spatial work on few channels) $\to 1\times1$ (expand channels back). It is literally the Inception bottleneck trick imported to keep very deep networks affordable.

> [!KEY]
> The 1x1 conv = a channel mixer/compressor that leaves space alone. 'Squeeze, then process' is what makes both Inception and deep ResNet computationally feasible.

descriptive.q: Compare VGG-16 and GoogLeNet on parameter efficiency. VGG has 138M parameters but GoogLeNet has only 5M, yet GoogLeNet is more accurate. Explain the two architectural decisions behind this 28x gap.
descriptive.answer:
Both networks were 2014 ImageNet contenders, but they made opposite bets on where to spend parameters.

**Where VGG wastes parameters.** Of VGG-16's **138M** parameters, roughly **124M live in the three fully-connected layers**. The final convolutional feature maps are *flattened* into an enormous vector and connected densely to large hidden layers — a huge, mostly redundant block of weights. The convolutional part of VGG is actually fairly lean; the FC head is the bloat.

**Decision 1 — Global Average Pooling (GAP) replaces large FC layers.** Instead of flattening, GoogLeNet **averages each channel's entire spatial map into a single number**, producing one value per channel feeding directly into the classifier. This eliminates the ~100M+ FC parameters in one stroke and also acts as a structural regularizer.

**Decision 2 — 1x1 bottleneck convolutions.** Inside each Inception module, $1\times1$ convs reduce the channel count (e.g. $256\to64$) before the expensive $3\times3$/$5\times5$ convolutions, slashing both parameters and FLOPs per module.

| | VGG-16 | GoogLeNet |
|---|---|---|
| Depth | 16 | 22 |
| Parameters | 138M | 5M |
| Classifier head | Large FC (~124M params) | Global Average Pooling |
| Channel control | None (uniform 3x3) | 1x1 bottlenecks |
| Top-5 error | 7.3% | 6.7% |

> [!KEY]
> 'Smarter beats bigger.' GoogLeNet is *deeper* (22 vs 16) and *more accurate* with **28x fewer** parameters, purely by removing the FC bloat (GAP) and controlling channel growth (1x1 bottlenecks). Note that skip connections are NOT involved here — those are ResNet's contribution a year later.

descriptive.q: List the five CNN design principles distilled in this session, and for each name the architecture that demonstrated it and the problem it solves.
descriptive.answer:
These five rules are the practical takeaway — the toolkit you carry into designing any CNN.

| # | Principle | Demonstrated by | Problem it solves |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Use small 3x3 filters stacked deep | VGG | Same receptive field as big filters but fewer parameters (27C^2 vs 49C^2) and more non-linearity |
| 2 | Halve spatial, double channels per stage | LeNet/VGG | Keeps per-layer computation balanced; the funnel trades location for feature richness |
| 3 | Use 1x1 convs for channel reduction (bottleneck) | Inception (reused in ResNet) | Controls the channel explosion; makes expensive 3x3/5x5 convs affordable |
| 4 | Add skip connections beyond ~20 layers | ResNet | Fixes the degradation problem and provides a gradient highway so very deep nets train |
| 5 | Global Average Pooling instead of large FC layers | GoogLeNet | Eliminates the 100M+ FC parameter bloat seen in VGG |

> [!EXAMPLE]
> These rules compose. ResNet-50's **bottleneck block** uses Rule 1 (3x3 core), Rule 3 (1x1 reduce/expand around it), and Rule 4 (skip connection wrapping all three) — three principles in a single building block, with Rule 5 (GAP) at the network's end.

> [!KEY]
> The narrative reduces to: small stacked filters + balanced funnel + 1x1 bottlenecks + global average pooling + skip connections. Together these turned a ~25%-error vision problem into a superhuman (3.6%) one in three years.

descriptive.q: AlexNet is often summarized as 'LeNet, scaled up.' Compare the two architectures, and explain why scaling up LeNet required several non-architectural innovations to actually work.
descriptive.answer:
**The architectural similarity.** AlexNet keeps LeNet's fundamental template — a funnel of convolution + pooling stages followed by fully-connected layers (Conv -> Pool -> ... -> FC). In that sense it is the same recipe: 5 conv + 3 FC layers in AlexNet versus LeNet's smaller conv/pool/FC stack.

| | LeNet-5 (1998) | AlexNet (2012) |
|---|---|---|
| Input size | 32 x 32 | 224 x 224 |
| Parameters | 60K | 60M |
| Depth | 5 layers | 8 layers |
| Activation | Sigmoid/Tanh | ReLU |
| Training | CPU, minutes | GPU, days |
| Task | 10 digits | 1000 classes |

**Why scaling required more than 'make it bigger.'** A 60M-parameter network on 224x224 images with 1000 classes runs into problems LeNet never faced, and each needed a fix:

- **Vanishing gradients / slow training.** LeNet's sigmoid/tanh saturate, killing gradients in a deeper/larger net. **ReLU** ($\max(0,x)$) does not saturate for positive inputs, giving fast, healthy gradients — about 6x faster training than tanh. This was the single biggest factor for training speed.
- **Overfitting from huge capacity.** 60M parameters can memorize. **Dropout (0.5)** in the FC layers randomly disables neurons during training, regularizing without extra data. **Data augmentation** (random crops, flips, color jitter) multiplies the effective dataset.
- **Raw compute.** Training 60M parameters was infeasible on CPUs; **GPU training** (split across 2 GPUs) made it possible.

> [!KEY]
> The lesson is *combination*: it was not any one trick but **ReLU + Dropout + Augmentation + GPU together** that let the 'same recipe, 1000x bigger kitchen' actually cook. Without these, simply enlarging LeNet would have trained too slowly and overfit badly. (Local Response Normalization was also used but is now obsolete, replaced by BatchNorm.)

topics.title: Transfer Learning
topics.summary: Transfer learning reuses a network pre-trained on a giant dataset (ImageNet) to solve a new task with little data and compute, either by freezing the backbone as a feature extractor or by fine-tuning some layers with a tiny learning rate. The same pre-trained backbones power object detection (Faster R-CNN, YOLO) and pixel-level segmentation (U-Net).

notes.heading: Why Transfer Learning? The Problem and the Big Idea
notes.body:
**Start with the pain.** The best image networks are monsters to train. ResNet-152 reached its famous ImageNet accuracy after about *two weeks of training on 8 GPUs using 1.2 million hand-labelled images*. Now look at a typical project: maybe 500 labelled photos, one laptop (or a free Colab GPU), no budget to label more, and it is due Friday. Training a deep CNN from scratch on 500 images would just overfit and fail.

**The escape hatch.** Someone already paid that two-week GPU bill for you. A network trained on ImageNet has learned to *see* — to detect edges, textures, shapes and object parts. Most of that visual knowledge is not specific to ImageNet's 1000 categories; it is generic. Transfer learning means keeping that hard-won knowledge and bolting a small new piece onto it for your task.

> [!KEY] Transfer learning = reuse the features a network already learned on a large dataset (almost always ImageNet) to solve a *different* task that has limited data and compute.

> [!ANALOGY] A chef trained in French cuisine can switch to Italian almost overnight. The knife skills, heat control and timing all transfer; only the specific flavours and recipes change. The pre-trained network is the trained chef; your task is the new cuisine.

There are exactly two ways to do it, and the whole session hangs on telling them apart:

1. **Feature extraction** — *freeze* the pre-trained network and train only a new head (classifier) on top.
2. **Fine-tuning** — *unfreeze* some of the pre-trained layers and let them adapt, using a much smaller learning rate.

[[fig:transfer-learning]]

notes.heading: What Each CNN Layer Actually Learns
notes.body:
Transfer learning works because of *what* a CNN learns at different depths. If you visualise the filters, a clear story emerges from shallow to deep.

| Depth | What it detects | How generic? |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 1 | edges, colours | universal — useful for *any* image |
| Layer 2 | textures | still very generic |
| Layer 3 | repeated patterns / motifs | starting to specialise |
| Layers 4-5 | object parts (a wheel, an eye, a petal) | task-specific |

> [!INTUITION] Every natural image — a cat, an X-ray, a satellite photo — is built from edges and textures. So the *early* layers of an ImageNet model are immediately useful on a brand-new domain. The *late* layers have learned 'this combination of parts means golden retriever', which only helps if your task looks like ImageNet.

> [!KEY] Early layers = generic (transfer everywhere). Late layers = specialised (transfer only to similar tasks). This single fact explains every transfer-learning decision in the session: you keep the generic layers and replace or adapt the specialised ones.

> [!EXAM] A classic question is *which layers transfer best across very different domains?* The answer is the **early** layers (edges, textures), because those features are universal — not the deep layers, even though they hold more information.

notes.heading: Feature Extraction: Freeze the Body, Train a New Head
notes.body:
**The plain idea.** Treat the pre-trained CNN as a fixed *feature machine*. You feed in your image, it spits out a feature vector (for ResNet-50 that is a 2048-number summary, for ResNet-18 a 512-number one), and you train a small new classifier on those numbers. The big network never changes.

**Why it is so cheap.** You only train the new head — often around 0.1% of all the parameters — so it runs fast and needs very little data. There is almost no risk of overfitting because there is almost nothing new to overfit *with*.

> [!MATH] 'Freezing' a layer means setting `requires_grad = False` for its weights. In words: the layer still does its job in the forward pass (it still produces features), but during backprop no gradient is stored for it, so the optimizer never updates those weights. They are bolted in place.

The PyTorch pattern is tiny:

```python
model = resnet50(weights='IMAGENET1K_V1')
# 1. Freeze every pre-trained parameter
for param in model.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False
# 2. Replace the final classifier with your own
model.fc = nn.Linear(2048, num_classes)  # new layer: requires_grad=True by default
# 3. Hand ONLY the new head to the optimizer
optimizer = Adam(model.fc.parameters(), lr=0.001)
```

The payoff: you train only about 0.1% of the parameters yet still get high accuracy.

> [!WARNING] A subtle point: the new `model.fc` you create has `requires_grad = True` automatically, so it *will* train even though everything else is frozen. But if you pass `model.parameters()` (everything) to the optimizer instead of `model.fc.parameters()`, you waste effort computing updates that are all zero anyway. Pass only the trainable head.

[[fig:feature-extract-vs-finetune]]

notes.heading: Fine-Tuning: Unfreeze Carefully, with a Tiny Learning Rate
notes.body:
When feature extraction is not enough — usually because your domain differs from ImageNet — you *unfreeze* some of the late layers and let them adapt to your data. This is fine-tuning.

**The one rule that matters most.** Use a learning rate 10-100x smaller on the pre-trained layers than on the new head.

> [!INTUITION] Picture the pre-trained weights as a beautifully tuned instrument. A normal-size learning-rate step is a sledgehammer — in the very first epoch it would smash the carefully learned features before they ever help you. This wreck is called **catastrophic forgetting**: the network forgets its ImageNet knowledge. A tiny learning rate nudges the features gently instead of erasing them.

A second, related rule: **train the new head first.** When you attach a fresh head, its weights are random, so it produces *large, noisy gradients* in the first updates. If the backbone is unfrozen at that moment, those wild gradients flow back and corrupt the good features. So: freeze the backbone, train the head for 1-2 epochs, *then* unfreeze.

Three fine-tuning strategies, from safe to bold:

| Strategy | What you unfreeze | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| Conservative | last 1-2 blocks only | small datasets; lowest risk |
| Aggressive | all layers, very small LR | large data or very different domain |
| Gradual unfreezing | head first, then one more block each epoch | beginners; safest overall |

> [!KEY] When in doubt, start frozen (feature extraction). Unfreeze more layers only if accuracy plateaus. Always train the head before unfreezing, and always use a much smaller LR on pre-trained layers than on the head.

notes.heading: The Decision Matrix: Data Size and Domain Similarity
notes.body:
How do you choose between feature extraction and fine-tuning? Two questions decide it: *how much labelled data do you have?* and *how similar is your domain to ImageNet?*

| | Small dataset | Large dataset |
|---|---|---|
| **Similar domain** | Feature extraction (freeze, train head) | Fine-tune all layers |
| **Different domain** | Fine-tune the last few layers | Fine-tune all (or even train from scratch) |

> [!EXAMPLE] *300 flower photos, 5 species.* Flowers look like ImageNet (similar domain) and 300 images is tiny. The answer is **feature extraction** — fine-tuning would overfit with so little data.

> [!EXAMPLE] *10,000 chest X-rays for pneumonia.* X-rays are a different domain from natural photos, and 10,000 is a decent amount of data. The answer is **fine-tuning** — the late layers must re-learn medical patterns.

**Domain shift.** A model trained on domain A often drops in accuracy on domain B — studio photos vs phone photos, US hospital X-rays vs a rural clinic's older machine, synthetic training images vs the messy real world. This gap is called **domain shift**.

> [!KEY] The simplest fix for domain shift is to fine-tune on a small labelled set from the *target* domain. Even 100-500 target samples can close most of the gap. Rule of thumb overall: start with feature extraction, unfreeze more if needed.

notes.heading: From Classification to Detection: WHAT and WHERE
notes.body:
Classification answers one question — *what is in this image?* ('a cat'). **Object detection** answers two — *what* AND *where* — returning a labelled bounding box for every object: 'cat at $(x_1,y_1,x_2,y_2)$, dog at $(x_3,y_3,x_4,y_4)$'. Here $(x_1,y_1)$ is the top-left corner of the box and $(x_2,y_2)$ the bottom-right corner — together they pin down the rectangle.

**The naive approach and why it dies.** Slide a window over the image at every position and every scale, and classify each crop. For a 1000x1000 image with about 100 scales and a stride of 10 pixels, that is *millions* of forward passes per image — hopelessly slow.

**Two-stage: Faster R-CNN.** A *Region Proposal Network* (RPN) looks at the shared backbone features once and proposes only about 2000 'interesting' regions. Stage two classifies each region and refines its box. Because the backbone is computed once and reused, this is orders of magnitude faster than sliding windows.

**Single-shot: YOLO (You Only Look Once).** Divide the image into an $S \times S$ grid; each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes with confidence scores plus $C$ class probabilities — all in *one* forward pass. Here $S$ is the grid size, $B$ how many boxes a cell may propose, and $C$ the number of classes. One pass means real-time speed (30+ FPS).

| | Two-stage (Faster R-CNN) | Single-shot (YOLO) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | slower | real-time |
| Accuracy | higher | slightly lower |
| Small objects | better | weaker |
| Typical use | medical, high-stakes | video, robotics |

> [!MATH] To score a predicted box we use **IoU** (Intersection over Union): $$\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{area of overlap}}{\text{area of union}}$$ In words: divide the area where the predicted box and the true box overlap by the total area they cover together. IoU = 1 is a perfect match, 0 means no overlap; a detection with IoU > 0.5 is usually counted as 'correct'.

> [!KEY] Both detector families sit on a pre-trained CNN backbone — transfer learning is the foundation. YOLO dominates industry because speed usually beats the last 1% of accuracy.

notes.heading: Segmentation: Labelling Every Pixel
notes.body:
Detection gives you rectangles, but a rectangle is a crude outline. **Segmentation** classifies *every pixel*, producing an exact mask of the object's shape.

- **Semantic segmentation:** label each pixel by class. All cats get painted the same colour — it does not separate individuals.
- **Instance segmentation:** like semantic, but it also distinguishes individuals (cat 1 vs cat 2 get different masks).

> [!INTUITION] Why bother when a box is easier? Medicine needs the *exact boundary* of a tumour, not a rectangle that includes healthy tissue. A self-driving car needs to know precisely which pixels are road vs sidewalk.

**U-Net: encoder-decoder with skip connections.** The classic architecture has two halves:

- **Encoder (contracting path):** repeatedly downsamples (e.g. 256x256 then 128, 64, 32, 16), building rich *semantic* features but throwing away fine spatial detail.
- **Decoder (expanding path):** upsamples back to full resolution (16 then 32, 64, 128, 256) so the output mask is the same size as the input.
- **Skip connections:** copy the high-resolution feature maps from each encoder level straight across to the matching decoder level.

> [!KEY] Skip connections preserve the fine spatial detail — edges, exact boundaries — that downsampling destroys. Without them the decoder only has the tiny compressed bottleneck to work from, so the predicted boundaries come out blurry and imprecise.

Upsampling in the decoder is done with transposed convolution, or bilinear interpolation followed by a normal convolution — essentially the reverse of pooling, expanding the spatial size back up. The same encoder-decoder-plus-skips idea (often with a pre-trained backbone as the encoder) powers medical imaging, autonomous driving and satellite land-use mapping.

notes.heading: A Practical Recipe and Cheat Sheet
notes.body:
Put it together into a workflow you can actually follow.

1. **Always start pre-trained.** Training from scratch is a last resort, used only with large data and a very different domain.
2. **Train the head first** (1-2 epochs, backbone frozen), then unfreeze if you need more.
3. **Use differential learning rates** — 10-100x smaller LR for pre-trained layers.
4. **Freeze batch norm** during fine-tuning: keep BN layers in eval mode so their running statistics, estimated from millions of ImageNet images, are not wrecked by your tiny, noisy batches.
5. **Augment your data** — flips, crops, colour jitter, rotation give 'free' extra training examples and fight overfitting.
6. **Use an LR schedule** (warmup + cosine or step decay) and try **progressive resizing** (train at 128x128, then 224x224).
7. **Monitor both losses.** If training loss falls but validation loss rises, you are overfitting — stop early.

[[fig:data-augmentation]]

> [!WARNING] The most common fine-tuning mistakes: using a full-size learning rate on pre-trained layers (catastrophic forgetting), unfreezing everything before the head is trained (random gradients corrupt good features), and leaving batch-norm statistics updating on tiny batches. All three quietly destroy the very knowledge you came for.

When you just need something that works:

| Task | Default choice | Alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Classification | ResNet-50 | EfficientNet |
| Detection (speed) | YOLOv8 | SSD |
| Detection (accuracy) | Faster R-CNN | DETR |
| Segmentation (medical) | U-Net | — |
| Segmentation (general) | DeepLabV3 | Segment Anything (SAM) |

> [!KEY] When in doubt: ResNet-50 backbone + transfer learning. It is 'boring' but it reliably works.

quiz.q: You have 300 labelled photos of flowers and want to classify 5 species using a ResNet pre-trained on ImageNet. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Fine-tune all layers with a normal-size learning rate so the network fully adapts
Feature extraction: freeze the backbone and train only a new classification head
Train a fresh ResNet from scratch so it is specialised to flowers
Unfreeze every layer but skip training the new head first
quiz.explanation: 300 images is very small and flowers are visually similar to ImageNet's natural images (similar domain). With so little data, feature extraction trains only the head and barely risks overfitting. Fine-tuning all layers (the tempting option) would overfit badly with so few images; training from scratch needs far more data.

quiz.q: You have 50,000 labelled satellite images for land-use classification, a domain quite unlike ImageNet's everyday photos. What is the best strategy?
options: Fine-tune the layers with a small LR on the backbone, because the domain differs and you have plenty of data
Feature extraction only, because you should always freeze the backbone
Use the pre-trained model's predictions directly with no training
Replace only the final layer and never touch any earlier layers
quiz.explanation: Different domain plus large dataset is the matrix cell that calls for fine-tuning (here, most or all layers with a small LR on pre-trained weights). The late layers must re-learn satellite-specific patterns, and 50,000 images is enough to do so without overfitting. Pure feature extraction would leave the specialised layers stuck on ImageNet objects that do not match satellite imagery.

quiz.q: Which layers of an ImageNet-pretrained CNN are most likely to transfer usefully to a completely different image domain such as medical scans?
options: The final fully-connected layer, because it makes the decision
The early layers, because they detect universal edges and textures
The deepest convolutional layers, because they hold the most information
None, because different domains share no transferable features
quiz.explanation: Early layers learn generic edges, colours and textures that appear in every natural image, so they transfer everywhere. The deep layers (the tempting 'most information' answer) encode ImageNet-specific object parts and transfer poorly to a different domain; the final FC layer is the most task-specific of all and is usually replaced.

quiz.q: During fine-tuning, why use a learning rate 10-100x smaller on the pre-trained layers than on the new head?
options: Smaller learning rates always give higher accuracy on every layer
A large LR would overwrite the useful pre-trained features in the first epoch (catastrophic forgetting)
Pre-trained layers have more parameters, so they need smaller steps for memory reasons
It makes training faster by skipping gradient computation in those layers
quiz.explanation: The pre-trained weights are already good; a full-size step would take a huge, destructive jump and erase that knowledge in the very first updates, which is catastrophic forgetting. A small LR adapts them gently. It is not a universal rule that smaller is always better (the head needs a normal LR to learn quickly), and a small LR does not skip gradient computation.

quiz.q: In PyTorch you set `param.requires_grad = False` on a layer. What actually happens during training?
options: The layer's outputs are set to zero during the forward pass
The layer is removed from the network and produces no output
The layer still produces features in the forward pass, but its weights never get updated
The layer trains, but only on every other batch
quiz.explanation: Freezing only switches off gradient tracking, so no update is computed for those weights, but the layer still runs normally in the forward pass and contributes its fixed features. It is not deleted and its outputs are not zeroed; the distractors confuse 'frozen weights' with 'no output', a common misconception.

quiz.q: Best practice says to train the new head for 1-2 epochs with the backbone frozen BEFORE unfreezing any pre-trained layers. Why?
options: A freshly initialised head produces large, noisy gradients that would corrupt the good backbone features if it were unfrozen
The optimizer cannot handle frozen and unfrozen layers at the same time
Batch norm only works when the head is trained first
It guarantees the model will never overfit
quiz.explanation: The new head starts with random weights, so early on it makes wild predictions and sends large error gradients backward. If the backbone is unfrozen at that moment, those noisy gradients flow into and damage the carefully learned features. Warming up the head first lets it settle before the backbone is exposed. The optimizer can certainly mix frozen and trainable layers, and nothing guarantees zero overfitting.

quiz.q: How does the Region Proposal Network (RPN) in Faster R-CNN improve on the naive sliding-window detector?
options: It still slides a window but uses a larger stride, cutting the count in half
It removes the need for a CNN backbone entirely
It classifies the whole image in a single pass without using any regions
It reuses the shared backbone features to propose only about 2000 candidate regions instead of testing millions of windows
quiz.explanation: Sliding windows run a forward pass at millions of positions and scales. The RPN computes the backbone features once and learns to propose only about 2000 promising regions, which are then classified, which is orders of magnitude less work. It does not remove the backbone (it shares it), and merely enlarging the stride (the near-miss distractor) would still leave far too many windows and hurt accuracy.

quiz.q: In YOLO, the image is divided into an S by S grid. What does each grid cell predict?
options: Only whether the cell contains any object, with no boxes or classes
A single pixel-level mask for the whole image
Region proposals that a second network then classifies
A few bounding boxes with confidence scores plus class probabilities, all in one forward pass
quiz.explanation: YOLO is single-shot: each cell predicts B boxes with confidences and C class probabilities simultaneously, so the whole image is detected in one pass, which is what makes it real-time. Producing proposals for a second stage describes Faster R-CNN (two-stage), and pixel masks describe segmentation, not detection.

quiz.q: For detecting many small objects in high-stakes medical images where accuracy matters more than speed, which detector family is generally the better fit?
options: Sliding-window classification, because it checks every position
Single-shot detectors like YOLO, because they are always more accurate
Two-stage detectors like Faster R-CNN, which are more accurate and stronger on small objects
A segmentation network, because boxes cannot be used in medicine
quiz.explanation: Two-stage detectors trade speed for higher accuracy and handle small objects better, which suits high-stakes, accuracy-first settings. YOLO is faster but slightly less accurate and weaker on small objects, so 'always more accurate' is false. Sliding windows are computationally hopeless, and detection (not segmentation) is exactly the box-producing task being asked about.

quiz.q: What is the main role of the skip connections in a U-Net, and what happens without them?
options: They carry high-resolution spatial detail from encoder to decoder; without them the decoder only has the compressed bottleneck and produces blurry boundaries
They speed up training by skipping layers, with no effect on output quality
They prevent the encoder from downsampling the image
They convert the segmentation mask into bounding boxes
quiz.explanation: Downsampling in the encoder discards fine spatial detail; skip connections copy that detail directly across to the decoder so it can reconstruct sharp boundaries. Remove them and the decoder must rebuild everything from the tiny bottleneck, giving blurry masks. They are not just a training shortcut, and they do not stop downsampling or change the task to detection.

quiz.q: An image contains three cats. A model outputs a mask that paints all cat-pixels the same colour but does not separate the three animals. What kind of segmentation is this?
options: Classification, because it outputs a single label for the image
Instance segmentation, because it separates each individual object
Object detection, because it draws one box per cat
Semantic segmentation, because it labels pixels by class but does not distinguish individuals
quiz.explanation: Semantic segmentation assigns a class to every pixel, so all cats share one colour and individuals are not separated, exactly what is described. Instance segmentation (the tempting option) would give each of the three cats its own distinct mask. Detection produces boxes and classification produces a single image label, neither of which is a pixel mask.

quiz.q: A predicted box and the ground-truth box overlap over an area of 60, and together they cover a union area of 100. Using the standard rule, is this counted as a correct detection?
options: Yes, because any non-zero overlap counts as a correct detection
No, because IoU = 60/100 = 0.6, which is below the usual threshold of 0.9
Yes, because IoU = 60/100 = 0.6, which is above the usual IoU > 0.5 threshold for a correct detection
No, because IoU should be the overlap divided by the predicted box's area, not by the union
quiz.explanation: IoU = area of overlap / area of union = 60/100 = 0.6, which clears the standard 0.5 cutoff, so it counts as correct. The wrong option about dividing by the predicted-box area mis-states the formula (the denominator is the union of both boxes). 'Any overlap counts' ignores the threshold, and 0.9 is not the usual cutoff.

descriptive.q: Compare feature extraction and fine-tuning. Explain how the network is changed in each, and use the data-size by domain-similarity matrix to say when you would pick each.
descriptive.answer:
**Both** start from the same place — a CNN already trained on ImageNet — but they differ in *what is allowed to change*.

| | Feature extraction | Fine-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| Backbone weights | frozen (`requires_grad=False`) | some or all unfrozen |
| What trains | only the new head | head + chosen backbone layers |
| Learning rate | normal (head only) | tiny on backbone, normal on head |
| Data needed | very little | more |
| Risk | low (little to overfit) | overfitting / catastrophic forgetting |
| Speed | very fast (~0.1% of params) | slower |

**Feature extraction** turns the pre-trained network into a fixed feature machine: an image goes in, a feature vector (e.g. 2048 numbers for ResNet-50) comes out, and a small new classifier learns on those numbers. The backbone never moves.

**Fine-tuning** additionally unfreezes some late layers so they can adapt to your data, using a learning rate 10-100x smaller on those pre-trained layers so their knowledge is nudged, not destroyed.

The choice follows two questions — *how much data?* and *how similar to ImageNet?*

| | Small dataset | Large dataset |
|---|---|---|
| Similar domain | Feature extraction | Fine-tune all |
| Different domain | Fine-tune last layers | Fine-tune all (or scratch) |

> [!KEY] Rule of thumb: start frozen (feature extraction); unfreeze more only if accuracy plateaus. Small + similar strongly favours feature extraction because there is too little data to safely fine-tune; large + different favours full fine-tuning because the specialised layers must be re-learned and there is enough data to do it.

descriptive.q: Explain catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuning. Why do we (a) use a much smaller learning rate on pre-trained layers and (b) train the new head before unfreezing the backbone?
descriptive.answer:
**Catastrophic forgetting** is when, during fine-tuning, the network's careful pre-trained features get overwritten and 'forgotten', usually because the weights took steps that were too big.

**(a) Small LR on pre-trained layers.**

> [!INTUITION] The pre-trained weights already sit in a good spot.
