The learning rate sets the *step size* of each update; a normal-size step is huge relative to how little these weights need to move, so in the first epoch it would fling them out of their good region and erase ImageNet knowledge. A 10-100x smaller LR makes gentle adjustments that adapt the features without destroying them.

The new head, by contrast, starts random and needs a *normal* LR to learn quickly — hence **differential learning rates**: small on the backbone, normal on the head.

**(b) Train the head first.** A freshly initialised head outputs near-random predictions, so the loss is large and the gradients flowing backward are large and noisy. If the backbone were unfrozen at that moment, those noisy gradients would pour into the good features and corrupt them. Training the head for 1-2 epochs with the backbone frozen lets the head reach sensible predictions first; only then is it safe to unfreeze, because now the backward signal is meaningful rather than random.

> [!KEY] Both practices protect the same thing: the pre-trained features. A small LR limits how far they can move; head-warmup ensures the gradients reaching them are sensible before they are allowed to move at all.

descriptive.q: Trace the evolution from sliding-window detection to Faster R-CNN to YOLO. Why is sliding window infeasible, what does each later method change, and when would you prefer each modern detector?
descriptive.answer:
**Sliding window (naive).** Slide a classifier window over every position and scale and classify each crop. For a 1000x1000 image with about 100 scales and stride 10, that is *millions* of forward passes per image — far too slow for real use, and most windows contain nothing.

**Faster R-CNN (two-stage).** Compute the CNN backbone features *once*; a Region Proposal Network (RPN) reads those shared features and proposes only about 2000 candidate regions. Stage two classifies each region and refines its box. By sharing the backbone and proposing instead of exhaustively scanning, it is orders of magnitude faster than sliding windows and very accurate.

**YOLO (single-shot).** Divide the image into an $S \times S$ grid; each cell predicts $B$ boxes (with confidences) and $C$ class probabilities in *one* forward pass — $S$ is the grid size, $B$ the boxes per cell, $C$ the number of classes. No separate proposal stage, so it runs in real time (30+ FPS).

| | Faster R-CNN | YOLO |
|---|---|---|
| Stages | two | one |
| Speed | slower | real-time |
| Accuracy / small objects | higher / better | slightly lower / weaker |
| Pick it for | medical, high-stakes accuracy | dashcam video, robotics, live feeds |

> [!KEY] Each step removes wasted work: Faster R-CNN replaces millions of windows with about 2000 proposals on shared features; YOLO removes the proposal stage entirely for a single pass. Both still rest on a pre-trained CNN backbone — transfer learning underpins modern detection.

descriptive.q: Describe the U-Net architecture and the role of skip connections. What would happen to the output without them? Distinguish semantic from instance segmentation.
descriptive.answer:
**U-Net** is an encoder-decoder built to label every pixel.

- **Encoder (contracting path):** repeatedly downsamples (e.g. 256 then 128, 64, 32, 16). Each step grows the *semantic* understanding ('this region is tumour-like') but discards fine spatial detail.
- **Decoder (expanding path):** upsamples back up (16 then 32, 64, 128, 256) so the output mask matches the input resolution. Upsampling uses transposed convolution or bilinear interpolation followed by a convolution — the reverse of pooling.
- **Skip connections:** copy each encoder level's high-resolution feature map straight across to the matching decoder level.

**Without skip connections** the decoder would have to reconstruct the full-resolution mask from only the tiny compressed bottleneck. It retains the *what* but has lost the *exactly where*, so boundaries come out blurry and imprecise — fatal for tasks like tracing a tumour edge.

> [!KEY] Skip connections re-inject the fine spatial detail (edges, exact boundaries) that downsampling destroyed, letting the decoder produce sharp, pixel-accurate masks.

**Semantic vs instance segmentation:**

| | Semantic | Instance |
|---|---|---|
| Labels | every pixel by class | every pixel by class *and* by individual |
| Three cats | all one colour | three separate masks |
| Question answered | 'which pixels are cat?' | 'which pixels are cat number 2?' |

Semantic segmentation cannot tell two cats apart; instance segmentation gives each its own mask.

descriptive.q: What is domain shift? Give two examples, explain why it happens, and describe the simplest fix and why freezing batch-norm matters during that fix.
descriptive.answer:
**Domain shift** is when a model trained on data from one distribution (domain A) is deployed on data from a different distribution (domain B) and its accuracy drops, even though the *task* is the same.

**Examples:**
- Crisp studio product photos at training time vs blurry phone-camera photos in production.
- X-rays from a modern US hospital machine vs a rural clinic's older scanner.
- (Also common: synthetic or simulated training images vs the messy real world.)

**Why it happens.** The network has tuned its features to the statistics of domain A — its lighting, noise, resolution, colour balance. Domain B has different statistics, so the learned features no longer line up and predictions degrade.

**Simplest fix:** fine-tune on a small *labelled* set from the target domain. Even 100-500 target samples can close most of the gap, because the late layers re-align to the new statistics while the generic early layers stay useful.

**Why freeze batch norm during that fix.**

> [!WARNING] Batch-norm layers carry running mean and variance statistics estimated from millions of ImageNet images. If you keep them in training mode while fine-tuning on a tiny target set, they get overwritten by noisy statistics from your small, possibly unrepresentative batches, destabilising training. Keeping BN in eval mode preserves those well-estimated statistics so the rest of the fine-tuning stays stable.

descriptive.q: You are given 1,000 labelled images in a domain somewhat different from ImageNet and a single free Colab GPU. Write a step-by-step fine-tuning recipe and justify each step.
descriptive.answer:
With about 1,000 images, a somewhat-different domain, and a single free GPU, the data is *small-to-medium* and the domain is *not identical* to ImageNet — so a careful, mostly-frozen fine-tune is ideal.

**Step-by-step recipe:**

1. **Start from a pre-trained backbone** (e.g. ResNet-50, `weights='IMAGENET1K_V1'`). Training from scratch on 1,000 images would overfit badly.
2. **Replace the final FC layer** with one sized to your number of classes; it is trainable by default.
3. **Freeze the backbone and train the head for 1-2 epochs.** This lets the random head settle so it stops emitting noisy gradients before any backbone layer is exposed.
4. **Unfreeze the last 1-2 blocks** (conservative, because the dataset is small) and continue with **differential learning rates** — e.g. about 1e-3 on the head and 1e-4 to 1e-5 on the unfrozen backbone — to avoid catastrophic forgetting.
5. **Keep batch norm in eval mode** so its ImageNet-estimated statistics are not corrupted by tiny batches.
6. **Augment heavily** (flips, crops, colour jitter, rotation) to manufacture extra effective data and curb overfitting.
7. **Use an LR schedule** (warmup + cosine decay) and consider progressive resizing (128x128 then 224x224) to speed up early training.
8. **Monitor train and validation loss and use early stopping.** With only 1,000 images, overfitting appears fast; if val loss rises while train loss falls, stop.

> [!KEY] The design follows the decision matrix: small data means stay mostly frozen (conservative fine-tune), and a slightly different domain means unfreeze the last block or two rather than nothing. Every safeguard — head-first, small backbone LR, frozen BN, augmentation, early stopping — exists to protect the pre-trained knowledge and prevent overfitting.

topics.title: Sequence Modeling & RNNs
topics.summary: Why ordinary networks fail on ordered data, and how RNNs fix it by sharing one cell across time and carrying a hidden-state memory; we then unroll the loop, train it with Backpropagation Through Time, and see why repeated multiplication makes gradients vanish (or explode), capping a vanilla RNN's memory at roughly 20 steps.

notes.heading: Why Ordinary Networks Choke on Sequences
notes.body:
The world is full of **sequences** — text, audio, stock prices, video frames, DNA. They all share one thing: **order carries meaning**. Shuffle the words of a sentence or the frames of a video and the meaning falls apart.

> [!INTUITION] "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" use the exact same words. The only difference is *order*. Any model that ignores order treats these as identical — clearly wrong.

A plain **MLP** (multi-layer perceptron) was built for fixed-size, unordered feature vectors. Forcing a sequence into it breaks in three ways:

| Problem | What goes wrong |
|---|---|
| **Fixed input size** | An MLP has a preset number of input neurons. A 5-word and a 500-word sentence can't both fit the same slots. |
| **No notion of order** | After flattening, position 1 and position 100 are just unrelated features — nothing tells the network they form an ordered sequence. |
| **Parameter explosion** | 500 words × 300-dim embeddings = 150,000 input neurons, each dragging its own weights. |

> [!KEY] Flattening a sentence into one long vector throws away its **temporal structure** — the very thing that makes it a sequence.

We also need **long-range dependencies**: in "The students who came from France ... speak French", the verb "speak" must agree with "students" — six words back. A good sequence model has to carry information across that gap.

notes.heading: The Big Idea: Share One Function Across Time
notes.body:
Instead of a new set of weights for every position, an RNN applies **the same small function at every timestep**.

> [!ANALOGY] Your brain doesn't grow a new neuron for each word you read. The *same* neural circuits process word 1 and word 1000. An RNN does exactly this: one reusable cell, applied again and again.

This single move solves all three MLP problems at once: it handles **any length** (just keep applying the function), uses a **constant number of parameters** no matter how long the input, and — crucially — gives the network a **memory**.

[[fig:rnn-cell]]

That memory is a vector called the **hidden state**, written $h_t$ — here $h_t$ is the network's running summary of everything it has seen up to time $t$ (the current step). At each step the cell takes two things: the **current input** $x_t$ (the new word or sample at time $t$) and the **previous hidden state** $h_{t-1}$ (its memory from the step before). It blends them and produces a **new hidden state** $h_t$, which is passed forward to the next step.

> [!KEY] An RNN is just a network with a feedback loop: its own output (the hidden state) is fed back in as part of its next input. That loop **is** its memory.

notes.heading: Inside the Cell: One Equation, Three Matrices
notes.body:
Here is the entire vanilla RNN in one line:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

> [!MATH] In words: to get the new memory $h_t$, take the new input $x_t$ and reshape it with the matrix $W_{xh}$; take the old memory $h_{t-1}$ and reshape it with the matrix $W_{hh}$; add them together along with a bias $b_h$ (a learnable constant offset); then squash the result with $\tanh$ so every number lands between $-1$ and $1$.

Glossing every symbol:
- $h_t$ — the **new hidden state** (memory after seeing step $t$).
- $h_{t-1}$ — the **previous hidden state** (memory before step $t$).
- $x_t$ — the **input** at step $t$ (e.g. the current word's vector).
- $W_{xh}$ — the **input-to-hidden** weights: how to process new input.
- $W_{hh}$ — the **hidden-to-hidden** weights: how to carry and update memory.
- $b_h$ — a **bias** vector, a learnable constant.
- $\tanh$ — the squashing **activation**, output in $[-1, 1]$.

A third matrix turns memory into predictions:

| Matrix | Connection | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{xh}$ | input → hidden | how to process new input |
| $W_{hh}$ | hidden → hidden | how to update memory |
| $W_{hy}$ | hidden → output | how to produce a prediction $y_t$ |

> [!KEY] **Three** weight matrices total — and they are **shared across every timestep**. That one fact is what everything else depends on.

> [!WARNING] A common error is thinking a length-$T$ sequence needs $T$ copies of the weights. No — there is **one** $W_{xh}$, **one** $W_{hh}$, **one** $W_{hy}$, reused $T$ times. So RNN parameters are $O(d^2)$ (constant), while a comparable MLP would be $O(T\cdot d^2)$ (growing with length).

In PyTorch the whole thing is `nn.RNN(input_size=50, hidden_size=128, batch_first=True)`; called on input shaped `(batch, seq_len, features)` it returns every hidden state plus the final one.

notes.heading: Unrolling Through Time & the Hidden State as Memory
notes.body:
A feedback loop is hard to reason about, so we **unroll** it: draw one copy of the cell per timestep, in a chain.

[[fig:rnn-unrolled]]

> [!KEY] The folded (looped) drawing and the unrolled chain are the **same network** with the **same shared weights** — just two ways of picturing it. The folded view is compact notation; the unrolled view is what actually happens during computation.

Processing "the cat sat":
- Start with $h_0 = 0$ — no prior memory.
- $h_1$ knows "the".
- $h_2$ knows "the cat".
- $h_3$ knows "the cat sat" — the whole phrase compressed into one fixed-size vector.

> [!ANALOGY] Reading a 300-page novel: by page 150 you hold a mental model of plot, characters and tension. That evolving mental model **is** the hidden state.

But the hidden state is a **lossy compression**. A fixed-size vector (say 256 numbers) must encode *everything* seen so far, so:
- older information gradually gets overwritten by newer information,
- the network has finite memory capacity,
- early tokens in long sequences get "forgotten".

> [!ANALOGY] It is a sticky note you keep erasing and rewriting — eventually the earliest notes are gone. Remarkably, *what* to keep is **learned** during training: Karpathy's character-level RNN spontaneously grew hidden units that track open/close quotes, line length and indentation, with nobody telling it to. This forgetting is exactly what leads to the vanishing gradient problem.

notes.heading: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)
notes.body:
How do we train weights that are reused at every step? With **Backpropagation Through Time**.

> [!MATH] Three steps: (1) **unroll** the RNN across all $T$ timesteps; (2) notice the unrolled graph looks like a very **deep feedforward network**, $T$ stages deep; (3) run **ordinary backpropagation** on it.

[[fig:bptt]]

> [!INTUITION] A 100-token sequence becomes backprop through a 100-layer network. Each timestep behaves like one layer, and gradients of the loss flow **backward through time**, from the last step toward the first.

Because the same $W_{hh}$ appears at every step, the gradient of the loss with respect to it is a **sum of contributions** from every timestep, and each contribution must travel back through all the steps in between. Travelling back one step multiplies by a **Jacobian** factor:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top}\,\operatorname{diag}\!\big(\tanh'(\cdot)\big)$$

> [!MATH] In words: to push the gradient back from step $t$ to step $t-1$, multiply by the transpose of the memory matrix $W_{hh}^{\top}$ and by a diagonal matrix of $\tanh$ slopes $\tanh'(\cdot)$. The slope of $\tanh$ is **always $\le 1$** (and near $0$ when the unit is saturated).

To reach from step $T$ all the way back to an early step $k$, you **chain (multiply) many of these factors together**:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} \;\propto\; \prod_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

> [!KEY] The error signal from a late step reaches an early step only after being multiplied by a **long product of nearly identical matrices**. Whether that product stays sensible, vanishes, or explodes is the whole story of the next section.

notes.heading: Vanishing & Exploding Gradients
notes.body:
Repeated multiplication is dangerous. Multiply a number a little below 1 by itself many times and it races to 0; a number a little above 1 races to infinity. The same happens to the matrix product from BPTT, governed by the **largest singular value** of $W_{hh}$ (roughly: its biggest stretch factor).

[[fig:vanishing-exploding]]

> [!MATH] If the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is **below 1**, the product $\to 0$: **vanishing gradients**. If it is **above 1**, the product $\to \infty$: **exploding gradients**.

> [!EXAMPLE] Suppose the dominant factor is about $0.9$ per step. Then over $T$ steps the surviving gradient scales like $0.9^{T}$:
>
> | Steps | $0.9^{\text{steps}}$ |
> |---|---|
> | 10 | 0.349 |
> | 20 | 0.122 |
> | 50 | 0.005 |
> | 100 | 0.00003 |
>
> By 50 steps only about 0.5% of the gradient survives — the signal from the loss **barely reaches early timesteps**.

Why does this matter? The network literally **cannot learn** that an input 50 steps ago matters for the current prediction. In "The students who came from France ... speak French", the gradient that would teach "speak" to agree with "students" has to travel six-plus steps back — and it vanishes on the way.

> [!KEY] In practice, vanilla RNNs fail to learn dependencies beyond roughly **20 tokens**. $\tanh$ makes this worse, not better: its slope is always $\le 1$, so every backward step can only shrink the signal.

notes.heading: Practical Fixes: Truncated BPTT & Gradient Clipping
notes.body:
Two engineering tricks make RNN training feasible — but only one of the two gradient problems is truly solved.

**Truncated BPTT** tackles cost. Full BPTT on a 10,000-token document means backprop through 10,000 stages — impractical. Instead, unroll only $K$ steps at a time (commonly $K = 20$ to $100$, e.g. $K = 35$), backprop **inside each chunk**, and carry the hidden state $h$ forward to the next chunk.

> [!WARNING] The trade-off: the model **cannot learn dependencies longer than $K$ steps**, because gradients never flow across a chunk boundary. You buy tractability by capping memory reach.

**Gradient clipping** tackles exploding gradients. If the gradient's norm exceeds a threshold $\tau$, scale it back down:

$$\text{if } \lVert \nabla \rVert > \tau:\qquad \nabla \leftarrow \tau\,\frac{\nabla}{\lVert \nabla \rVert}$$

> [!MATH] In words: measure the length $\lVert \nabla \rVert$ of the gradient vector; if it is bigger than a chosen threshold $\tau$, shrink it back to length $\tau$ while keeping its direction unchanged. In PyTorch: `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=5.0)`.

| Problem | Symptom | Fixed by clipping? |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploding** | loss → NaN, parameters → ∞ | **Yes** — clipping caps the step |
| **Vanishing** | early tokens never learned, no NaN | **No** — needs a new architecture |

> [!KEY] Clipping fixes *exploding*. *Vanishing* is the unsolved one — it needs gated architectures (LSTMs / GRUs, next session) that build "gradient highways" through time.

notes.heading: The Four RNN Patterns
notes.body:
The same RNN machinery gets wired into different **input-to-output shapes** depending on the task.

| Pattern | Input → Output | Example tasks |
|---|---|---|
| **Many-to-one** | sequence → single label | sentiment analysis, document classification, spam detection |
| **Many-to-many** | sequence → sequence | POS tagging, named-entity recognition, machine translation |
| **One-to-many** | single input → sequence | image captioning, music generation |

> [!INTUITION] **Many-to-one**: read the whole review, emit one verdict (positive / negative). **Many-to-many**: label every word as you go (noun, verb, ...). **One-to-many**: start from one image and unroll a whole caption.

> [!EXAM] You will often be asked to *classify a described task* into the right pattern. Sentiment of a tweet → many-to-one. Tagging each token → many-to-many. Generating a caption from a single image → one-to-many.

> [!KEY] Today's arc in one breath: sequences need order + variable length → RNNs share weights and keep a hidden-state memory → they are trained by BPTT → but vanishing gradients cap their reach at about 20 tokens. Next session, LSTMs and GRUs fix exactly that.

quiz.q: A team flattens each sentence into one long fixed-length vector and feeds it to a standard MLP. Which fundamental property of sequences does this destroy?
options: The order / temporal structure: after flattening, the MLP treats position 1 and position 100 as unrelated features with no built-in sense that they form an ordered sequence.
The numerical values of the words, which get rounded off during flattening.
The nonlinearity, because flattening removes the MLP's hidden layers.
The color information, which only CNNs can preserve.
quiz.explanation: Flattening keeps the numbers but discards their ordering, so 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' look identical to the MLP. The tempting 'values get rounded' option is wrong — the values survive; it is the order relationship between positions that is lost.

quiz.q: A vanilla RNN is run over a sequence of length T = 100. How many distinct (unique) weight matrices does it use across the whole sequence?
options: Three hundred: three new matrices per timestep.
One hundred: a separate weight matrix is created for each timestep.
Three: one input-to-hidden, one hidden-to-hidden, and one hidden-to-output, each reused at every timestep.
One: a single combined matrix handles input, memory, and output together.
quiz.explanation: The defining feature of an RNN is weight sharing: the same Wxh, Whh, Why are reused at all 100 steps, so only three matrices exist. The 'one hundred' option reflects the common misconception that each timestep gets its own weights — that would be MLP-style parameter explosion.

quiz.q: In $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$, what is the specific role of the matrix $W_{hh}$?
options: It converts the hidden state into an output prediction.
It maps the current input $x_t$ into the hidden space.
It transforms the previous hidden state, controlling how past memory is carried forward and updated into the new hidden state.
It rescales the hidden state to unit norm at every step.
quiz.explanation: Whh multiplies h_{t-1}, so it governs how memory propagates from step to step. Mapping the input is the job of Wxh, and producing predictions is the job of Why — those are the tempting mix-ups.

quiz.q: Which statement best describes the RNN hidden state $h_t$?
options: A fixed-size, lossy summary of all inputs up to time t; you generally cannot reconstruct the exact past inputs from it.
A lossless record from which every past input can be exactly recovered.
A vector that grows by one dimension each timestep to store the newest input.
A buffer that is reset to zero after every timestep.
quiz.explanation: h_t compresses the past into a constant-size vector, so information is necessarily lost (older content gets overwritten). The 'lossless / recoverable' option is wrong precisely because the size is fixed while the history keeps growing.

quiz.q: Why is training an RNN on a 100-token sequence said to resemble backpropagation through a 100-layer feedforward network?
options: The RNN instantiates 100 independent layers, each with its own separate weight matrices.
Unrolling copies the same cell once per timestep into a 100-stage chain, so gradients must flow back through all 100 stages, just like through 100 layers.
BPTT trains 100 separate small networks and averages their weights.
Each of the 100 tokens is routed to a different one of 100 permanently fixed hidden layers.
quiz.explanation: Unrolling stacks T copies of the shared cell, giving a depth-T computational graph, so backprop traverses 100 stages. The 'independent layers with separate weights' option misses the key point that the weights are shared, not distinct per layer.

quiz.q: The BPTT gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$ is proportional to a product $\prod_t \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ of per-step Jacobians. Why does this product structure cause trouble on long sequences?
options: The product always equals exactly one, so gradients can never update the weights.
Multiplying many similar Jacobian factors together drives the overall gradient toward zero or toward infinity exponentially, depending on the factors' magnitudes.
The factors add rather than multiply, so the gradient grows only linearly with sequence length.
The product removes the need to apply the chain rule across timesteps.
quiz.explanation: A long product of near-identical matrices behaves like raising a number to a high power: slightly below one shrinks to 0, slightly above one blows up. The 'always exactly one' option is false — nothing forces the Jacobian norm to equal one.

quiz.q: Under what condition on $W_{hh}$ do BPTT gradients vanish as they travel back through many timesteps?
options: Whenever the hidden size is larger than the input size.
When the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is above 1.
Whenever the learning rate is set too small.
When the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is below 1, so repeated multiplication drives the product toward 0.
quiz.explanation: A largest singular value (max stretch factor) below 1 means each backward step shrinks the signal, so over many steps it decays to about 0. A value above 1 causes the opposite problem — exploding gradients — which is the tempting mirror-image distractor.

quiz.q: Why does using $\tanh$ as the RNN activation tend to worsen vanishing (rather than exploding) gradients?
options: The derivative of $\tanh$ is always $\le 1$ (and near 0 when the unit saturates), so each backward step multiplies the gradient by a factor no larger than 1, shrinking it.
The derivative of $\tanh$ is always $\ge 1$, so it amplifies the gradient at every step.
$\tanh$ has no derivative, so no gradient can pass through it.
$\tanh$ outputs only positive numbers, which cancels the gradient.
quiz.explanation: tanh's slope maxes out at 1 and is smaller (even near 0) when saturated, so it can only shrink — never amplify — the signal passing back through it, reinforcing decay. The 'derivative >= 1' option has the inequality backwards.

quiz.q: The largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is about 0.9 and a sequence is 50 timesteps long. Roughly what fraction of the gradient from timestep 50 reaches timestep 1?
options: Exactly 0%, because the gradient is mathematically zero after enough steps.
About 90%, since one factor of 0.9 dominates the whole chain.
About 45%, since $0.9 \times 50 \approx 45$.
About 0.5%, since $0.9^{50} \approx 0.005$.
quiz.explanation: Each step multiplies by about 0.9, so 50 steps give 0.9^50 which is about 0.005, i.e. roughly 0.5% survives. The '45%' option wrongly multiplies instead of exponentiating, and 'exactly 0' is wrong because the value is tiny but strictly positive.

quiz.q: Which statement about gradient clipping is correct?
options: It solves vanishing gradients, while exploding gradients require a brand-new architecture.
It boosts tiny gradients back up, which is how it fixes vanishing gradients.
It fixes vanishing and exploding gradients equally well.
It rescales gradients whose norm exceeds a threshold, which tames exploding gradients but does not fix vanishing gradients.
quiz.explanation: Clipping only shrinks over-large gradients, so it stops the loss going to NaN (exploding) but cannot revive a signal that has already decayed to about 0 (vanishing). The options claiming it cures vanishing invert which problem clipping addresses; vanishing needs gated architectures like LSTMs.

quiz.q: What is the main trade-off of truncated BPTT with chunk length K?
options: It removes the hidden state, so the network loses all memory between chunks.
It backpropagates through the entire sequence yet somehow uses fewer parameters.
It saves compute by backpropagating only within K-step chunks (carrying the hidden state forward), but it cannot learn dependencies longer than K steps.
It guarantees the network can learn dependencies of arbitrary length.
quiz.explanation: Truncation caps how far gradients flow (to K steps) so long sequences become tractable, meaning anything longer than K is unlearnable — that is the trade. The hidden state is still carried forward across chunks (only the gradient is cut), so the 'removes the hidden state' option is wrong.

quiz.q: Classifying the overall sentiment (positive / negative) of a full movie review is an example of which RNN configuration?
options: One-to-many: a single input produces a sequence of outputs.
Many-to-one: a whole input sequence is mapped to a single output label.
Many-to-many: every input token gets its own output label.
One-to-one: one input maps to one output with no sequence involved.
quiz.explanation: The model reads the entire review (a sequence) and emits one verdict, which is the many-to-one pattern. One-to-many fits tasks like image captioning, and many-to-many fits per-token tasks like POS tagging — both produce sequence outputs, unlike a single sentiment label.

descriptive.q: Explain why a standard MLP cannot handle sequences well. List the three specific problems, and describe how 'weight sharing across time' fixes all of them.
descriptive.answer:
An MLP expects a **fixed-size, unordered feature vector**. Sequences violate both assumptions, which produces three concrete failures:

| Problem | Why it hurts |
|---|---|
| **Fixed input size** | The input layer has a preset neuron count, so a 5-word and a 500-word sentence cannot share the same network without padding to a maximum (wasting parameters). |
| **No notion of order** | Once flattened, position 1 and position 100 are just two unrelated features; the MLP has no built-in signal that they are consecutive in time. |
| **Parameter explosion** | A length-$T$ sequence of $d$-dim vectors needs $O(T\cdot d^2)$ weights — e.g. 500 words × 300 dims = 150,000 input neurons. |

> [!KEY] The fix is **weight sharing across time**: apply the *same* function (the same weight matrices) at every timestep, while passing a hidden state forward as memory.

This resolves all three at once:
- **Variable length** — the same cell is simply applied as many times as there are steps, so any length works.
- **Order** — the hidden state $h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, so the model processes inputs *in sequence* and earlier inputs shape the interpretation of later ones.
- **Parameter count** — there is exactly one $W_{xh}$, one $W_{hh}$, one $W_{hy}$, so the parameter count is $O(d^2)$, **constant** regardless of length.

> [!ANALOGY] Your brain doesn't grow a new neuron per word — the same circuits read word 1 and word 1000. An RNN reuses one cell the same way.

descriptive.q: Write the vanilla RNN recurrence equation. Identify the three weight matrices, state what each connects, and explain how the hidden state functions as memory (including why it is 'lossy').
descriptive.answer:
The recurrence is:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

with an output produced from the hidden state, e.g. $y_t = W_{hy} h_t$.

> [!MATH] To form the new memory $h_t$: reshape the current input $x_t$ with $W_{xh}$, reshape the previous memory $h_{t-1}$ with $W_{hh}$, add a bias $b_h$, and squash with $\tanh$ into $[-1, 1]$.

The three shared matrices:

| Matrix | Connects | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{xh}$ | input → hidden | how to process the new input |
| $W_{hh}$ | hidden → hidden | how to carry and update memory |
| $W_{hy}$ | hidden → output | how to turn memory into a prediction |

**Hidden state as memory.** Starting from $h_0 = 0$ (no prior memory), each step folds the new input into the running summary, so $h_t$ encodes everything from $x_1$ to $x_t$ in a single fixed-size vector. After "the cat sat", $h_3$ represents the whole phrase.

> [!KEY] It is a **lossy compression**: a fixed-length vector (say 256 numbers) must hold an ever-growing history, so older information is gradually overwritten and early tokens get forgotten. The network *learns* what to keep — Karpathy's char-RNN grew units tracking quotes and indentation on its own.

> [!ANALOGY] Like a sticky note you keep erasing and rewriting: capacity is finite, so the earliest notes eventually disappear. This finite capacity is the seed of the vanishing-gradient problem.

descriptive.q: Derive, at an intuitive level, why the BPTT gradient with respect to W_hh is a product of per-step Jacobians, and explain how the largest singular value of W_hh and the tanh derivative together decide whether gradients vanish or explode.
descriptive.answer:
**Setup.** BPTT unrolls the RNN so the loss $L$ at a late step depends on an early hidden state $h_k$ only *through* the chain $h_k \to h_{k+1} \to \dots \to h_T$. By the chain rule, the sensitivity of $h_T$ to $h_k$ is the product of one-step sensitivities:

$$\frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$$

Each one-step factor, for $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}x_t + W_{hh}h_{t-1} + b_h)$, is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top}\,\operatorname{diag}\!\big(\tanh'(\cdot)\big)$$

> [!MATH] Going back one step multiplies by two things: the memory matrix $W_{hh}^{\top}$, and a diagonal of $\tanh$ slopes $\tanh'(\cdot)$. Because $W_{hh}$ is **shared**, the *same* matrix shows up in every factor, so reaching back $T-k$ steps multiplies $T-k$ near-identical matrices.

**Why it vanishes or explodes.** Multiplying a matrix by itself many times is dominated by its **largest singular value** $\sigma$ (its biggest stretch factor). The product roughly scales like $\sigma^{T-k}$:

| Condition | Behaviour of the product |
|---|---|
| $\sigma < 1$ | $\sigma^{T-k} \to 0$ — **vanishing** gradients |
| $\sigma > 1$ | $\sigma^{T-k} \to \infty$ — **exploding** gradients |

[[fig:vanishing-exploding]]

**The tanh contribution.** $\tanh'(\cdot) \le 1$ always, and near $0$ when units saturate. So the diagonal factor can only **shrink** the signal — it never amplifies. This biases vanilla RNNs toward *vanishing*: even with $\sigma$ near 1, the tanh slopes pull the effective per-step factor below 1.

> [!EXAMPLE] With an effective factor of $0.9$, after 50 steps only $0.9^{50}\approx 0.005$ (about 0.5%) of the gradient survives — the loss signal barely reaches early timesteps, so long-range dependencies cannot be learned.

descriptive.q: Compare vanishing and exploding gradients: their causes, symptoms, and fixes. Why does gradient clipping solve one but not the other? What is the longer-term solution to vanishing?
descriptive.answer:
Both arise from the **same product of Jacobians** in BPTT, but at opposite ends.

| | Vanishing | Exploding |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ < 1 (and $\tanh' \le 1$); product $\to 0$ | Largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ > 1; product $\to \infty$ |
| **Symptom** | Early timesteps stop learning; loss stalls; *no* NaN | Loss → NaN, parameters → ∞; wildly unstable training |
| **Quick fix** | None that is simple — needs architecture change | **Gradient clipping** |

**Gradient clipping** rescales the gradient when its norm is too large:

$$\text{if } \lVert \nabla \rVert > \tau:\qquad \nabla \leftarrow \tau\,\frac{\nabla}{\lVert \nabla \rVert}$$

> [!MATH] Measure the gradient's length $\lVert \nabla \rVert$; if it exceeds a threshold $\tau$, shrink it back to length $\tau$ while keeping its direction. Code: `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=5.0)`.

> [!KEY] Clipping can only **cap** a too-large gradient — it cannot *recreate* a signal that has already decayed to nearly zero. So it cures exploding but is useless against vanishing.

**Longer-term solution to vanishing.** Change the architecture so gradients can flow without repeated shrinking: **LSTMs and GRUs** add gates and a cell-state "highway" that lets the signal pass across many steps roughly unchanged (next session). That, not clipping, is what actually solves vanishing.

descriptive.q: What is truncated BPTT, why is it needed, and what is its trade-off? Walk through a concrete example with a 10,000-token document and K = 35.
descriptive.answer:
**The need.** Full BPTT unrolls the *entire* sequence, so a 10,000-token document becomes a 10,000-stage graph. Storing every intermediate activation and backpropagating through all of them is enormously expensive in memory and compute — and the deepest gradients have usually vanished anyway, so most of that work teaches nothing.

**Truncated BPTT.** Process the sequence in **chunks of $K$ steps** (here $K = 35$). For each chunk:
1. Run the forward pass for 35 steps, building up the hidden state.
2. Compute the loss and backpropagate **only within those 35 steps**.
3. **Carry the hidden state $h$ forward** into the next chunk (so the forward memory continues), but **detach** it so gradients do not flow back across the chunk boundary.

So the 10,000-token document is handled as roughly $10000 / 35 \approx 286$ chunks. Each backward pass spans at most 35 stages instead of 10,000.

> [!WARNING] **Trade-off:** gradients never cross a chunk boundary, so the model **cannot learn dependencies longer than $K = 35$ steps**. A cue 100 tokens before the answer is invisible to learning, even though the forward hidden state still carries some of it. Typical $K$ ranges from 20 to 100.

> [!KEY] Truncated BPTT trades **long-range learning** for **computational feasibility**: it makes very long sequences trainable, but caps how far back the network can learn to look.

descriptive.q: Compare the four RNN task patterns with examples, then use the sentence 'The students who came from France ... speak French' to explain concretely how the vanishing gradient problem prevents an RNN from learning a long-range dependency.
descriptive.answer:
**RNN task patterns.** The same recurrent machinery is wired to different input/output shapes:

[[fig:rnn-task-types]]

| Pattern | Input → Output | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Many-to-one** | sequence → one label | sentiment analysis, document classification, spam detection |
| **Many-to-many** | sequence → sequence | POS tagging, named-entity recognition, machine translation |
| **One-to-many** | single input → sequence | image captioning, music generation |

(The lecture highlights these three; a degenerate one-to-one is just a plain feedforward step.)

**The long-range dependency.** In "The students who came from France ... speak French", the verb form "speak" must agree with the **subject** "students" — which sits *six or more words back*, separated by the relative clause "who came from France".

**Why a vanilla RNN fails to learn it.** To learn this agreement, BPTT must send the gradient from the "speak" prediction *back through every intervening step* to the hidden state that encoded "students". Each backward step multiplies by the Jacobian $W_{hh}^{\top}\operatorname{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot))$, whose effective factor is below 1.

> [!EXAMPLE] If that factor is about $0.9$, then even six steps back leaves $0.9^{6}\approx 0.53$, and a more realistic 20-step gap leaves $0.9^{20}\approx 0.12$ — and deeper clauses push it toward $0.9^{50}\approx 0.005$. The teaching signal that links "speak" to "students" shrinks to almost nothing.

> [!KEY] Because the gradient effectively never reaches "students", the network cannot learn that the distant subject controls the verb. In practice vanilla RNNs lose dependencies beyond roughly **20 tokens** — the motivation for LSTMs and GRUs.

topics.title: LSTMs & GRUs
topics.summary: LSTMs and GRUs add learnable gates that let a memory signal ride a near-additive 'conveyor belt' across time, so gradients stop vanishing and the network can learn dependencies hundreds of steps long. This session covers the LSTM cell's four equations, why the cell-state path keeps gradients alive ($\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=f_t$), the simpler two-gate GRU, and when to reach for bidirectional or deep/stacked RNNs.

notes.heading: The Problem: Why Vanilla RNNs Forget
notes.body:
Last week we hit a wall. A plain ("vanilla") RNN reuses the **same** weight matrix at every time step, and when we backpropagate the error through a long sequence it gets multiplied by that matrix over and over. Multiply something smaller than 1 by itself 30 times and it is basically zero — that is the **vanishing gradient** problem. In practice a vanilla RNN cannot learn dependencies longer than about **20 steps**.

Here is the culprit written out:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}\!\big(\tanh'(\cdot)\big)$$

In words: to push the error from one step back to the one before, you multiply by $W_{hh}^{\top}$ — the recurrent weight matrix, the **same** one at every step — and by the slope of the $\tanh$, written $\tanh'$, which is at most 1 and usually much less. Chain dozens of these together and the signal shrinks toward nothing (or, if the matrix is large, explodes).

> [!KEY] We need an architecture where the gradient can travel back across many steps **without** being repeatedly multiplied by $W_{hh}$. Every idea in this session exists to satisfy that one requirement.

The fix is **gating**: small sigmoid sub-networks that learn to open and close, deciding what to keep, what to overwrite, and what to reveal.

notes.heading: The Big Idea: A Conveyor Belt for Memory
notes.body:
[[fig:cell-state-highway]]

Picture a **conveyor belt** running straight across the top of the cell, left to right. Boxes of information ride along it. Most of the time the belt carries them forward **unchanged**. Now and then a worker reaches in to *remove* a box, *drop in* a new one, or *peek* at what is passing — but the belt itself is never scrambled.

That belt is the **cell state** $C_t$ — the LSTM's long-term memory at time step $t$ ("the running notes the cell keeps"). The workers are the **gates**.

> [!INTUITION] A gate is a tiny network ending in a sigmoid, so it outputs a number between 0 and 1 for **each** dimension of the memory. Treat it as a **dimmer switch**, not a light switch: 0 blocks completely, 1 passes completely, 0.5 lets half through. The sigmoid is written $\sigma$, and $\sigma(z)=\tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ always lands in $[0,1]$ — that is what makes it a perfect gate.

Why does a belt help? Because the only operations performed **on the belt** are element-wise multiply (dim the boxes) and element-wise add (drop boxes in). The symbol for element-wise multiply is $\odot$ — multiply matching positions, no matrix involved. That absence of matrix multiplication is exactly what rescues the gradient.

> [!KEY] Memory is the **default**; editing is the exception. Information rides the cell state untouched unless a gate deliberately changes it.

notes.heading: LSTM Anatomy I — Forget Gate and Input Gate
notes.body:
[[fig:lstm-cell]]

The LSTM decides what to do with its memory using gates that all read the **same** two things: the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ (what the cell was thinking last step) and the current input $x_t$ (what just arrived). These are glued into one longer vector, written $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ — that is **concatenation**: if $h$ is 128-dimensional and $x$ is 50-dimensional, the stack is 178-dimensional.

**The forget gate** — what to erase from memory:

$$f_t = \sigma\!\big(W_f\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_f\big)$$

Here $f_t$ is the forget gate's output (one value in $[0,1]$ per memory dimension), $\sigma$ is the sigmoid that squashes into $[0,1]$, $W_f$ is the forget gate's weight matrix, $[h_{t-1},x_t]$ is that concatenated input, and $b_f$ is a bias. A 0 means "erase this slot of memory," a 1 means "keep it."

> [!EXAMPLE] At a new paragraph with a new character, the forget gate can output near 0 on the dimensions holding the old character's details — clearing them to make room.

**The input gate** — what new information to write. It comes in two parts:

$$i_t = \sigma\!\big(W_i\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_i\big), \qquad \tilde{C}_t = \tanh\!\big(W_C\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_C\big)$$

$i_t$ is the input gate (a 0–1 dimmer deciding **how much** to write to each slot), and $\tilde{C}_t$ is the **candidate** — the actual new content, made by $\tanh$ so each value lands in $[-1,1]$. Their element-wise product $i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ is "the new information to add."

> [!WARNING] Do not confuse the two halves. The gate $i_t$ decides *which slots and how strongly*; the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ decides *what the values actually are*. The gate never creates content; the candidate never decides amount.

> [!KEY] In code the forget bias $b_f$ is often initialized to 1, which makes $f_t\approx 1$ at the start — the cell is biased toward **remembering by default** until it learns what is safe to forget.

notes.heading: LSTM Anatomy II — Cell-State Update and Output Gate
notes.body:
With the gates computed, the belt is updated by one short, powerful equation:

$$C_t = f_t\odot C_{t-1} + i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$$

Reading it in words: the new memory $C_t$ equals the **old memory** $C_{t-1}$ dimmed by the forget gate $f_t$ (some slots erased), **plus** the **candidate** $\tilde{C}_t$ dimmed by the input gate $i_t$ (some slots written). The symbol $\odot$ is element-wise multiply.

> [!MATH] "Keep a fraction of what I had, then add a fraction of what is new." The two fractions are independent — the LSTM can keep old memory AND write new memory at the same time.

> [!KEY] The center of the LSTM is an **addition**, not a multiplication. That is the whole trick — addition is what lets gradients pass through cleanly.

**The output gate** — what to reveal right now:

$$o_t = \sigma\!\big(W_o\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_o\big), \qquad h_t = o_t\odot\tanh(C_t)$$

$o_t$ is the output gate (a 0–1 dimmer), and the hidden state $h_t$ is a **filtered view** of the memory: squash the cell state with $\tanh(C_t)$, then let the output gate decide which parts to expose. This $h_t$ is what the network uses for predictions and what it passes to the next step.

> [!ANALOGY] You may know the entire plot of a story, but when asked "what color is the car?" you only say the relevant detail. The cell state $C_t$ holds everything; the output gate $o_t$ chooses what to say now.

Putting the pieces side by side:

| | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| Weight matrices | 1 | 4 ($W_f,W_i,W_o,W_C$) |
| Sigmoid gates | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) |
| State carried | $h$ | $h$ **and** $C$ |
| Parameters | 1x | ~4x |
| Usable range | ~20 steps | 100+ steps |

Four times the parameters, but it handles sequences 10–100x longer.

notes.heading: Why This Fixes Vanishing Gradients
notes.body:
Now we can see *why* gates fix the vanishing gradient. Differentiate the cell-state update $C_t=f_t\odot C_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ with respect to the previous cell state:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t$$

That is the entire story: the gradient handed back one step is simply **scaled element-wise by the forget gate** $f_t$ — no matrix, no $\tanh'$ factor on this path.

> [!INTUITION] If the forget gate stays near 1 over many steps, the gradient is multiplied by numbers close to 1 again and again — so it barely shrinks. The forget gate *learns* when to open this highway and when to close it.

Contrast the two recurrences directly:

| Path | Derivative per step | Behaviour over many steps |
|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN ($h$) | $W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot))$ | repeated **matrix** multiply → vanishes / explodes |
| LSTM ($C$) | $f_t$ | **element-wise** scale, can stay near 1 → survives |

Concretely, on a long sequence the gradient reaching time step 1 might be about $0.001\times$ the loss gradient in a vanilla RNN, but around $0.6\times$ in an LSTM.

> [!KEY] The cell state is a **gradient highway**: $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=f_t$ replaces the deadly repeated $W_{hh}$ multiply with a gentle element-wise scaling.

> [!INTUITION] This is the same idea as a **ResNet skip connection** — let the signal take an additive shortcut around the transformation — invented for LSTMs about 18 years earlier.

notes.heading: GRU — Fewer Gates, Similar Power
notes.body:
[[fig:gru-cell]]

The **GRU** (Gated Recurrent Unit) asks: can we get the benefits of gating with fewer moving parts? It makes two simplifications:

1. **Merge** the cell state and hidden state into a single state $h_t$ — no separate $C_t$.
2. Use **2 gates** instead of 3 — one gate does both forgetting and writing.

The equations:

$$z_t = \sigma\!\big(W_z\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]\big) \quad\text{(update gate)}$$
$$r_t = \sigma\!\big(W_r\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]\big) \quad\text{(reset gate)}$$
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh\!\big(W\cdot[r_t\odot h_{t-1},\,x_t]\big) \quad\text{(candidate)}$$
$$h_t = (1-z_t)\odot h_{t-1} + z_t\odot\tilde{h}_t \quad\text{(final state)}$$

Glossing them: $z_t$ is the **update gate** (how much to refresh), $r_t$ is the **reset gate** (how much old state to let into the candidate), $\tilde{h}_t$ is the candidate new state, and the last line blends old and new. Notice the reset gate sits *inside* the candidate, gating $h_{t-1}$ before it is used.

> [!KEY] The single update gate $z_t$ does the work of both LSTM gates: the $(1-z_t)$ portion **keeps** the old state (forget gate's job) and the $z_t$ portion **writes** the new candidate (input gate's job). They are tied together — keep more means write less.

> [!MATH] When $z_t=0$: $h_t=(1-0)\odot h_{t-1}+0\odot\tilde{h}_t=h_{t-1}$. The state passes through untouched — perfect memory and a clean identity path for the gradient.

| | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | 3 | 2 |
| Separate cell state | Yes | No |
| Parameters | ~4x RNN | ~3x RNN |
| Training speed | slower | ~20% faster |
| Performance | similar on most tasks | similar on most tasks |

> [!KEY] Rule of thumb: **start with a GRU** (simpler, faster). Switch to an LSTM for very long dependencies, when the GRU underperforms, or when you have plenty of data and compute. In practice the difference is often negligible.

notes.heading: Bidirectional RNNs — Reading Both Ways
notes.body:
[[fig:bidirectional-rnn]]

Everything so far reads **left to right**, so at each position the network only knows the **past**. But sometimes the clue you need is in the future:

- "The ___ barked loudly" — the word *barked* (which comes later) tells you the blank is *dog*.
- "Apple released a new phone" — fruit or company? The later words decide.
- Named-entity recognition: in "Washington visited Philadelphia," is *Washington* a person or a place? Context to the right helps.

A **bidirectional RNN** solves this by running **two** RNNs over the same sequence: a **forward** pass reading left to right and a **backward** pass reading right to left. At each position you **concatenate** the two hidden states:

$$h_t^{\text{out}} = \big[\,\overrightarrow{h_t}\,;\,\overleftarrow{h_t}\,\big]$$

Here $\overrightarrow{h_t}$ is the forward RNN's state at position $t$ (summarizing everything to the left) and $\overleftarrow{h_t}$ is the backward RNN's state (summarizing everything to the right). Stacked together, the output at each position has dimension $2\times\text{hidden\_size}$.

> [!KEY] The deciding question: **do you have the full sequence before you predict?** If yes, bidirectional gives both-sides context. If you are generating or streaming one token at a time, you cannot — there is no future to read.

| Use bidirectional when... | Do **not** use when... |
|---|---|
| Full sequence available first | Real-time / streaming input |
| Text classification | Autoregressive generation |
| Named-entity recognition | Language modeling (next-token) |
| POS tagging | You cannot see future tokens |
| Encoder in a seq2seq model | |

notes.heading: Deep / Stacked RNNs and Choosing an Architecture
notes.body:
You can also stack RNN layers on top of each other — feed layer 1's outputs as layer 2's inputs — to build a **deep RNN**. Each layer tends to capture a different level of abstraction, much like layers in a CNN:

- **Layer 1** → syntax (local word-level structure)
- **Layer 2** → semantics (meaning)
- **Layer 3** → discourse (how the whole thing hangs together)

In PyTorch this is one argument: `nn.LSTM(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers=3)`.

**Practical rules:**

1. **Depth:** typically 2–4 layers; returns diminish quickly beyond that.
2. **Dropout:** apply it **between** layers, not within the time-step recurrence. `nn.LSTM(..., dropout=0.3)` does this automatically. Dropping connections inside the recurrence would corrupt the memory the cell is trying to carry.
3. **Residual connections:** for 4+ layers, add skip connections between layers to keep gradients healthy.
4. **Famous examples:** Google NMT (2016) used an 8-layer LSTM; Deep Speech 2 (2016) used a 7-layer bidirectional network.

> [!KEY] The 2015–2017 NLP default was a **2-layer bidirectional LSTM with dropout** — the go-to architecture before transformers.

| Scenario | Architecture | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Short, simple task | Vanilla RNN / GRU | enough capacity |
| Long-range dependencies | LSTM or GRU | gates preserve memory |
| Need speed / fewer params | GRU | ~75% of LSTM params |
| Full sequence available | Bidirectional | both-direction context |
| Complex task, lots of data | Deep (2–4 layers) | hierarchical features |
| Maximum NLP performance | Deep BiLSTM | all of the above |

> [!EXAM] A 2024 reality check: transformers have largely replaced LSTMs for NLP, but gating remains essential knowledge — transformers reuse the same ideas (residual streams, multiplicative interactions). Expect questions on *why* gates fix gradients, not just *what* the equations are.

quiz.q: During backpropagation through an LSTM, the gradient of the cell state with respect to the previous cell state is $\partial C_t / \partial C_{t-1}$. What does this quantity equal, and why does it matter?
options: It equals the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$, just as in a vanilla RNN.
It equals the forget gate $f_t$ applied element-wise, so when $f_t\approx 1$ the gradient passes back almost unchanged.
It equals $o_t \odot \tanh'(C_t)$, the derivative of the output-gate path.
It equals zero, which is exactly why LSTMs never suffer from exploding gradients.
quiz.explanation: Because the cell-state update is additive ($C_t = f_t\odot C_{t-1} + i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$), differentiating gives $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=f_t$ — just the forget gate, applied element-wise (one number per dimension). When the forget gate is near 1 the gradient survives many steps. The tempting wrong choice is $W_{hh}$: that is precisely the vanilla-RNN term the LSTM was designed to avoid — there is no recurrent matrix multiply on the cell-state path.

quiz.q: If an LSTM's forget gate outputs all zeros at a given time step, what happens to the cell state?
options: The cell state doubles, since zero forgetting means everything is kept twice.
The cell state is preserved exactly, so $C_t = C_{t-1}$.
The old memory is completely erased, leaving $C_t = i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ (only the freshly written information).
The output gate is forced to zero as well, so $h_t = 0$.
quiz.explanation: $C_t=f_t\odot C_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$. With $f_t=0$ the first term vanishes, so $C_t=i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ — total amnesia about the past, keeping only new candidate info. 'Preserved exactly' is the $f_t=1$ case, the opposite. The output gate is computed independently from $f_t$, so it is not forced to zero.

quiz.q: In a GRU, at some time step the update gate evaluates to $z_t = 0$. What is the resulting hidden state $h_t$?
options: $h_t = h_{t-1}$ — the previous state passes through unchanged.
$h_t = \tilde{h}_t$ — the candidate completely overwrites the state.
$h_t = 0$ — the state is reset to zero.
$h_t = r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ — the reset gate alone decides the state.
quiz.explanation: The GRU mixes old and new with $h_t=(1-z_t)\odot h_{t-1}+z_t\odot\tilde{h}_t$. With $z_t=0$ this is $1\cdot h_{t-1}+0\cdot\tilde{h}_t=h_{t-1}$: an identity mapping, so the gradient flows back unchanged. $h_t=\tilde{h}_t$ is the $z_t=1$ case (full overwrite). The reset gate $r_t$ only shapes how the candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ is built; it does not appear in this final blend.

quiz.q: Which task is the WRONG place to use a bidirectional RNN?
options: Named-entity recognition on complete sentences.
Real-time autoregressive text generation, where each token is produced before the next one exists.
Part-of-speech tagging of a full document.
The encoder of a seq2seq model whose input sentence is fully available.
quiz.explanation: A bidirectional RNN needs the entire sequence up front because its backward pass reads from the end. Autoregressive generation produces one token at a time and literally cannot see future tokens, so a backward pass is impossible. The other three all have the whole input available before predicting, which is exactly when bidirectionality helps.

quiz.q: A bidirectional LSTM uses a hidden size of 128 in each direction. What is the dimension of the combined output vector at each time step?
options: 384, because forward, backward, and cell state are all stacked together.
128, because the two directions are averaged back down to one vector.
64, because the two directions split the hidden size between them.
256, because the forward and backward hidden states are concatenated.
quiz.explanation: At each position a bidirectional RNN concatenates the forward and backward hidden states, giving $2\times\text{hidden\_size}=2\times128=256$. Averaging down to 128 would throw away half the information and is not the default behaviour. The cell state is an internal quantity and is not part of the emitted output.

quiz.q: Within an LSTM cell, which component produces the candidate values that might be written into memory, and what is its output range?
options: The input gate $i_t$, with values in $[0,1]$.
The $\tanh$ candidate $\tilde{C}_t$, with values in $[-1,1]$.
The forget gate $f_t$, with values in $[0,1]$.
The output gate $o_t$, with values in $[-1,1]$.
quiz.explanation: $\tilde{C}_t=\tanh(W_C\cdot[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_C)$ creates the actual content to potentially add, and $\tanh$ squashes it into $[-1,1]$. The input gate $i_t$ is a sigmoid that only decides how much of that candidate to write (a 0–1 multiplier); it does not create the values. Confusing 'which/how much' (the gate) with 'what content' (the candidate) is the classic error.

quiz.q: How does a GRU's single update gate $z_t$ accomplish what an LSTM needs two separate gates for?
options: It uses $z_t$ to add new information and $(1-z_t)$ to keep old information, coupling forgetting and writing into one operation.
It runs the forget and input gates sequentially inside the same sigmoid.
It applies $z_t$ as a forget gate and $r_t$ as an input gate.
It learns two bias terms so one sigmoid can output two independent values.
quiz.explanation: The GRU blend $h_t=(1-z_t)\odot h_{t-1}+z_t\odot\tilde{h}_t$ ties the two jobs together: the fraction $z_t$ written in is exactly the fraction $(1-z_t)$ not kept from the old state. In an LSTM the forget gate $f_t$ and input gate $i_t$ are independent, so it can keep old AND write new freely. The option naming $r_t$ as an input gate is wrong — the reset gate only shapes the candidate.

quiz.q: Why does the LSTM's additive cell-state update help gradients survive, compared with the vanilla RNN's update?
options: Backprop through an addition just copies the gradient, so the cell-state path avoids the repeated matrix multiplication by $W_{hh}$ that shrinks gradients.
Addition produces larger numbers than multiplication, so the gradients are simply bigger.
The additive form removes all nonlinearities, making the whole network linear and easy to optimize.
Addition normalizes the cell state to unit variance at every step.
quiz.explanation: An addition node sends the incoming gradient to both of its inputs unchanged, so along the cell-state highway the gradient is only scaled element-wise by $f_t$ rather than multiplied by $W_{hh}^{\top}$ every step — that repeated matrix multiply is what causes vanishing/exploding gradients in RNNs. The update is not linear overall (the gates and $\tanh$ are nonlinear), and nothing here normalizes variance.

quiz.q: How many sigmoid gates and how many weight matrices does a standard LSTM cell have, compared to a vanilla RNN cell?
options: 3 sigmoid gates and 3 weight matrices, versus 0 gates and 1 matrix.
2 sigmoid gates and 3 weight matrices, versus 1 gate and 1 matrix.
4 sigmoid gates and 4 weight matrices, versus 1 gate and 2 matrices.
3 sigmoid gates and 4 weight matrices, versus 0 gates and 1 matrix for the vanilla RNN.
quiz.explanation: An LSTM has three sigmoid gates — forget, input, output — plus the $\tanh$ candidate, giving four weight matrices ($W_f,W_i,W_o,W_C$), roughly 4x the parameters of a vanilla RNN's single matrix. The candidate uses $\tanh$, not a sigmoid, so there are 3 sigmoids but 4 matrices — which is why '3 gates / 3 matrices' is wrong.

quiz.q: Why is the forget-gate bias $b_f$ commonly initialized to 1 (rather than 0) at the start of training?
options: Because a bias of 1 makes the sigmoid output exactly 0.5, the most neutral starting point.
So the forget gate starts near 0, forcing the network to relearn its memory from scratch each step.
So the forget gate starts near 1, biasing the cell toward remembering by default until it learns what to forget.
To guarantee the cell state stays normalized between 0 and 1.
quiz.explanation: A positive bias pushes the sigmoid output toward 1, so early in training $f_t\approx 1$ and the cell tends to preserve memory (and gradients) until gradient descent learns when forgetting helps. Starting near 0 would erase memory by default — the opposite of what we want. Also $\sigma(1)\approx 0.73$, not 0.5, so the 'neutral 0.5' claim is false.

quiz.q: In a stacked LSTM such as `nn.LSTM(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers=3, dropout=0.3)`, where is the dropout applied?
options: On the input embeddings before they enter the first layer.
Inside each cell at every time step, on the recurrent connection $h_{t-1}\to h_t$.
Only on the final output, after the last time step of the last layer.
Between the stacked layers (on the outputs passed from one layer up to the next), not within the time-step recurrence.
quiz.explanation: PyTorch's LSTM `dropout` argument drops activations on the connections between stacked layers, leaving the temporal recurrence intact. Dropping within the recurrence (the same mask hammering $h_{t-1}\to h_t$ every step) would disrupt the memory the cell is trying to carry, and is deliberately avoided. It is not limited to the final output, nor applied to the embeddings.

quiz.q: Which statement correctly contrasts a GRU with an LSTM?
options: A GRU always outperforms an LSTM on long-range dependencies because it has fewer gates.
A GRU keeps a separate cell state but drops the output gate, using 3 gates total.
A GRU merges the cell and hidden states into one state and uses 2 gates, giving about 75% of an LSTM's parameters with often-similar performance.
A GRU has more parameters than an LSTM because it computes a candidate for every gate.
quiz.explanation: A GRU has a single state $h_t$ (no separate $C_t$) and two gates (reset, update), giving roughly 3x-RNN parameters versus the LSTM's 4x — about 75% — with comparable accuracy on most tasks. It does not keep a separate cell state, so 'separate cell state, 3 gates' is wrong, and fewer gates does not guarantee better long-range performance — for the very longest dependencies LSTMs are often preferred.

descriptive.q: Write the LSTM cell-state update equation and derive why its additive form lets gradients flow across long sequences. Contrast this with the vanilla RNN.
descriptive.answer:
**The equation.**

$$C_t = f_t\odot C_{t-1} + i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$$

where $C_t$ is the new cell state (memory), $C_{t-1}$ is the old one, $f_t$ is the forget gate (0–1 per dimension), $i_t$ is the input gate, $\tilde{C}_t$ is the $\tanh$ candidate, and $\odot$ is element-wise multiply. In words: keep a learnable fraction of the old memory, then add a learnable fraction of new content.

**Why the additive form helps — the derivation.** Backpropagation needs $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}$. Since $C_{t-1}$ appears only in the first term and is multiplied element-wise by $f_t$:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t$$

So passing the gradient back one step just scales it element-wise by the forget gate. Across $k$ steps the cell-state gradient is roughly a product of those gates — a product of numbers that can each stay near 1, so it need not vanish.

**Contrast with the vanilla RNN.** There $h_t=\tanh(W_{hh}h_{t-1}+\dots)$, so

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot)).$$

Going back $k$ steps multiplies $k$ copies of this matrix together. Its repeated powers shrink toward 0 (vanishing) or blow up (exploding) depending on its largest singular value, and $\tanh'\le 1$ only makes the shrinking worse. The LSTM removes the matrix from the memory path entirely.

> [!KEY] Addition copies the gradient; the only thing touching it on the highway is the element-wise factor $f_t$. That is structurally the same trick as a ResNet skip connection, invented about 18 years earlier.

descriptive.q: Walk through the four core LSTM equations, naming each gate, its activation, and its job. Then trace how a single piece of information could be stored, kept for many steps, and finally revealed.
descriptive.answer:
An LSTM cell runs four computations each step. All gates read the same concatenated input $[h_{t-1},x_t]$ — last step's hidden state plus this step's input.

| Equation | Name | Activation | Job |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_t=\sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_f)$ | forget gate | sigmoid | how much old memory to keep (0 = erase, 1 = keep) |
| $i_t=\sigma(W_i[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_i)$ | input gate | sigmoid | how much new content to write |
| $\tilde{C}_t=\tanh(W_C[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_C)$ | candidate | tanh | what the new content is, in $[-1,1]$ |
| $o_t=\sigma(W_o[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_o)$ | output gate | sigmoid | which parts of memory to reveal now |

The state is then updated and read out:

$$C_t = f_t\odot C_{t-1} + i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t, \qquad h_t = o_t\odot\tanh(C_t).$$

**Tracing one fact** ("the hero is actually the villain"):

1. **Store:** when this clause arrives, the input gate $i_t$ opens on a few memory slots and the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ supplies the value — so $i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ writes the plot twist into $C_t$.
2. **Keep:** for the next many steps the forget gate stays near 1 on those slots ($f_t\approx 1$), so $f_t\odot C_{t-1}$ carries the fact forward untouched — and since $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=f_t\approx 1$, gradients survive too.
3. **Reveal:** much later, when the twist matters for a prediction, the output gate $o_t$ opens on those slots, so $h_t=o_t\odot\tanh(C_t)$ finally exposes the stored fact while keeping the rest of memory hidden.

> [!KEY] Three sigmoid gates (forget/input/output) plus one $\tanh$ candidate = four weight matrices. The sigmoids decide *how much / which*; the $\tanh$ candidate decides *what*.

descriptive.q: What happens if an LSTM's forget gate outputs all 1s at every time step? What if it outputs all 0s at every step? Discuss the effect on both memory and gradient flow.
descriptive.answer:
Recall the update $C_t=f_t\odot C_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ and the gradient identity $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=f_t$.

**Forget gate = 1 at every step.** Then $C_t=C_{t-1}+i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ — the old memory is preserved perfectly and new info is only added on top. Memory-wise, the LSTM never forgets; it accumulates. Gradient-wise, $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=1$, so the gradient flows back completely unchanged across arbitrarily many steps — the ideal gradient highway (the same behaviour as a GRU with $z_t=0$, or a ResNet identity skip).

**Forget gate = 0 at every step.** Then $C_t=i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$ — the past is wiped every step, so the cell keeps only the current candidate. The network becomes effectively memoryless, behaving like a feed-forward map of the current input with no long-term context. Gradient-wise, $\partial C_t/\partial C_{t-1}=0$, so the through-time gradient is cut: nothing from far in the past can influence learning — the vanishing-memory failure, now caused by the gate rather than by matrix shrinkage.

> [!WARNING] 'All 1s' does not mean 'do nothing.' New information is still written each step via $i_t\odot\tilde{C}_t$; what is preserved is the *old* content, not a frozen state.

> [!KEY] The forget gate is a learnable dial between these extremes: near 1 = remember and pass gradients; near 0 = forget and block them. Initializing $b_f$ to 1 starts the dial near the 'remember' end on purpose.

descriptive.q: Compare LSTM and GRU across gates, state, parameters, and speed, and give a concrete decision rule for which to use.
descriptive.answer:
Both are gated RNNs that beat the vanilla RNN's vanishing gradient by using additive, gated state updates. They differ in how much machinery they use.

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| Separate cell state $C_t$ | Yes (plus $h_t$) | No (single $h_t$) |
| Parameters | ~4x a vanilla RNN | ~3x (about 75% of LSTM) |
| Training speed | slower | ~20% faster |
| Performance | similar on most tasks | similar on most tasks |

**How the GRU does more with less.** Its update gate $z_t$ couples forgetting and writing in one shot: $h_t=(1-z_t)\odot h_{t-1}+z_t\odot\tilde{h}_t$, where $(1-z_t)$ keeps the old state and $z_t$ writes the new candidate — one knob instead of two independent ones. The LSTM keeps $f_t$ and $i_t$ separate (so it can keep old AND add new independently), and its output gate adds a separate 'what to reveal' control plus a protected internal $C_t$.

**Decision rule.**

- **Start with a GRU** — simpler, fewer parameters, faster, and usually just as accurate.
- **Switch to an LSTM** when dependencies are very long, when the GRU clearly underperforms, or when you have ample data/compute to spend on the extra capacity.
- For short or simple sequences the gap is often negligible, so prefer the cheaper model.

> [!KEY] Fewer gates is not 'worse' — for most tasks the GRU matches the LSTM at ~75% of the cost. Reach for the LSTM's extra cell state and output gate when the problem genuinely needs longer, more selective memory.

descriptive.q: Explain the bidirectional RNN: its architecture, the dimension of its outputs, the tasks it suits, and the tasks where it must not be used — and why.
descriptive.answer:
**The problem.** A standard (unidirectional) RNN reads left to right, so at position $t$ it has seen only the past. But disambiguation often needs the future: in "The ___ barked loudly" the later word *barked* identifies the blank as *dog*; in "Apple released a phone" the right-hand words decide fruit vs. company; in NER, the words after *Washington* tell you person vs. place.

**Architecture.** A bidirectional RNN runs two independent RNNs over the sequence:

- a **forward** RNN producing $\overrightarrow{h_t}$ (summarizes everything to the left), and
- a **backward** RNN producing $\overleftarrow{h_t}$ (summarizes everything to the right).

At each position the outputs are concatenated: $h_t^{\text{out}}=[\,\overrightarrow{h_t};\,\overleftarrow{h_t}\,]$, so the output dimension is $2\times\text{hidden\_size}$. Each cell can itself be an LSTM or GRU.

[[fig:bidirectional-rnn]]

**Use it when** the whole sequence is available before predicting: text classification, named-entity recognition, POS tagging, or as the encoder in a seq2seq model.

**Do not use it when** you cannot see the future at prediction time: real-time/streaming input, autoregressive generation, or next-token language modeling. The backward pass requires the end of the sequence, which simply does not exist yet in those settings.

> [!KEY] One question decides it: *do I have the full input before I predict?* Yes → bidirectional (both-side context). No (generating one token at a time) → unidirectional.

descriptive.q: Why do we stack RNN layers into 'deep' RNNs, and what practical rules govern depth, dropout placement, and residual connections? Where does the 2015–2017 NLP default fit in?
descriptive.answer:
**Why stack.** A single RNN layer can only build one level of representation. Stacking layers — feeding each layer's output sequence as the next layer's input — lets the network build hierarchical features, just like depth in a CNN. Empirically the levels specialize: lower layers capture local **syntax**, middle layers capture **semantics/meaning**, and higher layers capture **discourse**-level structure. More abstraction means more capacity for complex tasks with lots of data.

**Practical rules.**

1. **Depth:** 2–4 layers is typical; beyond that, returns diminish sharply.
2. **Dropout placement:** apply dropout between layers (on the activations passed upward), not within the time-step recurrence. PyTorch's `nn.LSTM(..., dropout=0.3)` does exactly this. Dropping inside the recurrence would repeatedly corrupt the memory the cell is carrying.
3. **Residual connections:** for 4+ layers, add skip connections between layers so gradients stay healthy through the depth (the same additive-shortcut idea that protects the cell state through time).
4. **Famous examples:** Google NMT (2016) used an 8-layer LSTM; Deep Speech 2 (2016) used a 7-layer bidirectional network.

**Where the default fits.** Combining the session's ideas, the 2015–2017 NLP standard was a 2-layer bidirectional LSTM with dropout: gates for long memory, two directions for full context, depth for hierarchy, and dropout for regularization.

> [!EXAM] Note the two *different* gradient highways at play: the cell state protects gradients through time, while residual connections protect them through depth. Deep BiLSTMs lean on both.

topics.title: Seq2Seq & Attention
topics.summary: This session shows how encoder-decoder (seq2seq) networks map a variable-length input sequence to a variable-length output by compressing the input into a single context vector, why that single vector becomes an information bottleneck for long sequences, and how attention fixes it by letting the decoder build a fresh weighted blend of all encoder states at every step. It covers alignment scores, Bahdanau (additive) vs. Luong (dot-product) scoring, the Query-Key-Value view, and why soft attention is differentiable and interpretable.

notes.heading: The Problem: Turning One Sequence Into Another
notes.body:
Some tasks take a whole sequence in and must produce a whole *new* sequence out: translate French to English, summarize a paragraph, answer a question. The catch is that the input and output are **both** sequences, but they need not have the same length or the same word order.

> [!INTUITION] You don't know in advance how long the output will be, and the words don't line up one-to-one. "Je suis un etudiant" (4 words) becomes "I am a student" (4 words here, but often the counts differ), and negation in French ("ne ... pas") maps to a single English word.

- **Input**: a variable-length sequence (e.g. a French sentence).
- **Output**: a variable-length sequence (the English translation).
- Lengths differ, word order differs, and mappings can be **one-to-many** or **many-to-one**.
- We need an architecture that maps **any length in -> any length out**.

> [!KEY] Seq2seq is about mapping a variable-length input sequence to a variable-length output sequence. A plain RNN that emits exactly one label per input step cannot do this on its own.

Historically this mattered: before 2014, phrase-based statistical machine translation gave choppy output like "The cat it is on the mat of the." The encoder-decoder architecture plus attention is what made neural machine translation fluent: "The cat is on the mat.\"

notes.heading: Encoder-Decoder: Read, Compress, Generate
notes.body:
The architecture is two RNNs glued together. The **encoder** reads the entire input and squeezes its meaning into a single vector; the **decoder** reads that vector and writes out the translation one word at a time.

[[fig:seq2seq]]

> [!ANALOGY] Reading a sentence, holding its meaning in your head, then saying it in another language. The "meaning in your head" is the **context vector** $c$.

**The encoder** processes input tokens left to right, one per timestep, updating its hidden state:

$$h_t = \text{RNN}(x_t,\; h_{t-1})$$

Here $h_t$ is the **hidden state** (the network's running memory) after reading token $t$; $x_t$ is the current input token; $h_{t-1}$ is the memory carried over from the previous step. It is usually an LSTM or GRU (from last session). The final hidden state $h_T$ is a **summary of the entire input**, and we hand it to the decoder as the context vector $c$. The encoder emits **no output tokens** of its own; it just builds a representation.

**The decoder** takes $c$ as its initial hidden state and generates tokens $y_1, y_2, \dots$ until it emits a special $\langle \text{END} \rangle$ token. It is **autoregressive**: each new word depends on all the words it has already produced.

> [!KEY] In the basic model the single vector $c$ is the **only** bridge between encoder and decoder. Everything the decoder knows about the input must pass through it.

> [!INTUITION] **Teacher forcing.** During *training* we feed the decoder the *correct* previous word (from the ground-truth translation) instead of its own guess, which keeps early training from spiralling off course. At *inference* there is no ground truth, so we feed the model its own previous prediction.

The whole pipeline is trained end-to-end with **cross-entropy** loss on the output tokens (Sutskever et al., 2014, "Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks").

> [!WARNING] Teacher forcing creates a train/test mismatch: the model learns on perfect history but at test time must lean on its own (possibly wrong) past outputs, so errors can compound.

notes.heading: The Information Bottleneck
notes.body:
The basic model forces the **entire** input through one fixed-size vector (say 256 numbers), no matter how long the sentence is. For a short sentence that is fine. For a long one there simply is not room: the early words get forgotten by the time encoding finishes.

> [!ANALOGY] "Summarize a 50-page book onto a single Post-it note, then recreate the whole book from just that note." The Post-it is the fixed context vector $c$.

- The entire input collapses into **ONE** vector (e.g. 256-dim), regardless of length.
- Short sequences fit; long sequences overflow the fixed capacity.
- Early words are "far away" in the computation graph by the end, so their signal fades (the same long-range forgetting that motivated LSTMs/GRUs).

**Evidence.** Plot BLEU score (a translation-quality metric) against sentence length. Basic seq2seq drops sharply as sentences get longer, while attention keeps quality high regardless of length.

> [!KEY] The bottleneck is **fixed capacity**: a single vector of size $d$ cannot hold unlimited information, so basic seq2seq quality degrades as input length grows.
