> [!EXAM] A classic question: *what exactly* does the decoder receive from the input in the basic model? Answer: only the encoder's **final hidden state** -- one fixed-size context vector.

notes.heading: Attention: Give the Decoder an Open Book
notes.body:
Instead of compressing everything into one vector and discarding the rest, **keep all of the encoder's hidden states** and let the decoder *choose which ones to look at* at each step.

[[fig:attention-mechanism]]

> [!ANALOGY] A **closed-book exam** forces you to cram everything into one summary; an **open-book exam** lets you flip back to the relevant page whenever you need it. Attention is the open book.

- Keep every encoder hidden state $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_T$.
- At each decoder step, build a **fresh** context vector by focusing on the states that matter *right now*.
- No single bottleneck: the decoder can reach back to any input position at any output step.

> [!KEY] Attention means: at every decoding step, compute a **new** weighted blend of *all* encoder states, where the weights say how relevant each input position is to the word currently being produced.

> [!WARNING] Attention does **not** remove the encoder's RNN. In Bahdanau/Luong seq2seq the encoder is still an RNN producing the $h_i$; attention just lets the decoder read all of those states instead of only the last one. Removing the RNN entirely is the **Transformer** -- next session.

notes.heading: Attention Step by Step
notes.body:
At each decoder step $t$, with current decoder state $s_t$ and encoder states $h_1, \dots, h_T$:

1. **Score** -- how relevant is each encoder state? Compute $e_{ti} = \text{score}(s_t, h_i)$. Here $e_{ti}$ is a raw relevance number for encoder position $i$ at output step $t$; $s_t$ is the decoder's current state ("what I'm looking for"); $h_i$ is encoder state $i$.
2. **Normalize** -- turn raw scores into weights that sum to 1 using softmax:
$$\alpha_{ti} = \frac{\exp(e_{ti})}{\sum_{j} \exp(e_{tj})}$$
Here $\alpha_{ti}$ is the **attention weight** -- the fraction of attention paid to position $i$; the denominator just sums $\exp$ over all positions $j$ so the weights form a probability distribution (all positive, adding to 1).
3. **Context** -- blend the encoder states using those weights:
$$c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{ti}\, h_i$$
Here $c_t$ is the fresh context vector for this step: a weighted average of all encoder states, leaning toward the relevant ones.
4. **Predict** -- feed the combination $[c_t ; s_t]$ (context concatenated with the decoder state) into the output layer to choose the next token.

> [!MATH] In words: score every input position against where the decoder is now, softmax the scores into weights that add to 1, take a weighted average of the encoder states, and use that blend to pick the next word.

> [!KEY] A **fresh** context vector $c_t$ at **every** decoding step -- that is exactly what kills the bottleneck.

> [!EXAMPLE] Suppose 3 encoder states with raw scores $e = [2.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.0]$. Softmax gives weights $\approx [0.67,\ 0.24,\ 0.09]$, so $c_t \approx 0.67\,h_1 + 0.24\,h_2 + 0.09\,h_3$ -- mostly $h_1$. If a later decoder step instead scored $h_3$ highest, the blend would shift toward $h_3$: the focus *moves* as decoding proceeds.

notes.heading: Scoring Functions: Bahdanau vs. Luong
notes.body:
Everything above leaves one blank: how do we compute the score $\text{score}(s, h)$? Two famous families answer this.

**Bahdanau (additive) attention** is a tiny one-hidden-layer neural network:

$$\text{score}(s, h) = v^\top \tanh(W_s\, s + W_h\, h)$$

Here $s$ is the decoder state and $h$ an encoder state; $W_s$ and $W_h$ are **learnable** weight matrices that transform each state, and $v$ is a **learnable** vector that collapses the result down to a single number. It is called "additive" because it **adds** the two transformed states inside the $\tanh$. The network *learns* what makes a decoder state and an encoder state relevant to each other (Bahdanau et al., 2015).

**Luong (multiplicative) variants** are simpler:

| Name | Formula | Extra parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Dot-product | $\text{score} = s^\top h$ | none |
| General (bilinear) | $\text{score} = s^\top W h$ | one matrix $W$ |
| Concat (= Bahdanau) | $\text{score} = v^\top \tanh(W[s; h])$ | $W, v$ |

> [!MATH] The **dot product** $s^\top h$ is just "multiply the two vectors element-by-element and add it all up" -- a single number measuring how aligned (similar) the decoder state and encoder state are. Bigger dot product -> bigger attention weight.

| | Dot-product | Additive (Bahdanau) |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | none | learnable $W_s, W_h, v$ |
| Speed | very fast (one matrix multiply) | slower (extra layer) |
| Expressiveness | less | more |
| Best for | large dimensions / modern models | small dimensions |

> [!KEY] Both answer the same question -- "how relevant is each encoder state?" -- with different scoring math. In practice **dot-product wins for modern model sizes**, and it is what Transformers use.

Preview: Transformers use **scaled** dot-product, dividing the score by $\sqrt{d_k}$ (where $d_k$ is the key dimension) to keep the numbers from getting too large. More on that next session.

notes.heading: Reading the Alignment Heatmap
notes.body:
Because attention assigns a weight $\alpha_{ti}$ to every (output word, input word) pair, we can simply **draw** those weights as a grid -- a heatmap. Bright cells show which source word the model looked at while producing each target word.

[[fig:attention-heatmap]]

> [!INTUITION] The model learns **word alignment** for free: nobody told it that English "I" lines up with French "Je." It discovered this purely from the translation objective.

- A roughly **diagonal** bright stripe = **monotonic** alignment: output word $k$ mostly attends to input word $k$ (same order in both languages).
- **Off-diagonal** bright cells = **reordering**: the languages place words in different orders (e.g. adjective-noun swaps), so the model attends *across* positions to compensate.

> [!KEY] Attention does not only boost accuracy -- it makes the model **interpretable**. The heatmap is a window into *why* the model produced each word.

> [!EXAM] Be ready to read a heatmap: a diagonal means same word order; off-diagonal means the model is reordering words between languages. And remember: this alignment is **learned without any alignment supervision**.

notes.heading: Attention as Query-Key-Value Lookup
notes.body:
There is a unifying way to think about attention, borrowed from databases.

> [!ANALOGY] A **soft dictionary lookup**. A **query** says what you are looking for; each entry has a **key** advertising what it offers and a **value** holding the actual content. You compare your query against every key, and instead of grabbing the single best match you take a *weighted blend* of all the values.

In seq2seq translation:

- **Query** $Q$ = the current decoder state $s_t$ -- "what am I looking for now?"
- **Keys** $K$ = the encoder states $h_i$ -- "what does each input position advertise?"
- **Values** $V$ = also the encoder states $h_i$ -- "what actually gets retrieved." (In basic seq2seq, keys and values are the same encoder states.)

Compact form:

$$\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

Here $Q K^\top$ produces all the query-key similarities (the scores); $\sqrt{d_k}$ scales them down ($d_k$ is the key dimension); softmax turns them into weights that sum to 1; and multiplying by $V$ takes the weighted sum.

| Hard lookup | Soft lookup (attention) |
|---|---|
| Pick the ONE best match (argmax) | Weighted blend of ALL values |
| Like a hash table | Like a fuzzy search |
| **Not** differentiable | **Fully** differentiable |

> [!KEY] Because attention is a *soft* (weighted) lookup it is differentiable, so the whole encoder + decoder + attention trains end-to-end with backprop from the translation loss alone. A *hard* argmax pick would break the gradient.

Beyond translation, the same "select the relevant info from a sequence" trick powers text summarization (attend to key sentences), image captioning (attend to image regions), speech recognition (attend to audio frames), and question answering (attend to relevant passages).

> [!EXAM] Know the QKV roles cold: in seq2seq, $Q$ = decoder state, $K = V$ = encoder hidden states, and the attention weights are the similarity between $Q$ and each $K$.

quiz.q: In the basic (no-attention) encoder-decoder, what information from the input does the decoder actually receive?
options: Every encoder hidden state, one per input token
The raw input tokens, re-fed at each decoder step
Only the encoder's final hidden state -- a single fixed-size context vector
A separate context vector recomputed at each decoder step
quiz.explanation: In basic seq2seq the only bridge is the encoder's last hidden state, packed into one fixed-size vector c. Giving the decoder every hidden state describes attention, not the basic model; recomputing a context vector per step is also attention; and the decoder never sees the raw input tokens directly.

quiz.q: Why does a basic seq2seq model's translation quality fall off sharply as input sentences get longer?
options: Longer sentences contain rarer words the softmax cannot represent
The cross-entropy loss grows automatically with sequence length
The decoder runs out of timesteps before it can emit an END token
A single fixed-size context vector cannot hold unlimited information, so early words are forgotten
quiz.explanation: This is the information bottleneck: the whole input is squeezed into one fixed-capacity vector, so long inputs overflow it and early words fade. Rare words and the magnitude of the loss are not the architectural cause, and the decoder can emit as many tokens as needed, so 'running out of timesteps' is wrong.

quiz.q: Teacher forcing during training means that, at each step, the decoder is fed:
options: the correct (ground-truth) previous token instead of its own prediction
its own predicted previous token, exactly as at inference time
the encoder's final hidden state at every step
the average of its prediction and the ground-truth token
quiz.explanation: Teacher forcing feeds the ground-truth previous word during training to stabilize learning; at inference there is no ground truth so the model feeds its own predictions. Feeding its own prediction during training is exactly what teacher forcing avoids; the context vector initializes the decoder once, not at every step; and averaging is not part of the method.

quiz.q: What is the defining difference in how attention versus basic seq2seq supplies context to the decoder?
options: Attention uses a larger single context vector; basic seq2seq uses a smaller one
Attention feeds raw input tokens; basic seq2seq feeds hidden states
Attention computes a fresh context vector at every decoding step; basic seq2seq reuses one fixed vector for the whole output
Attention removes the encoder; basic seq2seq keeps it
quiz.explanation: Attention builds a new weighted blend of all encoder states per decoding step, whereas basic seq2seq reuses a single context vector throughout. It is not about the size of the vector, neither feeds raw tokens, and attention does not remove the encoder RNN (that is the Transformer).

quiz.q: Which ordering correctly describes the per-step attention computation?
options: Weighted sum of encoder states, then score each state, then softmax
Softmax the encoder states, then score them, then take the argmax
Score each encoder state against the decoder state, then softmax the scores into weights, then weighted-sum the encoder states
Score each encoder state, then weighted-sum, then softmax the context vector
quiz.explanation: The order is score -> softmax (normalize) -> weighted sum (context). You must score first to know relevance, normalize those scores into weights that sum to 1, then blend. Softmaxing the states themselves or taking an argmax (that is hard attention) is wrong, and you cannot weight-sum before you have any weights.

quiz.q: What is the role of the softmax applied to the alignment scores in the attention mechanism?
options: It selects the single highest-scoring encoder state and discards the rest
It turns the raw scores into non-negative weights that sum to 1, so the context is a proper weighted average
It rescales every encoder state to unit length before scoring
It adds the decoder and encoder states together
quiz.explanation: Softmax converts raw scores into a probability distribution -- weights that are all non-negative and sum to 1 -- used to average the encoder states. Picking only the top one is hard attention (argmax), which soft attention deliberately avoids; softmax neither normalizes vector length nor adds states.

quiz.q: Bahdanau (additive) attention scores a decoder state s and encoder state h. What makes it 'additive' and what is learnable?
options: It adds the two transformed states inside a tanh, and the matrices Ws, Wh plus the vector v are learned -- a tiny one-layer neural net
It adds s and h directly with no parameters; nothing is learned
It adds the softmax weights together; only v is learned
It multiplies s and h; Ws and Wh are fixed constants
quiz.explanation: 'Additive' refers to summing the transformed states Ws*s + Wh*h inside the tanh, and the score is a small neural network with learnable Ws, Wh, and v. The plain dot product s-transpose-h has no parameters and is the multiplicative Luong variant, not Bahdanau.

quiz.q: In Luong dot-product attention, which encoder states end up with the highest attention weights at a given decoder step?
options: The states that appeared earliest in the input sequence
The states whose vectors are most similar (largest dot product) to the current decoder state
The states with the largest magnitude, regardless of the decoder state
The states selected by a separate learned gating network
quiz.explanation: Dot-product attention scores by s-transpose-h, so encoder states most aligned with the decoder state score highest and, after softmax, receive the most weight. Position in the sequence does not determine it, raw magnitude alone ignores the decoder state, and dot-product attention has no separate gating network.

quiz.q: Why can 'soft' attention be trained end-to-end with ordinary backpropagation while 'hard' attention cannot, directly?
options: Soft attention has fewer parameters, so its gradients are easier to compute
Soft attention is a differentiable weighted sum, whereas hard attention uses a non-differentiable argmax selection
Hard attention requires labeled alignments while soft attention does not
Soft attention skips the softmax, removing a source of vanishing gradients
quiz.explanation: Soft attention's weighted sum is smooth and differentiable, so gradients flow through the weights; hard attention's argmax pick is non-differentiable and needs RL-style methods such as REINFORCE. It is not about parameter count, soft attention actually uses softmax, and neither variant requires supervised alignments.

quiz.q: Casting seq2seq attention as a Query-Key-Value lookup, which assignment is correct?
options: Query = decoder state; Keys = encoder states; Values = encoder states
Query = encoder state; Keys = decoder state; Values = output tokens
Query = decoder state; Keys = output tokens; Values = decoder states
Query = encoder state; Keys = encoder states; Values = decoder state
quiz.explanation: The decoder state is the query (what we are looking for); the encoder states serve as both the keys (what each position advertises) and the values (what gets retrieved). Putting the query on the encoder side, or using output tokens as keys/values, breaks the lookup interpretation.

quiz.q: When training a seq2seq+attention translator, how does the model learn which source words align with which target words?
options: From a separate dataset of human-annotated word alignments fed in as labels
It is hard-coded to align position i with position i
By explicitly maximizing the diagonal of the attention heatmap during training
Purely from the translation (cross-entropy) objective -- alignment emerges with no alignment supervision
quiz.explanation: Alignment is never supervised; the attention weights are learned end-to-end from the single translation loss, and meaningful alignments emerge as a by-product. There is no alignment-label dataset, no hard-coded diagonal (which would forbid reordering), and nothing explicitly pushes the heatmap toward a diagonal.

quiz.q: An attention heatmap for a translation shows bright cells well off the main diagonal. What does this most directly indicate?
options: The model is overfitting and should be regularized
The encoder and decoder have different hidden sizes
The softmax temperature is set too high
Word reordering between the two languages -- output words attend to source words in a different position/order
quiz.explanation: Off-diagonal attention means target words align with source words in a different order, i.e. the languages reorder words (e.g. adjective-noun swaps) and the model attends across positions to handle it. It is not a sign of overfitting, mismatched sizes, or softmax temperature; a purely diagonal map would indicate monotonic (same-order) alignment.

descriptive.q: The basic encoder-decoder works for short sentences but degrades on long ones. Explain the architectural reason (the 'information bottleneck') in detail, and describe the empirical evidence presented for it.
descriptive.answer:
**The single bridge.** In basic seq2seq the encoder reads the input and produces hidden states $h_1, \dots, h_T$, but only the **final** one is passed on:

$$c = h_T, \qquad h_t = \text{RNN}(x_t, h_{t-1})$$

where $h_t$ is the running memory after token $t$, and $c$ is the lone **context vector** handed to the decoder as its initial state. Everything the decoder ever learns about the input must flow through this one fixed-size vector (e.g. 256 numbers).

**Why long inputs break.**

- **Fixed capacity.** A vector of size $d$ can store only so much. Short sentences fit; long sentences overflow it, so detail is lost.
- **Distance / forgetting.** Early words are many timesteps away from $h_T$ in the computation graph, so their contribution fades -- the same long-range forgetting that motivated LSTMs and GRUs.

> [!ANALOGY] It is like summarizing a 50-page book onto one Post-it note and then trying to recreate the whole book from just that note. The longer the book, the worse the reconstruction.

**Empirical evidence.** Plotting BLEU (a translation-quality score) against input sentence length shows basic seq2seq dropping **sharply** as sentences lengthen, whereas an attention model stays roughly flat and high. The gap widens with length, which is exactly the fingerprint of a capacity bottleneck rather than, say, a vocabulary or optimization problem.

> [!KEY] The bottleneck is the **fixed-size single context vector**; attention removes it by letting the decoder read all encoder states, a fresh blend at each step.

descriptive.q: Write out the attention mechanism at decoder step t as a sequence of steps. For each step state its inputs, the operation, and its output. Then explain in one sentence why this design eliminates the bottleneck.
descriptive.answer:
At decoder step $t$ we have the current decoder state $s_t$ and all encoder states $h_1, \dots, h_T$.

| Step | Inputs | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Score | $s_t$, each $h_i$ | $e_{ti} = \text{score}(s_t, h_i)$ | raw relevance scores $e_{ti}$ |
| 2. Normalize | scores $e_{ti}$ | $\alpha_{ti} = \dfrac{\exp(e_{ti})}{\sum_j \exp(e_{tj})}$ | attention weights $\alpha_{ti}$ (sum to 1) |
| 3. Context | weights $\alpha_{ti}$, states $h_i$ | $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{ti}\, h_i$ | context vector $c_t$ |
| 4. Predict | $c_t$, $s_t$ | feed $[c_t; s_t]$ to the output layer | next token $y_t$ |

Glossing the symbols: $e_{ti}$ is how relevant encoder position $i$ is to output step $t$; $s_t$ is "what the decoder is looking for now"; $h_i$ is encoder state $i$; $\alpha_{ti}$ is the share of attention given to position $i$ (a probability-like weight); $c_t$ is the freshly built blend of encoder states for this step.

> [!MATH] Score every input position against where the decoder is now, soften the scores into weights that add to 1, take a weighted average of the encoder states, then use that blend to choose the next word.

**Why it removes the bottleneck (one sentence):** because a **new** context vector $c_t$ is computed from **all** encoder states at **every** step, no single fixed vector ever has to carry the whole input, so even distant early words remain directly reachable.

descriptive.q: Contrast 'soft' and 'hard' attention. Why is soft attention trainable with plain backpropagation while hard attention is not? What would you need to train a hard-attention model?
descriptive.answer:
**Soft vs. hard.**

| | Hard lookup | Soft lookup (attention) |
|---|---|---|
| What it returns | the ONE best-matching value (argmax) | a weighted blend of ALL values |
| Mental model | hash table / exact match | fuzzy search |
| Differentiable? | **No** | **Yes** |

**Soft attention** forms the context as a continuous weighted sum, $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{ti} h_i$, where each weight $\alpha_{ti}$ comes from a softmax. Every operation -- score, softmax, weighted sum -- is smooth, so the loss has a well-defined gradient with respect to the scores and hence the network's parameters. Gradients flow back through the weights, nudging the model to put more weight where it helped.

**Hard attention** instead *selects* a single encoder position with an $\arg\max$ (or a sampled choice). $\arg\max$ is a step function: tiny changes in the scores leave the choice unchanged, then it jumps. Its derivative is zero almost everywhere, so ordinary backprop cannot send a useful gradient through the selection.

> [!KEY] Soft attention is chosen precisely because it is **differentiable**: it lets the whole encoder + decoder + attention train end-to-end from the translation loss alone, with **no alignment supervision**.

**To train hard attention** you need a method that does not rely on differentiating the selection, e.g. reinforcement-learning-style estimators such as **REINFORCE** (treat the choice as a stochastic action, use the loss as a reward signal) or other score-function / variance-reduced gradient estimators.

descriptive.q: Compare Bahdanau (additive) and Luong (dot-product) attention. Give their scoring formulas, parameter counts, speed and expressiveness, and say when you would prefer each. Why does dot-product dominate modern large models?
descriptive.answer:
**Scoring formulas.**

- Bahdanau (additive): $\text{score}(s, h) = v^\top \tanh(W_s s + W_h h)$ -- a tiny one-hidden-layer neural net. $W_s, W_h$ are learnable matrices that transform the decoder state $s$ and encoder state $h$; $v$ is a learnable vector that reduces the result to one number. "Additive" because the transformed states are **summed** inside the $\tanh$.
- Luong (dot-product): $\text{score}(s, h) = s^\top h$ -- just the dot product (element-wise multiply and sum), a single similarity number with **no** parameters. (Luong's "general" variant adds one matrix: $s^\top W h$.)

**Comparison.**

| | Dot-product (Luong) | Additive (Bahdanau) |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | none | learnable $W_s, W_h, v$ |
| Speed | very fast (one matrix multiply) | slower (extra layer) |
| Expressiveness | lower | higher |
| Scales to large dims | well | less well |

**When to prefer each.** Use **additive** when dimensions are **small** or you want the extra expressiveness of a learned scoring net. Use **dot-product** when dimensions are **large** and speed matters.

**Why dot-product dominates modern models.** It is a single, highly parallel matrix multiplication with no extra parameters, so it is fast and scales to the large hidden sizes used today, with accuracy nearly matching additive. That is why Transformers adopt it, in the **scaled** form

$$\frac{s^\top h}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

where dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ ($d_k$ = key dimension) keeps the dot products from growing too large as dimensionality increases.

> [!KEY] Same job, different scoring math; additive is more expressive but slower, dot-product is cheaper and parallel, so it wins at scale.

descriptive.q: Explain how attention can be framed as a Query-Key-Value lookup. Identify Q, K, and V for seq2seq translation, walk through the soft-lookup computation, and say how it differs from a classic hash-table lookup.
descriptive.answer:
**The framing.** Attention is a *soft* dictionary lookup. A **query** states what you want; each stored item has a **key** advertising what it offers and a **value** holding the content. You compare the query to every key and retrieve a weighted blend of the values.

**Roles in seq2seq translation.**

- **Query** $Q$ = the current decoder state $s_t$ -- "what am I looking for now?"
- **Keys** $K$ = the encoder states $h_i$ -- "what does each input position advertise?"
- **Values** $V$ = also the encoder states $h_i$ -- "what gets retrieved." (Keys and values are the same encoder states here.)

**The computation.**

$$\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

Reading it left to right: $Q K^\top$ gives every query-key similarity (the scores); dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ ($d_k$ = key dimension) keeps them from getting too large; softmax turns them into weights that sum to 1; multiplying by $V$ takes the weighted sum, producing the context vector.

**Versus a hash table.**

| Hash-table (hard) lookup | Attention (soft) lookup |
|---|---|
| Exact key match | Graded similarity to every key |
| Returns one value (argmax) | Returns a weighted blend of all values |
| Not differentiable | Differentiable -- trainable by backprop |

> [!KEY] A hash table grabs the single exact match; attention retrieves a *similarity-weighted average* of all values, and because that is smooth it can be trained end-to-end.

descriptive.q: Attention is praised for making models interpretable. Explain how, using the alignment heatmap. What do diagonal vs. off-diagonal patterns mean, and what is remarkable about how these alignments are obtained?
descriptive.answer:
**Where the picture comes from.** At each decoder step the model produces attention weights $\alpha_{ti}$ -- one number per (target word $t$, source word $i$) pair, all non-negative and summing to 1. Arranging these weights as a grid (targets on one axis, sources on the other) and shading by magnitude gives an **alignment heatmap**: bright cells show which source word the model attended to while emitting each target word.

[[fig:attention-heatmap]]

**Reading the patterns.**

- **Diagonal (monotonic):** target word $k$ attends mainly to source word $k$ -- the two languages share roughly the same word order.
- **Off-diagonal (non-monotonic):** bright cells away from the diagonal mean **reordering** -- e.g. adjective-noun swaps -- so the model reaches across positions to pull the right source word.

**Why this is interpretability.** For the first time we can *see why* the model produced each output word: the heatmap exposes the soft word-to-word correspondence the model used, turning a black box into something inspectable. This is why we say attention does not only raise accuracy, it also makes the model interpretable.

> [!KEY] What is remarkable: these alignments are **learned with no alignment supervision**. Nobody labels which words correspond; the alignments emerge purely from optimizing the translation (cross-entropy) loss end-to-end.

topics.title: Transformers & Self-Attention
topics.summary: This session introduces the Transformer and its core engine, self-attention, where every token looks at every other token in parallel using learned Query, Key, and Value projections; it then layers on multi-head attention, sinusoidal positional encoding, and the encoder-decoder block that powers GPT and BERT.

notes.heading: Why Transformers? Killing the Sequential Bottleneck
notes.body:
Before 2017, the best models for language were **RNNs** and **LSTMs**. Picture reading a sentence with one finger, moving word by word: to understand word 50 you must first crawl through words 1-49. That is exactly how an RNN works.

This causes two problems:

- **No parallelism.** Word $n$ cannot be processed until words $1 \dots n-1$ are done. Here $n$ is just the position of a word in the sentence. On a GPU (which loves doing thousands of things at once), this serial chain is painfully slow.
- **Forgetting.** Even LSTMs start to lose the thread beyond roughly 200 tokens (a *token* is one chunk of text, usually a word or word-piece).

Earlier work bolted an **attention** mechanism on top of RNNs to help the decoder peek back at the input, but the slow sequential RNN was still underneath. In 2017 Vaswani et al. asked the bold question in the title of their paper, *"Attention Is All You Need"*: what if we throw the RNN away entirely and build the whole model out of attention?

> [!KEY]
> A Transformer replaces the step-by-step recurrence of an RNN with **self-attention**, so all positions are computed **at the same time** instead of one after another. This single change is what unlocked modern large language models.

> [!ANALOGY]
> An RNN is like a relay race: each runner must wait for the baton. Self-attention is like putting every word in one room and letting them all talk to each other simultaneously.

notes.heading: Self-Attention: Every Word Looks at Every Word
notes.body:
The core idea is simple to say in words: **each token asks every other token "how relevant are you to me?" and then mixes in information from the ones that matter.** All of these questions are answered in a single parallel step, not in a chain.

[[fig:self-attention-qkv]]

Take the famous sentence:

> *"The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired."*

What does *"it"* refer to? You instantly know it means *the animal*, not *the street*. In self-attention, the token *"it"* will place a **high attention weight** on *"animal"* and low weight on the other words. Crucially, nobody hand-coded a rule for pronouns. The model **learns** these patterns from data; coreference (figuring out what *"it"* points to) simply *emerges* from training.

> [!INTUITION]
> "Attending to" a word just means **taking a weighted average that leans heavily toward that word**. If *"it"* attends to *"animal"* with weight 0.7, then 70% of *"it"*'s new representation is built from *"animal"*'s information.

Notice what we lost compared to an RNN: there is no built-in notion of order. Self-attention treats the input as a **set** of tokens, all visible at once. We will have to fix that later with positional encoding.

> [!EXAM]
> A classic question: "Why is self-attention faster to train than an RNN even though it does more comparisons?" Answer: every comparison is independent, so they all run **in parallel** in one step, whereas an RNN forces $n$ sequential steps.

notes.heading: Queries, Keys, and Values - A Library Search
notes.body:
How does a token actually "ask a question" and "offer information"? Each token's embedding is projected into **three** different vectors with three different learned weight matrices:

| Vector | Plain-English role | Question it answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Query** $q$ | what *this* token is looking for | "What am I searching for?" |
| **Key** $k$ | what *each* token advertises about itself | "What do I offer?" |
| **Value** $v$ | the actual content a token will hand over | "What information do I carry?" |

> [!ANALOGY]
> Think of a **library search**. You walk in with a question in your head (the **query**). Every book has a title on its spine (its **key**). You compare your question against all the titles, find the best matches, and then read the **contents** of those books (their **values**).

The math is just three matrix multiplications. For a token embedding $x_i$:

$$q_i = W_Q\, x_i \qquad k_i = W_K\, x_i \qquad v_i = W_V\, x_i$$

Here $x_i$ is the embedding vector of the $i$-th token (a list of numbers representing that word). $W_Q, W_K, W_V$ are three **separate learned weight matrices** -- the only things being trained here. Multiplying $x_i$ by each matrix produces its query $q_i$, key $k_i$, and value $v_i$.

> [!KEY]
> In **self**-attention, $Q$, $K$, and $V$ all come from the **same input sequence** -- they are just three different *views* of it, created by three different weight matrices. That is what the word "self" means.

> [!WARNING]
> A common confusion: people think $Q$, $K$, $V$ are three separate lookup tables or come from different layers. They do not. Same input, three learned projections. The matrices $W_Q, W_K, W_V$ are what differ.

notes.heading: Scaled Dot-Product Attention - The Engine
notes.body:
Now we combine the queries, keys, and values into one formula. In words: **compare every query to every key to get similarity scores, shrink those scores a bit, turn them into percentages with softmax, then take a weighted average of the values.**

$$\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

Let's gloss every symbol:

- $Q$ stacks all the query vectors as rows; $K$ stacks all the keys; $V$ stacks all the values.
- $QK^{\top}$ ("Q times K-transpose") computes the **dot product of every query with every key**. A dot product is large when two vectors point the same way, so this is a **similarity score**. The result is an $n \times n$ matrix (one score for each pair of tokens), where $n$ is the number of tokens.
- $d_k$ is the dimension (length) of each key/query vector. $\sqrt{d_k}$ is just its square root.
- $\mathrm{softmax}$ squashes each **row** of scores into positive numbers that **sum to 1** -- a probability distribution telling us how much weight each token gets.
- Multiplying by $V$ takes the **weighted average of the value vectors** using those probabilities.

[[fig:scaled-dot-product]]

> [!MATH]
> The formula reads as: "Match queries to keys to get scores; scale them down by $\sqrt{d_k}$; softmax each row into weights that add to 1; then read out a weighted blend of the values."

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Suppose 3 tokens and $d_k = 4$. Then $Q$ is $3\times 4$ and $K^{\top}$ is $4\times 3$, so $QK^{\top}$ is a $3\times 3$ **score matrix**. We divide every entry by $\sqrt{4}=2$, softmax each row, then multiply the $3\times 3$ weights by $V$ ($3\times 4$) to get a $3\times 4$ **output** -- one new vector per token, same shape we started with.

**Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** As $d_k$ grows, dot products grow too (more terms are summed), so the scores get large. Large scores make softmax extremely "peaked" -- it puts almost all weight on one token, and in that saturated region its **gradient is nearly zero**, so learning stalls.

$$\text{Var}(q \cdot k) \approx d_k \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \approx 1$$

In words: if each component of $q$ and $k$ has variance about 1, the dot product's variance is about $d_k$; dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ brings the variance back to about 1, keeping softmax in a healthy, learnable range.

> [!INTUITION]
> This is the **same idea as Xavier initialization** from earlier weeks: control the scale of activations so gradients stay healthy. Without scaling, big numbers saturate the softmax and freeze learning.

notes.heading: Multi-Head Attention - Many Perspectives at Once
notes.body:
A single attention computation can only learn **one kind** of relationship at a time. But language has many at once: grammar (subject-verb), meaning (synonyms), coreference (pronouns), nearby-word proximity, punctuation boundaries. One head cannot juggle all of these.

The fix is **multi-head attention**: run several attention computations ("heads") in parallel, each with its **own** $W_Q, W_K, W_V$, and let each specialize.

[[fig:multi-head-attention]]

How the dimensions work, in four steps:

1. **Split.** Divide the model width $d_{\text{model}}$ across $h$ heads, so each head works in a smaller dimension $d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$. Here $h$ is the number of heads.
2. **Attend.** Each head runs scaled dot-product attention independently in its small subspace.
3. **Concatenate.** Glue all the head outputs back together side by side.
4. **Project.** Multiply the concatenation by an output matrix $W_O$ to mix the heads and return to width $d_{\text{model}}$.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> The original Transformer uses $d_{\text{model}} = 512$ and $h = 8$ heads, so each head has $d_k = 512/8 = 64$ dimensions. Eight heads $\times$ 64 dims $= 512$ total -- **same parameter count and roughly the same compute as one big 512-dim head**, but you get 8 different "perspectives" on the input.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Like handing the same essay to **multiple reviewers**: one checks grammar, one checks logic, one checks style. Each reads the whole essay but looks for something different, and you combine their notes at the end.

> [!WARNING]
> Multi-head is **not** 8x more expensive than single-head. Because each head is $1/h$ as wide, the total work is about the same as one full-width head. The win is **diversity of relationships**, not extra capacity.

> [!EXAM]
> Expect a numeric question: "Given $d_{\text{model}}=512$ and $h=8$, what is $d_k$?" Answer: $512/8 = 64$.

notes.heading: Positional Encoding - Telling the Model the Word Order
notes.body:
Self-attention has a hidden weakness: it is **permutation-invariant**. It looks at all tokens as an unordered set, so on its own it cannot tell *"Dog bites man"* from *"Man bites dog"* -- same words, totally different meaning.

[[fig:positional-encoding]]

RNNs got order **for free** because they processed tokens one at a time. Transformers process everything at once, so we must **explicitly inject** position information. The Transformer does this by **adding a position-specific vector to each token's embedding** before attention. The chosen vectors are sinusoidal:

$$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d}}\right), \qquad PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d}}\right)$$

Glossing the symbols: $pos$ is the position of the token (0, 1, 2, ...); $i$ indexes the dimension within the encoding vector; $d$ is the encoding length. Even dimensions use $\sin$, odd dimensions use $\cos$, and the giant $10000^{2i/d}$ in the denominator makes early dimensions wiggle slowly (low frequency) and later dimensions wiggle fast (high frequency).

> [!ANALOGY]
> Imagine **page numbers in a shuffled deck of cards**. Without numbers you can never put the deck back in order. The sin/cos vector is a unique "page number" stamped onto each position so the model can recover order.

Why sinusoids specifically?

- **Unique fingerprint.** Each position gets a distinct combination of wave values, so no two positions look alike.
- **Nearby = similar.** Positions close together have similar encodings (high dot product); far-apart positions differ. The model can read **relative** distance from these signals.
- **Generalizes.** Because $\sin$ and $\cos$ are defined for any input, the scheme extends smoothly to sequences **longer than any seen during training**.

> [!KEY]
> Positional encoding does the opposite of permutation-invariance: it **breaks the symmetry** by giving every position a recognizable signature, so order matters again.

> [!WARNING]
> Positional encodings are **added** to the token embeddings, not concatenated, and they do **not** make attention permutation-invariant -- they are the very thing that removes that invariance.

notes.heading: The Full Transformer - Blocks, Masking, and Why It Beats RNNs
notes.body:
We now stack the pieces into the actual architecture. A Transformer **encoder block** has two sub-layers, each wrapped with a residual ("Add") connection and **LayerNorm** (the "Add & Norm" step):

1. **Multi-head self-attention** -- tokens exchange information.
2. **Position-wise feed-forward network (FFN)** -- a small MLP applied to each token separately.

Both sub-layers use **residual connections** -- exactly the ResNet trick from Week 4 -- so gradients flow cleanly. The original model stacks $N = 6$ such blocks.

The **decoder block** has **three** sub-layers instead of two:

1. **Masked self-attention** -- the decoder generates one token at a time, so position 5 must **not** peek at position 6. Masking sets the scores of all future positions to $-\infty$ before softmax; since $\mathrm{softmax}(-\infty) = 0$, those tokens get zero weight.
2. **Cross-attention** -- the **decoder supplies the queries**, while the **encoder output supplies the keys and values**. ("The decoder asks questions; the encoder has the answers.")
3. **Feed-forward network.**

> [!KEY]
> Masking is just "fill future scores with $-\infty$ before softmax." That single trick is what makes autoregressive generation (predicting the next word) honest -- the model can never cheat by looking ahead.

**Why does this beat RNNs?** Compare them head to head:

| Property | RNN / LSTM | Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Parallelizable | No | **Yes** |
| Path length between any two tokens | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Long-range dependencies | Poor / Good | **Excellent** |
| Attention memory cost | $O(1)$ | $O(n^2)$ |

The catch is the last row. Self-attention costs $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ time and memory: every one of $n$ tokens compares against all $n$ others ($n^2$ pairs), each comparison over $d$ dimensions. A recurrent layer instead costs $O(n \cdot d^2)$. So attention is **cheaper when $n < d$** but **more expensive for very long sequences** ($n$ in the thousands).

> [!INTUITION]
> The Transformer trades **memory for speed**: it pays $O(n^2)$ memory but does everything in **one parallel step** instead of $n$ sequential ones. On GPUs, for typical sequence lengths, that trade is a massive win. Only at very long lengths ($n \gtrsim 10000$) does $O(n^2)$ force "efficient attention" research.

> [!EXAM]
> Three favorites: (1) "Complexity of self-attention w.r.t. sequence length?" -> $O(n^2)$. (2) "Why masked attention in the decoder?" -> can't see future tokens during generation. (3) "One advantage and one disadvantage vs. LSTM?" -> advantage: parallel, $O(1)$ path; disadvantage: $O(n^2)$ memory.

quiz.q: In self-attention, where do the query, key, and value vectors for a given token come from?
options: From the same token embedding multiplied by three different learned weight matrices W_Q, W_K, and W_V
The query comes from the current token, while the key and value come from the previous token's hidden state
They are three separate vectors looked up from independent embedding tables
The query and key are identical, and only the value uses a separate projection
quiz.explanation: In self-attention all three come from the SAME input; the only difference is the learned projection matrix applied (W_Q, W_K, W_V). The 'previous hidden state' option describes an RNN, not self-attention -- there is no sequential dependence here, and Q/K/V are not looked up from separate tables.

quiz.q: What does the product QK-transpose compute inside scaled dot-product attention?
options: The final weighted sum of the value vectors
The attention weights, already normalized to sum to 1
A pairwise similarity (dot-product) score between every query and every key
The positional distance between each pair of tokens
quiz.explanation: QK-transpose is an n-by-n matrix of dot products -- raw similarity scores between each query and each key. They are NOT yet normalized (softmax does that next) and NOT the value-weighted output (multiplying by V comes last). Position is handled separately by positional encoding, not by this product.

quiz.q: Why is the score matrix divided by the square root of d_k before applying softmax?
options: To keep the variance of the dot products near 1 so softmax does not saturate and produce near-zero gradients
To normalize each row so the scores sum to exactly 1
To reduce the O(n^2) memory cost of the attention matrix
To guarantee the attention weights are non-negative
quiz.explanation: Dot products grow with d_k (variance about d_k), which pushes softmax into a peaked, saturated regime with vanishing gradients; dividing by sqrt(d_k) restores variance about 1. Making rows sum to 1 and forcing non-negativity are already done BY softmax, not by the scaling. Scaling does nothing to memory cost.

quiz.q: A Transformer uses d_model = 512 with 8 attention heads. What dimension d_k does each head operate in?
quiz.explanation: d_k = d_model / h = 512 / 8 = 64. Each head works in a 64-dim subspace; concatenating the 8 heads returns to 512. Picking 512 ignores the split; 4096 multiplies instead of dividing; 8 is the number of heads, not the per-head dimension.

quiz.q: After computing the scaled scores, why is a softmax applied to each row of the score matrix?
options: To inject positional order information into the scores
To make the attention output the same dimension as the input
To prevent the decoder from attending to future tokens
To turn raw similarity scores into a non-negative probability distribution that sums to 1, so values can be averaged by relevance
quiz.explanation: Softmax converts arbitrary real scores into weights that are non-negative and sum to 1, giving a proper weighted average of the value vectors. Matching the output dimension is handled by the value projection; blocking future tokens is masking; injecting order is positional encoding -- none of these is softmax's job.

quiz.q: Self-attention is 'permutation-invariant.' Without any positional information added, what does this imply?
options: Gradients vanish whenever the sequence is long
The model can only process sequences of a single fixed length
Each token is forced to attend only to itself
'Dog bites man' and 'Man bites dog' would yield the same set of token representations
quiz.explanation: Permutation-invariance means reordering the input tokens does not change the computed representations, so the two sentences (same word multiset) look identical to the model -- which is why positional encoding is needed. It has nothing to do with fixed lengths, self-only attention, or gradient vanishing.

quiz.q: How is masked self-attention in the decoder actually implemented?
options: Delete the value vectors of future tokens after the softmax step
Set the scores of future positions to negative infinity before softmax, so softmax assigns them weight 0
Skip the sqrt(d_k) scaling for any future position
Multiply each future token's input embedding by zero before the projections
quiz.explanation: Masking happens on the score matrix BEFORE softmax: future entries are set to -infinity, and softmax(-infinity)=0, so future tokens contribute nothing. Removing values after softmax would not zero out their already-computed weights; the masking is about scores, not scaling, and not about zeroing raw embeddings.

quiz.q: For a sequence of length n and model dimension d, how does the per-layer cost of self-attention scale, and how does that compare to a recurrent layer?
options: Self-attention is O(n * d^2); a recurrent layer is O(n^2 * d)
Self-attention is O(n * d); a recurrent layer is O(n^2 * d)
Self-attention is O(n^2 * d); a recurrent layer is O(n * d^2)
Both are O(n^2 * d^2)
quiz.explanation: Self-attention compares all n^2 token pairs across d dimensions, giving O(n^2 d); a recurrent layer does n sequential steps each costing d^2, giving O(n d^2). So attention is cheaper when n < d and more expensive for very long sequences. The other options swap or inflate these factors.

quiz.q: A key reason Transformers handle long-range dependencies better than RNNs is the path length between any two tokens. What is it for self-attention?
options: O(log n) -- attention forms a balanced hierarchical tree
O(n) -- the signal still passes through every token in between
O(1) -- every token directly attends to every other in a single step
O(n^2) -- matching the attention memory cost
quiz.explanation: In self-attention any token can attend to any other directly, so information travels in one hop: O(1) path length. RNNs have O(n) path length because the signal must pass step by step through intermediate tokens. There is no hierarchical tree, and path length is not the same as the O(n^2) memory cost.

quiz.q: With d_model = 512 split into 8 heads of 64 dimensions, how does multi-head attention compare in cost to a single 512-dim attention head?
options: About 8 times more expensive, since each head runs full 512-dim attention
Roughly the same total parameters and compute, but with 8 different subspace 'perspectives' on the input
About 8 times cheaper, since each head is smaller
Identical in every respect, including the relationships each head learns
quiz.explanation: Because each head is only 1/8 as wide (64 vs 512), the heads together cost about the same as one full-width head -- 8 x 64 = 512. The benefit is diversity: 8 heads attend to different representation subspaces. It is neither 8x more nor 8x less expensive, and the heads do NOT learn identical relationships (specialization emerges).

quiz.q: In the decoder's cross-attention sub-layer, which tensors come from the decoder and which from the encoder?
options: All of Q, K, and V come from the decoder's own previous layer
The encoder supplies the queries; the decoder supplies the keys and values
All of Q, K, and V come from the encoder output
The decoder supplies the queries; the encoder output supplies the keys and values
quiz.explanation: Cross-attention is 'the decoder asks questions, the encoder has the answers': queries come from the decoder, keys and values from the encoder output. If all three came from the decoder it would just be self-attention; reversing the roles would let the encoder query the decoder, which is not how generation works.

quiz.q: Why are sinusoidal positional encodings preferred over simply appending each token's integer index?
options: They are the only representation that softmax is able to process
They give each position a unique multi-frequency fingerprint and generalize to sequences longer than those seen in training
They make self-attention permutation-invariant
They remove the need for a separate value projection
quiz.explanation: Sin/cos at many frequencies give every position a distinct signature, make nearby positions similar, and (being defined for any input) extend to unseen lengths. Softmax can process any real numbers, so that is false; positional encoding REMOVES permutation-invariance rather than creating it; and it has nothing to do with the value projection.

descriptive.q: Derive, with a variance argument, why scaled dot-product attention divides the scores by the square root of d_k. What goes wrong if we skip this scaling, and which earlier technique is it analogous to?
descriptive.answer:
**The problem.** A query $q$ and key $k$ are vectors of length $d_k$. Their similarity is the dot product $q \cdot k = \sum_{j=1}^{d_k} q_j k_j$ -- a sum of $d_k$ products.

**Variance derivation.** Assume each component $q_j$ and $k_j$ is roughly independent with mean 0 and variance 1 (a standard initialization assumption). Then each product $q_j k_j$ has mean 0 and variance about 1, and summing $d_k$ of them gives:

$$\text{Var}(q \cdot k) \approx \sum_{j=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_j k_j) \approx d_k$$

So the typical magnitude of a score grows like $\sqrt{d_k}$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ rescales the variance back to about 1:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) \approx \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1$$

(Here $d_k$ is the per-head dimension, $q_j, k_j$ are individual components, and Var means statistical variance -- the spread of the values.)

**What breaks without scaling.** Large-magnitude scores push the softmax into a **saturated, peaked** regime: almost all weight lands on one token and the rest get nearly 0.

> [!WARNING]
> In the saturated region the softmax's gradient is almost zero, so backpropagation gets a vanishing signal and the attention weights essentially stop learning. The bigger $d_k$ is, the worse this gets.

**Analogy.** This is the **same principle as Xavier/Glorot initialization**: deliberately control the scale of activations so that signals -- and therefore gradients -- stay in a healthy range, neither exploding nor vanishing. Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is variance control for the attention logits.

descriptive.q: Compare the computational complexity of a self-attention layer with that of a recurrent layer for a sequence of length n and model dimension d. When is each cheaper, and why does the Transformer still win in practice on GPUs?
descriptive.answer:
**The two costs.**

| Layer type | Per-layer complexity | Sequential operations |
|---|---|---|
| Self-attention | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ (one parallel step) |
| Recurrent (RNN/LSTM) | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n)$ (n sequential steps) |

- **Self-attention: $O(n^2 d)$.** Every one of $n$ tokens compares against all $n$ tokens ($n^2$ pairs), and each comparison/aggregation runs over $d$ dimensions.
- **Recurrent: $O(n d^2)$.** There are $n$ time steps, and each step does a dense matrix multiply costing $d^2$.

(Here $n$ is sequence length, $d$ is the model/hidden dimension.)

**Which is cheaper?**

> [!KEY]
> Compare $n^2 d$ vs $n d^2$, i.e. $n$ vs $d$. Self-attention is **cheaper when $n < d$** (short sequences, wide models) and **more expensive when $n > d$** (very long sequences).

**Why the Transformer wins anyway.** Raw FLOP count is not the whole story -- **parallelism** is. The recurrent layer must run its $n$ steps *one after another* (the relay-race problem), which a GPU cannot parallelize. Self-attention does all its work in **one parallel matmul step** ($O(1)$ sequential depth), which is exactly what GPUs are built to accelerate.

> [!INTUITION]
> The Transformer trades **more memory** ($O(n^2)$ attention matrix) for **far less wall-clock time** by avoiding the sequential bottleneck. For typical lengths (e.g. $n = 512$ to $2048$) this trade is a huge win; only at very long lengths ($n \gtrsim 10000$) does the $O(n^2)$ term hurt enough to motivate 'efficient attention' research.

descriptive.q: Explain why the decoder uses masked self-attention while the encoder does not. Mechanically, what does masking do, and what would go wrong if it were omitted during training?
descriptive.answer:
**The asymmetry.** The **encoder** reads the entire input sentence at once -- there is nothing to hide, so every token may attend to every other token. The **decoder** generates output **autoregressively**, one token at a time, where each new token is predicted from the tokens already produced.

> [!KEY]
> During generation, position 5 only knows positions 1-4. Letting it attend to position 6 would be **looking at the answer** -- a token that does not exist yet at inference time.

**Mechanics of masking.** Masking is applied to the score matrix **before softmax**. For any pair $(i, j)$ where $j > i$ (a future position), we set the score to $-\infty$:

$$\text{score}_{ij} \leftarrow -\infty \quad \text{for } j > i$$

Then, because

$$\mathrm{softmax}(-\infty) = 0,$$

every future position receives **exactly zero attention weight**. Token $i$'s output becomes a weighted average over positions $1 \dots i$ only. (Here $i$ is the query position, $j$ the key position.)

**What breaks without masking?**

> [!WARNING]
> During **training** we feed the whole target sentence in parallel (teacher forcing). Without a mask, position $i$ could attend to the true future tokens it is supposed to predict -- it would simply **copy the answer**. The model would reach near-perfect training loss but **completely fail at inference**, where future tokens are unavailable. Masking forces the model to learn from the past only, matching train-time and test-time conditions.

So: encoder = no mask (full context allowed); decoder = causal mask (future hidden), implemented as $-\infty$ scores before softmax.

descriptive.q: Walk through scaled dot-product attention step by step for a 3-token sequence with d_k = 4 (and d_v = 4), giving the matrix shapes at each stage. Then state what the final output represents.
descriptive.answer:
We have $n = 3$ tokens, key/query dimension $d_k = 4$, value dimension $d_v = 4$.

**Setup.** Stack the per-token vectors into matrices:
- $Q$: shape $3 \times 4$ (one query row per token)
- $K$: shape $3 \times 4$
- $V$: shape $3 \times 4$

**Step 1 -- Scores.** Multiply queries by transposed keys:

$$S = QK^{\top}, \qquad (3 \times 4)(4 \times 3) = 3 \times 3$$

$S_{ij}$ is the dot-product similarity between token $i$'s query and token $j$'s key. (Shape $3\times3$ = one score for every ordered token pair.)

**Step 2 -- Scale.** Divide every entry by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{4} = 2$:

$$S' = \frac{S}{2}$$

Same $3 \times 3$ shape; this keeps the variance near 1 so softmax does not saturate.

**Step 3 -- Softmax.** Apply softmax to **each row** of $S'$:

$$A = \mathrm{softmax}_{\text{row}}(S')$$

$A$ is still $3 \times 3$, but now every row is non-negative and sums to 1 -- these are the **attention weights**.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> If row 1 of $A$ is $[0.1,\ 0.7,\ 0.2]$, token 1 draws 10% of its new content from token 1, 70% from token 2, and 20% from token 3.

**Step 4 -- Weighted sum.** Multiply the weights by the values:

$$\text{Output} = A\,V, \qquad (3 \times 3)(3 \times 4) = 3 \times 4$$

**What it represents.** The output is $3 \times 4$ -- **one new vector per token, same shape as the input**. Each output vector is a relevance-weighted blend of all the value vectors, so every token has now absorbed context from the tokens it attends to.

| Stage | Operation | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Scores | $QK^{\top}$ | $3 \times 3$ |
| Scale | $/\sqrt{4}$ | $3 \times 3$ |
| Softmax | row-wise | $3 \times 3$ |
| Output | $AV$ | $3 \times 4$ |

descriptive.q: Why is multi-head attention more powerful than a single large attention head? Explain what different heads learn and exactly how the split, concatenate, and project steps keep the cost roughly constant.
descriptive.answer:
**The limitation of one head.** A single attention head produces **one** set of attention weights, so it can capture essentially **one type of relationship** at a time. But a sentence has many simultaneous relationships -- syntax, semantics, coreference, proximity -- and one head cannot represent all of them at once.

**The multi-head idea.** Run $h$ heads **in parallel**, each with its own $W_Q, W_K, W_V$, so each head looks at a **different representation subspace** of the input. Specialization is **learned, not programmed**; in practice heads end up doing things like:

- Head 1: subject-verb agreement (syntax)
- Head 2: adjective-noun proximity
- Head 3: pronoun -> antecedent (coreference)
- Head 4: punctuation / clause boundaries

> [!ANALOGY]
> Like several reviewers reading the same essay -- one checks grammar, one checks logic, one checks style -- then you merge their notes.

**The mechanics (and why cost stays flat).**

1. **Split.** Instead of one head of width $d_{\text{model}}$, use $h$ heads each of width $d_k = d_{\text{model}}/h$.
2. **Attend.** Each head runs scaled dot-product attention in its $d_k$-dim subspace, producing a $d_k$-wide output per token.
3. **Concatenate.** Glue the $h$ head outputs side by side: $h \times d_k = d_{\text{model}}$ wide again.
4. **Project.** Multiply by output matrix $W_O$ to mix information across heads and return to $d_{\text{model}}$.

> [!KEY]
> Because each head is $1/h$ as wide, $h$ small heads cost about the same as one full $d_{\text{model}}$ head. Example: $d_{\text{model}}=512$, $h=8$ gives $d_k=64$, and $8 \times 64 = 512$. **Same parameter budget, 8 different perspectives.**

> [!WARNING]
> Do not assume multi-head means 8x the compute. The splitting is what keeps the total cost roughly constant; the payoff is **diversity of relationships**, not extra width.

descriptive.q: What exactly happens if positional encoding is removed from a Transformer trained on language? Contrast this with how an RNN obtains position information, and explain why sinusoidal encodings can generalize to unseen sequence lengths.
descriptive.answer:
**Removing positional encoding.** Self-attention is **permutation-invariant**: it treats the input as an unordered **set** of tokens. With no position signal, reordering the words does not change any computed representation.

> [!KEY]
> Without positional encoding the model becomes a glorified **bag of words**: *"Dog bites man"* and *"Man bites dog"* produce identical token representations, so the model literally cannot tell who is biting whom. Word order -- which carries much of language's meaning -- is lost.

**Why RNNs do not need this.** An RNN consumes tokens **one at a time** in order, carrying a hidden state forward. Position is baked in *for free* by the sequential processing: the 5th token is, by construction, processed after the 4th. A Transformer sees everything at once, so it must **explicitly inject** order.

**How the fix works.** We **add** a position vector to each token embedding:

$$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d}}\right), \qquad PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{\,2i/d}}\right)$$

where $pos$ is the token position, $i$ indexes the dimension, and $d$ is the encoding length. Early dimensions use **low-frequency** waves; later dimensions use **high-frequency** waves.

**Why it generalizes to longer sequences.**

- **Unique fingerprint.** The combination of many frequencies gives each position a distinct signature, like a unique page number.
- **Smooth relative structure.** Nearby positions have similar encodings (high dot product), distant ones differ, so the model can read **relative** distances.
- **Defined everywhere.** $\sin$ and $\cos$ are defined for *any* real input, so positions **beyond the training range** still receive sensible, in-distribution encodings -- unlike a fixed learned lookup table that simply has no entry for an unseen position.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Like numbering pages in a shuffled deck: without the numbers you cannot reconstruct the order; the sin/cos vector is the page number stamped on each position.

topics.title: BERT & GPT
topics.summary: This session shows how the Transformer became the backbone of modern NLP through the pre-train-once, fine-tune-cheaply paradigm, contrasting BERT (bidirectional, encoder-only, trained by masked language modeling, best for understanding) with GPT (left-to-right, decoder-only, trained by next-token prediction, best for generation). It also covers subword tokenization (BPE and WordPiece), why tokenization quietly determines what a model can do, and practical issues like the O(n^2) cost of attention and how to choose a model for a task.

notes.heading: The Pre-training Revolution: Learn Language Once, Specialize Cheaply
notes.body:
Before 2018, almost every NLP task built its own model **from scratch** — a sentiment model, a separate named-entity model, a separate question-answering model — each needing its own pile of hand-labeled data. After 2018 the field flipped to a two-stage recipe: **pre-train one giant model on oceans of unlabeled text, then fine-tune it cheaply for each task.** The lecture calls this as big a moment for language as ImageNet was for vision.

> [!INTUITION] The trick is to learn *language itself* first (grammar, word meanings, world facts) from free text, and only afterwards teach the model your specific job. The hard, expensive part (understanding English) is done once and reused everywhere.

Why does this work? Two kinds of data behave very differently:

| Data type | Cost | Size | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Labeled** | Expensive (humans annotate) | Small (thousands) | "this review is positive" |
| **Unlabeled** | Free (the internet) | Massive (billions of words) | raw Wikipedia text |

The key insight both Google and OpenAI had at the same time: **if you can fill in blanks correctly, you must understand language.** That single self-supervised idea turns the free, unlabeled internet into a teacher — no human annotation needed.

> [!ANALOGY] Pre-training is **medical school**: 8 years, expensive, done once. Fine-tuning is **residency**: about a year, cheap, repeated per specialty. You would never send someone through all of medical school again just to retrain them as a cardiologist.

[[fig:pretrain-finetune]]

> [!KEY] One expensive pre-training run produces a model that knows language; many cheap fine-tunings adapt it to specific tasks. With ~1000 labeled examples and 3 epochs you can hit 85%+ accuracy — training from scratch would need roughly 100x more data.

> [!EXAM] Expect a "from scratch vs. fine-tune" scenario (e.g. 500 customer emails). The right answer is almost always **fine-tune a pre-trained model**: too few labels to learn language from zero, and the pre-trained model already knows it.

notes.heading: BERT: Reading in Both Directions to Understand
notes.body:
**BERT** stands for **Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers.** It uses *only the encoder* half of the Transformer (no decoder), and its superpower is reading a word using context from **both sides at once** — everything to the left *and* everything to the right.

> [!INTUITION] To truly understand a word you usually need what comes after it, not just before. Reading left-to-right only is like covering the second half of every sentence with your hand.

Consider the word "bank":

- "The bank was **flooded**." -> a *river* bank (the clue "flooded" is on the **right**).
- "The bank approved the **loan**." -> a *financial* bank (the clue "loan" is on the **right**).

In both cases the disambiguating word sits to the right of "bank." A left-to-right model that has only read "The bank was..." cannot yet tell which meaning is intended. BERT, reading both directions simultaneously, can.

[[fig:bert-vs-gpt]]

> [!KEY] BERT = an **open-book exam**: it can look at the whole page (all words, both directions) before deciding what each word means. This is why BERT is the tool of choice for *understanding* tasks: classification, named-entity recognition (NER), and question answering.

> [!WARNING] "Bidirectional" does **not** mean BERT reads the sentence twice (once forward, once backward) like a bidirectional RNN that concatenates two passes. In BERT, a single self-attention layer lets every word attend to every other word — both sides — in **one** simultaneous operation. That very property is also exactly why BERT cannot generate text (next section's punchline).

notes.heading: Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and the 15% Rule
notes.body:
How do you train a bidirectional model without giving it the answer? You can't ask it to "predict the next word" — it can already see the next word. BERT's solution is **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)**: hide some words and ask the model to fill in the blanks using all surrounding context.

> [!ANALOGY] MLM is a **fill-in-the-blank exam**: "The cute little [MASK] sat on the [MASK]." To answer "cat" and "mat" you must read the *entire* sentence — which forces bidirectional understanding.

**Procedure:** randomly select **15%** of tokens; the model must predict those hidden tokens from context. But there is a subtle twist in *how* those 15% are corrupted:

| Treatment of the 15% | Proportion | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Replace with `[MASK]` | 80% | The main training signal |
| Replace with a **random** word | 10% | Stops the model from blindly trusting visible tokens |
| **Keep unchanged** | 10% | Teaches that *any* token could secretly be the target |

> [!KEY] Why not mask 100%? Because **the `[MASK]` token never appears at fine-tuning time.** If the model only ever saw `[MASK]`, it would be confused by real, clean sentences later. Mixing in random and unchanged tokens closes this **pre-training / fine-tuning mismatch** and forces BERT to build a good representation of *every* token, not just masked ones.

> [!MATH] The training loss is cross-entropy over the masked positions: $L=-\sum_{i\in M}\log p_\theta(x_i\mid x_{\setminus M})$. In words: for each masked position $i$ in the masked set $M$, take the probability $p_\theta$ the model assigns to the *true* word $x_i$ given all the *unmasked* tokens $x_{\setminus M}$, take its log, and sum the negatives. Higher probability on the correct word -> lower loss.

BERT was also pre-trained with **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** — guessing whether sentence B really follows sentence A — but later work (RoBERTa) showed NSP adds little and dropped it. Treat NSP as historical detail, MLM as the core idea.

> [!EXAM] A classic question: "Why mask randomly instead of always masking the *last* word?" Answer: always-masking-the-last-word is just left-to-right prediction — that turns BERT into GPT and throws away the right-side context that makes BERT bidirectional.

notes.heading: GPT: Predicting the Next Word, One at a Time
notes.body:
**GPT** stands for **Generative Pre-trained Transformer.** It uses *only the decoder* half of the Transformer and is **autoregressive**: it predicts the **next token** given all previous tokens, then appends that token and repeats.

> [!ANALOGY] GPT is your phone's **autocomplete**, scaled up enormously. "The capital of France is ___" -> "Paris." "GPT wears blinders — it can only look at what came before."

The mechanism that enforces "look only backward" is the **causal mask**: token at position $i$ is allowed to attend only to positions $1\ldots i$, never to the future.

> [!MATH] Causal masking means: for query position $i$, attention weights to any position $j>i$ are forced to $0$ (implemented by adding $-\infty$ to those scores before softmax, so $\text{softmax}$ sends them to $0$). In words: each word can borrow information from earlier words, but the door to future words is bolted shut.

**Training vs. generation:**

- **Training** uses *teacher forcing*: at every position the model predicts the next real token in parallel, and is corrected against the ground-truth continuation.
- **Generation** is sequential: (1) predict the next-token probability distribution, (2) **sample** a token from it, (3) append it, (4) repeat until a stop token. Every word ChatGPT emits is produced this way — one token at a time.

How "adventurous" the sampling is gets controlled by knobs:

- **Temperature** $T$: scales the logits before softmax, $\text{softmax}(z_i/T)$. Here $z_i$ is the raw score for token $i$ and $T$ is the temperature. **Low $T$ (~0.1)** sharpens the distribution -> deterministic, safe; **high $T$ (~1.5)** flattens it -> creative, risky.
- **Top-k / Top-p**: only sample from the most likely candidates (top $k$ tokens, or the smallest set whose probabilities sum to $p$) — this prunes the long tail of nonsense words.

> [!KEY] GPT = **writing an essay**: it commits to one word, then the next, always forward. That left-to-right discipline is what makes it a generator — and it is enforced purely by the causal attention mask.

notes.heading: Scaling, Emergent Abilities, and Few-Shot Learning
notes.body:
GPT's headline story is **scale**. As parameters grew, the model didn't just get a bit better — it gained abilities **nobody explicitly trained**.

| Model | Parameters | Key achievement |
|---|---|---|
| GPT-1 (2018) | 117M | Proved the concept |
| GPT-2 (2019) | 1.5B | "Too dangerous to release" |
| GPT-3 (2020) | 175B | Few-shot learning emerges |
| GPT-4 (2023) | ~1.7T (est.) | Multimodal, powers ChatGPT |

> [!INTUITION] **Emergent abilities** = skills that appear suddenly at scale. Nobody programmed GPT-3 to do arithmetic or translate languages; it picked them up as a side effect of getting very good at predicting the next word on huge, varied text.

The most surprising emergent skill is **few-shot (in-context) learning**: you give a few examples *in the prompt* and the model follows the pattern — **with no weight updates at all.**

- **Zero-shot:** "Translate English to French: Hello ->"
- **One-shot:** "Hello -> Bonjour. Goodbye ->"
- **Few-shot:** 5 examples, then a new query.

> [!KEY] In-context learning means the model adapts purely from the prompt — its weights never change. This is why **prompt engineering** matters: you are "programming" the model with examples instead of gradient steps.

> [!WARNING] Don't confuse **few-shot / in-context learning** (no training, examples live only in the prompt, forgotten after the call) with **fine-tuning** (real gradient updates that permanently change the weights). They feel similar from the outside but are mechanically opposite.

> [!EXAM] If a question describes a model that translates from 2-3 prompt examples and asks what's surprising, the answer is: it's **few-shot / in-context learning**, surprising because **no weight updates** occur — the adaptation is pure pattern recognition from context.

notes.heading: BERT vs. GPT: Same Transformer, Different Mask
notes.body:
Here is the punchline of the whole session: **BERT and GPT are both Transformers. The single architectural difference between them is the attention mask.**

| | BERT | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Encoder-only | Decoder-only |
| Context | Bidirectional (see everything) | Left-to-right (see the past) |
| Training | MLM (fill in blanks) | Next-token prediction |
| Attention mask | Full (every token sees all) | Causal (token $i$ sees $1\ldots i$) |
| Strength | Understanding | Generation |
| Typical uses | Classification, NER, QA | Chatbots, writing, code |

[[fig:attention-masks]]

> [!INTUITION] Picture the attention grid: which words is each word allowed to look at? BERT's grid is **fully filled in** (look anywhere). GPT's grid is **lower-triangular** (look only at yourself and earlier words). Flip that one mask and an "understander" becomes a "generator."

This also explains, cleanly, **why BERT cannot generate text.** To produce word 5 autoregressively you must *not* yet know words 6, 7, 8. But BERT sees all positions at once, so to "generate" word 5 it would illegally peek at words 6+. There is **no causal ordering**, so there is no well-defined notion of "the next word."

> [!WARNING] A tempting wrong answer is "BERT can't generate because it has no decoder." The deeper, correct reason is the **mask / bidirectionality**: BERT's full attention has no left-to-right order to generate along. Architecture labels (encoder vs. decoder) are really just shorthand for which mask is used.

> [!KEY] Remember the one-liner: **"Same Transformer, different attention masks. BERT understands; GPT generates."** That single sentence answers most BERT-vs-GPT exam questions.

notes.heading: Tokenization: Turning Text into Numbers (BPE & WordPiece)
notes.body:
Models eat **numbers**, not letters. **Tokenization** is the step that chops raw text into pieces ("tokens") and maps each to an integer ID. There are three strategies, and the winner is a compromise:

| Strategy | "unhappiness" becomes | Problem |
|---|---|---|
| **Word-level** | 1 token | Vocabulary *explodes* (every word form needs a slot; rare words become "unknown") |
| **Character-level** | 11 tokens (u-n-h-a-p-...) | Sequences get *far too long* |
| **Subword (BPE/WordPiece)** | "un" + "happi" + "ness" | **Best of both worlds** |

> [!INTUITION] Subword tokenization is the **Goldilocks** solution: keep common words whole, but split rare words into reusable pieces. Fragments like "un-" and "-ness" show up in hundreds of words, so a modest vocabulary covers almost everything — and truly novel words never become a useless "unknown" token.

**Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)** — used by GPT-2/3/4 (~50K vocab) — builds the vocabulary by *greedily merging the most frequent adjacent pair*:

1. Start with a character vocabulary.
2. Count all adjacent character pairs in the corpus.
3. **Merge the single most frequent pair** into a new token.
4. Repeat $N$ times, where $N=$ (desired vocab size) $-$ (initial characters).

[[fig:bpe-tokenization]]

> [!EXAMPLE] Training toward the word "lower": start from `l, o, w, e, r`. If `l`+`o` is the most frequent pair, merge -> `lo, w, e, r`. Next `lo`+`w` -> `low, e, r`. Then `low`+`e`... and so on, until common chunks like "low" and full words like "lower" become single tokens. Common words end up whole; rare words stay split into frequent subwords.

**WordPiece** (BERT's tokenizer, ~30K vocab) is the same idea with one change: instead of merging the *most frequent* pair, it merges the pair that most increases the **likelihood** of the training data. It marks continuations with a `##` prefix: "playing" -> ["play", "##ing"]. In practice BPE and WordPiece give very similar results.

> [!KEY] BPE = **frequency-based** merging; WordPiece = **likelihood-based** merging. Different algorithm, same goal: subword tokens. Both keep common words whole and break rare words into reusable pieces.

> [!EXAM] Be ready to state the *exact* difference between BPE and WordPiece (frequency vs. likelihood merging) and to recognize the `##` continuation marker as a WordPiece signature.

notes.heading: Practical Considerations: Cost, Context, and Choosing a Model
notes.body:
Two practical realities dominate how these models are used.

**1. Tokenization secretly decides capability.** Because numbers are tokenized inconsistently, the model often *cannot see individual digits*. "427" might become ["42", "7"]; "15213" might become ["152", "13"]. The model operates on these arbitrary chunks, not on the digits 1-5-2-1-3 — which is exactly why LLMs hallucinate on simple arithmetic. The same effect makes other languages costlier: the same sentence in Japanese can use ~3x more tokens than English, meaning higher API cost and shorter effective context.

> [!KEY] "The tokenizer is the most overlooked part of the system — but it determines what the model can even *see*." Many "reasoning" failures are really tokenization failures.

**2. Attention is quadratic.** Self-attention compares every token with every other token, so it costs $O(n^2)$ in memory and compute, where $n$ is the sequence length.

> [!MATH] The score matrix $QK^\top$ has shape $n\times n$ — one entry per pair of tokens. Here $Q$ is the queries and $K$ the keys, both with one row per token; $K^\top$ is $K$ transposed. Double the length to $2n$ and you get a $2n\times 2n=4n^2$ matrix: **2x longer -> 4x the attention memory.** This is also why API pricing (per token) means long prompts cost quadratically more to *process*.

| Model | Max tokens |
|---|---|
| BERT | 512 |
| GPT-3 | 2,048 |
| GPT-4 | 128,000 |

Mitigations exist at every level: **FlashAttention** (smarter engineering), **sparse attention** (algorithmic), and **linear attention** (research).

**Choosing a model — decision guide:**

| Task | Model family |
|---|---|
| Classification / NER / QA | BERT (RoBERTa, DeBERTa) |
| Generation / dialogue | GPT family |
| Small dataset | Always **fine-tune**, never from scratch |
| Domain-specific | BioBERT, CodeBERT, etc. |
| Budget-constrained | DistilBERT (66M params, ~97% of the quality) |

> [!INTUITION] The **full modern NLP pipeline** is always the same: Raw text -> Tokenizer -> Token IDs -> Embeddings -> Transformer layers -> Task head -> Output. Whether you use BERT or GPT, only the Transformer variant and the pre-training objective change; the surrounding plumbing is identical.

> [!EXAM] Map task to model on sight: *understanding* (classify/tag/extract spans) -> BERT-family; *generation* (write/chat/code) -> GPT-family; *small labeled set* -> fine-tune a pre-trained model.

quiz.q: BERT and GPT are both Transformers of similar size, yet one understands and the other generates. What is the single architectural difference responsible for this split?
