options: BERT has more layers while GPT has more attention heads
The attention mask: BERT uses full (bidirectional) attention, GPT uses a causal (left-to-right) mask
BERT uses self-attention while GPT uses cross-attention
GPT uses positional encodings and BERT does not
quiz.explanation: The lecture's central point: 'Same Transformer, the only difference is the attention mask.' BERT lets every token attend to all others (full attention); GPT forbids attending to future tokens (causal mask), which creates the left-to-right order needed to generate. Layer/head counts differ between specific models but are not the defining difference, and both use positional encodings, so those options miss the core idea.

quiz.q: Why is BERT, despite 'understanding' language extremely well, unable to generate text autoregressively?
options: It sees all positions at once with no causal ordering, so producing word 5 would require illegally peeking at words 6+
It was never trained on enough text to learn grammar for generation
It is too small to store enough vocabulary to produce fluent sentences
Its softmax output layer can only emit class labels, not vocabulary tokens
quiz.explanation: BERT's bidirectional (full) attention means every position can see every other position simultaneously. Autoregressive generation requires that word 5 be produced without yet knowing words 6+, which BERT's mask does not enforce, so there is no well-defined 'next word'. The size and training-data options are wrong (BERT is large and well-trained); the 'no decoder / only class labels' framing is the tempting trap, but the real reason is the absence of causal ordering, not the output layer.

quiz.q: In BERT's MLM, of the 15% of tokens chosen for prediction, only 80% are actually replaced with [MASK] (10% become a random word, 10% are left unchanged). Why not replace 100% with [MASK]?
options: Masking 100% would make pre-training take far longer to converge
100% masking would exceed the model's maximum sequence length
The [MASK] token never appears during fine-tuning, so 100% masking would create a pre-training/fine-tuning mismatch
Leaving some tokens visible is required so the cross-entropy loss stays positive
quiz.explanation: At fine-tuning and inference time, real sentences contain no [MASK] tokens. If BERT only ever saw [MASK] during pre-training, it would be poorly calibrated for clean inputs later, a train/test mismatch. The 10% random and 10% unchanged tokens force it to build good representations for every token. Convergence speed, sequence length, and 'keeping loss positive' are not the stated reasons.

quiz.q: Consider 'The bank was flooded' vs. 'The bank approved the loan.' Why does correctly interpreting 'bank' demonstrate the need for bidirectional context?
options: The word 'The' on the left already fixes the meaning before 'bank' is read
'bank' is a rare word that only subword tokenization can handle
Both sentences require translating into another language to resolve meaning
The disambiguating word ('flooded' / 'loan') appears to the RIGHT of 'bank', so left-to-right context alone is insufficient
quiz.explanation: In both sentences the clue that fixes the meaning of 'bank' (flooded -> river, loan -> financial) comes AFTER it. A left-to-right model reading only 'The bank was...' cannot yet disambiguate; BERT's bidirectional attention uses the right-hand context. Tokenization and translation are unrelated, and 'The' carries no disambiguating signal.

quiz.q: Self-attention has O(n^2) memory cost in sequence length n. If you double the input length from n to 2n, the attention memory grows by what factor, and why?
options: 2x, because each token simply gets one more neighbor to attend to
Unchanged, because attention memory depends only on the hidden size, not on n
8x, because attention is cubic in sequence length
4x, because the QK^T score matrix is n-by-n, so 2n-by-2n has 4n^2 entries
quiz.explanation: Attention computes a score between every pair of tokens, forming an n-by-n matrix (QK^T). Doubling n gives a 2n-by-2n = 4n^2 matrix, a 4x increase, hence quadratic scaling. It is not linear (2x), not cubic (8x), and it does depend on n, so the 'unchanged' option is wrong. This is also why long prompts cost quadratically more to process.

quiz.q: GPT-3 can translate languages given just 2-3 examples written into the prompt, producing correct outputs immediately. What is this ability called, and what makes it notable?
options: Few-shot (in-context) learning, notable because there are NO weight updates, the model adapts purely from prompt examples
Fine-tuning, notable because only the task head is retrained
Transfer learning, notable because the early layers are reused
Data augmentation, notable because the examples expand the training set
quiz.explanation: Providing examples in the prompt and having the model follow the pattern with no gradient steps is few-shot / in-context learning. The surprising part is that no weights change, adaptation is pure pattern recognition from context. Fine-tuning DOES update weights (so it is the tempting wrong choice but mechanically opposite); transfer learning and data augmentation describe other things entirely.

quiz.q: An LLM confidently gives wrong answers to simple multi-digit arithmetic. Which explanation best matches the tokenization argument from the lecture?
options: The model lacks a calculator module, so it guesses randomly
Floating-point rounding inside the network corrupts integer math
Arithmetic requires bidirectional attention, which generative models lack
Numbers are tokenized inconsistently (e.g. '15213' -> '152' + '13'), so the model never sees individual digits to operate on
quiz.explanation: Subword tokenizers split numbers into arbitrary chunks ('427' -> '42'+'7'), so the model operates on inconsistent pieces rather than the underlying digits, breaking digit-by-digit arithmetic. It is a representation/visibility problem, not a missing calculator, not about attention direction, and not floating-point rounding.

quiz.q: In Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), how is the vocabulary actually built from a starting character set?
options: It splits every word into characters and keeps them as the final vocabulary
It repeatedly finds the MOST FREQUENT adjacent pair of tokens and merges it into a new token, for a fixed number of merges
It merges the pair that most increases the likelihood of the training corpus
It assigns each unique word in the corpus its own token until the vocabulary is full
quiz.explanation: BPE starts from characters, counts adjacent pairs, merges the single most frequent pair, and repeats N times (N = desired vocab size minus initial characters). 'Likelihood-based merging' describes WordPiece, not BPE, so it is the most tempting distractor. Keeping characters or whole words describes character-level and word-level tokenization, which BPE is designed to avoid.

quiz.q: BERT uses WordPiece while GPT uses BPE. What is the actual algorithmic difference between them?
options: WordPiece works at the character level while BPE works at the word level
WordPiece merges pairs by training-data likelihood; BPE merges by raw frequency, though both produce subword tokens
WordPiece produces whole words only; BPE produces single characters only
WordPiece is bidirectional and BPE is left-to-right, matching their parent models
quiz.explanation: Both are subword algorithms with the same goal; the difference is the merge criterion: BPE picks the most frequent adjacent pair, WordPiece picks the pair that most increases corpus likelihood (and marks continuations with '##'). In practice results are similar. The other options confuse subword tokenization with character/word level or wrongly attribute attention direction to a tokenizer.

quiz.q: When sampling from GPT with the softmax-temperature formula softmax(z_i / T), what is the effect of choosing a LOW temperature such as T = 0.1?
options: It flattens the distribution, making outputs more random and creative
It disables sampling entirely so the model outputs the [MASK] token
It sharpens the distribution toward the highest-probability tokens, making outputs more deterministic and safe
It increases the vocabulary size available for sampling
quiz.explanation: Dividing the logits z_i by a small T magnifies their differences before softmax, peaking the distribution on the top token(s), so generation is more deterministic and 'safe'. High T (e.g. 1.5) flattens the distribution for creative, riskier output, which is the opposite, the tempting wrong answer. Temperature does not emit [MASK] (that is BERT) or change vocabulary size.

quiz.q: You have 500 labeled customer-support emails and need an urgent/normal classifier. Train a Transformer from scratch or fine-tune pre-trained BERT, and why?
options: Fine-tune BERT, because 500 labels are far too few to learn language from zero, while BERT already knows language and only needs to learn the task
Train from scratch, so the model specializes only on your domain vocabulary
Train from scratch, because fine-tuning would overwrite BERT's useful knowledge
Neither, 500 examples is too few for any Transformer-based approach to work
quiz.explanation: With only 500 labels, training from scratch would overfit badly, there is nowhere near enough data to learn grammar and meaning. Fine-tuning starts from BERT's pre-trained language knowledge and just teaches the task (a few epochs, small lr), typically reaching 85%+ accuracy. Fine-tuning adapts rather than 'overwrites' knowledge, and small data is exactly the case where fine-tuning shines.

quiz.q: Why is subword tokenization (BPE/WordPiece) generally preferred over pure word-level tokenization for large language models?
options: Word-level tokenization makes sequences far too long to process
Subword tokenization lets the model see individual digits, fixing arithmetic
Word-level tokenization causes the vocabulary to explode and turns rare/unseen words into unknown tokens, whereas subwords reuse frequent pieces and handle novel words
Subword tokenization removes the need for embeddings
quiz.explanation: Word-level vocabularies must store every word form and still fail on unseen words (mapping them to UNK); subword units like 'un-' and '-ness' recur across many words, giving a compact vocabulary that gracefully splits rare words. Pure character-level (not word-level) is what makes sequences too long. Subwords actually worsen digit visibility, and embeddings are still required.

descriptive.q: Explain BERT's Masked Language Modeling objective in full: what is masked, the 80/10/10 split within the chosen 15%, the reason for each part, and why 15% (not 100%) of tokens are selected. Why does this objective force bidirectional understanding?
descriptive.answer:
**The objective.** MLM trains BERT by hiding some tokens and asking it to reconstruct them from the surrounding context on *both* sides. Concretely, ~**15%** of tokens in each sequence are selected for prediction.

**The 80/10/10 split** (of the selected 15%):

| Treatment | Share | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Replace with `[MASK]` | 80% | The main training signal, the model learns to infer hidden words |
| Replace with a **random** word | 10% | Stops the model from blindly trusting visible tokens; it must keep a real representation of every position |
| **Keep unchanged** | 10% | Teaches that any token, even an untouched one, could be the prediction target |

**Why not 100% `[MASK]`?** Because at fine-tuning and inference time, real text contains **no `[MASK]` tokens**. If BERT only ever saw `[MASK]` during pre-training, it would face a distribution it never trained on later, a **pre-training / fine-tuning mismatch**. The random and unchanged tokens make BERT build robust contextual representations for *all* tokens, not just masked slots.

> [!KEY] 100% masking would optimize for a situation (sentences full of [MASK]) that never occurs downstream.

**Why 15%?** It balances **enough training signal** (mask too few and the model barely learns per sentence) against **enough visible context** (mask too many and there isn't enough surrounding text to infer the answer).

**Why this forces bidirectional understanding.** To fill "The cute little [MASK] sat on the [MASK]" with "cat" and "mat", the model must read the *entire* sentence, words on the left and the right. Crucially, BERT must **not** be allowed to just predict the next word left-to-right; if you always masked the last word, the task would collapse into left-to-right prediction (i.e. GPT) and discard right-side context.

> [!MATH] Loss: $L=-\sum_{i\in M}\log p_\theta(x_i\mid x_{\setminus M})$ , the summed negative log-probability the model assigns to each true masked token $x_i$ (indices $i$ in masked set $M$) given the unmasked tokens $x_{\setminus M}$. Lower loss = higher probability on the correct word.

(Historical note: BERT also used **Next Sentence Prediction**, but RoBERTa showed it was largely unnecessary, so MLM is the core idea to remember.)

descriptive.q: Compare BERT and GPT across architecture, attention pattern, training objective, and typical use cases. Then justify the claim that 'the ONLY architectural difference is the attention mask.'
descriptive.answer:
**Side-by-side comparison:**

| Dimension | BERT | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Encoder-only | Decoder-only |
| Attention | Bidirectional (full) | Left-to-right (causal) |
| Training objective | Masked Language Modeling (fill blanks) | Next-token prediction (autoregressive) |
| Strength | Understanding | Generation |
| Typical uses | Classification, NER, QA, paraphrase | Chatbots, writing, code |

[[fig:attention-masks]]

**Why 'only the mask' is the real difference.** Both models are stacks of the same Transformer block (self-attention + feed-forward, residuals, layer norm). The thing that distinguishes an "encoder" from a "decoder" in this context is simply **which positions each token is allowed to attend to**:

- **BERT (full mask):** every token attends to every other token, both directions. The attention grid is fully filled. This makes it ideal for building a deep representation of an existing sentence, but provides no left-to-right order to generate along.
- **GPT (causal mask):** a token at position $i$ may attend only to positions $1\ldots i$. The grid is lower-triangular. Future positions are blocked (their scores set to $-\infty$ before softmax). This creates the strict ordering that lets the model generate one token at a time.

> [!KEY] Flip BERT's full mask to a causal mask and you have turned an "understander" into a "generator." The training objective then follows naturally: a causal model is trained to predict the next token; a bidirectional model is trained to fill masked blanks (it cannot do next-token prediction because it can already see the next token).

**Consequence for capability:** This is exactly why BERT cannot generate (no causal order -> no defined 'next word') and why GPT, though a strong generator, was not originally optimized for the deep bidirectional understanding that classification/NER benefit from.

descriptive.q: Walk through Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) step by step with a small worked example, then contrast it with WordPiece. Finally, explain why subword tokenization beats both word-level and character-level tokenization.
descriptive.answer:
**BPE algorithm:**

1. Start with a **character** vocabulary.
2. Count every **adjacent pair** of tokens in the corpus.
3. **Merge the single most frequent pair** into one new token.
4. Repeat $N$ times, where $N=$ (desired vocab size) $-$ (initial characters).

[[fig:bpe-tokenization]]

> [!EXAMPLE] Building toward the word "lower" from `l, o, w, e, r`:
> - `l`+`o` is most frequent -> merge to **`lo`**, giving `lo, w, e, r`.
> - `lo`+`w` next -> **`low`**, giving `low, e, r`.
> - `low`+`e` ... and so on.
>
> After enough merges, common chunks ("low") and frequent whole words become single tokens, while rare words remain split into frequent subwords. GPT-2/3/4 use BPE with ~50K vocab.

**BPE vs. WordPiece:**

| | BPE (GPT) | WordPiece (BERT) |
|---|---|---|
| Merge criterion | Most **frequent** adjacent pair | Pair that most increases corpus **likelihood** |
| Continuation marker | (none special) | `##`, e.g. "playing" -> ["play", "##ing"] |
| Vocab size | ~50K | ~30K |
| Result in practice | Subword tokens | Subword tokens, similar quality |

> [!KEY] Different algorithm, same idea, BPE merges by frequency, WordPiece merges by likelihood; both yield reusable subword pieces.

**Why subword beats the alternatives:**

- **Word-level** gives each whole word a token. The vocabulary *explodes* (every inflection/spelling needs a slot) and any unseen word becomes a useless `UNK`.
- **Character-level** never has unknown words, but sequences become *very long* (11 tokens for "unhappiness"), which is slow and wastes the O(n^2) attention budget.
- **Subword** is the Goldilocks middle: keep common words whole, split rare ones into frequent fragments ("un-", "-ness") that recur across hundreds of words. A modest vocabulary then covers nearly everything, and novel words degrade gracefully into known pieces instead of `UNK`.

descriptive.q: Self-attention is described as O(n^2). Derive why doubling the sequence length quadruples the attention memory, and discuss the practical consequences for context length, API pricing, and the engineering mitigations mentioned.
descriptive.answer:
**The derivation.** Self-attention compares **every token with every other token**. For a sequence of length $n$, the model forms a score matrix $QK^\top$:

> [!MATH] $Q$ (queries) and $K$ (keys) each have one row per token, so $QK^\top$ has shape $n\times n$, one similarity score for every ordered pair of tokens. Here $K^\top$ is the key matrix transposed. The number of entries is $n^2$.

Now double the length to $2n$. The score matrix becomes $2n\times 2n = 4n^2$ entries, i.e. **4x** the memory and compute of the $n^2$ case. Hence the famous **quadratic** ($O(n^2)$) scaling: *2x longer -> 4x cost*.

**Practical consequences:**

| Model | Max tokens |
|---|---|
| BERT | 512 |
| GPT-3 | 2,048 |
| GPT-4 | 128,000 |

- **Context length is precious.** Because cost grows quadratically, supporting longer contexts is expensive, which is why max-token limits exist and why long context windows were a hard-won achievement.
- **API pricing.** Pricing is per token, and longer prompts cost **quadratically** more to *process* (not just linearly), since the attention work itself grows with $n^2$. Multilingual users feel this twice: a Japanese sentence can use ~3x more tokens than English, so it is both costlier and consumes the context budget faster.

**Mitigations** (each at a different level):

- **FlashAttention** — an *engineering* optimization that computes exact attention with far less memory traffic.
- **Sparse attention** — an *algorithmic* change where each token attends to only a subset of positions.
- **Linear attention** — a *research* direction that approximates attention with sub-quadratic cost.

> [!KEY] The quadratic wall is the fundamental reason long-context LLMs are hard, and most of the listed tricks are attempts to dodge the $n^2$ term without losing too much quality.

descriptive.q: What is few-shot (in-context) learning, why was its emergence in GPT-3 surprising, and how does it differ mechanically from fine-tuning? Connect your answer to the idea of emergent abilities at scale.
descriptive.answer:
**What it is.** Few-shot (in-context) learning means you place a handful of examples **inside the prompt** and the model continues the pattern, with **no weight updates at all**:

- **Zero-shot:** "Translate English to French: Hello ->"
- **One-shot:** "Hello -> Bonjour. Goodbye ->"
- **Few-shot:** 5 examples, then a new query.

The model infers the task purely from the in-context examples and produces the right output.

**Why it was surprising.** Normally, teaching a model a new task means **training**, running gradient descent to change its parameters. GPT-3 showed that a sufficiently large model can adapt to a new task with **zero gradient steps**, the adaptation lives entirely in the forward pass over the prompt. Nobody explicitly trained GPT-3 to translate or do arithmetic; these **emergent abilities** appeared as a byproduct of scaling up next-token prediction on diverse text.

| | Few-shot / in-context | Fine-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| Weight updates? | **None** | Yes (gradient steps) |
| Where the "learning" lives | In the prompt (temporary) | In the weights (permanent) |
| Persistence | Forgotten after the call | Retained across all future inputs |
| Data needed | A few examples in the prompt | A labeled dataset + training run |

> [!WARNING] These feel similar from outside but are mechanically opposite. Few-shot learning changes nothing about the model, swap the prompt and the "skill" vanishes; fine-tuning permanently rewrites the weights.

**Connection to scaling.** Few-shot learning is the headline **emergent ability**: it did not really work at GPT-1/2 scale and became reliable around GPT-3's 175B parameters. This is why **prompt engineering** matters, with a large enough model you 'program' behavior by choosing examples in context rather than by training.

> [!KEY] In-context learning = adaptation with no weight change; its sudden appearance at scale is the canonical example of emergent abilities.

descriptive.q: A teammate proposes building three separate from-scratch models for sentiment, NER, and question answering on modest labeled datasets. Explain the pre-train/fine-tune paradigm as a better alternative, why it works, and how you would choose the right model family for each task.
descriptive.answer:
**The better alternative: pre-train once, fine-tune many times.** Instead of three expensive from-scratch models, pre-train (or simply download) **one** model that has already learned language from massive unlabeled text, then attach a small **task head** and fine-tune it briefly on each labeled dataset.

[[fig:pretrain-finetune]]

> [!ANALOGY] Pre-training is **medical school** (long, expensive, done once); each fine-tuning is a **residency** (short, cheap, per specialty). You don't repeat medical school to become a cardiologist.

**Why it works.** Language knowledge, grammar, word meanings, world facts, is **reusable across tasks**. Self-supervised pre-training (MLM for BERT, next-token prediction for GPT) extracts this knowledge from free internet text without human labels. Fine-tuning then only has to learn the *task-specific* mapping on top of an already-capable representation.

> [!KEY] One expensive pre-training + cheap task adaptation. With ~1000 labeled examples and ~3 epochs you can reach 85%+ accuracy; from scratch you'd need roughly 100x more data and would likely overfit on a modest dataset.

**Choosing the model family per task:**

| Task | Model family | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Sentiment** (classification) | BERT-family (RoBERTa/DeBERTa) | Understanding task; `[CLS]` -> linear -> softmax |
| **NER** (tag each token) | BERT-family | Bidirectional context helps label each token; per-token head |
| **Question answering** (find a span) | BERT-family | Predict start/end positions over the passage |

All three are *understanding* tasks, so a bidirectional encoder (BERT) is the natural fit; you would reserve the **GPT family** for *generation/dialogue*. Additional practical levers from the lecture:

- **Small dataset** -> always fine-tune, never from scratch.
- **Domain-specific** text -> a specialized variant (BioBERT, CodeBERT).
- **Budget-constrained** -> DistilBERT (66M params, ~97% of the quality).

> [!EXAM] The decision rule to memorize: understanding tasks -> BERT-family; generation -> GPT-family; few labels -> fine-tune a pre-trained model.

topics.title: Hands-on: ANN Regression
topics.summary: An end-to-end ANN regression workflow in PyTorch: standardise the data, build an MLP, train it with the five-step loop, monitor train/val curves using MSE and R-squared, regularise against overfitting, and benchmark honestly against classical ML baselines to learn when deep learning actually helps on tabular data.

notes.heading: Start with a baseline: classical ML vs deep learning on tabular data
notes.body:
Before writing a single line of PyTorch, ask a blunt question: **does this problem even need a neural network?** On small, spreadsheet-style ("tabular") data, the honest answer is often *no*.

> [!INTUITION] A neural network is a giant, flexible curve-fitter with thousands of knobs. With only a few thousand rows of data, there is not enough signal to set all those knobs reliably, so the network ends up memorising noise instead of learning the pattern. A Random Forest, which averages many simple decision trees, is far more robust on that little data.

The whole point of this session is captured by the California Housing experiment: we predict house prices with a Linear Regression, a Random Forest, and an MLP, and the MLP does **not** crush the baselines. That is the lesson, not a failure.

| Model | Best for | On small tabular data |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Linear Regression | Linear, low-noise relationships | Fast, interpretable baseline |
| Random Forest | Non-linear, mixed types, missing values | Usually wins, little tuning |
| MLP (PyTorch) | Custom architectures, large data, GPU | Rarely better below ~10K rows |

> [!KEY] Always establish a simple classical baseline (Linear Regression, Random Forest) *before* reaching for deep learning. Deep learning earns its keep when data is large (100K+ rows) or the structure is too complex for tree splits, e.g. images, text, audio.

> [!EXAM] Expect a question that gives you "RF R-squared = 0.82, MLP R-squared = 0.79" and asks whether the MLP is useless. The expected answer: not useless, but a 3-point gap rarely justifies the extra tuning, training time, and lost interpretability of the deep model.

notes.heading: The end-to-end recipe: five steps that never change
notes.body:
Every regression (and classification) model you build in PyTorch follows the same five-step recipe. Memorise the shape of it once and you can build any of them.

1. **Preprocess** — split into train/val/test, then standardise features (StandardScaler: zero mean, unit variance).
2. **Define the model** — stack `Linear` layers with `ReLU` activations between them; the last layer outputs the number you want to predict.
3. **Choose a loss** — `MSELoss` for regression (how far off are the numbers), `BCELoss` for binary classification.
4. **Optimise** — `Adam` adjusts the weights to push the loss down.
5. **Monitor** — watch the training and validation loss curves to catch overfitting early.

> [!KEY] Only steps 2 and 3, the architecture and the loss, change between projects. The loop in step 4 (`zero_grad` then `forward` then `loss` then `backward` then `step`) is *identical* whether you are predicting house prices, generating Shakespeare, or translating French. Learn it cold here.

> [!ANALOGY] Think of it like cooking from a recipe card. The five steps are the card. For a regression dish you pick the "MSE" sauce; for classification you pick "BCE". The kitchen workflow never changes.

notes.heading: Step 1 - Preprocessing: why you must standardise (and split first)
notes.body:
A neuron computes a **weighted sum** of its inputs. If one feature (say, median income) ranges 0 to 15 and another (population) ranges 0 to 35,000, the big-numbered feature produces huge weighted sums and huge gradients, drowning out the small one. Training crawls or wobbles.

**StandardScaler** fixes this by rescaling every feature onto the same footing:

$$x' = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Here $x$ is the original feature value, $\mu$ ("mu") is that feature's **average** over the training set, $\sigma$ ("sigma") is its **standard deviation** (how spread out it is), and $x'$ is the rescaled value. After this, every feature has average 0 and spread 1, so no single feature dominates the gradients.

> [!MATH] In words: subtract the mean and divide by the spread. A value exactly average becomes 0; a value one standard deviation above average becomes +1.

> [!WARNING] **Fit the scaler on the training set only**, then apply that same $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to the validation and test sets. If you compute $\mu, \sigma$ over all the data, information about the test set leaks into training, a subtle bug called *data leakage* that makes your reported scores look too good.

After scaling, convert the NumPy arrays to `torch.tensor` (float32) so PyTorch can run gradients through them.

> [!EXAM] "What breaks if you skip StandardScaler?" Answer: features on large scales dominate the weighted sums and gradients, so the network converges slowly, gets stuck, or is biased toward the large-magnitude features.

notes.heading: Steps 2 and 3 - Building the regression MLP and picking MSE
notes.body:
For California Housing we feed in 8 features and want 1 number out (the price), so the network is shaped `[8 -> 64 -> 32 -> 1]`: an input of 8, two hidden layers of 64 and 32 neurons, and a single output neuron.

[[fig:mlp-architecture|A multi-layer perceptron: inputs on the left flow through hidden layers to the output. For regression the final layer is a single neuron with no squashing function.]]

Between the linear layers we put **ReLU**, which simply keeps positive numbers and zeros out negatives. This is what lets the network bend into non-linear shapes instead of being one big straight line. One hidden layer transforms its input like:

$$h = \text{ReLU}(Wx + b)$$

where $x$ is the input vector, $W$ is the layer's weight matrix (how strongly each input feeds each neuron), $b$ is the bias (a per-neuron offset), and $h$ is the layer's output passed on to the next layer.

> [!WARNING] The output neuron for **regression has NO activation** — it must be free to produce any real number (a scaled house price could be 0.5 or 4.8). Do *not* put a sigmoid there; sigmoid squashes the answer into (0, 1) and belongs only on the *classification* MLP.

The loss for regression is **Mean Squared Error**:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

Here $n$ is the number of examples, $y_i$ is the true price of example $i$, $\hat{y}_i$ ("y-hat") is the model's prediction, and squaring the difference punishes big misses far more than small ones.

> [!KEY] Regression: last layer is a single plain (linear) neuron plus `MSELoss`. Binary classification: last layer is one neuron plus `sigmoid` plus `BCELoss`. Matching the output activation to the loss is the single most common place beginners slip.

notes.heading: Step 4 - The PyTorch training loop: five lines you will write forever
notes.body:
Training is just **rolling the loss downhill**. At each step the optimiser measures the slope of the loss with respect to every weight (the gradient) and nudges each weight a little in the downhill direction.

[[fig:gradient-descent-1d|Gradient descent nudges each weight downhill on the loss curve; the step size is the learning rate.]]

The update rule for a single weight is:

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$$

where $w$ is the weight, $\eta$ ("eta") is the **learning rate** (how big a step to take), and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$ is the gradient, i.e. how much the loss $L$ would change if you wiggled $w$. The minus sign means "go opposite the slope", which is downhill.

In PyTorch the loop is exactly five moves:

```python
for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    optimizer.zero_grad()        # 1. wipe last step's gradients
    y_pred = model(X_train)      # 2. forward pass
    loss = criterion(y_pred, y)  # 3. compute the loss
    loss.backward()              # 4. backprop: fill in gradients
    optimizer.step()             # 5. update the weights
```

> [!WARNING] If you forget `zero_grad()`, PyTorch **adds** the new gradients on top of the old ones instead of replacing them. Your steps become wrong and training destabilises. Clear them every iteration.

> [!INTUITION] `backward()` is the chain rule running automatically; it figures out, for every weight at once, which direction reduces the loss. `step()` is the actual nudge. `Adam` is a smarter version of plain gradient descent that adapts the step size per weight and usually converges faster on tabular data.

notes.heading: Step 5 - Monitoring: loss curves, overfitting, and R-squared
notes.body:
You judge training by plotting **two** curves over epochs: the loss on the training data and the loss on a held-out validation set.

[[fig:overfitting-curve|Training loss keeps falling, but when validation loss turns upward the model has started memorising; that is where to stop.]]

- Both high and flat: **underfitting** (model too simple or trained too little). Add capacity or train longer.
- Train low, val rising: **overfitting** (memorising noise). Regularise, get more data, or stop early.

To measure validation loss without polluting the gradients, switch the model to evaluation mode and turn off gradient tracking:

```python
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    val_loss = criterion(model(X_val), y_val)
```

`model.eval()` tells layers like Dropout to behave deterministically; `torch.no_grad()` saves memory and signals that you are not training here.

For a final scorecard on regression we report **R-squared**, the coefficient of determination:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

The top sum is your model's squared errors; the bottom sum is the error of a dumb model that always guesses the **average** price $\bar{y}$ ("y-bar"). So $R^2 = 1$ is perfect, $R^2 = 0$ means "no better than guessing the mean", and $R^2$ can even go **negative** if your model is worse than that.

> [!EXAM] "Train loss falls smoothly but val loss oscillates wildly" has two classic causes: learning rate too high (gradients overshoot; diagnose by cutting LR 10x) or batch size too small (noisy gradient estimates; increase batch size).

notes.heading: Regularization: Dropout and L2 weight decay
notes.body:
When the train/val gap yawns open, you fight overfitting with **regularisation**, anything that discourages the network from memorising. Two tools dominate on tabular MLPs.

**Dropout** randomly switches off a fraction of neurons on each training step (e.g. 30%). Because no neuron can rely on any particular partner being present, the network is forced to spread its knowledge around instead of building fragile, co-adapted shortcuts.

**L2 weight decay** adds a penalty for large weights directly to the loss:

$$L_{\text{reg}} = L + \lambda \sum_i w_i^2$$

Here $L$ is the ordinary loss (e.g. MSE), $w_i$ is each weight, and $\lambda$ ("lambda") controls how hard you push the weights toward zero. Smaller weights make a smoother, simpler function that generalises better. In PyTorch you get it for free by passing `weight_decay=1e-4` to Adam.

| | Dropout | L2 weight decay |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Mechanism | Randomly zeroes activations | Penalises large weights |
| PyTorch | `nn.Dropout(0.3)` | `Adam(..., weight_decay=lambda)` |
| Active when | Training only (off at eval) | Every step |

> [!WARNING] Regularisation does **not** always help. If your model is already underfitting, adding Dropout or weight decay just makes both train and val error worse. Reach for these tools only when you see a clear train-much-better-than-val gap.

> [!KEY] First response to an overfitting MLP: try Dropout (e.g. 0.3). A large train-versus-val gap signals memorisation, which Dropout targets directly.

notes.heading: Reading the results: when does deep learning actually win?
notes.body:
Put the bar chart side by side: Linear Regression, Random Forest, MLP, each with its MSE and R-squared. On California Housing the Random Forest typically edges out the MLP, and that result is the real lesson of the session.

> [!INTUITION] The trade-off is **bias versus variance**. A model that is too simple has high *bias*: it underfits (a straight line through curved data). A model that is too flexible for the data has high *variance*: it overfits, swinging wildly to chase noise. A Random Forest averages many trees to keep variance low *for free*, which is why it shines on small data. An MLP has the flexibility but needs lots of data to tame its variance.

Deep learning starts to win when:

- **Scale**: data grows to 100K+ rows, giving the network enough signal.
- **Complexity**: the relationships are too tangled for axis-aligned tree splits, and especially for *unstructured* data like images, audio, and text, where classical ML has no good hand-built features.

> [!KEY] Professional habit: always benchmark against a simple baseline first, and only adopt the heavier model when it buys a meaningful, reproducible gain, not a 1-2 point flutter that could be noise.

> [!ANALOGY] Using a deep MLP on 300 rows of tabular data is like hiring a 12-piece orchestra to play "Happy Birthday". It *can* do it, but a single guitarist (Random Forest) gets the job done with far less fuss.

quiz.q: You feed raw California Housing features into an MLP without standardising. Median income spans 0 to 15 while population spans 0 to 35,000. What is the most likely consequence?
options: The model trains fine because neural networks are scale-invariant by design
Gradient updates are dominated by the large-magnitude feature, so the network converges slowly or to a biased solution
The MSE loss becomes negative, which halts training
ReLU activations automatically cancel out the scale difference
quiz.explanation: A neuron computes a weighted sum, so a feature with values up to 35,000 produces far larger sums and gradients than one capped at 15, dominating every update. StandardScaler rescales each feature to zero mean and unit variance to balance them. Neural nets are NOT scale-invariant, and MSE (a sum of squares) can never be negative.

quiz.q: For the California Housing regression MLP, what activation should sit on the single output neuron?
options: Sigmoid, to keep predictions in a valid range
No activation - a plain linear output
ReLU, to match the hidden layers
Softmax, to normalise the output
quiz.explanation: Regression must predict any real number, so the output neuron is linear (no squashing). Sigmoid would clamp predictions to (0,1), making correct house prices unreachable; it belongs only on a binary-classification output with BCELoss. ReLU would forbid negative outputs, and softmax is for multi-class probability vectors.

quiz.q: Which loss function pairs correctly with which task?
options: MSELoss for both, since it always measures error
BCELoss for regression; MSELoss for binary classification
CrossEntropyLoss for regression; MSELoss for binary classification
MSELoss for regression; BCELoss for binary classification
quiz.explanation: MSE measures squared distance between predicted and true numbers (regression); BCE measures the gap between predicted probabilities and 0/1 labels (binary classification). Using MSE for both ignores that classification outputs are probabilities needing a log-based loss, which trains poorly and miscalibrates.

quiz.q: Your regression model reports R-squared = 0.0 on the test set. What does this indicate?
options: The model predicts no better than always guessing the mean target value
The model's predictions are perfectly correlated with the truth
The model has zero error on every example
An R-squared of 0 is impossible, so there must be a bug
quiz.explanation: R-squared = 1 - (model squared error)/(mean-baseline squared error). When the two are equal the ratio is 1 and R-squared is 0, meaning the model matches a constant mean predictor. R-squared = 1 (not 0) would mean zero error, and R-squared can even go negative when a model is worse than the mean, so 0 is entirely possible.

quiz.q: In a PyTorch training loop you omit optimizer.zero_grad(). What happens?
options: The learning rate is silently doubled
Nothing - PyTorch resets gradients automatically each step
The forward pass throws a runtime error
Gradients from previous iterations accumulate and corrupt the weight updates
quiz.explanation: PyTorch ADDS new gradients into the existing .grad buffers each time backward() runs, so without zero_grad() the gradients pile up across steps and the update direction becomes wrong, destabilising training. PyTorch does not auto-reset, the forward pass is unaffected, and the learning rate is unchanged.

quiz.q: Why wrap validation in model.eval() and torch.no_grad()?
options: To put Dropout/BatchNorm into deterministic inference mode and skip building the gradient graph
To increase the learning rate during validation
To apply data augmentation only on the validation set
Because validation requires a different loss function than training
quiz.explanation: model.eval() switches stochastic layers like Dropout off so evaluation is deterministic, and torch.no_grad() stops PyTorch tracking operations for backprop, saving memory and time during a pass where no weights are updated. It does not change the learning rate, add augmentation, or change the loss function.

quiz.q: Passing weight_decay=1e-4 to the Adam optimiser is equivalent to which technique?
options: Reducing the learning rate to 1e-4
Dropout with rate 1e-4
L2 regularisation - penalising the sum of squared weights
L1 regularisation - penalising the sum of absolute weights
quiz.explanation: weight_decay adds lambda * sum(w_i^2) to the loss, shrinking all weights toward zero - exactly L2 regularisation. It is unrelated to Dropout (which zeroes activations), is not the learning rate, and is L2 not L1 (L1 uses absolute values and must be coded manually in PyTorch).

quiz.q: Training loss keeps falling but validation loss bottoms out and then rises. What is the best interpretation and fix?
options: The model is overfitting; add regularisation or stop early at the validation minimum
The model is underfitting; add more layers and train longer
The learning rate is too small; increase it 10x
The validation set is too large; shrink it
quiz.explanation: A falling train loss with a rising val loss is the textbook signature of overfitting (late memorisation). The fixes are early stopping, Dropout, weight decay, or more data. Underfitting would show both curves high; the learning rate and val-set size are not the issue here.

quiz.q: What is the correct way to apply StandardScaler across train/val/test splits?
options: Fit a separate scaler on each split independently
Fit on the full dataset before splitting, for consistent statistics
Fit on the training set only, then transform val and test with those same statistics
Fit on the test set so the model sees realistic ranges
quiz.explanation: The scaler must learn mu and sigma from training data only and reuse them on val/test, otherwise test-set statistics leak into training and inflate your scores (data leakage). Fitting on the full data or on the test set is leakage; fitting each split separately makes them incomparable and still leaks.

quiz.q: On the roughly 20K-row California Housing data, the Random Forest slightly beats the MLP. What is the best explanation?
options: The MLP would always win with more hidden layers regardless of data size
MLPs cannot model non-linear relationships, unlike Random Forests
Random Forests minimise MSE exactly while MLPs only approximate it
Tree ensembles control variance well on limited tabular data, while MLPs need much more data to tame their flexibility
quiz.explanation: Random Forests average many decorrelated trees (bagging), which cancels variance with little tuning, so they shine on small tabular data. MLPs are flexible (low bias) but high variance, needing far more data to set their many weights. MLPs CAN model non-linearity via ReLU, and simply stacking more layers on scarce data worsens overfitting rather than guaranteeing a win.

quiz.q: Your training loss jumps around violently and sometimes becomes NaN. What is the most likely single cause?
options: The learning rate is too low, so the model is stuck
The learning rate is too high, so updates overshoot and diverge
MSELoss is the wrong loss for this regression task
The hidden layers use ReLU instead of sigmoid
quiz.explanation: Wild oscillation and NaNs are the classic symptom of an oversized learning rate: each step w <- w - eta * grad overshoots the minimum and can diverge. The fix is to cut the learning rate about 10x (and/or clip gradients). A too-low LR makes loss creep down slowly (not explode), MSE is correct for regression, and ReLU in hidden layers is standard and good.

quiz.q: How does a nn.Dropout(0.3) layer behave at training time versus at evaluation time?
options: It is only active during evaluation to test robustness
It zeroes 30% of activations in both training and evaluation
It randomly zeroes about 30% of activations during training but passes everything through (scaled) at evaluation
It permanently removes 30% of the network's weights after training
quiz.explanation: Dropout is a training-only stochastic regulariser: it randomly drops activations each training step to prevent co-adaptation, but at eval (model.eval()) it is disabled so the full, deterministic network is used (with appropriate scaling). It is not active at eval, and it never deletes weights permanently.

descriptive.q: Walk through the five-step PyTorch training loop line by line. What does each call do, and why is zero_grad() necessary?
descriptive.answer:
The loop is five lines that repeat once per epoch (or per batch). Here is the skeleton with the job of each line.

```python
optimizer.zero_grad()        # 1
y_pred = model(X_train)      # 2
loss = criterion(y_pred, y)  # 3
loss.backward()              # 4
optimizer.step()             # 5
```

1. **`zero_grad()`** clears the gradient buffers. PyTorch *accumulates* gradients by adding into `.grad` each time `backward()` runs, so if you do not wipe them, this step's update mixes in leftovers from previous steps.
2. **forward pass** runs the input through every layer to produce predictions $\hat{y}$.
3. **loss** - `criterion` (here `MSELoss`) measures how far predictions are from targets, returning a single number $L$.
4. **`backward()`** runs backpropagation (the chain rule) to compute $\partial L/\partial w$ for every weight $w$ at once, storing them in each parameter's `.grad`.
5. **`step()`** applies the update $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\partial L/\partial w$ (Adam adapts $\eta$ per weight), nudging every weight downhill.

> [!KEY] The order matters: you must `zero_grad` before `backward`, and `backward` before `step`. Forgetting `zero_grad` is the classic bug - gradients silently accumulate and training destabilises.

This exact five-step loop is reused in every later project; only the model and the criterion change.

descriptive.q: Why do tree-based models like Random Forest often beat MLPs on small tabular datasets? Frame your answer with the bias-variance trade-off.
descriptive.answer:
On small tabular datasets, tree ensembles like Random Forest usually match or beat MLPs. The reason is the **bias-variance trade-off**.

Total expected error splits into three pieces:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y-\hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{(\text{bias})^2}_{\text{too simple}} + \underbrace{\text{variance}}_{\text{too sensitive}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

where bias is error from oversimplifying, variance is how much the fitted model swings when the training data changes, and $\sigma^2$ is noise no model can remove.

- An **MLP** is highly flexible, so it has low bias but **high variance**: with only a few thousand rows it has too many knobs and not enough data to set them, so it chases noise (overfits).
- A **Random Forest** averages many decorrelated decision trees (bagging). Averaging directly **cancels variance** without raising bias much, and it does this with almost no tuning, handling non-linearity, mixed feature types, and missing values gracefully.

| | MLP on small tabular | Random Forest |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bias | Low | Low to medium |
| Variance | High (needs lots of data) | Low (bagging) |
| Tuning effort | High | Low |
| Handles raw mixed/missing data | Poorly | Well |

> [!KEY] Deep learning's flexibility is a liability when data is scarce. It pays off only when there is enough data (100K+ rows) or when the structure is too complex for tree splits, especially unstructured data (images, text, audio) where there are no good hand-built features.

descriptive.q: Define R-squared, interpret what its value means (including 0 and negative values), and explain how it differs from raw MSE and why we report it.
descriptive.answer:
**R-squared (the coefficient of determination)** answers: *how much better is my model than just guessing the average every time?*

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_i (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

- $y_i$ - true value of example $i$.
- $\hat{y}_i$ - the model's prediction.
- $\bar{y}$ - the mean of all true values (the "dumb" baseline that always predicts the average).
- The **numerator** is your model's total squared error; the **denominator** is the squared error of the mean-only baseline.

Interpreting the value:

| R-squared | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| 1.0 | Perfect - zero error |
| 0.8 | Explains 80% of the variance in the target |
| 0.0 | No better than always predicting the mean |
| below 0 | **Worse** than predicting the mean (possible on the test set) |

> [!INTUITION] R-squared is a *normalised* score: unlike raw MSE it does not depend on the units of the target, so you can compare it across datasets and say "this model explains 79% of the variance".

**MSE versus R-squared**: MSE is what you *optimise* during training (it has gradients and lives in the target's units squared), but it is hard to interpret alone - is an MSE of 0.3 good? R-squared is what you *report* because it is scale-free and tells a human how useful the model is. They are two views of the same squared-error quantity.

descriptive.q: Explain data leakage in preprocessing. What is the correct way to apply StandardScaler across splits, and what goes wrong if you fit it on all the data?
descriptive.answer:
**Data leakage** is when information that would not be available at prediction time sneaks into training, making your test scores look better than reality.

The correct StandardScaler workflow:

```python
scaler.fit(X_train)                 # learn mu, sigma from TRAIN only
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_val   = scaler.transform(X_val)   # reuse train's mu, sigma
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)  # reuse train's mu, sigma
```

The scaler must learn its mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ from the **training set only**, then apply those same numbers to validation and test.

**What goes wrong if you fit on all the data first?** The $\mu$ and $\sigma$ then encode facts about the test set (its mean, its spread). The model is effectively given a peek at the test distribution, so:

- Validation and test scores come out **optimistically high**.
- The estimate no longer reflects performance on truly unseen data, which is the entire purpose of the test set.

> [!WARNING] The same rule applies to *any* fitted preprocessing - imputation values, encoders, feature selection: learn them on train, apply to val/test. Splitting **before** preprocessing is the safe habit.

> [!ANALOGY] Fitting the scaler on all the data is like letting students see the exam questions while you write the answer key. The grades look great but say nothing about real understanding.

descriptive.q: Compare Dropout and L2 weight decay as regularisers for the regression MLP: mechanism, PyTorch usage, behaviour at eval, and when each helps. When does regularisation backfire?
descriptive.answer:
Both Dropout and L2 weight decay fight **overfitting**, but through different mechanisms.

**Dropout** randomly zeroes a fraction $p$ of neuron activations on each training step. Because no neuron can depend on a specific partner always being present, the network spreads its representation out instead of building fragile co-adapted shortcuts. It is active **only during training**; at evaluation all neurons pass through (scaled).

**L2 weight decay** adds a penalty for large weights to the loss:

$$L_{\text{reg}} = L + \lambda \sum_i w_i^2$$

where $L$ is the task loss (MSE), $w_i$ each weight, and $\lambda$ the strength. Pushing weights toward zero yields a smoother, lower-variance function. In PyTorch it is simply `Adam(..., weight_decay=lambda)`.

| | Dropout | L2 weight decay |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Mechanism | Randomly drops activations | Shrinks weight magnitudes |
| Where | `nn.Dropout(p)` in the model | `weight_decay` in the optimiser |
| Active at eval? | No | Yes (it shaped the weights) |
| Good when | Wide nets, strong co-adaptation | Almost always a safe default |

**When each helps:** L2 is a cheap, smooth default to keep on most MLPs. Dropout shines when the network is wide and clearly memorising; on small tabular MLPs a rate around 0.3 is typical. They can be combined.

> [!WARNING] Regularisation does **not** always help. If the model is already *underfitting* (both train and val error high), adding Dropout or weight decay only worsens both - you should *increase* capacity instead. Reach for regularisation only when you see a clear train-much-better-than-val gap.

descriptive.q: What happens, and how would you diagnose it, if you (a) skip StandardScaler, (b) put a sigmoid on the regression output, or (c) set the learning rate too high?
descriptive.answer:
Each of these is a classic bug with a recognisable signature.

**(a) Skipping StandardScaler.** Features on wildly different scales (income 0 to 15 versus population 0 to 35,000) produce wildly different weighted sums and gradients. The large-magnitude feature dominates every update, so the loss decreases painfully slowly, gets stuck, or the network is biased toward that feature.
*Diagnose:* loss barely moves or moves erratically; fix by standardising (zero mean, unit variance).

**(b) Putting a sigmoid on the regression output.** Sigmoid squashes the output into $(0,1)$:

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$$

But house prices range well outside $(0,1)$, so the network literally **cannot** produce the right answer. Predictions saturate near 0 or 1 and the MSE plateaus high.
*Diagnose:* predictions clamped in a tiny range; fix by using a plain linear output neuron for regression. (Sigmoid belongs only on the *classification* output with BCELoss.)

**(c) Learning rate too high.** The update $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\partial L/\partial w$ takes steps so large that it overshoots the minimum and bounces to the other side, possibly diverging to NaN.
*Diagnose:* loss oscillates violently or explodes to NaN; fix by reducing the learning rate about 10x (and/or add gradient clipping).

> [!KEY] Three different symptoms, three different fixes - learning to read the loss curve and the prediction range is the core debugging skill this session builds.

topics.title: Hands-on: Neural Style Transfer
topics.summary: Neural style transfer repaints a content photo in the texture of a style image by freezing a pretrained VGG19 and optimizing the generated image's pixels to minimize a composite loss: content loss from a deep layer's feature maps, style loss from Gram-matrix channel correlations across several layers, plus a total-variation smoothness term.

notes.heading: The Big Picture: One Photo + One Painting -> One New Image
notes.body:
Neural style transfer (NST) takes two pictures and makes a third. You give it a **content** photo (say, a selfie) and a **style** image (say, Van Gogh's *Starry Night*), and it produces an image that keeps the *objects and layout* of the photo but repaints them in the *colors, brushstrokes and textures* of the painting.

> [!ANALOGY] Imagine handing a photograph and a famous painting to a skilled artist and saying: "Paint what's in the photo, but in this artist's style." NST automates exactly that instruction.

[[fig:neural-style-transfer]]

The surprising part: we never train a new network. We borrow a network that already knows how to *see* images - a pretrained **VGG19** classifier - and use it purely as a measuring device. It hands us two scores:

- a **content score**: how different is the generated image from the photo, in terms of *what objects sit where*?
- a **style score**: how different is the generated image from the painting, in terms of *textures and color patterns*?

We then nudge the *pixels* of a third image until both scores shrink.

> [!KEY] NST optimizes an **image**, not a network. VGG19's weights stay frozen; the only thing that changes is the generated picture's pixels.

notes.heading: The CNN Feature Hierarchy: Style Lives Shallow, Content Lives Deep
notes.body:
A convolutional network does not understand an image all at once - it builds understanding in **layers**. The first layers react to tiny local patterns: edges, color gradients, little blobs. Each deeper layer combines the layer below it, so it responds to larger and more meaningful things: corners, then textures, then object parts, then whole objects.

[[fig:conv-feature-maps]]

This low-to-high ladder is exactly what makes NST possible, because **style and content live on different rungs**:

| Layer | Receptive field | Detects | Used for |
|---|---|---|---|
| conv1_1 | 3x3 | edges, color gradients | style (fine texture) |
| conv2_1 | 7x7 | corners, simple patterns | style (textures) |
| conv3_1 | 15x15 | textures, simple shapes | style (patterns) |
| conv4_1 | 31x31 | object parts | style (high-level) |
| conv4_2 | 31x31 | object parts (same block) | **content** |
| conv5_1 | 63x63 | semantic objects | style (semantics) |

The **receptive field** is how much of the original image one neuron "sees." A conv1_1 neuron sees only a 3x3 patch - perfect for capturing a single brushstroke. A conv5_1 neuron sees a 63x63 region - big enough to recognize "this is a sky" or "this is a face."

> [!INTUITION] **Style = local texture statistics**, best read from shallow layers with small receptive fields. **Content = global object layout**, best read from deep layers with large receptive fields.

> [!KEY] Content is read from a **single deep layer (conv4_2)**; style is read from **several** layers spanning shallow to deep, so the transferred texture is multi-scale (fine grain + larger motifs).

notes.heading: Content Loss: Keeping the "What"
notes.body:
Content loss answers one question: does the generated image contain the *same stuff in the same places* as the photo? We answer it by pushing both images through VGG19 and comparing their feature maps at the content layer, **conv4_2**.

A **feature map** is the network's internal description of the image at a layer - a stack of $C$ grids, one per filter (channel). If two images produce nearly identical feature maps at conv4_2, they contain the same objects in the same arrangement, even if their colors and textures differ wildly.

> [!MATH] Content loss is the (half) summed squared difference between the two feature maps:
> $$L_{content} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,k}\left(F^{l}_{ik}[x] - F^{l}_{ik}[p]\right)^2$$
> Here $F^{l}[x]$ is the feature map of the **generated** image $x$ at layer $l$, $F^{l}[p]$ is the feature map of the **content photo** $p$, $i$ indexes the channel, and $k$ indexes the spatial position. "Square the disagreements and add them up" simply means: wherever the two descriptions differ, the penalty grows quickly.

Why conv4_2 and not something shallower or deeper? It is **deep enough** to encode *semantic* structure (objects, scene layout) but **not so deep** that fine spatial detail is washed away. The original paper chose it deliberately for that balance.

> [!WARNING] Content loss compares **feature maps**, which still remember *where* things are. That is the exact opposite of style loss, which deliberately throws location away (next section). Confusing the two is a classic mistake.

notes.heading: Style as Correlations: The Gram Matrix
notes.body:
Style is harder to pin down than content. The swirls and colors of *Starry Night* appear *all over* the canvas - there is no single correct place for a swirl. So a good style descriptor must ignore *where* a texture appears and capture only *what textures appear together*. That descriptor is the **Gram matrix**.

> [!INTUITION] Suppose one channel of a feature map detects "blue swirl" and another detects "thick curved stroke." If those two channels tend to light up at the *same places* across the painting, the Gram matrix records a large correlation between them. That co-occurrence pattern - not its location - is what we call "style."

Take the feature map at layer $l$ and flatten each channel into one long vector, then correlate every channel with every other channel:

$$G^{l}_{ij} = \frac{1}{C_l N_l}\sum_{k=1}^{N_l} F^{l}_{ik}\,F^{l}_{jk}$$

Glossing every symbol:

- $F^{l} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_l \times N_l}$ - the feature map at layer $l$, reshaped to $C_l$ channels (rows) by $N_l = H_l \times W_l$ spatial positions (columns).
- $G^{l}_{ij}$ - the entry in row $i$, column $j$: how strongly channel $i$ and channel $j$ fire **together**.
- $\sum_{k=1}^{N_l}$ - add up over **every** spatial position $k$. This sum is what erases location: a swirl in the corner and a swirl in the center both pour into the same total.
- $\frac{1}{C_l N_l}$ - a normalizer (number of channels times number of positions).

> [!KEY] The Gram matrix is the feature map times its own transpose: $G = \tfrac{1}{C_l N_l}FF^{\top}$. It measures **pairwise channel correlations** and discards spatial position - making style location-invariant.

> [!EXAMPLE] Compute a style image's Gram matrix, then **roll the image 50 pixels** sideways and recompute. The two Gram matrices come out almost identical (tiny MSE), because shifting pixels does not change *which channels co-fire* - only *where* they fire. That shift-invariance is precisely what makes the Gram matrix a style descriptor rather than a content one.

Why the normalization by $C_l N_l$? Without it, the raw sum grows with the layer's size, so a big layer automatically produces larger gradients and dominates the style loss. Dividing by $C_l N_l$ keeps every layer on a comparable scale, so your style weights are not secretly layer-dependent.

The **style loss** at a layer compares the two Gram matrices:

$$L^{l}_{style} = \frac{1}{4}\left\| G^{l}[x] - G^{l}[a] \right\|_{F}^{2}$$

- $G^{l}[x]$ - Gram matrix of the **generated** image; $G^{l}[a]$ - Gram matrix of the **style (art)** image $a$.
- $\left\| \cdot \right\|_{F}^{2}$ - the **Frobenius norm squared**: square every entry of the difference matrix and add them all up (the matrix version of "sum of squared differences").

The total style loss sums $L^{l}_{style}$ over all chosen style layers, blending fine and coarse textures.

notes.heading: The Composite Loss and Total-Variation Smoothing
notes.body:
The generated image is steered by **one combined objective** that balances three competing pulls:

$$L = \alpha\,L_{content} + \beta\,L_{style} + \gamma\,L_{TV}$$

- $\alpha$ (content weight) - how hard we pull toward the photo's structure.
- $\beta$ (style weight) - how hard we pull toward the painting's textures.
- $\gamma$ (TV weight) - how hard we pull toward a *smooth* image.

> [!MATH] In words: "Make the picture show the photo's objects ($\alpha L_{content}$), painted in the artist's style ($\beta L_{style}$), without grainy noise ($\gamma L_{TV}$)." The **ratio** $\beta/\alpha$ is what really controls the style-versus-content trade-off, not either weight alone.

The third term, **total-variation (TV) loss**, fights one specific ugliness. Because the content and style gradients adjust each pixel somewhat independently, neighboring pixels can drift wildly apart, producing high-frequency speckle ("checkerboard" artifacts). TV loss penalizes exactly that:

$$L_{TV} = \sum_{i,j}\Big(\left| I_{i,j+1}-I_{i,j} \right|^2 + \left| I_{i+1,j}-I_{i,j} \right|^2\Big)$$

- $I_{i,j}$ - the pixel at row $i$, column $j$ of the **generated** image.
- The two terms measure how different each pixel is from its **right** neighbor ($I_{i,j+1}$) and its **bottom** neighbor ($I_{i+1,j}$).
- Summing these squared differences rewards images where adjacent pixels change *gradually* - i.e. smooth ones.

> [!EXAMPLE] To make the output "look more like the content photo and less like the painting," increase $\alpha$. Since $L = \alpha L_{content}+\beta L_{style}+\gamma L_{TV}$, a larger $\alpha$ makes content mismatches more expensive, so the optimizer protects the photo's structure at the cost of style fidelity.

> [!WARNING] If $\beta$ is far larger than $\alpha$, style overpowers everything and the recognizable content dissolves into pure texture. A tiny $\beta$ just hands back the original photo. Always tune the **ratio**, not one weight in isolation.

notes.heading: Optimizing the Image, Not the Network (Frozen VGG + LBFGS)
notes.body:
Here is the idea that surprises everyone: **VGG19 never learns anything during NST.** Its millions of weights are downloaded already trained (on ImageNet) and then **frozen**. The variable we optimize is the generated image itself - every pixel is a parameter.

[[fig:transfer-learning]]

This is **transfer learning** in its purest form: we reuse a network's already-learned ability to see edges, textures and objects, and never touch those weights. If we *did* update VGG19's weights, we would destroy the very representations that let us measure content and style in the first place - and there is no label to train them toward anyway.

> [!KEY] In NST the **image is the parameter** and the **network is a fixed ruler**. Gradients flow all the way back through frozen VGG19 to the pixels, telling each pixel how to change to lower the loss.

The optimizer is usually **LBFGS**, not Adam. Why?

| | Adam (first-order) | LBFGS (quasi-Newton) |
|---|---|---|
| Information used | gradient only | gradient + curvature estimate |
| Batching | supports mini-batches | needs full-batch each step |
| Best for | training weights on big datasets | smooth, low-dimensional problems |
| Typical NST iterations | ~500-1000 | ~300 |

In NST the entire "dataset" is a *single* image, the pixel landscape is smooth, and we want fast convergence - exactly the regime where LBFGS's curvature shortcuts shine. For training real network weights on millions of noisy examples, Adam wins instead.

> [!INTUITION] The generated image normally **starts as a copy of the content photo**, then is gradually repainted toward the style. Starting from pure Gaussian noise also works but converges much more slowly.

> [!EXAM] A classic exam question: "Why optimize pixels instead of weights?" Answer: VGG19 is used as a *fixed feature extractor*; its frozen pretrained weights are what define content and style, so we change the image, not the network.

notes.heading: The Tuning Knobs: Hyperparameters and Common Fixes
notes.body:
Once the machinery runs, NST becomes a tuning exercise. Each knob trades off against the others, so change one variable at a time:

| Parameter | Typical range | Effect | How to tune |
|---|---|---|---|
| content weight $\alpha$ | 1-100 | higher => preserves structure | raise if output loses its shape |
| style weight $\beta$ | $10^4$-$10^6$ | higher => stronger style | lower if content disappears |
| TV weight $\gamma$ | $10^{-4}$-$10^{-2}$ | higher => smoother output | raise if result is noisy |
| iterations | 300-1000 | more => better convergence | stop when loss plateaus |
| image size | 256-512 px | larger => finer detail, slower | 512 on a Colab GPU |
| # style layers | 1-5 | more => richer multi-scale style | start with all 5 |

When something looks wrong, the symptom usually points straight at the fix:

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| noisy / grainy output | TV weight too low | increase $\gamma$ by 10x |
| content structure lost | style weight too high | reduce $\beta$ by 10x |
| unnatural colors | style dominates the color channels | use color-preserving NST (match luminance only) |
| optimization diverges | learning rate too high | lower the LBFGS LR, or try Adam |
| out-of-memory on Colab | image too large | drop to 256 px / use gradient checkpointing |
| very slow convergence | initialized from noise | initialize from the content image |

> [!EXAM] Expect "which knob fixes which symptom" questions. Memorize the directions: grainy => raise $\gamma$; content lost => lower $\beta$; lost overall shape => raise $\alpha$.

notes.heading: Beyond the Basics: Texture Synthesis, Multi-Style, and Video
notes.body:
Every advanced variant is just the same three-term recipe, $L = \alpha L_{content}+\beta L_{style}+\gamma L_{TV}$, with one ingredient swapped:

- **Neural texture synthesis** - set $\alpha = 0$ (no content term), start from noise, and minimize style loss only. The result is a pure texture grown from the style image's statistics. Shallow layers alone give fine grain; using all five style layers gives rich, multi-scale texture.
- **Multi-style blending** - combine two paintings by mixing their style losses: $L_{style} = w_1 L_{style_1} + w_2 L_{style_2}$, where $L_{style_1}$ and $L_{style_2}$ are the style losses against each painting and $w_1, w_2$ set the blend (e.g. 0.7 / 0.3).
- **Color-preserving NST** - transfer style only to the **luminance** (brightness) channel in LAB color space, then recombine with the photo's original A/B color channels, so the photo keeps its own colors.
- **Video style transfer** - apply NST frame by frame. Done naively, each frame is optimized **independently**, so the output *flickers*. A **temporal consistency loss** $L_{temp} = \left\| I_t - \mathrm{warp}(I_{t-1}, \phi_{t-1 \to t}) \right\|^2$ ties consecutive frames together, where $\phi$ is the optical-flow field mapping frame $t-1$ onto frame $t$.

> [!INTUITION] Notice the pattern: drop content ($\alpha=0$) for textures, add a second style term for blends, restrict the channel for color preservation, or add a frame-to-frame term for video. Same loss skeleton, different emphasis.

> [!EXAM] Know two facts cold: **texture synthesis = style-only ($\alpha=0$) starting from noise**, and **naive video NST flickers because frames are optimized independently** (fixed by a temporal consistency loss).

quiz.q: In neural style transfer, gradient descent updates the pixels of the generated image while VGG19's weights stay fixed. Why are the network weights frozen rather than trained?
options: Training VGG19's weights would overfit to the single style image
Backpropagation in a pretrained network can only reach the inputs, never the weights
The image pixels carry more information than the weights, so they converge faster
VGG19 is used as a fixed feature extractor; changing its weights would destroy the pretrained representations that define both content and style
quiz.explanation: VGG19 is borrowed purely as a measuring device: its ImageNet-trained filters are what let us read out content (deep feature maps) and style (Gram correlations). Updating those weights would wreck the very representations the losses depend on, so they are frozen and the image becomes the parameter. The backprop claim is false - gradients can reach weights fine; we simply choose not to update them.

quiz.q: You want the stylized output to look more like the original content photo and less like the painting. Given the objective $L = \alpha L_{content} + \beta L_{style} + \gamma L_{TV}$, what should you change?
options: Decrease $\gamma$, the TV weight
Increase $\beta$, the style weight
Increase $\alpha$, the content weight, so content mismatches cost more
Increase the number of style layers used
quiz.explanation: A larger content weight $\alpha$ makes deviations from the photo's feature maps more expensive, so the optimizer protects content structure at the expense of style. Increasing $\beta$ does the opposite (more style); $\gamma$ only controls smoothness; adding style layers enriches style, not content. What really matters is the ratio beta/alpha.

quiz.q: What does the Gram matrix capture about a feature map that the raw feature map does not?
options: Pairwise correlations between feature channels, while discarding spatial location
The exact spatial position of each detected texture
The semantic class label predicted for the image
The total number of channels in the layer
quiz.explanation: The Gram matrix is $G = \tfrac{1}{C_lN_l}FF^{\top}$: each entry is how strongly two channels co-activate, summed over all spatial positions. The sum over positions throws away *where* a texture is, keeping only *which textures co-occur* - the essence of style. Raw feature maps still encode location (which is why they are used for content). It is not a class label or a channel count.

quiz.q: The normalized Gram matrix divides by $C_l N_l$ (channels times spatial positions). Why is this normalization described as critical?
options: It turns the Gram matrix into a valid probability distribution
Without it, gradient magnitudes scale with layer size, making the effective style weight depend on the layer
It guarantees the Gram matrix is invertible
It removes all negative entries from the matrix
quiz.explanation: The raw sum $\sum_k F_{ik}F_{jk}$ grows with the number of channels and positions, so larger/deeper layers would produce systematically bigger Gram values and gradients, silently dominating the multi-layer style loss. Dividing by $C_lN_l$ puts every layer on a comparable scale so your chosen style weights mean the same thing everywhere. It has nothing to do with probabilities, invertibility, or sign.

quiz.q: Why does NST read style from shallow convolutional layers but content from a deep layer?
options: Shallow layers have small receptive fields capturing local textures and brushstrokes (style); deep layers have large receptive fields capturing semantic objects (content)
Shallow layers are cheaper to compute, so they are assigned the harder style task
Deep layers detect color and texture while shallow layers detect whole objects
Shallow layers have more channels, which makes them better at content
quiz.explanation: A conv1_1 neuron sees only a 3x3 patch - ideal for brushstrokes and color blobs that define artistic style. A conv5_1 neuron sees a 63x63 region - large enough to recognize whole objects and scene layout, which define content. Option C reverses the hierarchy; compute cost and channel count are not the reason.

quiz.q: Total-variation (TV) loss is added to the NST objective. Which artifact does it suppress, and how?
options: High-frequency speckle / checkerboard noise, by penalizing squared differences between adjacent pixels
Loss of content structure, by amplifying the content gradient
A singular Gram matrix, by adding a small value to its diagonal
Color shifts from the style image, by matching the luminance channel
quiz.explanation: TV loss is $\sum_{i,j}(|I_{i,j+1}-I_{i,j}|^2 + |I_{i+1,j}-I_{i,j}|^2)$ - it penalizes large jumps between a pixel and its right/bottom neighbors, which encourages smooth, gradual changes and removes the grainy checkerboard noise that arises when pixels are adjusted independently. It is unrelated to content structure, Gram singularity, or color matching.

quiz.q: Why is LBFGS, rather than Adam, the standard optimizer for the image-pixel optimization in NST?
options: LBFGS supports mini-batching, which is essential for optimizing images
The pixel space is smooth and the whole dataset is a single image, so the full-batch quasi-Newton method converges fast (~300 iterations)
LBFGS avoids computing gradients, so it is faster per step
LBFGS updates the network weights more accurately than Adam
quiz.explanation: LBFGS is a quasi-Newton method that exploits curvature and needs a full-batch evaluation each step - perfect when the entire 'dataset' is one image and the loss surface over pixels is smooth, giving fast convergence in roughly 300 iterations. Mini-batching is an Adam strength, not LBFGS's; LBFGS very much uses gradients; and in NST no weights are updated at all.

quiz.q: The original NST paper reads content from conv4_2 specifically. What is the rationale for that choice?
options: It is the very first convolutional layer of the network
It is the only layer that has a Gram matrix
It has the smallest receptive field, preserving the finest detail
It is deep enough to capture semantic structure, but not so deep that fine spatial detail is lost
quiz.explanation: conv4_2 sits at a sweet spot: deep enough to encode objects and layout (semantics), yet still retaining enough spatial structure to anchor the content. Every layer can produce a Gram matrix, so that is false; conv4_2 has a fairly large receptive field, not the smallest; and it is deep in the network, not the first layer.

quiz.q: You compute a style image's Gram matrices, then roll the image 50 pixels sideways and recompute. What do you expect, and why?
options: The number of channels changes, altering the Gram matrix shape
The Gram matrices change drastically, because they encode the spatial position of each feature
The content loss changes but the style Gram matrices are undefined after a shift
The Gram matrices are nearly identical, because the sum over all spatial positions makes style location-invariant
quiz.explanation: The Gram entry $\sum_k F_{ik}F_{jk}$ sums over every position $k$; a spatial roll merely reorders those terms, leaving the totals (and thus the Gram matrix) essentially unchanged - tiny MSE. This shift-invariance is the whole point of using Gram matrices for style. Raw feature maps would change a lot, but their correlations do not.

quiz.q: To perform pure neural texture synthesis (generate a texture from a style image with no content photo), how do you configure the standard NST pipeline?
options: Set $\beta = 0$ and optimize the content loss only
Set $\alpha = 0$, initialize from Gaussian noise, and optimize the style loss only
Maximize $\gamma$ and remove the style image entirely
Use only the fully-connected (FC) layers of VGG19
quiz.explanation: Texture synthesis drops the content term ($\alpha=0$), starts from noise (there is no photo to preserve), and minimizes style loss alone, so the image grows into a texture matching the style image's channel statistics. Setting $\beta=0$ would remove style entirely; $\gamma$ only smooths; and NST uses the convolutional blocks, not the discarded FC layers.

quiz.q: How does initializing the generated image as a copy of the content photo compare to initializing it from Gaussian noise?
options: Both converge identically, since LBFGS ignores the starting point
Noise initialization is required, because content initialization makes the loss exactly zero
Content initialization converges faster and tends to preserve content structure; noise converges much more slowly
Content initialization makes style transfer impossible
quiz.explanation: Starting from the content image places the optimizer near a good content solution, so it converges quickly and naturally protects structure; starting from noise is a valid but slow path (and the default for texture synthesis). Content init does not zero the loss (style loss is still large), LBFGS is sensitive to initialization, and starting from content certainly does not prevent stylization.

quiz.q: The per-layer style loss is $L^{l}_{style} = \tfrac{1}{4}\lVert G^{l}[x] - G^{l}[a] \rVert_F^2$. In plain terms, what is it measuring?
options: The cross-entropy between two predicted class distributions
The pixel-wise mean squared error between the generated and style images
The squared Frobenius distance between the Gram matrices of the generated and style images
