The cosine similarity of the raw feature maps, without forming Gram matrices
quiz.explanation: The Frobenius norm squared sums the squared differences of every entry of $G^{l}[x]-G^{l}[a]$, i.e. how far the generated image's channel-correlation pattern is from the style image's. It operates on Gram matrices, not raw pixels (that would force an exact copy and ignore the location-invariance we want) and is not a cross-entropy or a Gram-free cosine measure.

descriptive.q: Define the Gram matrix used in NST, derive its form, and explain precisely why it captures *style* while discarding spatial information. Include the role of the $C_l N_l$ normalization and the shift-invariance demonstration.
descriptive.answer:
**Setup.** Take the feature map at layer $l$, $F^{l} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_l \times N_l}$, where $C_l$ is the number of channels (filters) and $N_l = H_l \times W_l$ is the number of spatial positions after flattening height and width into one axis. Row $i$ of $F^{l}$ is the response of filter $i$ at every location.

**The Gram matrix.**
$$G^{l}_{ij} = \frac{1}{C_l N_l}\sum_{k=1}^{N_l} F^{l}_{ik}\,F^{l}_{jk} \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad G = \tfrac{1}{C_l N_l}FF^{\top}$$
Entry $G^{l}_{ij}$ is the (normalized) dot product of channel $i$'s and channel $j$'s activation vectors. It is large when filters $i$ and $j$ tend to fire at the **same places** across the image.

**Why this is style.** Style is about *which textures co-occur* (e.g. a "blue swirl" channel firing wherever a "thick stroke" channel fires), not about *where* they sit. The sum over $k$ runs over **all** positions, so a swirl in the corner and a swirl in the center contribute to the same total - location is integrated out, and only the co-activation pattern survives.

> [!KEY] Raw feature maps remember *where*; Gram matrices remember only *what-with-what*. That is exactly the content/style split.

| | Raw feature map $F^{l}$ | Gram matrix $G^{l}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Keeps spatial location? | yes | no (summed away) |
| Describes | objects and layout | texture correlations |
| Used for | content loss | style loss |

**Shift-invariance check.** Roll the image 50 px and recompute. A spatial roll merely **reorders** the $k$ terms in $\sum_k F_{ik}F_{jk}$; the multiset of values is unchanged, so the sum (and hence $G$) is essentially unchanged - the before/after Gram MSE is near zero. Raw feature maps, by contrast, change substantially because positions moved.

**Why normalize by $C_l N_l$.** The raw sum scales with the layer's size, so deeper/larger layers would produce systematically bigger Gram values and gradients and would dominate the multi-layer style loss. Dividing by $C_l N_l$ puts all layers on a comparable scale, so the style weights are not secretly layer-dependent.

descriptive.q: Compare content loss and style loss in NST. For each, state what it measures, which layer(s) it uses, its formula, and whether it preserves spatial location. Then explain why these design choices follow from the CNN feature hierarchy.
descriptive.answer:
**Content loss** asks: are the same objects in the same places? It compares feature maps at a single **deep** layer (conv4_2):
$$L_{content} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,k}\left(F^{l}_{ik}[x] - F^{l}_{ik}[p]\right)^2$$
where $F^{l}[x]$ and $F^{l}[p]$ are the feature maps of the generated image $x$ and content photo $p$, $i$ is the channel and $k$ the position. It **preserves spatial location** - position is exactly what we want to match.

**Style loss** asks: do the same textures co-occur? It compares **Gram matrices** at **several** layers (conv1_1 ... conv5_1):
$$L^{l}_{style} = \frac{1}{4}\left\| G^{l}[x] - G^{l}[a] \right\|_F^2, \qquad L_{style} = \sum_l L^{l}_{style}$$
where $G^{l}[a]$ is the Gram matrix of the style image $a$ and $\lVert\cdot\rVert_F^2$ is the Frobenius norm squared. It **discards spatial location** (the Gram sum over positions integrates it out).

| Aspect | Content loss | Style loss |
|---|---|---|
| Question | same objects, same places? | same textures co-occurring? |
| Operates on | raw feature maps | Gram matrices |
| Layers | one deep layer (conv4_2) | several (conv1_1..conv5_1) |
| Spatial location | preserved | discarded |
| Distance | summed squared difference | squared Frobenius distance |

**Why this follows from the hierarchy.** Shallow layers have small receptive fields and respond to local textures/brushstrokes - the raw material of artistic style, and because style appears everywhere we deliberately drop location via the Gram matrix and read *multiple* layers for multi-scale texture. Deep layers have large receptive fields and respond to semantic objects and layout - the raw material of content, and because layout *is* positional we keep the feature map (location intact) and a single layer (conv4_2) suffices.

> [!INTUITION] Content = "what is where" -> location-preserving, deep, one layer. Style = "what goes with what" -> location-free Gram, shallow-to-deep, many layers.

descriptive.q: Explain the claim "NST optimizes the image, not the network." Describe what is held fixed, what is the optimization variable, how gradients flow, why we use LBFGS, and how initialization choices affect the result.
descriptive.answer:
**What is fixed vs variable.** VGG19 is downloaded **pretrained** on ImageNet and **frozen** - none of its millions of weights change. The optimization variable is the **generated image** itself: a tensor of shape roughly $H \times W \times 3$, where every pixel is a parameter we are free to adjust.

**Why not train the weights.** VGG19 is used as a *fixed feature extractor*. Its trained filters are precisely what let us read content (deep feature maps) and style (Gram correlations). If we updated those weights we would corrupt the representations the losses rely on - and there is no classification label to train them toward in the first place.

> [!KEY] The image is the parameter; the network is a frozen ruler. We descend on pixels, not weights.

**How gradients flow.** We forward the current image through frozen VGG19, evaluate $L = \alpha L_{content} + \beta L_{style} + \gamma L_{TV}$, then backpropagate. Gradients pass *through* the frozen layers (the layers still have derivatives even though their weights are not updated) and land on the input pixels, each gradient saying "raise or lower me to reduce the loss." An optimizer step then nudges the pixels.

**Why LBFGS.** LBFGS is a quasi-Newton optimizer that approximates curvature and requires a full-batch evaluation each step. In NST the entire 'dataset' is a single image and the pixel loss surface is smooth, so curvature information pays off and convergence is fast (~300 iterations vs ~500-1000 for Adam). For training real network weights over large noisy datasets, Adam's mini-batch robustness wins instead.

**Initialization.** The image normally starts as a **copy of the content photo**, which places the optimizer near a good content solution -> fast convergence and preserved structure. Starting from **Gaussian noise** also works but converges much more slowly; it is the natural choice for pure texture synthesis ($\alpha = 0$), where there is no content to preserve.

descriptive.q: Write out the full NST objective, explain each term, then explain total-variation loss in detail: its formula, the artifact it prevents, and why that artifact arises. Finish by mapping three common failure symptoms to the weight you would change.
descriptive.answer:
**The objective.**
$$L = \alpha\,L_{content} + \beta\,L_{style} + \gamma\,L_{TV}$$
- $\alpha L_{content}$ - pulls the image toward the photo's object structure (feature-map match at conv4_2).
- $\beta L_{style}$ - pulls it toward the painting's textures (Gram-matrix match across layers).
- $\gamma L_{TV}$ - pulls it toward smoothness.

The trade-off that matters most is the **ratio** $\beta/\alpha$: large ratio = stylized, small ratio = nearly the original photo.

**Total-variation loss.**
$$L_{TV} = \sum_{i,j}\Big(\left| I_{i,j+1}-I_{i,j} \right|^2 + \left| I_{i+1,j}-I_{i,j} \right|^2\Big)$$
where $I_{i,j}$ is the generated pixel at row $i$, column $j$; the two terms penalize how different each pixel is from its **right** neighbor $I_{i,j+1}$ and its **bottom** neighbor $I_{i+1,j}$.

**The artifact and why it arises.** When content and style gradients adjust each pixel **independently**, neighboring pixels can land at very different values, producing high-frequency speckle - "checkerboard" noise. TV loss makes large neighbor-to-neighbor jumps expensive, so the optimizer favors images whose pixels vary **gradually**, smoothing the speckle away.

> [!WARNING] Too much $\gamma$ over-smooths and blurs genuine texture; too little lets the grain return. It is a balance.

**Symptom-to-knob map.**

| Symptom | Change |
|---|---|
| noisy / grainy output | increase $\gamma$ (more smoothing) |
| content structure lost | decrease $\beta$ (less style pressure) |
| output loses its overall shape | increase $\alpha$ (more content pressure) |

descriptive.q: Describe the feature hierarchy of VGG19 in terms of receptive fields, and explain why this hierarchy dictates which layers NST uses for style versus content. Why is conv4_2 chosen for content, and why are multiple layers used for style?
descriptive.answer:
**The hierarchy.** A CNN builds representations bottom-up. Early layers respond to tiny local patterns; each deeper layer composes the one below, so it responds to larger, more abstract structures. The **receptive field** - how much of the original image one neuron sees - grows with depth:

| Layer | Receptive field | Detects | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| conv1_1 | 3x3 | edges, color gradients | style (fine texture) |
| conv2_1 | 7x7 | corners, simple patterns | style |
| conv3_1 | 15x15 | textures, simple shapes | style |
| conv4_1 | 31x31 | object parts | style (high-level) |
| conv4_2 | 31x31 | object parts | **content** |
| conv5_1 | 63x63 | semantic objects | style (semantics) |

**Style -> shallow.** Artistic style is local: brushstrokes, color blobs, repeated textures. Neurons with **small receptive fields** capture exactly these. Reading style from shallow layers (and collapsing location with the Gram matrix) gives a description of texture that does not care where it occurs.

**Content -> deep.** Content is the global arrangement of objects. Neurons with **large receptive fields** encode that semantic layout. Because deep feature maps still retain *where* things are, they make a good content target.

**Why conv4_2 for content.** It is a deliberate sweet spot from the original paper: **deep enough** to encode semantic structure (objects, scene layout), but **not so deep** that fine spatial detail is lost. A much deeper layer would blur the geometry; a much shallower one would lock in pixel-level appearance and fight the style transfer.

**Why multiple style layers.** Each layer captures texture at a different scale - conv1_1 fine grain, conv5_1 large motifs. Summing style loss over conv1_1 ... conv5_1 yields **multi-scale** style. Using only conv1_1 gives fine-grain texture; combining all five gives the rich, layered look that makes NST convincing.

> [!INTUITION] Small receptive field = "what does a brushstroke look like" (style). Large receptive field = "what is the scene" (content).

descriptive.q: Compare LBFGS and Adam for optimization. Explain why LBFGS is preferred for the image-optimization in NST but Adam is preferred for training neural-network weights, referencing batching, curvature, dimensionality, and convergence.
descriptive.answer:
**The two optimizers.**

| | Adam (first-order) | LBFGS (quasi-Newton) |
|---|---|---|
| Information used | gradient only (with momentum + adaptive scaling) | gradient **plus** an approximation of curvature (second-order) |
| Batching | works with mini-batches | requires a **full-batch** evaluation each step |
| Sweet spot | high-dimensional, noisy objectives | smooth, low-dimensional objectives |
| Convergence on smooth problems | slower (~500-1000 iters in NST) | faster (~300 iters in NST) |

**Why LBFGS wins in NST.** The whole 'dataset' is a **single image**, so there is no need for mini-batching - a full-batch step is cheap and exact. The loss surface over pixels is **smooth**, so LBFGS's curvature estimate points it efficiently toward minima, converging in far fewer iterations. The problem is effectively low-dimensional and deterministic, which is precisely LBFGS's regime.

**Why Adam wins for training weights.** Training a network means optimizing **millions of parameters** over **millions of examples**, which forces stochastic **mini-batches**. Each mini-batch gradient is noisy, and a full-batch second-order method is infeasible at that scale and brittle under noise. Adam's per-parameter adaptive step sizes and momentum make it robust and general-purpose in that high-dimensional, stochastic setting, even if it needs more iterations on a perfectly smooth toy problem.

> [!KEY] LBFGS: few parameters, full batch, smooth -> use curvature, converge fast (NST image). Adam: many parameters, mini-batches, noisy -> robust first-order, the default for training weights.

> [!WARNING] A common confusion is thinking LBFGS is "always better because it uses second-order info." It is better only when the objective is smooth and full-batch evaluation is affordable - both true for a single image, both false for large-scale weight training.

topics.title: Hands-on: Shakespeare Text Generator
topics.summary: Build a character-level Shakespeare text generator with an LSTM: feed characters in one at a time, predict the next, and loop the model's own output back as input. The session centers on how the LSTM's gated cell state preserves long-range memory and how temperature (plus top-k and nucleus) sampling trades coherence against creativity at generation time.

notes.heading: The Big Picture: Writing Shakespeare One Character at a Time
notes.body:
Imagine a typing assistant that has read all of Shakespeare and only ever answers one question: *given the characters so far, what is the single most likely next character?* That is the entire job of our model. To produce a whole play we just ask that question over and over, each time gluing the predicted character onto the end and feeding the longer string back in. This loop, predict a piece, append it, feed it back, is called **autoregressive generation**.

> [!INTUITION] The model never "writes a sentence." It writes a letter, then re-reads everything including that new letter, then writes the next letter. Coherent words and lines *emerge* from thousands of these tiny one-step decisions.

We work at the level of **characters**, not words. The vocabulary is therefore tiny, roughly 65 distinct symbols (letters, digits, punctuation, spaces, newlines) in Tiny Shakespeare. The payoff is striking: the model gradually learns a hierarchy entirely on its own, **characters to spelling to words to grammar to style**, with no dictionary and no tokenizer.

[[fig:rnn-unrolled|The same cell is reused at every character position; during generation the character predicted at one step becomes the input at the next.]]

> [!KEY] Generation is a loop. Seed the model with a prompt like "HAMLET:", predict the next character, append it, and repeat. The training objective (predict the next character) and the generation loop are two sides of the same coin.

notes.heading: Inside the LSTM Cell: Three Gates and a Memory
notes.body:
A plain recurrent network keeps a single running summary (the hidden state) and rewrites it from scratch at every step, so old information is quickly painted over. An **LSTM** (Long Short-Term Memory) fixes this by carrying a separate **memory line**, the *cell state*, and using small neural "valves" called **gates** to decide what to erase, what to add, and what to reveal.

[[fig:lstm-cell]]

At each step the cell looks at the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ (the short-term summary so far) and the current input $x_t$ (this character's embedding), and computes three gates:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \qquad i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \qquad o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

Here $\sigma$ is the **sigmoid**, which squashes any number into the range $0$ to $1$; think of each gate value as a dial from "fully closed" (0) to "fully open" (1). $W_f, W_i, W_o$ are learned weight matrices and $b_f, b_i, b_o$ are biases, while $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ just means we stack the old summary and the new input into one vector.

- $f_t$ is the **forget gate**, how much of the old memory to keep.
- $i_t$ is the **input gate**, how much of the new candidate to write in.
- $o_t$ is the **output gate**, how much of the memory to reveal as this step's hidden state.

The new content to *consider* writing is the candidate $\tilde{c}_t = \tanh(W_c [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c)$, where $\tanh$ squashes values into $-1$ to $1$. Then the memory and the visible output update as:

$$c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t \qquad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)$$

The symbol $\odot$ means **element-wise multiply** (multiply matching positions). So $c_t$, the new cell state (long-term memory), is the old memory $c_{t-1}$ scaled by how much we keep, plus the new candidate scaled by how much we let in. The hidden state $h_t$ (what the next layer and the output see) is that memory passed through $\tanh$ and filtered by the output gate.

> [!MATH] In words: "Keep this fraction of what I remembered, add this fraction of what I just saw, then show this fraction of the result." Three independent dials, all learned from data.

> [!EXAMPLE] Suppose one dimension of the cell state is tracking "who is currently speaking." While Hamlet speaks, the forget gate on that dimension stays near $1$ (keep remembering). The instant a new speaker label appears, the network learns to push $f_t \approx 0$ there, wiping the old speaker so the new context can be written in.

notes.heading: The Cell-State Highway: Why LSTMs Beat Vanilla RNNs
notes.body:
Why go to all this gate trouble? Because of a problem that cripples plain RNNs over long sequences: the **vanishing (and exploding) gradient**.

When we train on a sequence of length $T$, the learning signal (gradient) has to travel backward from the end all the way to the start. In a vanilla RNN with update $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$, that backward trip multiplies by the same recurrent matrix $W_{hh}$ at every step:

$$\frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_1} = \prod_{t=2}^{T} W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}\big(\tanh'(z_t)\big)$$

Do not panic at the symbols: $\frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_1}$ just measures "how much the state at the end depends on the state at the start," and the $\prod$ (product) says we multiply one factor per time step. The size of each factor is governed by the **spectral radius** $\rho(W_{hh})$, loosely the largest stretch factor of the matrix.

> [!WARNING] If $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$ the gradient shrinks exponentially: for $\rho = 0.9$ and $T = 100$, $0.9^{100} \approx 2.6\times10^{-5}$, essentially zero, so early characters get almost no learning signal. If $\rho > 1$ it blows up toward infinity. Repeated multiplication is the villain.

The LSTM swaps that **multiplicative** chain for the **additive** cell-state update. Following the memory line backward, the gradient passes mostly through the forget gate:

$$\frac{\partial c_t}{\partial c_{t-1}} \approx f_t$$

When the context is worth keeping, the network learns $f_t \approx 1$, so the gradient sails across hundreds of steps without being crushed. That is the **gradient highway**.

[[fig:cell-state-highway]]

LSTMs solve *vanishing* gradients architecturally. They do **not** automatically solve *exploding* gradients; for that we still use **gradient clipping**: `clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=5)` rescales all gradients if their combined norm exceeds 5, preventing one catastrophic update from destabilizing training.

> [!KEY] Additive memory update = gradient highway (fixes vanishing). Gradient clipping = a safety rail on the size of updates (fixes exploding). They are different tools for the two opposite failure modes.

notes.heading: From Characters to Vectors: Embeddings and the Full Pipeline
notes.body:
A character like "e" is just an index, say number 17 in our vocabulary. The network cannot multiply by the letter "e," so we first turn each character into a list of numbers it *can* work with. There are two ways to do this:

| | One-hot encoding | Learned embedding |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | sparse, length $V$ (one 1, rest 0) | dense, length $d$ (e.g. $d=128$) |
| Parameters | none (fixed) | $V \times d$ learned values |
| Similarity | every character equally far apart | similar characters end up near each other |

Here $V$ is the **vocabulary size** (about 65) and $d$ is the chosen **embedding dimension**. `nn.Embedding(V, 128)` is simply a lookup table: character index $i$ returns row $i$ of a weight matrix, and that row is updated by backpropagation like any other weight. It is mathematically the same as multiplying a one-hot vector $e_i$ by a weight matrix $W$ (which picks out row $i$), but computed as a fast lookup instead of a full matrix multiply.

> [!INTUITION] Because the rows are learned, the model can discover that "e" and "E" play similar roles and nudge their vectors close together, something a fixed one-hot encoding can never express.

The full model is a short stack:

**Embedding -> 2-layer LSTM (hidden size 256) -> Linear output layer**

The Linear layer maps each step's hidden state $h_t$ to $V$ raw scores called **logits**, one score per possible next character. These logits feed the loss during training and the sampler during generation.

> [!WARNING] When batching sequences of different lengths we **pad** the short ones with a PAD token so the tensors line up, and tell the loss to ignore those positions (`ignore_index` in CrossEntropyLoss). If you forget, the model wastes capacity predicting padding, and may even emit stretches of blank padding at inference time.

notes.heading: Sampling and Temperature: The Heart of Generation
notes.body:
After the Linear layer we have logits, raw scores for each of the ~65 characters. A **softmax** turns them into probabilities, and we draw the next character at random *in proportion to those probabilities*. Drawing randomly (rather than always taking the top score) is what makes the text feel alive.

**Temperature** $T$ is a single knob that reshapes the distribution before we sample. We divide every logit by $T$, then softmax:

$$p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}$$

Here $z_i$ is the logit (score) for character $i$, and $p_i$ is its probability after temperature; the denominator just makes the probabilities sum to 1.

> [!MATH] Dividing by a small $T$ (say 0.2) multiplies every score by 5, *exaggerating* the gaps so the best character dominates: a peaked, conservative distribution. Dividing by a large $T$ (say 2.0) *shrinks* the gaps so all characters look similar: a flat, adventurous distribution.

> [!ANALOGY] Temperature is like the boldness dial on an improv actor. Low temperature: they stick to the safe, obvious line (coherent but repetitive). High temperature: they take wild risks (creative but often nonsense). $T \approx 0.8$ is the sweet spot most people prefer.

| Temperature | Distribution shape | Generated text |
|---|---|---|
| $T=0$ | one-hot (greedy / argmax) | deterministic; often loops |
| $T=0.2$ | very peaked | conservative, repetitive but coherent |
| $T=0.5$ | moderately peaked | safe, grammatical, predictable |
| $T=0.8$ | slightly spread | **recommended**, balanced |
| $T=1.0$ | the raw model distribution | unmodified sampling |
| $T=1.5$ | flattened | creative but often incoherent |
| $T=2.0$ | near-uniform | mostly random characters |

> [!WARNING] **Greedy decoding** ($T \to 0$, always argmax) sounds safe but is the worst for variety: with no randomness the model falls into loops like "the the the the." Sampling injects randomness *proportional to the model's confidence*, almost deterministic when one character clearly dominates, genuinely varied when several are plausible.

The inference loop in code: take the last 100 characters as context, run the model, grab the final-step logits, divide by temperature, softmax, then `torch.multinomial` to sample one character, and repeat.

notes.heading: Beyond Temperature: Top-k and Nucleus Sampling
notes.body:
Temperature reshapes the *whole* distribution but never removes the long tail of absurd characters; at high $T$ it can still occasionally pick something ridiculous. Two smarter strategies prune the tail first.

1. **Top-k sampling.** Keep only the $k$ highest-scoring characters (e.g. $k=40$), zero out the rest, renormalize, then sample. This blocks the junk tail but uses a *fixed* cut-off regardless of how confident the model is.
2. **Nucleus (top-p) sampling.** Keep the smallest set of characters whose probabilities *add up* to at least $p$ (e.g. $p=0.9$), then sample from just those. The set size **adapts**: when the model is sure, a couple of characters already reach $0.9$, so the set is tiny; when it is unsure, many characters are needed, so the set grows.

| Strategy | Candidate set | Adapts to confidence? |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | all characters, rescaled | no |
| Top-k | fixed top $k$ | no |
| Nucleus (top-p) | smallest set summing to $p$ | yes |

> [!KEY] Top-p is confidence-aware: a small set when the distribution is peaked, a large set when it is flat. That is why modern LLMs (GPT-style systems) reach for nucleus sampling by default. Temperature and top-p are often combined.

> [!EXAM] Be ready to say *which* method dynamically resizes its candidate pool (top-p), *which* uses a constant pool (top-k), and that pure temperature never drops any token at all, it only reweights them.

notes.heading: Measuring Quality: Perplexity and a Debugging Checklist
notes.body:
Reading samples by eye is fun but subjective. The standard number for language models is **perplexity**, which measures how *surprised* the model is by held-out text. It is just the exponential of the cross-entropy loss:

$$\mathrm{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right) = e^{H}$$

Here $N$ is the number of characters, $p(x_i \mid x_{<i})$ is the probability the model gave the *true* next character given everything before it, and $H$ is the average cross-entropy loss. Lower is better.

> [!INTUITION] $\mathrm{PPL} = k$ means that, on average, the model is as uncertain as if it were guessing uniformly among $k$ equally likely characters. Random guessing over 65 characters gives $\mathrm{PPL} = 65$; a decent CharLSTM might reach $\mathrm{PPL} \approx 4$, as if narrowing each choice down to about 4 options.

To track it, after each epoch compute `ppl = math.exp(val_loss)` and plot it next to the loss curve; you should watch perplexity fall from ~65 toward single digits as samples go from gibberish to recognizable Shakespeare.

When things go wrong, this checklist maps symptoms to fixes:

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Loss flat from epoch 1 | learning rate near 0 / bad optimizer setup | check optimizer; try LR = 0.002 |
| Loss becomes NaN | exploding gradients | add `clip_grad_norm_(..., 5)` |
| Only spaces / newlines generated | temperature too low or PAD leaking in | raise $T$; exclude PAD from sampling |
| Endless repetition | greedy decoding / $T$ too low | sample with $T \approx 0.8$ |
| Very slow (>2 min/epoch) | running on CPU | move model and data to GPU |

> [!EXAM] A favourite trap: perplexity is **not** accuracy. $\mathrm{PPL}=4$ does not mean "4% correct"; it means an effective uniform choice among 4 characters, and corresponds to a cross-entropy of $\ln 4 \approx 1.39$ nats, not 4.

notes.heading: From CharLSTM to GPT: The Same Idea, Scaled Up
notes.body:
The model you built has only about 2 million parameters and trains in minutes, yet it shares its DNA with the largest language models. Compare the family tree:

| Model | Parameters | Data | Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|
| CharLSTM (this session) | ~2M | 1.1 MB | 2-layer LSTM |
| Karpathy's char-rnn | ~10M | 1 MB | 3-layer LSTM |
| GPT-1 (2018) | 117M | BookCorpus | 12-layer Transformer |
| GPT-2 (2019) | 1.5B | WebText | 48-layer Transformer |
| GPT-3 (2020) | 175B | 570 GB | 96-layer Transformer |

The big jump was the **architecture change from LSTM to Transformer**. An LSTM must process characters strictly one after another, so it is hard to parallelize across a long sequence; a Transformer looks at all positions at once via attention, which lets it train on vastly more data far faster.

> [!KEY] What did **not** change: the training objective (predict the next token), the cross-entropy loss, and the autoregressive generation loop with temperature / top-p sampling are *identical* to what you used today. You have already built a tiny GPT in spirit.

| Architecture | Gates | Gradient flow | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vanilla RNN | none | vanishes past ~20 steps | very short sequences |
| LSTM | 3 (f, i, o) | cell-state highway | long sequences; default choice |
| GRU | 2 (r, z) | good | faster training, similar quality |
| Bi-LSTM | 3 + reverse | excellent | encoding, not generation |
| Transformer | none | direct via attention | long-range, parallelizable |

> [!WARNING] A bidirectional LSTM reads the sequence both ways, so it cannot be used for left-to-right *generation*: it would need to see the very future you are trying to predict. It is for encoding and understanding, not autoregressive writing.

quiz.q: During sampling you divide the logits by a temperature $T = 0.2$ before the softmax. Compared with $T = 1.0$, what happens to the probability distribution over the next character?
options: It becomes flatter, with every character moving toward equal probability, making the output more creative
It becomes more peaked, the top characters gain even more probability, so the output is conservative and repetitive
It is unchanged, because dividing every logit by the same constant cancels out inside the softmax
It instantly becomes a true one-hot vector, identical to taking the argmax
quiz.explanation: Dividing logits by $T=0.2$ multiplies every score by 5, which exaggerates the gaps and sharpens the distribution (peaked, conservative). Flattening is what happens for $T>1$. It is not unchanged: softmax is nonlinear, so rescaling the logits genuinely changes the probabilities. A true one-hot vector only appears in the limit $T \to 0$, not at $T=0.2$.

quiz.q: A student sets $T=0$ (always pick the argmax) and finds the model outputs "the the the the...". What is the best explanation?
options: The model was undertrained; a fully trained model would never repeat under greedy decoding
Greedy decoding is deterministic and tends to fall into high-probability loops, with no randomness to escape them
Dividing the logits by zero produced NaNs that the model rendered as repeated words
The forget gate failed to activate, so the cell state never cleared
quiz.explanation: Greedy decoding always picks the single most likely character, so it can lock into a high-probability loop with no randomness to break out. Even well-trained models loop under greedy decoding, so undertraining is not the cause. $T=0$ is implemented as argmax (not a literal division by zero). The forget gate is unrelated to this decoding behavior.

quiz.q: `clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=5)` is applied during training. Which statement is correct?
options: It prevents vanishing gradients by keeping the gradient norm at least 5
It clips each weight to lie within $[-5, 5]$ after the optimizer step
It supplies the LSTM's long-term memory, replacing the need for the gates
It rescales gradients when their global norm exceeds 5, guarding against exploding gradients, but does not fix vanishing gradients
quiz.explanation: Gradient clipping rescales the gradients (not the weights) when their combined norm passes the threshold, which addresses *exploding* gradients. Vanishing gradients are handled by the LSTM architecture (the additive cell state), not by clipping. It does not enforce a minimum norm, and the model's memory comes from the cell state and gates, not from clipping.

quiz.q: Why does the LSTM cell-state update $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ help gradients flow across hundreds of timesteps?
options: The cell state is detached from the computation graph, so gradients never reach it
The multiplication by the forget gate $f_t$ guarantees the gradient grows at every step
The $\tanh$ squashing of the candidate keeps every gradient exactly equal to 1
The additive update lets gradients pass backward through $c_{t-1}$ without being repeatedly multiplied by a learned weight matrix
quiz.explanation: The key is the additive path: $\partial c_t / \partial c_{t-1} \approx f_t$, so the gradient is scaled by a learnable dial near 1 rather than multiplied by a weight matrix at every step. A forget gate near 1 *preserves* the gradient, it does not guarantee growth. The $\tanh$ does not fix gradients at 1, and detaching the cell state would block learning entirely.

quiz.q: In a vanilla RNN the backward gradient across $T$ steps scales like a product of $W_{hh}$ terms. With spectral radius $\rho(W_{hh}) = 0.9$ and $T = 100$, the gradient at the first step is about $2.6\times10^{-5}$. What does this illustrate?
options: Vanishing gradients: repeated multiplication by a factor below 1 shrinks the signal exponentially, so early steps barely learn
Exploding gradients: the signal grows because $0.9^{100}$ is large
A bug, because gradients should never change magnitude during backpropagation
That gradient clipping at 5 would have prevented the shrinkage
quiz.explanation: $0.9^{100} \approx 2.6\times10^{-5}$ is tiny, the hallmark of vanishing gradients from repeated multiplication by a factor below 1. It is not exploding (that needs $\rho>1$). Gradient magnitude changing across layers is expected from the chain rule, not a bug. Clipping caps *large* norms; it cannot rescue an already-vanished gradient.

quiz.q: A trained CharLSTM reports a validation perplexity of 4 on Tiny Shakespeare (65-character vocabulary). How should you read that number?
options: The model gets 4% of characters correct
Its cross-entropy loss equals 4 nats per character
The model has 4 layers of effective depth
At each step it is about as uncertain as choosing uniformly among 4 characters, far better than the 65 of random guessing
quiz.explanation: Perplexity is $e^{H}$, and $\mathrm{PPL}=4$ means the model is as uncertain as a uniform choice among 4 options, much better than $\mathrm{PPL}=65$ for random guessing. It is not an accuracy percentage and has nothing to do with layer count. The corresponding cross-entropy is $\ln 4 \approx 1.39$ nats, not 4.

quiz.q: Which sampling method automatically uses a small candidate set when the model is confident and a larger set when it is unsure?
options: Greedy decoding, which keeps exactly one token
Top-k sampling, which always keeps exactly $k$ tokens regardless of the distribution
Nucleus (top-p) sampling, which keeps the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds $p$
Pure temperature sampling, which keeps all tokens but rescales them
quiz.explanation: Nucleus sampling adapts its set size to the distribution: a peaked distribution reaches the cumulative threshold $p$ with few tokens, a flat one needs many. Top-k uses a fixed $k$, greedy keeps exactly one token, and temperature sampling never prunes, it keeps the full vocabulary and only reweights it.

quiz.q: When the text transitions from one speaker to another (e.g. a new "HAMLET:" line begins), which LSTM mechanism is best suited to clear the previous speaker's stored context?
options: The forget gate driving $f_t$ toward 0 for the dimensions holding the old speaker's information
The input gate driving $i_t$ toward 0 so that nothing new is written
The output gate masking the hidden state so the cell state is overwritten
The embedding layer assigning the new speaker a zero vector
quiz.explanation: The forget gate controls how much of $c_{t-1}$ to retain; pushing $f_t \to 0$ on the relevant dimensions erases the old speaker. The input gate governs *writing new* information, not erasing old. The output gate only controls what is exposed, not the stored content. The embedding layer encodes inputs and does not erase memory.

quiz.q: The model uses `nn.Embedding(vocab_size, 128)` instead of feeding one-hot vectors. Which statement is most accurate?
options: The embedding randomly compresses characters and is never updated during training
One-hot vectors are denser and use fewer parameters than embeddings
The embedding is a learned lookup table, mathematically a linear layer applied to a one-hot vector, that can place similar characters near each other
Embeddings remove the need for the final Linear output layer
quiz.explanation: An embedding is $W e_i$ for one-hot $e_i$, implemented as a lookup of row $i$ and updated by backpropagation, so similar characters can be learned to lie near each other. It is definitely updated during training. One-hot vectors are sparse with no learnable parameters, not dense. The output Linear layer is still needed to map the hidden state to per-character logits.

quiz.q: Prompted with "HAM", the model produces "HAMLE". What does this most directly demonstrate?
options: It has learned local character n-gram statistics but generates one character at a time with no lookahead to plan a whole word
It has memorized the entire training corpus verbatim
It uses bidirectional context, reading future characters before predicting
Temperature must have been set to 0 for this to happen
quiz.explanation: Continuing "HAM" into "HAMLE" shows the model captured local character statistics, yet it produces each character independently with no plan for the whole word. It does not imply verbatim memorization of the corpus. Generation is strictly left-to-right (causal), so it is not bidirectional, and this behavior is independent of the temperature setting.

quiz.q: Compared with a word-level model, a key DISADVANTAGE of character-level modeling is that:
options: It needs a much larger vocabulary, often 50,000+ tokens
It must capture dependencies across many more time steps, since a single word spans several characters
It cannot handle invented words or source code
It requires a separate tokenizer to be trained first
quiz.explanation: Because each word is several characters, word-level structure becomes a long-range dependency at the character level, forcing the model to remember across far more steps. The other options are actually *advantages* of character-level modeling: a tiny (~65) vocabulary rather than 50k+, the ability to handle novel words and code, and no tokenizer required.

quiz.q: Why is sampling next characters in proportion to the model's probabilities (rather than taking the argmax) described as injecting randomness "proportional to confidence"?
options: Because the softmax converts logits into a one-hot vector before sampling
Because multinomial sampling ignores the probabilities and picks uniformly at random
When the model is confident one character dominates the distribution, so sampling almost always picks it; when unsure, probability is spread so several characters are plausible
Because the temperature is recomputed for every character based on the current loss
quiz.explanation: Sampling honors the shape of the distribution: a peaked (confident) distribution is nearly deterministic, while a flat (uncertain) one yields varied draws. Multinomial sampling respects the probabilities rather than choosing uniformly. Softmax produces a full distribution, not a one-hot vector, and temperature is a fixed hyperparameter at inference, not recomputed per character from the loss.

descriptive.q: Explain why the LSTM cell state acts as a "gradient highway" while a vanilla RNN's hidden state does not. Reference both gradient expressions.
descriptive.answer:
**Short answer:** A vanilla RNN propagates gradients backward through a *repeated multiplication* by the same recurrent weight matrix, which shrinks or grows the signal exponentially with sequence length. The LSTM replaces that multiplicative chain with an *additive* cell-state update whose backward path is governed by the forget gate, letting gradients pass almost unchanged across hundreds of steps.

**Vanilla RNN.** The state update is $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$. To learn from a loss at the end of a length-$T$ sequence, backpropagation computes

$$\frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_1} = \prod_{t=2}^{T} W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(\tanh'(z_t)).$$

The product ($\prod$) has one factor per step. Its magnitude is set by the **spectral radius** $\rho(W_{hh})$, the largest stretch factor of the matrix. If $\rho < 1$ the gradient decays like $\rho^{T}$; for $\rho=0.9, T=100$ that is about $2.6\times10^{-5}$, so the first characters receive essentially no learning signal (**vanishing**). If $\rho>1$ it diverges (**exploding**).

**LSTM.** The memory line updates additively: $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$, where $\odot$ is element-wise multiply and $f_t \in (0,1)$ is the forget gate. Differentiating along the memory path,

$$\frac{\partial c_t}{\partial c_{t-1}} \approx f_t.$$

There is no repeated multiplication by a learned weight matrix on this path. When the network decides the context matters it sets $f_t \approx 1$, and the gradient flows backward almost undiminished, the **gradient highway**. When context should be dropped it sets $f_t \approx 0$, deliberately cutting the signal there.

> [!KEY] Multiplicative chain (RNN) leads to exponential vanish/explode; additive memory (LSTM) scales the gradient by a learned dial near 1, enabling long-range learning. Note the LSTM fixes *vanishing* architecturally; *exploding* is still handled separately by gradient clipping.

[[fig:cell-state-highway]]

descriptive.q: Derive the effect of temperature on the softmax. What happens as $T \to 0$ and $T \to \infty$, and why is $T \approx 0.8$ usually preferred?
descriptive.answer:
We sample the next character from $p_i = \dfrac{\exp(z_i/T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j/T)}$, where $z_i$ is the logit for character $i$ and $T>0$ is the temperature.

**Effect of $T$.** Dividing the logits by $T$ rescales the *gaps* between them before the softmax exponentiates. For two characters with logits $z_a > z_b$, their probability ratio is

$$\frac{p_a}{p_b} = \exp\!\left(\frac{z_a - z_b}{T}\right).$$

- **$T < 1$** multiplies the gap $z_a - z_b$ by $1/T > 1$, so the ratio grows and the distribution **sharpens** (peaky, conservative).
- **$T > 1$** divides the gap, so the ratio shrinks toward 1 and the distribution **flattens** (uniform, adventurous).

**Limits.**
- As $T \to 0^{+}$: the largest logit's probability tends to 1 and all others to 0. The distribution becomes one-hot, which is **greedy / argmax** decoding, fully deterministic and prone to loops.
- As $T \to \infty$: every $z_i/T \to 0$, so $\exp(\cdot)\to 1$ and all characters approach equal probability $1/V$, output becomes random noise.

**Why $T \approx 0.8$.** It is a compromise on the **coherence vs diversity** trade-off. Too low and the text is grammatical but repetitive and loop-prone; too high and it is creative but degenerates into nonsense and misspellings. Around $0.8$ the sampler keeps the model mostly on track while still allowing enough variation to read naturally and avoid loops.

> [!EXAMPLE] Logits $z = [2, 1, 0]$. At $T=1$ the probabilities are about $[0.67, 0.24, 0.09]$. At $T=0.5$ (sharper) they are about $[0.87, 0.12, 0.02]$. At $T=2$ (flatter) they are about $[0.51, 0.31, 0.19]$. Same scores, very different boldness.

> [!WARNING] Temperature reweights every character but never deletes any; even at $T=0.8$ a very unlikely character can occasionally be drawn. To hard-prune the tail you need top-k or top-p on top of temperature.

descriptive.q: Compare temperature, top-k, and nucleus (top-p) sampling. When does each fail, and why do modern LLMs prefer top-p?
descriptive.answer:
All three control the **diversity** of generated text but cut the distribution in different ways.

| Method | What it does | Candidate set | Main weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | scales logits by $1/T$, then softmax over the full vocab | all characters | never removes the long tail; high $T$ can still emit junk |
| Top-k | keep the $k$ highest logits, renormalize, sample | fixed size $k$ | wrong size for the moment: too big when confident, too small when unsure |
| Nucleus (top-p) | keep the smallest set with cumulative probability $\ge p$ | adapts to the distribution | needs a sensible $p$; still has a tunable cut-off |

**Where each fails.**
- **Temperature alone:** because the whole vocabulary stays in play, at high $T$ it occasionally samples an absurd character and breaks words; at low $T$ it collapses toward greedy and loops.
- **Top-k:** the fixed $k$ ignores context. If the model is almost certain (one character at 0.99), a large $k$ still admits many near-zero distractors; if the model is genuinely torn among 100 options, a small $k$ chops off plausible ones.
- **Top-p:** mostly robust, but a poorly chosen $p$ that is too high reintroduces the tail, while too low makes it overly conservative.

**Why top-p wins for LLMs.** Nucleus sampling is **confidence-adaptive**: when the model is sure, the cumulative mass reaches $p$ after one or two characters (tiny set, near-greedy, coherent); when it is unsure it admits many characters (large set, diverse). This automatically matches the candidate pool to how peaked the distribution is, exactly the behavior you want across the wildly varying contexts in real text. It is usually combined with a moderate temperature.

> [!KEY] Temperature reshapes everything and prunes nothing; top-k prunes a fixed amount; top-p prunes adaptively. That adaptivity is why GPT-style models default to nucleus sampling.

descriptive.q: Compare character-level and word-level language modeling. Give two advantages and two disadvantages of the character-level approach, and explain the long-range dependency implication.
descriptive.answer:
**Character-level** models predict the next *character* from a tiny vocabulary (~65 symbols); **word-level** models predict the next *word/token* from a large vocabulary (50,000+).

**Advantages of character-level**
1. **No tokenizer and an open vocabulary.** It works directly on any text, including invented words, code, math, URLs and markup, and can never hit an "unknown word" because every string is just characters.
2. **Tiny, fixed vocabulary.** About 65 characters versus 50k+ tokens means a small embedding table and a small output softmax (cheap per-step classification).

**Disadvantages of character-level**
1. **Much longer sequences for the same content.** A sentence is several times more characters than words, so the model must remember dependencies across many more time steps.
2. **More compute per "semantic unit."** Generating one word costs several forward passes (one per character), making training and inference slower per meaningful chunk.

**Long-range dependency implication.** Because a single word spans several characters, *word-level structure becomes a long-range dependency at the character level*. To spell a word correctly and keep grammar consistent, the model must carry information across far more steps than a word-level model would, roughly "100 characters" instead of "20 words" to cover the same span. This is precisely why an LSTM, with its gradient-highway cell state, is essential here: a vanilla RNN's gradients would vanish long before reaching back across a whole clause.

> [!EXAM] A common framing: "Why is the LSTM, rather than a vanilla RNN, the natural choice for character-level modeling?" Answer: because character-level turns ordinary word and grammar structure into long-range dependencies, and only the gated cell state preserves gradients over those distances.

descriptive.q: Why sample instead of greedily decoding? What goes wrong with greedy decoding, and how do temperature and top-k/top-p navigate the coherence-diversity trade-off?
descriptive.answer:
**Greedy decoding** takes the single highest-probability character at every step. Because it is fully deterministic and only looks one step ahead, it tends to fall into **high-probability attractor loops**, producing text like "the the the the" or a repeated phrase, and it yields the *same* output every time from a given seed. There is no randomness available to break out of a loop, and locally optimal characters do not add up to a globally good sentence (the model has no lookahead to plan).

**Sampling** instead draws the next character at random *in proportion to the model's probabilities*. This injects randomness **proportional to confidence**: when one character clearly dominates, sampling almost always picks it (so confident predictions stay reliable); when several characters are plausible, sampling spreads its choices (so the text varies and avoids loops). The result reads as natural, non-repetitive prose.

**Navigating coherence vs diversity.**

| Knob | Push toward coherence | Push toward diversity |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature $T$ | lower $T$ (sharpen) | higher $T$ (flatten) |
| Top-k | smaller $k$ | larger $k$ |
| Top-p | smaller $p$ | larger $p$ |

- **Temperature** trades off smoothly: low $T$ gives coherent but repetitive text (toward greedy); high $T$ gives creative but incoherent text.
- **Top-k / top-p** add a guardrail by *removing the absurd tail* before sampling, so you can keep diversity without the rare nonsense character; top-p does this adaptively.

> [!KEY] Greedy maximizes local probability and loses globally (loops, no variety). Sampling honors the distribution's shape; temperature and top-k/top-p are the dials that set where you sit on the coherence-diversity spectrum. $T \approx 0.8$ (optionally with top-p) is the usual sweet spot.

descriptive.q: Walk through the full CharLSTM pipeline from a seed string to a generated character, and explain where gradient clipping and perplexity fit in. How does this scale to GPT?
descriptive.answer:
**Generation pipeline (one step).** Starting from a seed such as "HAMLET:":
1. **Encode.** Map each character to its integer index via `char2idx`, and keep the last ~100 indices as context (the model's window).
2. **Embed.** `nn.Embedding(V, 128)` looks up a dense 128-dim vector per character, a learned lookup table equivalent to $W e_i$ for one-hot $e_i$.
3. **Recur.** The 2-layer LSTM (hidden size 256) processes the sequence; at each step it updates its cell state $c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t$ and hidden state $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)$.
4. **Project.** A Linear layer maps the final step's $h_t$ to $V$ **logits**, one raw score per possible next character.
5. **Temper and sample.** Divide the logits by temperature $T$, apply softmax to get probabilities $p_i = \exp(z_i/T)/\sum_j \exp(z_j/T)$, then draw one character with `torch.multinomial` (optionally after top-k or top-p pruning).
6. **Append and loop.** Glue the new character onto the context and return to step 1.

**Where training pieces fit.**
- **Training** uses the *same* forward pass, but instead of sampling it compares the logits with the true next character using cross-entropy loss (which takes raw logits, not softmaxed probabilities), then backpropagates.
- **Gradient clipping**, `clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=5)`, sits between `loss.backward()` and `optimizer.step()`. It rescales gradients whose global norm exceeds 5, preventing a single exploding update from producing NaNs.
- **Perplexity**, $\mathrm{PPL} = e^{H}$ where $H$ is the validation cross-entropy, is computed each epoch (`math.exp(val_loss)`) to track progress; it falls from about 65 (random over the vocabulary) toward single digits as the samples improve.

**Scaling to GPT.** Swap the LSTM for a Transformer and the same recipe scales to billions of parameters: the next-token objective, cross-entropy loss, and autoregressive sample-and-append loop are unchanged. The Transformer's advantage is that it processes all positions in parallel through attention (no strictly sequential recurrence), so it trains efficiently on far more data, which is what took models from about 2M parameters (this session) to GPT-3's 175B.

> [!KEY] The CharLSTM you built is GPT in miniature: an identical training loop and generation paradigm, just a recurrent core instead of an attention core, and megabytes of data instead of hundreds of gigabytes.

topics.title: Hands-on: Seq2Seq Translation
topics.summary: Build an English to French translator with an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) RNN, see why a single fixed-size context vector becomes a bottleneck, and add Bahdanau attention so the decoder builds a fresh, focused summary of the source at every output word. Along the way: teacher forcing and exposure bias, reading attention heatmaps as learned word alignment, greedy vs beam-search decoding, and scoring quality with BLEU.

notes.heading: The job: turn one sequence into another
notes.body:
Machine translation is a **sequence-to-sequence** problem: you read a sentence in one language and produce a sentence in another. The two sides usually have **different lengths** (5 English words might become 7 French words), and the words do not line up one-for-one. So we need an architecture that maps a variable-length input to a variable-length output.

The classic answer is an **encoder-decoder** (also called **seq2seq**). It is literally two RNNs glued together:

- The **encoder** reads the source sentence one word at a time and updates its hidden state. After the last word, its hidden state is a summary of the whole sentence.
- The **decoder** is a second RNN that starts from that summary and generates the target sentence one word at a time, feeding each word it produces back in as the next input.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Think of a human interpreter at a conference. First they *listen* to the whole sentence and form a mental picture of its meaning (the encoder). Then they *speak* the translation, word by word, working from that mental picture (the decoder). Listening and speaking are two separate phases.

[[fig:seq2seq]]

A few concrete choices this session makes:

- We write the source as $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x})$ — here each $x_i$ is one English word and $T_x$ is the number of source words. The target is $y = (y_1, \dots, y_{T_y})$, each $y_t$ one French word.
- The encoder uses a **GRU** (gated recurrent unit) rather than an LSTM, because a GRU has only **2 gates** (reset $r$ and update $z$) instead of the LSTM's 3, so it has fewer parameters and trains faster with comparable quality.
- The encoder is **bidirectional**: it reads the sentence left-to-right *and* right-to-left, then concatenates the two hidden states. So each position gets context from both sides. With hidden size $256$, each encoder output $h_i$ ends up $512$-dimensional ($256$ forward + $256$ backward).
- We keep **two separate embedding tables** — one that turns English word-ids into vectors, one for French — because the two vocabularies are different.

> [!KEY]
> Seq2seq = encoder RNN (reads source) + decoder RNN (writes target). The whole point of attention, below, is to fix *how the decoder gets information from the encoder*.

notes.heading: The bottleneck problem: one vector to carry everything
notes.body:
In the plain encoder-decoder, the only thing connecting the encoder to the decoder is the encoder's **final hidden state** — a single fixed-size vector (say 256 numbers). Everything the decoder will ever know about the source has to be squeezed into those 256 numbers: the meaning of each word, the word order, the grammar, who did what to whom.

> [!INTUITION]
> Imagine being told to summarise an entire paragraph using exactly one short fixed-length sticky note, and then someone has to reconstruct the paragraph word-for-word from only that note. For a short sentence it is fine. For a long one, details get overwritten — the note simply does not have room. That overflowing sticky note is the **information bottleneck**.

Why long sentences specifically? A 20-word sentence carries far more semantic information than a 256-dimensional vector can reliably hold. As the encoder keeps updating its single hidden state word by word, **later words tend to overwrite earlier ones**. Empirically, BLEU (the quality score, below) drops sharply once sentences pass roughly **20 words** — the model starts dropping or scrambling content from the beginning of the sentence.

> [!KEY]
> Without attention, the encoder must compress ALL source information — meaning, order, grammar — into ONE fixed-size vector. That single vector is a hard ceiling on how much the decoder can recover, and it is the reason performance collapses on long sentences.

This is exactly the problem the next sections solve: instead of forcing the decoder to live off one frozen summary, we let it look back at *every* encoder state, fresh, at every output step.

notes.heading: Attention: let the decoder look back at every word
notes.body:
**Attention** removes the bottleneck with a simple idea: keep *all* the encoder's per-word hidden states $h_1, \dots, h_{T_x}$ around, and let the decoder, at each output step, build a **custom summary of the source aimed at the word it is about to produce**.

> [!INTUITION]
> When the interpreter is about to say the French word for "cat", they mentally glance back at the word "cat" in the English sentence — not at the whole sentence equally. For the next French word they glance somewhere else. Attention is that re-focusing glance, made into math, and crucially it is **learned**, not hard-coded.

[[fig:attention-mechanism]]

At every decoder timestep $t$, attention runs **three steps**:

1. **Score** — measure how relevant each source position $i$ is to what the decoder is doing right now. This gives one raw number (a score) per source word.
2. **Normalise** — pass those scores through a softmax so they become **weights that are all positive and add up to 1** — a probability distribution over the source positions.
3. **Aggregate** — take a **weighted average** of the encoder states using those weights. The result is the **context vector** $c_t$: a summary of the source tilted toward the positions the decoder cares about right now.

The decoder then uses $c_t$ (combined with its own state and the previous output word) to predict the next French word.

> [!KEY]
> Attention replaces ONE fixed context vector with a DIFFERENT context vector at every output step, each one a focused, weighted blend of all the encoder's hidden states. Nothing is forced through a single bottleneck anymore.

Because the context is recomputed every step from the full set of encoder states, long-sentence performance no longer falls off a cliff — the decoder can always reach back to the exact words it needs.

notes.heading: Bahdanau attention, every symbol in plain English
notes.body:
Here is the actual Bahdanau (a.k.a. *additive*) attention, the three steps written as formulas. We gloss every symbol as we go.

**Step 1 - Score.** Compare the decoder's current state with each encoder state:
$$e_{ti} = v^\top \tanh\!\left(W_1\, s_{t-1} + W_2\, h_i\right), \quad i = 1, \dots, T_x$$

> [!MATH]
> In words: take the decoder's previous hidden state $s_{t-1}$ (where the decoder currently is in producing the output) and the $i$-th encoder state $h_i$ (what the model understood about source word $i$). Push each through its own weight matrix ($W_1$ and $W_2$), add them, squash with $\tanh$, and finally dot with a learned vector $v$ to collapse it to a single number $e_{ti}$ — the **compatibility score** between output step $t$ and source word $i$. $W_1$, $W_2$, and $v$ are *learned* parameters.

A **high** $e_{ti}$ means the decoder state and source word $i$ are highly compatible in the learned attention space — i.e. *source word $i$ is especially relevant for producing output word $t$*. This is the model's learned **word alignment** ("to generate *chat*, attend to *cat*").

**Step 2 - Normalise.** Turn the raw scores into weights:
$$\alpha_{ti} = \frac{\exp(e_{ti})}{\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{tj})}$$

> [!MATH]
> This is a softmax over the source positions. $\exp(\cdot)$ makes everything positive; dividing by the sum over all positions $j$ makes the weights add to 1. So $\alpha_{ti}$ is "the fraction of attention output word $t$ pays to source word $i$." For a fixed $t$, the $\alpha_{ti}$ over all $i$ form a probability distribution.

**Step 3 - Aggregate.** Blend the encoder states by those weights:
$$c_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{ti}\, h_i$$

> [!MATH]
> The context vector $c_t$ is a **weighted average** of the encoder hidden states $h_i$, weighted by attention $\alpha_{ti}$. Words the model deems relevant (high $\alpha$) contribute more. Because $t$ changes each step, $c_t$ is recomputed for every output word.

**Why "soft"?** This is **soft attention**: it spreads weight smoothly over *all* positions. The alternative, **hard attention**, would pick exactly one position. The decisive advantage:

> [!KEY]
> Soft attention is **fully differentiable** — gradients flow back through the $\alpha$ weights into the encoder, so the entire model trains end-to-end with ordinary backpropagation. Hard attention makes a discrete pick, which is not differentiable and needs reinforcement learning to train. That differentiability is why soft attention won.

For grounding, here are the tensor shapes for a batch of 64 sentences, max length 10, hidden size 256:

| Component | Symbol | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Encoder outputs (Bi-GRU) | $H$ | [64, 10, 512] |
| Attention scores | $e_t$ | [64, 10] |
| Attention weights | $\alpha_t$ | [64, 10] (sum to 1) |
| Context vector | $c_t$ | [64, 512] |
| Decoder hidden | $s_t$ | [64, 256] |
| Output logits | $y_t$ | [64, tgt vocab] |

notes.heading: Reading an attention heatmap: alignment you can see
notes.body:
Because every $\alpha_{ti}$ is a number between 0 and 1, you can stack them into a grid — output words down one axis, source words along the other — and colour each cell by its weight. That picture is an **attention heatmap**, and it is one of the most satisfying things to look at because it shows the **word alignment the model learned on its own**, without ever being told which word maps to which.

[[fig:attention-heatmap|Each row is one output (French) word; each column an input (English) word. Bright cells show where the decoder looked.]]

**Near-diagonal heatmap.** If the bright cells run roughly along the diagonal, the $i$-th output word mostly attends to the $i$-th input word — a **monotonic alignment**. For English to French this is expected, because both languages share broadly the same **Subject-Verb-Object (SVO)** word order, so words line up in nearly the same order.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Translating "I love cats" to "J'aime les chats", attention is roughly diagonal: *J'* and *aime* light up over *I love*, *chats* lights up over *cats*. The small off-diagonal blob, e.g. "black cat" becoming "chat noir" (adjective *after* the noun in French), is the model correctly handling a local reordering.

**Crossing / off-diagonal heatmap.** If the bright cells cross over each other, the two languages reorder words. The classic case is **German to English**: German often puts the verb at the end of a clause, so the heatmap shows long crossing lines as the decoder reaches far back (or forward) to find the verb.

> [!WARNING]
> A diagonal heatmap is a sign the model learned good alignment; a **diffuse, smeared** heatmap (attention spread thinly everywhere) is a warning sign — it often accompanies **hallucination**, where the output contains words unrelated to the source. The fix is usually more training or regularisers like label smoothing, not "more attention".

> [!EXAM]
> Expect to be shown a heatmap and asked what it implies about word-order alignment. Diagonal = monotonic / similar word order (EN-FR); crossing = reordering (DE-EN verb-final); smeared = poorly learned / hallucination risk.

notes.heading: Teacher forcing and exposure bias
notes.body:
When the decoder generates word $t$, it needs the *previous* word as input. During **training** you have two choices for that previous word:

- Feed the **real** previous target word from the reference translation - this is **teacher forcing**.
- Feed the word the model **itself just predicted** - this is free-running.

Teacher forcing is like a student doing a long division where, after each step, the teacher quietly corrects any mistake before the next step. The student never compounds errors, so they **converge much faster**. In this session teacher forcing is applied with probability around 0.5.

> [!WARNING]
> Teacher forcing creates **exposure bias**. At **test time** there is no teacher: the decoder must feed itself its own (sometimes wrong) previous prediction. But during training it almost always saw *correct* previous words. So at test time it faces a distribution of inputs it was never trained on, and one early mistake can snowball. This train/test mismatch is exposure bias.

> [!ANALOGY]
> A driving student who only ever practised on a perfectly clear road (teacher fixing every error) panics the first time they hit real traffic. They were never *exposed* to their own mistakes during practice.

**Curriculum learning** bridges the gap. Instead of a fixed teacher-forcing ratio, you start near **1.0** (almost always show the truth, for fast early learning) and gradually anneal it down toward **0.0** (let the decoder rely on its own outputs), so by the end of training the model has practised the same self-feeding it will do at test time.

> [!KEY]
> Teacher forcing = train on ground-truth previous tokens (fast convergence). Exposure bias = the resulting train/test mismatch because the test-time decoder feeds on its own predictions. Curriculum learning = anneal the teacher-forcing ratio 1.0 -> 0.0 to close that gap.

> [!EXAM]
> A favourite trap: "does higher teacher forcing always give better final BLEU?" No - very high teacher forcing speeds convergence but can worsen test-time robustness via exposure bias; the best setting is usually intermediate or annealed.

notes.heading: Decoding: greedy vs beam search
notes.body:
Once trained, the decoder produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary at each step. How you turn those distributions into an actual sentence is the **decoding** strategy.

**Greedy decoding** simply takes the single highest-probability word at every step. Fast, but **myopic**: it commits to each word with no way to undo it. A locally-best first word can force a globally-worse sentence.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Greedy decoding is like navigating by always turning toward whichever street looks best *right now*. You can walk into a dead end that a little look-ahead would have avoided.

**Beam search** keeps the **top-$k$ partial translations** (the "beams", e.g. $k=3$) alive at once instead of one. At each step it expands every beam over the vocabulary, scores each extension by its **cumulative log-probability**, and keeps only the best $k$ overall. A beam finishes when it emits the end-of-sentence token `<EOS>` or hits a max length.

> [!MATH]
> We score a candidate sequence by $\sum_t \log p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ - the sum of the log-probabilities of each chosen word given everything before it and the source $x$. We use **log**-probabilities (and add them) because multiplying many small probabilities underflows to zero; adding logs is the numerically stable equivalent.

**Length normalisation.** There is a built-in bias: every extra word adds another **negative** log-probability (since $\log p < 0$ for $p < 1$), so longer sequences accumulate a lower score. Left alone, beam search prefers **short** translations and drops words.

> [!KEY]
> Fix it by dividing the cumulative log-probability by the output length (length normalisation): score $= \frac{1}{T_y}\sum_t \log p(y_t \mid \cdot)$. This compares the *average* per-word log-probability, so a longer but better translation is no longer unfairly penalised.

notes.heading: Measuring quality: the BLEU score
notes.body:
You need a number to compare models. **BLEU** (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) measures **how many n-grams of the model's output also appear in a reference (human) translation** - i.e. n-gram *precision* - with a penalty for being too short.

$$\mathrm{BLEU} = \mathrm{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

> [!MATH]
> $p_n$ is the **modified n-gram precision**: of all the n-grams (groups of $n$ consecutive words) in the output, the fraction that also occur in the reference. $w_n = 1/N$ are equal weights (commonly $N=4$, so unigrams through 4-grams). $\mathrm{BP}$ is the **brevity penalty** - a multiplier $\le 1$ that punishes outputs shorter than the reference (so you cannot game BLEU by emitting just one very-likely word). The $\exp$-of-sum-of-logs is just a geometric mean of the precisions.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> Reference: "the cat is on the mat". Hypothesis: "the cat sat on the mat".
> - Unigram precision $p_1 = 5/6$ ("sat" is the one wrong word).
> - Bigram precision $p_2 = 3/5$ ("the cat", "on the", "the mat" match; "cat sat", "sat on" do not).
> - Same length, so $\mathrm{BP} = 1$.
> - $\mathrm{BLEU} \approx \exp\!\big(0.5\log\tfrac{5}{6} + 0.5\log\tfrac{3}{5}\big) \approx 0.54.$

**Interpreting a raw number.** BLEU is **context-dependent**. On standard benchmarks human translators score around $0.6$ and Google Translate around $0.4$. A BLEU of $0.15$ is "low quality" academically - but for a small model trained on only ~10K pairs for ~25 epochs it still shows the model learned real alignment and handles simple sentences. Always compare against a baseline (e.g. the no-attention model) rather than judging the absolute value.

> [!WARNING]
> BLEU's big limitation: it **penalises valid paraphrases**. A perfect translation that happens to use different-but-correct words scores low because its n-grams do not match the single reference. That is why metrics like METEOR, chrF, or BERTScore exist - they reward synonyms, recall, or embedding similarity.

| Metric | What it measures | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| BLEU | N-gram precision | Penalises valid paraphrases |
| ROUGE-L | Longest common subsequence | Recall-oriented |
| METEOR | Precision + recall + order | Language-dependent |
| BERTScore | Contextual embedding similarity | Needs a pretrained BERT |
| chrF | Character-level F-score | - (good for morphologically rich languages) |

quiz.q: In a plain encoder-decoder *without* attention, why does translation quality fall off sharply for sentences longer than about 20 words?
options: The decoder runs out of vocabulary entries once a sentence exceeds 20 words.
The entire source must be compressed into a single fixed-size vector, so for long sentences earlier information gets overwritten and cannot be reliably recovered.
Longer sentences contain more PAD tokens, which dominate the loss and stop learning.
The softmax over source positions becomes numerically unstable for long sentences.
quiz.explanation: The bottleneck is that all meaning, order, and grammar are squeezed into one fixed-size encoder vector; long sentences carry more information than it can hold, so content (especially early words) is overwritten. Vocabulary size is unrelated to sentence length, so the 'runs out of vocabulary' option is wrong; and a plain (no-attention) model has no softmax over source positions at all.

quiz.q: In Bahdanau attention, $e_{ti} = v^\top \tanh(W_1 s_{t-1} + W_2 h_i)$. A large value of $e_{ti}$ most directly means:
options: Source position $i$ is highly relevant for generating output word $t$ - the model is aligning them.
Source word $i$ has already been translated and should now be ignored.
The decoder hidden state $s_{t-1}$ has saturated and stopped changing.
Source position $i$ marks the start of a new clause in the sentence.
quiz.explanation: $e_{ti}$ is a learned compatibility score between the decoder's state and encoder state $h_i$; high means they align, so word $i$ matters for output $t$ ('to produce chat, attend to cat'). A high score signals *attend here now*, the opposite of 'ignore it'; saturation and clause boundaries are not what the score measures.

quiz.q: Which statement about the attention weights $\alpha_{ti}$ produced by the softmax is correct?
options: They can be negative when a source word is irrelevant to the current output.
For a fixed source position $i$, they sum to 1 across all output steps.
For a fixed output step $t$, they are non-negative and sum to 1 across the source positions - a probability distribution over the source.
They are computed once after encoding and reused unchanged for every decoder step.
quiz.explanation: Softmax normalises over the source index $i$ for each $t$, so the weights are non-negative and sum to 1 across source positions. They are not normalised across output steps; softmax outputs are always non-negative (never negative); and they are recomputed at every decoder step because $s_{t-1}$ changes each step.

quiz.q: The context vector $c_t = \sum_i \alpha_{ti} h_i$ is best described as:
options: The running sum of all decoder hidden states produced so far.
The encoder's final hidden state, identical for every decoder step.
The embedding of the single most-attended source word.
A weighted average of the encoder hidden states, recomputed at each decoder step.
quiz.explanation: $c_t$ blends the encoder states $h_i$ using the attention weights $\alpha_{ti}$, and because the weights change every step, so does $c_t$. The 'final hidden state, same every step' option describes the no-attention bottleneck, which is exactly what attention replaces; and $c_t$ is a soft blend, not a single hard-picked word's embedding.

quiz.q: Why is *soft* attention preferred over *hard* attention for training a seq2seq model with backpropagation?
options: Soft attention uses strictly fewer parameters than hard attention.
Hard attention always attends to the wrong source position.
Soft attention is fully differentiable, so gradients flow through the weights and the model trains end-to-end; hard attention makes a discrete pick and needs reinforcement learning.
Hard attention cannot be evaluated at test time.
quiz.explanation: The decisive point is differentiability: soft attention's weighted average lets gradients pass through the alpha weights into the encoder for standard backprop, while hard attention's discrete selection is non-differentiable and requires RL. Hard attention does not 'always' pick wrong positions, parameter count is not the distinguishing issue, and hard attention can be run at test time.

quiz.q: Teacher forcing speeds up training, but its main downside is:
options: It makes training converge much more slowly.
Exposure bias - at test time the decoder must condition on its own predictions, a distribution it rarely saw during training.
It forces the source and target vocabularies to be identical.
It removes the need for an attention mechanism.
quiz.explanation: Teacher forcing trains the decoder almost always on ground-truth previous words, so at test time (where it feeds on its own possibly-wrong outputs) it faces an unfamiliar input distribution - exposure bias. It actually speeds convergence (not slows it), has nothing to do with vocabulary sharing, and does not replace attention.

quiz.q: How does curriculum learning address exposure bias from teacher forcing?
options: It gradually lowers the teacher-forcing ratio from about 1.0 toward 0.0, so the decoder progressively practises relying on its own outputs.
It raises the teacher-forcing ratio over time to stabilise the decoder near the end.
It sorts training sentences from longest to shortest each epoch.
It freezes the encoder and trains only the decoder for the first half of training.
quiz.explanation: Curriculum learning anneals teacher forcing 1.0 -> 0.0: start with mostly ground-truth inputs for fast early learning, then shift toward self-generated inputs so training matches test-time conditions. Increasing the ratio would worsen exposure bias; sentence sorting and encoder freezing are unrelated techniques.

quiz.q: An attention heatmap for English->French shows a strong near-diagonal pattern. What does this reveal?
options: Heavy reordering between the two languages requiring long-range attention jumps.
A roughly monotonic word alignment, consistent with the two languages sharing similar (SVO) word order.
The encoder must be unidirectional for the diagonal to appear.
The model failed to learn any alignment and is attending uniformly.
quiz.explanation: A diagonal means output word $i$ mostly attends to input word $i$ - monotonic alignment, expected because English and French share broadly SVO order. Crossing patterns (not diagonals) indicate heavy reordering, e.g. German verb-final; the diagonal does not depend on encoder directionality; and a *diffuse/uniform* heatmap (not a sharp diagonal) would signal failure to learn alignment.

quiz.q: Reference: 'the cat is on the mat'. Hypothesis: 'the cat sat on the mat'. Which statement about its BLEU is correct?
options: BLEU rewards the hypothesis for using the valid synonym 'sat'.
BLEU here measures the recall of reference n-grams, not their precision.
The brevity penalty is below 1 because the hypothesis is one word too long.
Unigram precision is 5/6 and bigram precision is 3/5, with brevity penalty 1 because the lengths match.
quiz.explanation: Five of six unigrams match (only 'sat' is wrong) giving p1 = 5/6; three of five bigrams match ('the cat','on the','the mat') giving p2 = 3/5; equal length means BP = 1. BLEU is a *precision* metric (not recall); the lengths are equal so BP is not below 1; and BLEU does not reward paraphrases - it penalises 'sat' because it is not in the reference.

quiz.q: In beam search, why divide a candidate's cumulative log-probability by its length (length normalisation)?
options: Each extra word adds a negative log-probability, so without normalising, the search systematically prefers shorter sequences.
To make the beam search run faster by pruning more aggressively.
To force every surviving beam to have exactly the same length.
To convert the summed log-probabilities back into ordinary probabilities.
quiz.explanation: Because $\log p < 0$ for $p<1$, longer sequences accumulate a more negative total, so raw cumulative log-prob favours short outputs; dividing by length compares the *average* per-word log-prob and removes that bias. It is not a speed trick, does not equalise beam lengths, and dividing by length does not undo the log.

quiz.q: Compared with Bahdanau (additive) attention, Luong's dot-product score $e_{ti} = s_t^\top h_i$ has which property?
options: It is the only attention variant that is differentiable.
It introduces more learned parameters than Bahdanau's additive score.
It works with mismatched encoder/decoder hidden sizes out of the box.
It introduces no learned attention parameters but requires the encoder and decoder hidden sizes to match.
quiz.explanation: A plain dot product $s_t^\top h_i$ has no weight matrices, so it adds no attention parameters, but the two vectors must have equal dimension for the dot product to be defined. Bahdanau's additive score has *more* parameters ($W_1,W_2,v$); a raw dot product needs matching dims; and both additive and dot-product attention are differentiable.
