quiz.q: The English encoder is a bidirectional GRU with hidden size 256. What is the dimension of each encoder output $h_i$ that the attention scores are computed against?
options: 768, because the context vector is added to the embedding.
256, because both directions share the same hidden state.
512, because the forward and backward hidden states (256 each) are concatenated.
128, because bidirectionality halves the hidden size to save memory.
quiz.explanation: A bidirectional GRU runs two passes and concatenates their hidden states, so 256 forward + 256 backward = 512-dimensional outputs (matching the table's H shape [64,10,512]). 256 would be a single direction; 768 is the decoder input size (embedding 256 + context 512), not $h_i$; and bidirectionality concatenates rather than halving.

descriptive.q: Explain the 'information bottleneck' in a seq2seq model without attention, why performance degrades on long sentences, and precisely how attention removes the bottleneck.
descriptive.answer:
**The bottleneck.** In a plain encoder-decoder, the only bridge from encoder to decoder is the encoder's **final hidden state** - one fixed-size vector (e.g. 256-dim). The decoder must reconstruct the entire target from just that vector, so *all* source information - word meanings, word order, grammar, who-did-what-to-whom - has to coexist in those few hundred numbers.

> [!INTUITION]
> It is like summarising a whole paragraph onto a single short sticky note and then asking someone to rebuild the paragraph word-for-word from only the note. Short inputs fit; long inputs overflow.

**Why long sentences break.** A 20-word sentence carries far more information than a 256-dim vector can reliably store. As the encoder updates its single state word by word, **later words overwrite earlier ones**, so content from the start of the sentence is lost. Empirically BLEU drops sharply past ~20 words.

**How attention removes it.** Attention keeps *all* per-word encoder states $h_1,\dots,h_{T_x}$ and lets the decoder build a fresh summary each step:
$$e_{ti}=v^\top\tanh(W_1 s_{t-1}+W_2 h_i),\quad \alpha_{ti}=\frac{\exp(e_{ti})}{\sum_j \exp(e_{tj})},\quad c_t=\sum_i \alpha_{ti}h_i$$
Here $s_{t-1}$ is the decoder's current state, $h_i$ the $i$-th encoder state, $\alpha_{ti}$ the learned weight (how much output $t$ looks at source $i$), and $c_t$ the context vector. Because $c_t$ is recomputed every step from the **full** set of encoder states, no information is forced through a single frozen vector - the decoder can always reach back to the exact source words it needs, so long-sentence quality no longer collapses.

> [!KEY]
> No-attention = one fixed vector for the whole sentence (hard ceiling). Attention = a different, focused context vector per output word, drawn from every encoder state.

descriptive.q: Walk through the three steps of Bahdanau attention with the math glossed, and explain why it is called 'soft alignment' and why differentiability matters.
descriptive.answer:
**Step 1 - Score.** $e_{ti}=v^\top\tanh(W_1 s_{t-1}+W_2 h_i)$. Take the decoder's previous state $s_{t-1}$ (where the output stands now) and encoder state $h_i$ (what was understood about source word $i$); project each through a learned matrix, add, squash with $\tanh$, and dot with learned vector $v$ to get one scalar compatibility score $e_{ti}$. High $e_{ti}$ = source word $i$ is relevant to output word $t$.

**Step 2 - Normalise.** $\alpha_{ti}=\dfrac{\exp(e_{ti})}{\sum_{j}\exp(e_{tj})}$. Softmax over source positions turns raw scores into weights that are all positive and sum to 1 - a probability distribution telling us what fraction of attention output $t$ pays to each source word.

**Step 3 - Aggregate.** $c_t=\sum_i \alpha_{ti}h_i$. The context vector is a weighted average of encoder states; relevant words (high $\alpha$) dominate. The decoder uses $c_t$ to predict $y_t$.

**Why 'soft alignment'.** Instead of *hard*-picking one source word (a 0/1 choice), the model spreads weight smoothly over all positions - a **soft** distribution that approximates an alignment. The whole pattern of $\alpha_{ti}$ across $i$ and $t$ *is* a learned, probabilistic word alignment (the heatmap).

> [!KEY]
> Differentiability is the payoff. A weighted average is a smooth function of the $\alpha$ (and hence of $W_1,W_2,v$ and the encoder), so gradients flow through attention all the way back to the encoder, and the whole system trains end-to-end with ordinary backprop. Hard attention's discrete pick has no usable gradient and needs reinforcement learning - more complex, higher variance. That is the practical reason soft attention is the default.

descriptive.q: Define teacher forcing and exposure bias, explain the train/test mismatch, and describe how curriculum learning helps. What happens if you train with a teacher-forcing ratio of exactly 1.0 throughout?
descriptive.answer:
**Teacher forcing.** During training, when predicting word $t$ the decoder is fed the **ground-truth** previous target word rather than its own prediction. This stops errors from compounding within a sentence, so the model converges much faster (in this session it is used with probability ~0.5).

**Exposure bias.** At **test time** there is no ground truth - the decoder must feed itself its **own** previous prediction, which may be wrong. But it was trained almost entirely on correct previous words, so at test time it encounters an input distribution it was never *exposed* to. One early mistake can then snowball.

> [!ANALOGY]
> A student who only practised with a teacher silently fixing every step before the next one panics when, in the real exam, their own earlier mistake feeds the next step.

**Train/test mismatch.** Training distribution of 'previous word' = mostly correct (truth). Test distribution = the model's own outputs. The decoder is optimised for the first but deployed on the second - the core of exposure bias.

**Curriculum learning.** Anneal the teacher-forcing ratio from ~1.0 down to ~0.0 over training: early on, mostly show truth (fast learning); later, mostly let the decoder consume its own outputs, so by the end it has practised exactly the self-feeding it does at test time, shrinking the mismatch.

**Teacher forcing = 1.0 throughout?** Training converges fast and the *training* loss looks great, but the decoder **never** practises recovering from its own mistakes. At test time exposure bias is at its worst - small errors cascade, hurting test BLEU and producing fragile, drift-prone translations.

> [!EXAM]
> 'Does more teacher forcing always help?' No: it helps convergence but can hurt test-time robustness; an intermediate or annealed ratio usually wins.

descriptive.q: Compare greedy decoding with beam search for generating translations. Explain length normalisation, including why beam search is biased toward short outputs without it.
descriptive.answer:
**Greedy decoding.** At each step pick the single highest-probability word. It is fast and simple but **myopic**: it commits irrevocably to each word, so a locally attractive choice can lock the model into a globally worse sentence with no way to backtrack.

**Beam search.** Keep the **top-$k$** partial hypotheses (beams, e.g. $k=3$) alive simultaneously. Each step: expand every beam over the vocabulary, score each extension, and keep the best $k$ overall. A beam finishes at `<EOS>` or max length. This explores several promising paths, so it usually finds higher-probability (often better) sequences than greedy.

| | Greedy | Beam search (k) |
|---|---|---|
| Hypotheses kept | 1 | k |
| Can revise early choices | No | Partly (k paths compete) |
| Cost | Lowest | ~k x greedy |
| Quality | Baseline | Usually higher |

**Scoring.** A sequence's score is $\sum_t \log p(y_t\mid y_{<t},x)$ - the sum of log-probabilities of each word given the prefix and source $x$. We add **logs** instead of multiplying probabilities to avoid numerical underflow from many tiny factors.

**The short-output bias.** Every word's $\log p$ is **negative** (because $p<1$). So each additional word makes the cumulative score *more negative*. When beams of different lengths compete on raw cumulative log-prob, the **shorter** beam wins simply for having fewer negative terms - the model truncates and drops words.

> [!KEY]
> Length normalisation divides the cumulative log-prob by the output length: score $=\frac{1}{T_y}\sum_t \log p(y_t\mid\cdot)$. This compares the **average** per-word log-probability, so a longer-but-genuinely-better translation is judged fairly and the truncation bias disappears.

descriptive.q: Explain the BLEU score: compute it for the worked example, explain the brevity penalty and a key limitation, and interpret what a BLEU of 0.15 means for a small model.
descriptive.answer:
**What BLEU measures.** BLEU scores a translation by **n-gram precision** against a reference: of the n-grams (runs of $n$ consecutive words) the model produced, what fraction also appear in the reference? It combines several $n$ and multiplies by a brevity penalty:
$$\mathrm{BLEU}=\mathrm{BP}\cdot\exp\!\Big(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n\log p_n\Big)$$
where $p_n$ is modified n-gram precision, $w_n=1/N$ equal weights, and $\mathrm{BP}$ the brevity penalty.

**Worked example.** Reference: 'the cat is on the mat'; Hypothesis: 'the cat sat on the mat'.
- $p_1 = 5/6$ - five unigrams match, only 'sat' is wrong.
- $p_2 = 3/5$ - bigrams 'the cat', 'on the', 'the mat' match; 'cat sat', 'sat on' do not.
- Equal length so $\mathrm{BP}=1$.
- $\mathrm{BLEU}\approx \exp(0.5\log\frac56 + 0.5\log\frac35)\approx 0.54$.

**Brevity penalty.** Precision alone could be gamed by outputting one very likely word (precision 1, but the translation is missing everything). $\mathrm{BP}\le 1$ multiplies the score down when the output is shorter than the reference, forcing the model to cover the content, not just be locally precise.

> [!WARNING]
> Key limitation: BLEU **penalises valid paraphrases**. A perfectly correct translation that uses different (but synonymous) words has non-matching n-grams and scores low against a single reference. This is why METEOR, chrF, and BERTScore exist.

**Interpreting 0.15.** It is **context-dependent**. Academically it is low - human translators score ~0.6 and Google Translate ~0.4 on standard benchmarks. But for a small model trained on ~10K pairs for ~25 epochs, 0.15 shows it learned non-trivial alignment and handles simple patterns. The honest read is *relative*: compare it against the no-attention baseline to quantify what attention contributed, rather than judging the absolute number.

descriptive.q: Compare Bahdanau (additive) attention with Luong's dot-product and general variants - score functions, parameters, dimension requirements, and when each is used - and explain what near-diagonal vs crossing attention heatmaps reveal.
descriptive.answer:
**Score-function comparison.**

| Variant | Score $e_{ti}$ | Learned params | Dim requirement | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bahdanau (additive) | $v^\top\tanh(W_1 s + W_2 h)$ | $W_1, W_2, v$ | Handles unequal dims (projections fix it) | Default, most flexible |
| Luong dot-product | $s^\top h$ | none | Encoder/decoder dims **must match** | Fast, when dims already equal |
| Luong general | $s^\top W h$ | $W$ | Bilinear $W$ bridges unequal dims | Flexible, fewer params than additive |

Glossing: $s$ is the decoder state, $h$ an encoder state, $v/W/W_1/W_2$ learned parameters. **Bahdanau** adds the projected states inside a $\tanh$ then collapses with $v$ - most parameters, most flexible, and naturally handles a 512-dim bidirectional $h$ with a 256-dim $s$. **Luong dot-product** is just the inner product - zero attention parameters and very fast, but $s$ and $h$ must have equal dimension for the dot product to exist. **Luong general** inserts a matrix $W$, regaining flexibility at the cost of one weight matrix.

> [!KEY]
> Trade-off: additive (Bahdanau) buys flexibility with parameters; dot-product (Luong) buys speed but demands matching dimensions; general is the middle ground.

**Reading the heatmap.** Stack the weights $\alpha_{ti}$ into a grid (output words x input words) and colour by weight.

[[fig:attention-heatmap|Bright cells show where each output word looked in the source.]]

- **Near-diagonal:** output word $i$ attends mostly to input word $i$ - a roughly **monotonic** alignment. Expected for English->French because both are broadly SVO, so words line up in similar order.
- **Crossing / off-diagonal:** the languages **reorder** material. The textbook case is German->English: German's verb-final clauses force long crossing lines as the decoder reaches across to find the verb. A small local off-diagonal (e.g. 'black cat' -> 'chat noir', adjective after noun) is the model correctly handling local reordering.
- **Diffuse/smeared:** attention spread thinly everywhere usually signals poorly learned alignment and accompanies **hallucination** (output words unrelated to the source).

topics.title: Hands-on: Mini-Transformer from Scratch
topics.summary: A hands-on build of a small Transformer from first principles: scaled dot-product self-attention, multi-head attention, positional encoding, the Add & Norm / feed-forward block, and the causal mask that powers the decoder, ending with how this exact mini-transformer scales up to GPT.

notes.heading: The Big Idea: Trade Recurrence for Parallel Attention
notes.body:
An RNN or LSTM reads a sentence the way you read a sentence out loud: one word at a time, carrying a running memory forward. That is slow (you cannot start word 5 until word 4 is done) and the memory of early words fades. A **Transformer throws out the step-by-step loop entirely**. It lays all the words on the table at once and lets every word look directly at every other word in a single shot.

> [!INTUITION] Picture a meeting where everyone speaks one-at-a-time down a line (RNN) versus a meeting where everyone can glance at everyone else simultaneously and decide who to listen to (Transformer). The second is faster and nobody at the end forgets what the first person said.

The engine that lets each word "look at" the others is **self-attention**. For a sequence of $n$ tokens with model width $d$, self-attention costs about $O(n^2 d)$ operations.

> [!MATH] $O(n^2 d)$ in words: for **each** of the $n$ tokens we compare it against **all** $n$ tokens (that is the $n^2$), and each comparison touches a vector of width $d$. Here $n$ is the number of tokens (sequence length) and $d$ is $d_{model}$, the width of each token's vector.

This is **quadratic in sequence length** (double the sentence, quadruple the work) but **constant in depth** along the sequence — there is no chain of $n$ sequential steps to wait through. That is exactly what GPUs love: thousands of independent comparisons computed in parallel.

> [!KEY] The Transformer's superpower is not a fancier neuron — it is replacing a *sequential* recurrence with an *all-at-once* attention computation, unlocking parallelism that RNNs structurally cannot have.

notes.heading: Self-Attention as a Database Lookup (Q, K, V)
notes.body:
The cleanest way to understand attention is as a **soft database lookup**. In a real database you have a *query*, you match it against *keys*, and you retrieve the matching *value*. Self-attention does the same, except the match is fuzzy: instead of returning one row, it returns a blended mixture of all the values, weighted by how well each key matched.

Every token produces three vectors by multiplying its embedding by three learned weight matrices:

$$Q = XW^Q, \qquad K = XW^K, \qquad V = XW^V$$

> [!MATH] $X$ is the stack of token vectors (one row per token). $W^Q, W^K, W^V$ are three learned weight matrices. $Q$ (**query**) is "what am I looking for?", $K$ (**key**) is "what do I advertise about myself?", and $V$ (**value**) is "what I actually hand over if you pick me."

The full operation is:

$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

> [!MATH] Read left to right: $QK^\top$ is every query dotted with every key, giving a grid of raw **similarity scores**. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ (the square root of the key/query width $d_k$) keeps those scores from blowing up. **softmax** turns each row of scores into positive weights that sum to 1. Multiplying by $V$ blends the values using those weights.

The grid in the middle is the **attention matrix** $A = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$. Entry $A_{ij}$ says "how much token $i$ attends to token $j$," and crucially **every row sums to 1** — each token spends exactly one unit of attention, distributed across all tokens.

[[fig:scaled-dot-product]]

> [!EXAMPLE] Take the word **cat** in "the cat sat." Its query is compared to the keys of "the", "cat", "sat". Suppose the softmaxed weights come out $[0.1, 0.6, 0.3]$. The new vector for "cat" is $0.1\,V_{the} + 0.6\,V_{cat} + 0.3\,V_{sat}$ — mostly itself, but flavoured by "sat." Those weights sum to $1.0$.

> [!ANALOGY] $Q$/$K$/$V$ is like dating apps: your *query* is your search filter, each person's *key* is their profile, and their *value* is the actual conversation you get if you match.

notes.heading: Why Divide by √dₖ? The Scaling Trick
notes.body:
This little $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the denominator is one of the most-tested details of the whole architecture, and the reason is subtle. It exists to keep the **softmax from saturating**.

A dot product $q\cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ is a **sum of $d_k$ terms**. If the individual numbers in $q$ and $k$ have roughly unit variance, then summing $d_k$ of them makes the result's variance grow with $d_k$ — so with a large $d_k$ the raw scores can become very large positive or negative numbers.

> [!INTUITION] softmax of *moderate* scores like $[2, 1, 0]$ gives a soft spread of weights. softmax of *huge* scores like $[200, 100, 0]$ gives essentially $[1, 0, 0]$ — one token grabs almost all the attention. That extreme region is called **saturation**, and there the gradient is nearly zero, so the model can barely learn.

Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ rescales the dot product so its variance is back around $1$ regardless of how wide the vectors are.

> [!MATH] If each $q_i$ and $k_i$ has variance $1$, then $\text{Var}(q\cdot k) = d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ divides the variance by $(\sqrt{d_k})^2 = d_k$, bringing it back to $\approx 1$. Here $d_k$ is the dimension of each query/key vector.

> [!WARNING] A common debugging symptom: "attention weights are all uniform" usually means the dot products are too *small* (under-scaled), while "loss is NaN" or "one token gets all the attention" can come from missing or wrong scaling making logits too *large*. The fix is to verify the $\sqrt{d_k}$ divisor is actually applied before softmax.

> [!EXAM] Expect to be asked *what problem large $d_k$ causes* (softmax saturation / vanishing gradients) and *why $\sqrt{d_k}$ specifically* (it normalises the variance of a sum of $d_k$ unit-variance products back to 1).

notes.heading: Multi-Head Attention: Many Perspectives at Once
notes.body:
One attention computation produces **one** way of mixing tokens. But language has many kinds of relationship at once — grammar, meaning, position. **Multi-head attention** runs several attention computations in parallel, each in its own smaller subspace, so different heads can specialise.

With $h$ heads, each head works in a subspace of size $d_k = d_{model}/h$ using its own projections $W_j^Q, W_j^K, W_j^V$. The heads' outputs are concatenated and passed through one final projection:

$$\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)\,W^O$$

> [!MATH] $h$ is the number of heads. $d_k = d_{model}/h$ means we *split* the model width across heads rather than making the model bigger — 4 heads on a width-128 model each work in a width-32 subspace. $W_j^Q,W_j^K,W_j^V$ are that head's own projection matrices. $W^O$ is one shared output matrix that re-mixes the concatenated heads back to width $d_{model}$.

[[fig:multi-head-attention]]

> [!INTUITION] Splitting into $h$ heads is like sending $h$ specialists to read the same sentence: one tracks subject-verb agreement, one tracks who "it" refers to, one tracks nearby words. Then you staple their notes together and summarise. Total reading budget is unchanged; you just spend it on diverse views instead of one averaged view.

> [!EXAMPLE] $d_{model}=128$, $h=4 \Rightarrow d_k = 32$. Each head outputs a width-32 vector; concatenating 4 of them gives back $4\times 32 = 128$, then $W^O$ (a $128\times128$ matrix) mixes them. So multi-head attention does **not** increase the output width — it diversifies the computation that produced it.

> [!WARNING] Don't confuse "more heads" with "more capacity per head." Because $d_k = d_{model}/h$, adding heads *shrinks* each head. The guideline from the course: keep $d_{model}/n_{heads} \ge 16$ so each head has enough room to be useful.

notes.heading: Positional Encoding: Giving Order Back
notes.body:
Self-attention has a quiet flaw: it is **permutation-invariant**. Because it compares every token to every token with no notion of "left" or "right," the sentences "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" would produce the *same* set of vectors. That is a disaster — order carries meaning.

The fix is **positional encoding**: before attention, we *add* to each token's embedding a unique "position fingerprint" so the model can tell slot 1 from slot 5. The Transformer uses fixed **sinusoidal** encodings:

$$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right), \qquad PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$

> [!MATH] $pos$ is the position in the sequence (0, 1, 2, ...). $i$ indexes the dimension. Even dimensions use $\sin$, odd dimensions use $\cos$. The $10000^{2i/d_{model}}$ term makes each dimension oscillate at a **different frequency** — low dimensions wiggle fast, high dimensions wiggle slowly — so the combination of all dimensions is a unique pattern per position, encoding position at multiple scales at once.

[[fig:positional-encoding]]

> [!ANALOGY] Think of the hands of many clocks running at different speeds — seconds, minutes, hours, days. Read all of them together and you get a unique timestamp. Sinusoidal PE is exactly that: many sine/cosine "clocks" at different speeds whose combined reading pins down the position.

> [!KEY] Positional encoding is **added**, not concatenated, to the token embeddings — and it is what lets an order-blind attention mechanism understand sequences. Nearby positions get similar fingerprints, so the cosine similarity between position $0$ and position $k$ decays smoothly as $k$ grows.

> [!EXAM] A classic ablation question: remove PE and ask which inputs break. Multi-digit numbers (e.g. "1 2 3" = one-hundred-twenty-three vs "3 2 1" = three-hundred-twenty-one) break first, because without PE their embeddings are just *permutations* of each other and the model cannot tell hundreds from units.

notes.heading: The Transformer Block: Add & Norm + Feed-Forward
notes.body:
Attention is only half of a Transformer block. Each block stacks **two sub-layers** — multi-head self-attention, then a small position-wise feed-forward network (FFN) — and wraps each one in a **residual connection plus layer normalisation** ("Add & Norm").

The sub-layer pattern is:

$$\text{output} = \text{LayerNorm}\big(x + \text{Sublayer}(x)\big)$$

> [!MATH] $x$ is the input to the sub-layer. $\text{Sublayer}(x)$ is either the attention or the FFN. The $x + \dots$ is the **residual (skip) connection**: the input is added back so gradients can flow straight through even in deep stacks. $\text{LayerNorm}$ then re-centres and re-scales the result.

[[fig:transformer-block]]

**Why residuals?** At depth $>10$ layers, gradients struggle to reach early layers. The skip path $x + F(x)$ gives the gradient a shortcut, exactly like in ResNet (Week 4) — this is what makes deep Transformers trainable.

**Why LayerNorm and not BatchNorm?** LayerNorm normalises **each token across its own feature dimension**, with no dependence on the batch:

$$\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \frac{x-\mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2+\epsilon}}\cdot\gamma + \beta, \quad \mu = \tfrac{1}{d}\textstyle\sum_i x_i,\;\; \sigma^2 = \tfrac{1}{d}\textstyle\sum_i (x_i-\mu)^2$$

> [!MATH] $\mu$ is the mean of one token's features, $\sigma^2$ their variance, $\epsilon$ a tiny constant for numerical safety. $\gamma$ (scale) and $\beta$ (shift) are learnable so the network can undo the normalisation if it wants. Because $\mu,\sigma$ are computed **per token**, LayerNorm is independent of batch size and works for any sequence length.

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Normalises across | the batch (per feature) | features (per token) |
| Needs fixed batch size? | yes | no |
| Works for variable-length sequences? | poorly | yes |
| Used in Transformers? | no | **yes** |

The FFN itself is two linear layers with a non-linearity between them, applied identically to every position — typically with hidden width $d_{ff} = 4\times d_{model}$.

> [!KEY] One block = self-attention (mix across tokens) + FFN (process each token), each protected by Add & Norm. Stacking these blocks is what gives the Transformer its depth.

notes.heading: Masks and the Decoder: No Peeking at the Future
notes.body:
So far attention has let every token see every other token. That is fine for an **encoder**, which reads a whole input at once (bidirectional). But a **decoder generates output left-to-right**, and during training we feed it the entire target sequence in parallel (teacher forcing). If we did nothing, position 3 could look at positions 4, 5, ... — it would *cheat* by reading the very answer it is supposed to predict.

The **causal mask** prevents this. Before softmax, we set every "future" score to $-\infty$:

> [!MATH] We use an **upper-triangular mask**: for output position $i$, any key position $j>i$ has its score replaced by $-\infty$. After softmax, $e^{-\infty}=0$, so the attention weight $\alpha_{ij}=0$ for all $j>i$. In words: each token may attend to itself and earlier tokens only, never to tokens that come after it.

[[fig:attention-masks]]

The decoder actually uses **two** attention layers, and the distinction is a favourite exam target:

| Decoder attention | What it attends to | Mask? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Masked self-attention** | the output tokens generated so far | causal (upper-triangle $-\infty$) | build context from the partial output without seeing the future |
| **Cross-attention** | the **encoder's outputs** (the full source) | none | inject source information into each decoding step |

> [!WARNING] A telltale bug: "the decoder generates the same token over and over" usually means the **causal mask is missing or wrong**. With no mask, training-time and inference-time behaviour diverge badly and the model never learns proper left-to-right generation.

> [!ANALOGY] Teacher forcing with a causal mask is like a fill-in-the-blank exam where each blank is covered until you reach it: you can use everything you have already written, but the future answers stay hidden.

> [!EXAM] Be able to state precisely: masked self-attention attends to *previous decoder outputs* (causal), cross-attention attends to *all encoder outputs* (unmasked). The encoder's self-attention has **no** mask at all.

notes.heading: Putting It Together — and Scaling Up to GPT
notes.body:
Let us trace the full data flow for the toy task of mapping digits to words, generating the first output token for input "1 2 3":

1. **Source embedding + PE.** The digits become token embeddings of shape roughly $[1,3,128]$ (batch 1, 3 tokens, width 128), and positional encodings are added.
2. **Encoder ($\times 2$ layers).** Each layer = self-attention + FFN, each wrapped in Add & Norm. Output: a context-rich $[1,3,128]$ representation of the source.
3. **Decoder start.** Feed the start token $\langle SOS\rangle$ + PE into **masked self-attention** (only sees itself so far).
4. **Cross-attention** lets that decoder position attend over the full encoder output (the source "1 2 3").
5. **FFN**, then a final **linear layer** maps to vocabulary logits $[1,1,\text{vocab}]$, **softmax** to probabilities, and **argmax** picks the word — e.g. "one."

> [!KEY] Encoder = understand the whole input in parallel; Decoder = generate one token at a time, using its own past (masked self-attention) plus the source (cross-attention).

**Counting parameters (intuition).** For $d_{model}=128$, $4$ heads, $d_{ff}=256$, vocab $20$: each encoder layer's attention costs about $4\times(3\times 32\times 128 + 128^2)\approx 32\text{K}$ and its FFN about $128\times256 + 256\times128 \approx 65\text{K}$, so $\approx 97\text{K}$ per encoder layer. A decoder layer adds a second (cross) attention, $\approx 129\text{K}$. With 2 encoder + 2 decoder layers plus small embeddings, the whole model is $\approx 460\text{K}$ parameters.

> [!INTUITION] The architecture you just built **is GPT** — a decoder-style Transformer trained to predict the next token. The differences between this and GPT-3 are *quantitative*, not architectural:

| | Mini-Transformer | GPT-3 |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | ~460K | 175B |
| Layers | 2 enc + 2 dec | 96 (decoder-only) |
| $d_{model}$ | 128 | 12{,}288 |
| Heads | 4 | 96 |
| Training tokens | <1K | 300B |

The math, the training loop, and the autoregressive generation procedure are **identical**. Scaling laws show performance improves smoothly with size — there is no secret new component between your model and a frontier LLM.

quiz.q: In $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})\,V$, what does the matrix $QK^\top$ (before scaling and softmax) actually represent?
options: The normalised attention weights that already sum to 1 per row
The final blended output vector for each token
A grid of raw similarity scores between every query and every key
The positional encoding added to each token
quiz.explanation: $QK^\top$ dots each query against each key, producing an $n\times n$ grid of raw similarity (alignment) scores. It is NOT yet the output (that needs multiplying by $V$) and NOT yet normalised — the rows only sum to 1 after softmax. Positional encoding is unrelated; it is added to embeddings earlier.

quiz.q: Why is the attention score divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$ specifically (rather than, say, $d_k$ or $1$)?
options: Because a dot product of two unit-variance vectors of length $d_k$ has variance $d_k$, so dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores variance ≈ 1
Because it makes the attention weights in each row sum exactly to 1
Because it converts the scores into a probability distribution without softmax
Because $\sqrt{d_k}$ equals the number of attention heads
quiz.explanation: A dot product is a sum of $d_k$ products; with unit-variance entries its variance grows to $d_k$, so dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ divides variance by $d_k$, returning it to ≈1 and keeping softmax out of saturation. Summing to 1 is the job of softmax, not the scaling. The scaling does not replace softmax, and $\sqrt{d_k}$ has nothing to do with head count.

quiz.q: Without the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling and with a large $d_k$, what goes wrong during training?
options: The attention weights become negative and break the value mixing
The softmax saturates — one position gets weight ≈ 1, the rest ≈ 0, and gradients vanish
The model can no longer attend to more than $d_k$ tokens
Positional encodings stop being unique across positions
quiz.explanation: Large $d_k$ makes the logits large, pushing softmax into a near one-hot output where gradients are essentially zero, so learning stalls. Softmax outputs are always non-negative, so weights never go negative. There is no $d_k$ cap on how many tokens can be attended to, and scaling is independent of positional encoding.

quiz.q: A model has $d_{model}=512$ and uses $8$ attention heads. What is the dimension $d_k$ of each head's query/key subspace, and what happens to total output width?
options: $d_k = 8$, and the output width becomes 64
$d_k = 512$, and the output width becomes $8\times512 = 4096$
$d_k = 4096$, and the output width stays 512
$d_k = 64$, and the concatenated heads return to width 512
quiz.explanation: Multi-head splits the model width: $d_k = d_{model}/h = 512/8 = 64$. Each head outputs width 64; concatenating 8 of them gives $8\times64=512$, then $W^O$ remixes back to $d_{model}$. Multi-head does not increase output width — it diversifies the computation. Treating $d_k=d_{model}$ is the single-head case, not 8 heads.

quiz.q: What is the main benefit of using multiple attention heads instead of one head with the full $d_{model}$ width?
options: It removes the need for positional encoding
Different heads can specialise in different relationships (syntax, coreference, proximity) simultaneously
It makes the attention computation linear, $O(n)$, instead of quadratic
It guarantees each row of the attention matrix sums to more than 1
quiz.explanation: Each head attends in its own subspace and can learn a distinct pattern, so the model captures several kinds of relationship at once instead of a single averaged one. Heads do not replace positional encoding (order is still injected separately), the cost remains $O(n^2 d)$, and softmax always makes each row sum to exactly 1.

quiz.q: Self-attention without positional encoding is described as 'permutation-invariant.' What does this mean in practice?
options: Each token can only attend to itself
The attention weights are guaranteed to be uniform across all positions
Reordering the input tokens produces the same set of output representations, so word order is lost
The model becomes invariant to the choice of vocabulary size
quiz.explanation: With no position signal, attention treats the tokens as an unordered set, so 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' yield the same representations — order information is gone, which is why PE is added. It does not force uniform weights, does not restrict tokens to self-attention, and has nothing to do with vocabulary size.

quiz.q: You remove positional encoding from a trained digit-to-word transformer. Which input class is expected to fail FIRST, and why?
options: Three-digit numbers, because distinguishing hundreds/tens/units requires order that PE provided
Single-digit numbers, because they have the fewest tokens to attend over
Two-digit numbers, because their embeddings are largest in magnitude
All classes fail equally, since PE affects every token identically
quiz.explanation: Three-digit numbers fail first: '1 2 3' and '3 2 1' are different outputs but, without PE, their embeddings are just permutations of each other, so the model cannot tell hundreds from units. Single-digit inputs can often be read from the embedding alone. Magnitude of embeddings is not the issue, and the failure is clearly position-dependent, so it is not uniform.

quiz.q: In the Transformer sub-layer $\text{output}=\text{LayerNorm}(x+\text{Sublayer}(x))$, what is the specific role of the '$x+$' term?
options: It is the positional encoding being added back at every layer
It is a residual connection giving gradients a shortcut so deep stacks stay trainable
It is the causal mask that blocks future tokens
It is dropout applied to the sub-layer output
quiz.explanation: Adding the input back is a residual (skip) connection, exactly like ResNet: it lets gradients bypass the sub-layer, which is essential past ~10 layers. It is not positional encoding (added once at the input), not the mask (which sets future scores to $-\infty$ inside attention), and not dropout.

quiz.q: Why does the Transformer use LayerNorm rather than BatchNorm?
options: LayerNorm normalises across the batch, which stabilises attention scores
LayerNorm is faster because it uses no learnable parameters
BatchNorm cannot be used with residual connections
LayerNorm normalises per token across features, so it is batch-size independent and handles variable-length sequences
quiz.explanation: LayerNorm computes mean and variance per token over its own features, so it does not depend on batch size and works for any sequence length — ideal for variable-length text. It DOES have learnable $\gamma,\beta$, so 'no parameters' is false. BatchNorm works with residuals fine in CNNs; the real problem is its batch dependence. LayerNorm normalises across features, not the batch.

quiz.q: In a decoder, what is the difference between masked self-attention and cross-attention?
options: Masked self-attention attends to previous decoder outputs (causally); cross-attention attends to the encoder's outputs
Masked self-attention attends to the encoder outputs; cross-attention attends to future decoder tokens
Both attend to the encoder outputs, but cross-attention adds a causal mask
Masked self-attention is used only at inference; cross-attention only during training
quiz.explanation: Masked self-attention lets each decoder position use the tokens generated so far (causal mask blocks the future); cross-attention lets it read the full encoder output to bring in source information. Cross-attention is NOT masked and does not look at the encoder for the first option's wrong variant. Both are used in training and inference, so the timing claim is false.

quiz.q: A decoder is generating the same token repeatedly during training/inference. Which bug is the most likely cause?
options: Positional encoding was added instead of concatenated
The learning rate warmup was too long
$d_{ff}$ was set to exactly $4\times d_{model}$
The causal (upper-triangular) mask is missing, so the model 'cheats' on the future and never learns left-to-right generation
quiz.explanation: Repeated tokens are the classic signature of a missing causal mask: without it the decoder sees future targets during teacher-forced training, so its learned behaviour collapses at inference. Warmup length affects stability, not this pattern; $d_{ff}=4d_{model}$ is the recommended setting; and PE is supposed to be added, not concatenated.

quiz.q: What is the computational complexity of self-attention for a sequence of length $n$ and model width $d$, and why does it matter for long sequences?
options: $O(n^2)$ independent of $d$, since width does not affect attention cost
$O(n d)$ — each token does a constant amount of work, so cost is linear in length
$O(n^2 d)$ — every token attends to every token, so cost grows quadratically with sequence length
$O(d^2)$ independent of $n$, since attention is parallel over the sequence
quiz.explanation: Each of $n$ tokens is compared to all $n$ tokens ($n^2$ comparisons), each over width-$d$ vectors, giving $O(n^2 d)$. The $n^2$ term is why memory and compute balloon for long contexts. It is not linear (that ignores the all-pairs comparison), the width $d$ genuinely enters the cost, and $n$ does not drop out just because the work is parallelised.

descriptive.q: Derive why attention scores are divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$. Start from the variance of a dot product and explain, step by step, what problem large $d_k$ causes without scaling and how the division fixes it.
descriptive.answer:
**The setup.** A single attention score is a dot product $q\cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$, where $q,k$ are query/key vectors of length $d_k$ (the per-head key dimension).

**Step 1 — variance grows with $d_k$.** Assume each component $q_i,k_i$ is independent with mean 0 and variance 1. Each product $q_i k_i$ then has mean 0 and variance 1. The dot product is a **sum of $d_k$ such products**, so:
$$\text{Var}(q\cdot k)=\sum_{i=1}^{d_k}\text{Var}(q_i k_i)=d_k$$
In words: the more dimensions you sum over, the larger the typical magnitude of the score. With $d_k=64$ the scores have standard deviation $\approx 8$.

**Step 2 — large scores saturate softmax.** softmax turns a row of scores into weights. When the scores are large and spread out (e.g. $[200,100,0]$), softmax outputs essentially one-hot $[1,0,0]$.

> [!INTUITION] In that near one-hot regime the softmax is 'saturated': its output barely changes when the inputs change, so its gradient is almost zero. The model receives almost no learning signal — a vanishing-gradient problem created purely by score magnitude.

**Step 3 — the fix.** Divide every score by $\sqrt{d_k}$ before softmax:
$$\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{q\cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)=\frac{\text{Var}(q\cdot k)}{(\sqrt{d_k})^2}=\frac{d_k}{d_k}=1$$
This restores the variance to $\approx 1$ **regardless of width**, keeping logits in softmax's responsive (non-saturated) range so gradients flow.

> [!KEY] $\sqrt{d_k}$ is the exact factor that cancels the $\sqrt{d_k}$ growth in the standard deviation of a sum of $d_k$ unit-variance terms. Use $d_k$ (not $\sqrt{d_k}$) and you'd over-shrink; use $1$ and softmax saturates.

descriptive.q: The decoder uses two distinct attention mechanisms — masked self-attention and cross-attention. Explain the specific purpose of each, what each attends to, and why the mask appears in one but not the other.
descriptive.answer:
A decoder block contains **three** sub-layers in order: masked self-attention, cross-attention, then a feed-forward network — each wrapped in Add & Norm. The two attention layers play very different roles.

**Masked self-attention.**
- **Attends to:** the decoder's own tokens generated so far (the partial output).
- **Purpose:** build context from what has already been produced, so each new token is consistent with the earlier output.
- **Why masked:** during training we feed the entire shifted target in parallel (teacher forcing). Without a mask, position $i$ could read positions $j>i$ — i.e. the future answer it is meant to predict. The **causal (upper-triangular) mask** sets those future scores to $-\infty$ so that after softmax $\alpha_{ij}=0$ for all $j>i$.

> [!MATH] $\alpha_{ij}$ is the attention weight from output token $i$ to token $j$. Setting score$_{ij}=-\infty$ for $j>i$ gives $e^{-\infty}=0$, enforcing left-to-right (causal) generation.

**Cross-attention.**
- **Attends to:** the **encoder's outputs** — the full encoded source sequence. Here the *queries* come from the decoder, but the *keys and values* come from the encoder.
- **Purpose:** inject source information into each decoding step — this is the bridge between input and output.
- **Why no mask:** the entire source is known up front and is not being predicted, so there is no future to hide. The decoder is allowed to look at all source positions at once.

| | Masked self-attention | Cross-attention |
|---|---|---|
| Query from | decoder | decoder |
| Key/Value from | decoder (its own outputs) | **encoder outputs** |
| Mask | causal (upper-triangle $-\infty$) | none |
| Purpose | use generated context, no cheating | incorporate the source |

> [!WARNING] A missing causal mask makes the decoder collapse (often repeating one token), because training-time leakage of the future means the learned behaviour fails at inference where the future genuinely isn't available.

descriptive.q: Walk through the full data flow as the transformer processes input '1 2 3' and generates the FIRST output token 'one'. Name each component and the shape/transformation at each step.
descriptive.answer:
**1. Source embedding + positional encoding.** The three digits are mapped to token embeddings, producing a tensor of shape roughly $[1,3,128]$ (batch 1, 3 tokens, $d_{model}=128$). Sinusoidal positional encodings of the same shape are **added** so the model knows '1' is in slot 0, '2' in slot 1, '3' in slot 2.

> [!MATH] Shape $[1,3,128]$ = (batch size 1, sequence length 3, model width 128). Adding PE keeps the shape unchanged; it only injects order.

**2. Encoder ($\times 2$ layers).** Each encoder layer applies:
- **Self-attention** (no mask, bidirectional) — every source token mixes information from all three.
- **Add & Norm** — residual + LayerNorm.
- **FFN** (two linear layers, width $d_{ff}$) applied per position.
- **Add & Norm** again.
After 2 layers the encoder emits a context-rich source representation of shape $[1,3,128]$.

**3. Decoder start.** Feed the start token $\langle SOS\rangle$ (plus its PE) into the decoder, shape $[1,1,128]$.

**4. Masked self-attention.** With only one token so far, the causal mask is trivial — it attends to itself.

**5. Cross-attention.** The decoder position uses its query against the **encoder output's** keys/values, reading the whole source '1 2 3.' Add & Norm.

**6. FFN + Add & Norm.** Position-wise processing of the decoder representation.

**7. Output head.** A final **linear layer** projects the decoder vector to vocabulary logits of shape $[1,1,\text{vocab}]$, **softmax** turns them into a probability distribution, and **argmax** selects the highest-probability word — 'one.'

> [!KEY] Encoder understands all source positions in parallel; the decoder produces 'one' by combining its own (here empty) context via masked self-attention with the encoded source via cross-attention, then projecting to the vocabulary.

descriptive.q: Compare LayerNorm and BatchNorm in the context of a Transformer. Why is LayerNorm the right choice, and what would break if you used BatchNorm instead?
descriptive.answer:
**The core difference is the axis of normalisation.**

- **BatchNorm** normalises **each feature across the batch**: for feature $j$ it computes the mean/variance over all examples in the mini-batch. It therefore depends on batch statistics.
- **LayerNorm** normalises **each token across its own features**: for one token it computes
$$\text{LayerNorm}(x)=\frac{x-\mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2+\epsilon}}\cdot\gamma+\beta,\quad \mu=\tfrac1d\sum_i x_i,\;\sigma^2=\tfrac1d\sum_i(x_i-\mu)^2$$

> [!MATH] $x$ is one token's feature vector of width $d$; $\mu,\sigma^2$ are that token's own mean and variance over its $d$ features; $\epsilon$ avoids division by zero; $\gamma,\beta$ are learnable scale/shift so the layer can recover any scale it needs. Crucially, no other token or example enters the formula.

**Why LayerNorm fits Transformers.**
1. **Variable-length sequences.** Sentences have different lengths; LayerNorm's per-token computation is unaffected by how many tokens are in the batch or sequence.
2. **Batch-size independence.** Training and inference (often batch size 1, autoregressive) behave identically — no separate running-statistics machinery is needed.

| | BatchNorm | LayerNorm |
|---|---|---|
| Normalises over | batch (per feature) | features (per token) |
| Depends on batch size | yes | no |
| Variable-length sequences | problematic | fine |
| Train vs inference behaviour | differs (uses running stats) | identical |

**What breaks with BatchNorm.** Its statistics become noisy and meaningless when batch size is small or when sequences are padded to different lengths — padding tokens contaminate the per-feature batch statistics, and inference at batch size 1 diverges from training. The normalisation that should stabilise training instead injects batch-dependent noise.

> [!KEY] Per-token (LayerNorm) beats per-batch (BatchNorm) whenever batch size is small/variable and sequence length changes — which is the normal case in sequence modelling.

descriptive.q: What happens if you replace multi-head attention (4 heads) with a single head of the same total width ($d_k=d_{model}$)? Discuss the effect on expressiveness, attention-pattern diversity, and parameter count.
descriptive.answer:
**Parameter count is roughly unchanged.** With $h$ heads each of width $d_k=d_{model}/h$, the per-head projections $W_j^Q,W_j^K,W_j^V$ are $d_{model}\times d_k$ matrices, and there are $h$ of them. The total projection size is $h\times(d_{model}\times d_{model}/h)=d_{model}^2$ — the same as one full-width head. So switching to a single head barely changes parameter count (the output projection $W^O$ is also $d_{model}\times d_{model}$ in both cases).

> [!MATH] $d_k=d_{model}/h$ means heads *split* the width rather than add to it. 4 heads of width 32 on a 128-wide model still total 128 — multi-head diversifies the computation, it does not enlarge it.

**Expressiveness / pattern diversity drops.** This is the real cost.
- A single head produces **one** attention distribution per token — effectively one averaged way of relating tokens.
- Four heads each operate in a different 32-dimensional subspace and can specialise: one head may track positional proximity, another syntactic dependency, another semantic coreference. The course's ablation literally asks you to visualise all 4 heads and observe *diverse* patterns.

> [!INTUITION] One head is one specialist forced to summarise everything; four heads are four specialists with different focuses whose notes get stapled together by $W^O$. The information per token width is the same, but it is spent on multiple complementary views instead of a single blurred average.

**Practical guidance from the course.** Keep $d_{model}/n_{heads}\ge 16$ so each head has enough room. A single head of full width still works but tends to give less expressive, more uniform attention and worse generalisation on tasks needing several relationship types at once.

> [!KEY] Multi-head ≈ free diversity: same parameters and same output width, but the model captures several relationships simultaneously instead of one averaged one.

descriptive.q: Explain why self-attention enables far more parallelism than an RNN, and discuss the trade-off this introduces. Include the relevant complexity and what it implies for long sequences.
descriptive.answer:
**Why RNNs are inherently sequential.** An RNN computes hidden state $h_t$ from $h_{t-1}$ and input $x_t$. Because $h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, you cannot compute step $t$ until step $t-1$ finishes. For a length-$n$ sequence this is a chain of $n$ dependent operations — an $O(n)$-deep sequential path that no amount of hardware can parallelise away. It also makes early information fade across the long chain.

**Why self-attention is parallel.** Self-attention computes all token interactions from the same input simultaneously:
$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V)=\text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

> [!MATH] $QK^\top$ is a single matrix multiply producing all $n\times n$ pairwise scores at once; there is no token-by-token recurrence. Every position's output can be computed in parallel because none of them waits on another's hidden state.

So along the sequence the Transformer has **constant depth** (a fixed number of layers), not $O(n)$ depth. GPUs, which are built for large parallel matrix multiplies, can therefore process the whole sequence at once.

**The trade-off: quadratic cost.** That all-pairs comparison costs:
$$O(n^2 d)$$

> [!MATH] $n$ = sequence length, $d$ = model width. Each of $n$ tokens compares against all $n$ tokens ($n^2$ pairs), each comparison over width-$d$ vectors. The $n^2$ term is the price of letting every token see every token.

**Implications for long sequences.**
- Memory and compute grow **quadratically** with length — doubling the context quadruples attention cost. The course flags max sequence length as a key hyperparameter precisely because of this $O(n^2)$ memory pressure.
- RNNs, by contrast, are $O(n)$ in length but pay with sequential (non-parallel) execution and vanishing gradients over long chains.

| | RNN/LSTM | Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Path length along sequence | $O(n)$ sequential | $O(1)$ (constant depth) |
| Parallelisable over positions | no | yes |
| Cost vs length | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2 d)$ |
| Long-range information | fades | direct (every pair connected) |

> [!KEY] The Transformer trades cheaper-but-sequential recurrence for more-expensive-but-parallel attention: $O(n^2 d)$ work that a GPU can do all at once, with every token one hop from every other. The cost is quadratic scaling in sequence length.

topics.title: Hands-on: nanoGPT + LoRA
topics.summary: This hands-on session trains a small GPT (nanoGPT) from scratch on WikiText-103 and then fine-tunes a pretrained GPT-2 efficiently with LoRA. The core ideas: scale in parameters and tokens drives quality, character-level vs subword tokenisation is a real trade-off, and LoRA freezes the giant pretrained weights while training tiny low-rank adapters (<1% of parameters) to redirect knowledge to a new domain.

notes.heading: The Big Picture: Build a GPT, Then Cheaply Redirect a Bigger One
notes.body:
This session has two halves that mirror how real language models are made and used.

**Half 1 - nanoGPT from scratch.** You start with random weights and train a tiny decoder-only Transformer (about 10 million parameters) on roughly 30 MB of text. After a few minutes on a free GPU it produces coherent English prose. This is **pretraining**: teaching a blank network the statistics of language by predicting the next token, over and over.

**Half 2 - GPT-2 + LoRA.** Instead of training a model from zero, you take a model that someone already pretrained on billions of words (GPT-2, 117M parameters) and *adapt* it to a new domain (e.g. Python code) by training only a sliver of new parameters. This is **fine-tuning**, made cheap by a trick called LoRA.

> [!ANALOGY] Pretraining is like sending someone through years of general schooling - expensive, slow, builds broad knowledge from nothing. Fine-tuning with LoRA is like giving an already-educated employee a one-week course to specialise. You do not re-teach them to read; you just nudge what they already know toward a new job.

[[fig:pretrain-finetune]]

> [!KEY] Pretraining builds knowledge from scratch and needs data **proportional to model size**. Fine-tuning reuses that knowledge and can adapt the model with a tiny fraction of the compute. LoRA is the technique that makes fine-tuning almost free.

The single insight tying the session together is **scale**: nanoGPT (~10M params) on ~30MB gives readable text; GPT-2 (117M params) trained on ~40B tokens of internet text encodes vast world knowledge. More parameters *and* more tokens both push quality up - the heart of modern LLM scaling laws.

notes.heading: Inside nanoGPT: A Decoder-Only Transformer That Only Looks Backward
notes.body:
nanoGPT is a **decoder-only** Transformer. Unlike BERT, which reads a sentence in both directions to *understand* it, GPT reads strictly left-to-right and is built to *generate* the next token. The mechanism that enforces this is **causal (masked) self-attention**: when predicting the token at position $t$, the model is only allowed to attend to positions $1$ through $t$ - never the future.

Here $t$ is the position of the token we are currently predicting, and 'attend to' means 'look at and gather information from'. Hiding the future tokens forces the model to genuinely *predict* rather than peek at the answer.

[[fig:attention-masks|Left: BERT-style bidirectional attention (every token sees every other). Right: GPT-style causal mask - each token only sees itself and the tokens before it.]]

> [!INTUITION] Imagine writing a sentence word by word with everything to your right covered by a card. You can use what you have already written, but you must guess what comes next. The causal mask is that card.

**Each decoder block, in pre-norm order**, does two things:

$$x \leftarrow x + \text{CausalSelfAttn}(\text{LN}(x))$$
$$x \leftarrow x + \text{MLP}(\text{LN}(x))$$

Reading this in words: take the running representation $x$ (a stack of vectors, one per token), normalise it with LayerNorm ($\text{LN}$), run causal self-attention, and **add** the result back to $x$ (that '+' is the residual/skip connection). Then do the same with a small feed-forward network ($\text{MLP}$). The arrow $\leftarrow$ just means 'update $x$ to this new value'.

> [!MATH] The two lines say: 'new x = old x plus what attention learned' and then 'new x = that x plus what the MLP learned.' The residual '+' lets information and gradients flow straight through the block, so deep stacks train without the signal dying out.

**Session config** (the actual numbers you train with):

| Param | Value | What it controls |
|---|---|---|
| n_layer | 6 | number of decoder blocks |
| n_head | 8 | attention heads (must divide n_embd) |
| n_embd | 512 | model dimension (vector size per token) |
| block_size | 256 | context window; memory grows $O(n^2)$ |
| vocab_size | ~95 | ASCII character vocabulary |

Parameter breakdown: token embedding ($95 \times 512 \approx 49\text{K}$) + positional embedding ($256 \times 512 \approx 131\text{K}$) + 6 blocks ($\approx 10\text{M}$) $\approx 10\text{M}$ total. Here $95 \times 512$ means 95 possible characters each mapped to a 512-dimensional vector. It trains in about 8 minutes on a Colab T4.

notes.heading: Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm: Why LayerNorm Goes First
notes.body:
A subtle but exam-worthy detail: nanoGPT puts **LayerNorm before** each sub-layer (pre-norm), whereas the *original* 2017 Transformer put it **after** (post-norm). LayerNorm is the operation that re-centres and re-scales a vector so its values are not too big or too small.

> [!INTUITION] Think of the residual stream - the running sum $x$ that flows from the bottom of the network to the top - as a highway. **Pre-norm** cleans up a *copy* of the traffic before sending it into a sub-layer, then merges the result back onto the highway, which stays untouched. **Post-norm** re-normalises the *whole highway* after every merge, which rescales the gradient paths unevenly and gets shaky as the road gets long (deep).

The practical payoff: pre-norm keeps gradient magnitudes stable as depth grows, so the model trains reliably **without** careful learning-rate warmup tuning. Post-norm networks tend to become unstable at large depth and often need a warmup schedule to train at all.

> [!KEY] Pre-norm = `x + Sublayer(LN(x))`. The skip path is a clean, un-normalised highway, so gradients reach the early layers intact. This is the single biggest reason modern GPTs use pre-norm.

> [!EXAM] A classic question gives you the two formulas and asks which is more stable at depth and why. Answer: pre-norm, because LayerNorm sits *before* the sub-layer so the residual highway is never rescaled, keeping gradient magnitudes consistent through many layers.

notes.heading: Tokenisation: Characters vs Subwords (BPE)
notes.body:
Before any text reaches the model it must become numbers. nanoGPT and GPT-2 make opposite choices, and the choice ripples through everything.

**Character-level (nanoGPT).** Every character is its own token, giving a tiny vocabulary of about 95 ASCII symbols. No tokenizer library is needed - you just map each character to an integer. It is fully transparent and trivial to set up. The cost: sentences become **long** token sequences (one token per letter), so the model must use its limited context window on raw spelling and learn word structure from scratch.

**Subword / Byte Pair Encoding (GPT-2).** BPE starts from single characters and repeatedly **merges the most frequent adjacent pairs**, building a vocabulary of ~50,000 subword units (common words become single tokens; rare words split into pieces). Sentences become much **shorter** in tokens, rare words are handled gracefully, and the model spends its capacity on meaning rather than spelling. The cost: you must ship a pretrained tokenizer.

[[fig:bpe-tokenization]]

> [!ANALOGY] Character tokens are like spelling out every word letter by letter over the phone - universal but slow. BPE is like having agreed-upon shorthand for common words - you say far less to convey the same thing, and you can still spell out an unusual name when needed.

| | Character-level (nanoGPT) | BPE subword (GPT-2) |
|---|---|---|
| Vocabulary size | ~95 | ~50,000 |
| Tokens per sentence | many (long) | few (short) |
| Library needed | none | pretrained tokenizer (e.g. tiktoken) |
| Rare words | spelled out, robust | split into subword pieces |
| Transparency | total | opaque merges |

> [!KEY] The tokenisation choice is a genuine trade-off: it directly affects **sequence length**, how much **model capacity** is spent on spelling vs meaning, and the **quality** of generation. Smaller vocab = simpler setup but longer sequences; larger vocab = efficient sequences but a tokenizer dependency.

> [!WARNING] With character-level models, a single unicode character that slips past the ASCII filter can poison generation (garbage output). Filtering to the ~95-char ASCII set is part of the data pipeline, not an afterthought.

notes.heading: LoRA: Freeze the Giant, Train a Tiny Patch
notes.body:
Full fine-tuning updates **every** weight of a pretrained model - for GPT-2 that is all 117M parameters, and you must store a whole new 500 MB copy per task. **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)** avoids this by freezing the original weights and learning a small, low-rank *correction*.

For a frozen pretrained weight matrix $W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, LoRA computes:

$$h = W_0 x + \Delta W x = W_0 x + BA\,x, \qquad \Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r}\,BA$$

Glossing every symbol: $x$ is the input vector to this layer; $W_0$ is the **frozen** pretrained weight matrix (never updated); $h$ is the layer output; $\Delta W$ is the **learned correction** we add. That correction is built from two skinny matrices - $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}$ (squeezes the $d$-dimensional input down to a tiny $r$ dimensions) and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ (expands it back to $d$). The number $r$ is the **rank**, and crucially $r \ll d$ ('much smaller than'); $\alpha$ is a scaling constant.

[[fig:lora]]

> [!INTUITION] You are not rewriting the textbook (that is $W_0$, frozen). You are adding a thin sticky-note correction $BA$ on top. Because the note must pass through a narrow $r$-dimensional bottleneck, it cannot say much - just enough to *redirect* existing knowledge toward the new domain, not relearn it.

**Why this saves so many parameters.** A full update $\Delta W$ for a $768 \times 768$ matrix would be $768 \times 768 = 589{,}824$ trainable numbers. LoRA with $r = 8$ trains only $A$ ($8 \times 768 = 6{,}144$) plus $B$ ($768 \times 8 = 6{,}144$) $= 12{,}288$ - about **48x fewer**. Across all of GPT-2 that is ~700K trainable params out of 117M, just **0.6%**.

> [!MATH] $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r} BA$ reads as: 'the weight change equals a small scale factor times (tall-skinny B) times (short-wide A).' Multiplying a $d\times r$ by an $r\times d$ gives back a full $d\times d$ shape, but it can only ever be rank $r$ - a deliberately limited, low-rank patch.

> [!KEY] LoRA = $W_0$ frozen + trainable low-rank $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r}BA$, with $r \ll d$. Train <1% of parameters; keep the original knowledge intact.

notes.heading: LoRA Details That Matter: Initialisation, alpha/r Scaling, and Targets
notes.body:
Two design choices make LoRA train smoothly, and both are favourite exam targets.

**1. Initialisation so you start exactly at the pretrained model.** $A$ is initialised from a small random Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, but $B$ is initialised to **all zeros**. Since $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r}BA$ and $B = 0$, at the very first step $\Delta W = 0$. Here $\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ just means 'random numbers centred at 0 with small spread $\sigma$'.

> [!INTUITION] By starting the correction at exactly zero, the model's first predictions are *identical* to the original pretrained model - we have changed nothing yet. Training then gently grows the correction from there, so fine-tuning never 'kicks' the model away from its good starting point. (If both $A$ and $B$ were random, the model would start corrupted and have to recover.)

**2. The $\alpha/r$ scaling keeps the update size stable across ranks.** Without it, doubling $r$ would roughly double the magnitude of $BA$, forcing you to re-tune the learning rate every time you change rank. Dividing by $r$ (and multiplying by a chosen $\alpha$, usually set equal to $r$) keeps the effective update magnitude roughly constant, so you can sweep $r$ without re-tuning.

**3. Which layers get LoRA (target modules).** The original paper applies LoRA only to the attention query and value projections, $W_Q$ and $W_V$. Adding keys, the output projection, and the feed-forward (MLP) layers raises quality but costs more parameters - a knob you turn when quality plateaus.

| LoRA hyperparameter | Typical range | Default recommendation |
|---|---|---|
| Rank $r$ | 4-64 | start at 8, sweep if budget allows |
| $\alpha$ | usually $= r$ | set equal to $r$ |
| Dropout | 0.05-0.1 | 0.05 for most tasks |
| Target modules | q,v -> all | add more if quality plateaus |
| Learning rate | 1e-4 - 3e-4 | higher than full fine-tuning |

> [!EXAM] Expect to be asked: 'Why is $B$ initialised to zero?' (so $\Delta W = 0$ at init -> training starts from the exact pretrained predictions) and 'What does $\alpha/r$ do?' (keeps update magnitude constant as $r$ changes, so the learning rate need not be re-tuned).

notes.heading: Deploying LoRA: Tiny Adapters, One Shared Base Model
notes.body:
The biggest practical win of LoRA shows up at deployment. After fine-tuning, the only new thing you save is the **adapter** - just the $A$ and $B$ matrices, around **2 MB**. The full fine-tuned model would be ~**500 MB**.

But there is a catch you must remember: the adapter is *only the correction*. To actually run inference you need the **frozen base model present too**, because the effective weight is reconstructed on the fly:

$$W_{\text{eff}} = W_{\text{frozen}} + BA$$

In words: the weight the model actually uses is the original pretrained weight plus the adapter's low-rank patch. Without $W_{\text{frozen}}$, the 2 MB of $A,B$ are meaningless.

> [!KEY] One pretrained base model can host **many** swappable domain adapters (one for Python, one for legal text, one for poetry...). You ship one big 500 MB base once, then distribute many 2 MB adapters - hugely efficient for storage and distribution.

> [!WARNING] The adapter must be paired with the **exact same base model variant** it was trained on. The debugging checklist lists 'adapter loads but no change' with the cause 'wrong base model' - mismatching GPT-2 sizes at train vs inference silently breaks it.

**Beyond LoRA - the PEFT family.** LoRA is one member of *parameter-efficient fine-tuning*:

| Method | Idea | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Prefix tuning | prepend learnable 'soft-prompt' tokens | ~0.1% params; less stable on small data |
| Adapter layers | insert a $d\to r\to d$ bottleneck MLP | more params than LoRA; adds inference latency |
| QLoRA | quantise base to 4-bit, then LoRA | fine-tunes 65B models on one 48GB GPU; production standard |
| DoRA | split $\Delta W$ into magnitude + direction | often beats LoRA at the same rank |

> [!EXAM] Why is shipping a 2 MB adapter better than a 500 MB full model, and what must be present at inference? Answer: the adapter stores only $A,B$; the frozen base model must also be loaded so $W_{\text{eff}} = W_{\text{frozen}} + BA$ can be formed. One base, many adapters.

notes.heading: Scaling Laws, Perplexity, and Reading the Training Curves
notes.body:
**Scaling laws** explain *why* bigger helps. Kaplan et al. (2020) found that a language model's loss falls along a smooth **power law** as you grow the number of parameters $N$, training tokens $D$, or compute $C$:

$$L(N) \approx \left(\frac{N_c}{N}\right)^{\alpha_N}$$

In words: the loss $L$ shrinks predictably as $N$ (model size) grows; $N_c$ and $\alpha_N$ are just constants fitted from experiments. The key message is that quality is *predictable* from scale - and the same shape holds for data $D$ and compute $C$.

**Chinchilla (2022)** refined this: for a fixed compute budget, you should **not** simply build the biggest model. Instead, train a somewhat smaller model on **more data** - parameters and tokens should scale **roughly equally**. nanoGPT (~3M params, ~1M tokens) and GPT-3 (175B params, 300B tokens) sit at the two ends of this curve.

> [!KEY] Kaplan: loss drops as a smooth power law in size, data, and compute. Chinchilla: data is as important as parameters - a smaller model trained on more tokens often beats a bigger under-trained one at the same compute.

**Perplexity** is how we report progress in human-readable units. It is just the exponential of the cross-entropy loss:

$$\text{PPL} = e^{H}$$

where $H$ is the cross-entropy (the average 'surprise', in nats, at each token). A perplexity of 10 means the model is, on average, as unsure as if it were choosing uniformly among 10 options. **Lower PPL = less surprised = better.** The session target is val PPL < 15 by step 1500.

> [!EXAMPLE] If validation cross-entropy loss is $H = 2.3$, then $\text{PPL} = e^{2.3} \approx 10$. If it is $H = 2.5$, $\text{PPL} = e^{2.5} \approx 12.2$ - both under the target of 15.

> [!WARNING] A telling failure mode: if training loss is **stuck at $\ln(\text{vocab})$** - e.g. $\ln(65) \approx 4.17$ for a 65-character vocab - the model is predicting a **uniform distribution** and is effectively not learning. The cause is usually that the model is not being updated (forgot `model.train()` or the optimizer step). Steadily *decreasing* loss but lingering spelling errors at 1500 steps is **normal** early word-level learning on a big corpus - just train longer (e.g. 3000 steps).

quiz.q: Using LoRA with rank $r=8$ on a single $768\times768$ weight matrix, how many trainable parameters does the adapter add for that matrix?
quiz.explanation: LoRA trains A ($8\times768=6{,}144$) and B ($768\times8=6{,}144$), totalling 12,288 - about 48x fewer than a full update. 589,824 is the full $768\times768$ update (the whole point of LoRA is to avoid that). 6,144 counts only one of the two matrices; 1,536 ($2\times768$) forgets the factor of $r$.

quiz.q: In LoRA, matrix $A$ is initialised from a small Gaussian while $B$ is initialised to all zeros. What is the purpose of setting $B=0$ at the start?
options: To make A and B symmetric so the update is guaranteed full-rank
To halve the memory needed to store the adapter
So the initial update $\Delta W = BA = 0$, making the model's first predictions identical to the pretrained model
To prevent the gradients of A from vanishing during the first epoch
quiz.explanation: With $B=0$, $\Delta W=\frac{\alpha}{r}BA=0$ at init, so fine-tuning begins from the exact pretrained predictions and grows the correction gently. It is not about memory (both matrices are stored regardless), and LoRA's update is intentionally low-rank, never forced full-rank.

quiz.q: The $\alpha/r$ scaling factor in $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r}BA$ exists primarily to:
options: Normalise the input $x$ before it enters the frozen layer
Increase the effective rank of the adapter beyond $r$
Keep the magnitude of the update roughly constant as the rank $r$ is changed, so the learning rate need not be re-tuned
Add dropout-like regularisation to the low-rank update
quiz.explanation: As $r$ grows, the raw product $BA$ tends to grow too; dividing by $r$ (with $\alpha$ usually set equal to $r$) keeps the update magnitude stable, so you can sweep ranks without re-tuning the learning rate. It does not change the rank (still exactly $r$), does not normalise inputs, and is not a regulariser.

quiz.q: Why is a decoder-only model like GPT trained with a causal (masked) self-attention pattern rather than the bidirectional attention used by BERT?
options: Each position must predict the next token using only earlier tokens; seeing future tokens would let it copy the answer
Causal masking reduces the number of parameters in the attention layer
Bidirectional attention cannot be computed on a GPU for long sequences
GPT needs cross-attention to an encoder, which requires masking
quiz.explanation: Autoregressive generation predicts token $t$ from tokens $1..t-1$; the causal mask hides future tokens so the model genuinely predicts instead of peeking. Masking changes which positions are visible, not the parameter count. Decoder-only GPT has no separate encoder, so there is no cross-attention involved.

quiz.q: nanoGPT uses pre-norm (LayerNorm before each sub-layer) instead of the original Transformer's post-norm. What is the main benefit?
options: It reduces the total number of parameters in each block
It keeps gradient magnitudes stable through depth, so the model trains reliably without careful warmup tuning
It allows a larger vocabulary without extra embedding parameters
It speeds up inference by skipping LayerNorm at test time
quiz.explanation: Pre-norm normalises a copy fed into the sub-layer while leaving the residual highway untouched, so gradients reach early layers intact and deep stacks train stably without warmup hacks. It does not change parameter count or vocabulary, and LayerNorm still runs at inference.

quiz.q: A nanoGPT validation cross-entropy loss settles at $H = 2.3$ nats. What is the approximate perplexity, and is it within the session target of PPL < 15?
options: About 0.1, which is within target
About 2.3, which is within target
About 23, which exceeds target
About 10, which is within target
quiz.explanation: Perplexity is $e^{H}=e^{2.3}\approx 10$, comfortably under 15. The other options confuse PPL with the loss itself (2.3), multiply by 10 (23), or invert the exponential (0.1).

quiz.q: You fine-tuned GPT-2 with LoRA and saved a 2 MB adapter. A teammate loads only that 2 MB file on a fresh machine and gets errors. What is missing?
options: Nothing - a LoRA adapter is fully self-contained and runs alone
The training dataset, which must be reloaded to reconstruct the weights
The optimizer state, without which the adapter cannot run a forward pass
The frozen pretrained base model, needed to form $W_{\text{eff}} = W_{\text{frozen}} + BA$ at inference
quiz.explanation: The adapter stores only $A$ and $B$; inference reconstructs $W_{\text{eff}}=W_{\text{frozen}}+BA$, so the frozen base model must be present. The dataset and optimizer state are training artefacts, irrelevant at inference. The adapter is explicitly not self-contained.

quiz.q: Which is a genuine advantage of GPT-2's BPE subword tokenisation over nanoGPT's character-level scheme?
options: It needs no tokenizer library, making setup simpler
Sentences become far shorter in tokens, so the model spends capacity on meaning rather than spelling
It uses a smaller vocabulary, reducing the embedding table
It is fully transparent, mapping each ASCII character to one integer
quiz.explanation: BPE merges frequent character pairs into subword units, so common words become single tokens and sequences are much shorter and more efficient. The 'no library', 'smaller vocabulary', and 'transparent ASCII' properties all describe character-level tokenisation, not BPE (BPE has a ~50K vocab and needs a pretrained tokenizer).

quiz.q: During nanoGPT training (vocab size 65), the loss is stuck at exactly 4.17 and never decreases. What does this most likely indicate?
options: The model is overfitting, so the loss has saturated
The model has converged to the optimal solution for this dataset
The model is predicting a near-uniform distribution and is not being updated - $\ln(65)\approx 4.17$
The learning rate is slightly too high, causing minor oscillation around the optimum
quiz.explanation: The cross-entropy of a uniform distribution over $V$ classes is $\ln(V)$; $\ln(65)\approx 4.17$ means the model assigns equal probability to every character - it is not learning (e.g. forgot model.train() or the optimizer step). A converged or overfit model would have *low* loss, not the uniform-baseline value.

quiz.q: Chinchilla (2022) revised the scaling-law guidance. For a fixed compute budget, what does it recommend?
options: Always pick the largest possible model regardless of how much data you have
Train a somewhat smaller model on more data - parameters and tokens should scale roughly equally
Keep the data fixed and grow only the parameter count
Ignore data size, since loss depends only on compute
quiz.explanation: Chinchilla showed that for a given compute budget many models were under-trained: a smaller model on more tokens often wins, so data and parameters should scale together. The other options describe the pre-Chinchilla 'just make it bigger' mindset that it corrected.

quiz.q: In LoRA, choosing a small rank $r \ll d$ for the update $\Delta W = BA$ primarily limits:
options: How expressive the weight update can be - it is constrained to a low-rank subspace
The maximum input sequence length the model can process
The vocabulary size the fine-tuned model can output
The learning rate that may be used during fine-tuning
quiz.explanation: A product $BA$ of a $d\times r$ and an $r\times d$ matrix can have rank at most $r$, so a small $r$ deliberately restricts the update to a low-rank subspace - few independent directions of change. Sequence length, vocabulary, and learning rate are unrelated to the rank of the adapter.

quiz.q: QLoRA differs from plain LoRA mainly in that it:
options: Quantises the frozen base model to 4-bit before applying LoRA, letting very large models fine-tune on a single GPU
Replaces the low-rank matrices with full-rank ones for higher quality
Trains the base weights and the adapters jointly
Removes the need for a base model at inference time
quiz.explanation: QLoRA quantises the frozen base to 4-bit and then trains LoRA adapters on top, dramatically cutting memory so even 65B models fit on one 48GB GPU. It does not make the update full-rank, still freezes the base (only adapters train), and still needs the base model at inference.

descriptive.q: Compare pretraining a model from scratch with fine-tuning a pretrained model using LoRA. What does each require, and when would you choose one over the other?
descriptive.answer:
**Pretraining from scratch** starts with **random weights** and trains on a large corpus by predicting the next token over and over. It builds knowledge from nothing, so it needs data **proportional to model size** plus large compute: nanoGPT (~10M params) needs only ~30MB of text and ~8 minutes on a T4, but GPT-2 (117M) was trained on ~40B tokens, and GPT-3 (175B) on 300B tokens.

**Fine-tuning with LoRA** starts from **already-pretrained weights**, freezes them, and injects small low-rank adapters $\Delta W = \frac{\alpha}{r}BA$ that are trained to redirect the model toward a new domain. Here $W_0$ stays frozen and only the tiny $A,B$ matrices learn - <1% of parameters - so it needs little data and little compute.

[[fig:pretrain-finetune]]

| | Pretraining from scratch | LoRA fine-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| Starting weights | random | pretrained (frozen) |
| Trainable params | all | <1% (low-rank adapters) |
| Data / compute | huge, scales with model size | small |
| Output to store | full model | ~2 MB adapter per task |

> [!KEY] Choose **pretraining from scratch** when no suitable pretrained model exists for your setting (novel architecture, very different modality, or a teaching exercise like nanoGPT). Choose **LoRA** whenever a good pretrained model already exists - you get domain adaptation for a fraction of the cost and can keep many lightweight adapters per base model.

descriptive.q: LoRA decomposes a weight update $\Delta W \in \mathbb{R}^{d\times d}$ as $BA$ with $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d\times r}$ and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{r\times d}$. Explain why this reduces the number of trainable parameters, and give exact counts for $d=768, r=8$.
descriptive.answer:
A full update would have one trainable number for **every entry** of the $d\times d$ matrix: $d^2$ parameters. LoRA instead writes the update as a product of two **skinny** matrices that pass information through a narrow rank-$r$ bottleneck, so the parameter count becomes $d\times r + r\times d = 2dr$ instead of $d^2$.
